topics.title: Sources of Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources of Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste** is defined as any **discarded material**, **abandoned material**, **rejected material**, or **residue** arising from **human and animal activities** that is *not free-flowing* (semi-solid, solid, or contained gaseous material) and is intended for **disposal, recovery, recycling, or reuse**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT861 – Module 1)** and aligned with the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000 (MSW Rules)**, solid waste can be broadly categorized by its **point of generation**, called the **source**.

### Sources — The KTU Standard Classification

| Sl. No. | Source Category | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Residential / Domestic | S1 |
| 2 | Commercial / Mercantile | S2 |
| 3 | Institutional | S3 |
| 4 | Construction & Demolition (C&D) | S4 |
| 5 | Industrial | S5 |
| 6 | Municipal Services / Street Sweeping | S6 |
| 7 | Biomedical / Healthcare Facilities | S7 |
| 8 | Agricultural | S8 |
| 9 | Mining & Mineral Processing | S9 |
| 10 | E-Waste (Electrical & Electronic) | S10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to identify each source, the **nature of waste generated**, **typical composition**, and the **appropriate management strategy** for each category.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city is like a human body**. Just as the body produces different kinds of waste (sweat, urine, CO₂, food scraps), a city produces different types of solid waste from different "organs" of its functioning:

- **Homes (Residential)** → Like the body's *digestive system*, producing **organic kitchen waste** and packaging.
- **Offices & Shops (Commercial)** → Like the *respiratory system*, exhaling **paper, cardboard, and packaging** constantly.
- **Hospitals (Biomedical)** → Like a *contagious zone* producing **infectious waste** that needs special handling (similar to quarantine waste).
- **Factories (Industrial)** → Like *industrial smoke*, generating **specialized chemical & process waste**.

The "**source**" is simply the **origin point** — the place where waste enters the management pipeline. Without identifying the source correctly, engineers cannot design proper collection routes, segregation protocols, or treatment methods.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Per capita waste generation (India, urban):** **0.5 kg/person/day** (CPCB standard reference).
> - **Density of MSW:** **400 – 600 kg/m³** (loose), **800 – 1000 kg/m³** (compacted).
> - **Average moisture content:** **40 – 60 %** (Indian urban MSW).
> - **Calorific value of MSW:** **1000 – 1500 kcal/kg** (varies with composition).
> - **Specific gravity of MSW:** **0.4 – 0.9** (compared to water = 1.0).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Source-Wise Classification

### 2.1.1 Source 1 — Residential / Domestic Solid Waste (S1)

This is the **single largest contributor** to the **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** stream in India (~50–60 % by mass).

- **Origin:** Single-family homes, apartments, hostels, residential colonies.
- **Typical Composition (by mass):**
  - **Organic / Biodegradable fraction:** 40 – 60 %
    - Kitchen waste (vegetable peels, leftover food).
    - Garden waste (leaves, grass).
  - **Recyclables:** 15 – 30 %
    - Paper, cardboard, plastics, glass, metals.
  - **Inerts:** 10 – 15 %
    - Construction debris, dust, sand.
  - **Hazardous Domestic Waste (HDW):** 1 – 5 %
    - Batteries, paints, e-waste, expired medicines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Residential waste has the **highest organic fraction**, making it ideal for **composting, anaerobic digestion (biogas)**, and **vermicomposting**.

### 2.1.2 Source 2 — Commercial / Mercantile Waste (S2)

- **Origin:** Shops, offices, hotels, restaurants, markets, malls.
- **Distinguishing Feature:** **High packaging fraction** (cardboard, plastics, thermocol).
- **Restaurant / Hotel Waste:** 70 – 80 % **organic** (food scraps) — excellent for **on-site biogas plants**.
- **Office Waste:** 60 – 80 % **paper** — ideal for **paper recycling**.

### 2.1.3 Source 3 — Institutional Waste (S3)

- **Origin:** Schools, colleges, government offices, religious places, prisons.
- **Composition:** Similar to commercial but with **lower per-capita generation**.
- **Special Concern:** Religious institutions generate **flower waste** (especially in Kerala during festivals), which is **biodegradable but bulky**, often causing **drainage blockages**.

### 2.1.4 Source 4 — Construction & Demolition Waste (C&D) (S4)

- **Origin:** Building construction, renovation, demolition sites.
- **Composition:** Concrete, bricks, tiles, wood, metals, plaster, glass.
- **Volume:** **~30 % of total urban solid waste globally** by volume.
- **KTU Note:** C&D waste is **inert**, so it is usually sent to **landfill (inert cell) or recycled** into aggregates.

### 2.1.5 Source 5 — Industrial Solid Waste (S5)

- **Origin:** Manufacturing plants, refineries, chemical industries.
- **Critical Categories:**
  - **Hazardous Waste:** As per **Hazardous & Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**.
  - **Non-hazardous / Process Waste:** Fly ash, slag, sludge, scrap metal.
- **Kerala Special Case:** Coir, cashew, tea, rubber, and spice-processing industries generate **large volumes of agro-industrial waste**.

### 2.1.6 Source 6 — Municipal Services Waste (S6)

- **Origin:** Street sweeping, drain cleaning, public park maintenance, **sewage treatment plant (STP) sludge**.
- **Generation Rate:** ~**0.1 – 0.2 kg/capita/day** (added on top of domestic).
- **Composition:** High **silt and inert fraction**.

### 2.1.7 Source 7 — Biomedical / Healthcare Waste (S7)

- **Origin:** Hospitals, clinics, blood banks, pathology labs, veterinary hospitals.
- **Generation Rate:** **0.5 – 2.0 kg/bed/day** (varies with hospital type).
- **Categories (per BMW Rules 2016, Amended 2018):**
  - **Yellow Bag** — Anatomical, soiled, chemical.
  - **Red Bag** — Contaminated recyclable plastic.
  - **White (Puncture-proof)** — Sharps (needles, scalpels).
  - **Blue Box** — Glassware, metallic body implants.

> [!WARNING]
> Biomedical waste is **never co-mingled** with MSW. It requires **dedicated handling, autoclaving/incineration**, and **bar-coded tracking** (as per BMWM Rules 2018).

### 2.1.8 Source 8 — Agricultural Solid Waste (S8)

- **Origin:** Farms, plantations, animal husbandry, fisheries.
- **Examples:** Crop residues (rice straw, sugarcane bagasse), animal dung, coconut husks, coir pith.
- **Significance for Kerala:** **Coconut husk, areca leaf, rubber wood waste, and pineapple leaf fiber** are major agricultural residues.
- **Valorization:** Vermicomposting, biogas, biomass pellets, paper-making, mulching.

### 2.1.9 Source 9 — Mining & Mineral Processing Waste (S9)

- **Origin:** Mines, quarries, mineral processing plants.
- **Examples:** Overburden, tailings, slag, red mud (from alumina industry).
- **Hazardous nature:** Often contains **heavy metals** (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr).
- **Major challenge:** **Acid Mine Drainage (AMD)**.

### 2.1.10 Source 10 — E-Waste (S10)

- **Origin:** Discarded computers, mobile phones, TVs, batteries, appliances.
- **Generation Rate (India, 2021 est.):** **~3.2 million tonnes/year**.
- **Governing Rule:** **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 (India)**.
- **Toxic constituents:** Pb, Cd, Hg, Be, BFRs (Brominated Flame Retardants).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Even though this topic is primarily descriptive, the following **engineering formulas** are essential for KTU numerical problems.

| Sl. No. | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Per Capita Waste Generation** | $q = \dfrac{W}{P \times t}$ | $\text{kg/person/day}$ |
| 2 | **Total MSW Generated in a City** | $W = q \times P \times t$ | $\text{kg/day}$ |
| 3 | **Volume of MSW (Loose)** | $V_L = \dfrac{W}{\rho_L}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ |
| 4 | **Volume of MSW (Compacted)** | $V_C = \dfrac{W}{\rho_C}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ |
| 5 | **Volume Reduction Factor** | $\text{VRF} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_C}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Density at Compaction** | $\rho_C = \rho_L \times \text{VRF}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 7 | **Moisture Content** | $M(\%) = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | **Specific Gravity** | $G_s = \dfrac{\rho_{waste}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Component-wise Mass** | $W_i = W_{total} \times \dfrac{\%_i}{100}$ | $\text{kg/day}$ |
| 10 | **Number of Collection Trips** | $N_{trips} = \dfrac{W_{day}}{C_{vehicle} \times n_{compactors}}$ | Trips/day |

**Where:**
- $q$ = per capita waste generation rate.
- $W$ = total waste generated.
- $P$ = population served.
- $t$ = time period (usually 1 day).
- $\rho_L, \rho_C$ = loose and compacted densities.
- $W_w, W_d$ = wet and dry weight of waste sample.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planning:** Per-capita generation is used to size **landfills, transfer stations, and composting plants**.
- **Vehicle Routing:** Determines the **number and capacity** of collection vehicles.
- **Circular Economy:** Source-based data enables **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** programs for plastics and e-waste.
- **Kerala Context:** The **Suchitwa Mission** and **Kerala State Waste Management Policy (2022)** rely on **source segregation at the household level (S1)** as the foundational step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Per Capita Waste Generation Rate

The **per capita waste generation rate ($q$)** is the foundational engineering parameter of every solid waste management system. Below is the formal KTU-style derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{day} &= \text{Total waste generated per day (kg/day)} \\[4pt]
P_{n} &= \text{Number of persons in the served zone} \\[4pt]
q &= \text{Per capita waste generation rate (kg/person/day)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Establish the Fundamental Relation

By **definition**, per capita generation is the **total waste divided by the population served per unit time**:

$$
q = \dfrac{W_{day}}{P_{n} \times 1}
$$

### Step 3 — Time Normalization

If waste is measured over $t$ days (not necessarily 1 day):

$$
W_{day} = \dfrac{W_{t}}{t}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
q = \dfrac{W_{t}}{P_{n} \times t}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for per capita waste generation.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A town has a population of **85,000** and generates **42.5 tonnes** of waste per day. Calculate the per capita waste generation rate.

**Step 4a — Convert units to SI:**

$$
W_{day} = 42.5 \text{ tonnes/day} = 42{,}500 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 4b — Substitute into the formula:**

$$
q = \dfrac{W_{day}}{P_{n}} = \dfrac{42{,}500 \text{ kg/day}}{85{,}000 \text{ persons}}
$$

**Step 4c — Simplify:**

$$
q = 0.5 \text{ kg/person/day}
$$

This **exactly matches the CPCB reference value** for Indian urban areas, confirming the result is realistic.

### Step 5 — Reverse Calculation (Design Problem)

**Problem:** A new apartment complex houses **1,200 residents**. The CPCB per capita rate is **0.5 kg/person/day**. Calculate the **daily waste generation** and the **volume of a container** (density of MSW = 500 kg/m³).

**Step 5a — Daily mass:**

$$
W_{day} = q \times P_{n} = 0.5 \times 1{,}200 = 600 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 5b — Daily volume (loose):**

$$
V_L = \dfrac{W_{day}}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{600 \text{ kg/day}}{500 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 5c — Practical container sizing:**

Assuming waste is stored for **3 days** before collection:

$$
V_{3\,day} = 1.2 \times 3 = 3.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

Therefore, a **4 m³ closed container** is sufficient.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Source-Wise Composition (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Source | Dominant Waste Type | Typical % Organic | Density (kg/m³) | Treatment Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S1 Residential** | Kitchen + Packaging | 40 – 60 % | 100 – 200 (loose) | Composting, AD, Vermicomposting |
| **S2 Commercial** | Paper + Plastic | 20 – 40 % | 80 – 150 | Recycling, MRF |
| **S3 Institutional** | Paper + Food | 30 – 50 % | 100 – 180 | Composting + Recycling |
| **S4 C&D** | Concrete, Tiles | <5 % | 1,200 – 1,800 | Crushing, Reuse as aggregate |
| **S5 Industrial** | Process Sludge, Slag | Variable | 600 – 1,500 | Secured Landfill, Recycle |
| **S6 Municipal** | Silt, Sweepings | 20 – 35 % | 400 – 600 | Inert Landfill |
| **S7 Biomedical** | Anatomical, Sharps | <10 % | 200 – 300 | Incineration, Autoclaving |
| **S8 Agricultural** | Crop Residue, Manure | 80 – 95 % | 150 – 300 | Biogas, Vermicompost, Pellets |
| **S9 Mining** | Tailings, Overburden | <2 % | 1,500 – 2,500 | Backfilling, Stabilization |
| **S10 E-Waste** | Plastics + Metals + Glass | <10 % | 100 – 500 | Dismantling, Hydrometallurgy |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Volume Reduction Factor (VRF)

This is **frequently asked** in KTU ESE numericals.

### Step 1 — Definition

The **Volume Reduction Factor** is the **ratio of the loose volume of waste to its compacted volume**:

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_C}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $V = W/\rho$

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{W/\rho_L}{W/\rho_C}
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $W$

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{\rho_C}{\rho_L}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical

**Given:** $\rho_L = 200 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_C = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{800}{200} = 4
$$

**Interpretation:** Compaction **reduces the volume by a factor of 4**. This is critical for **landfill life extension**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Waste Flow from Source to Disposal

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    segB[Source Segregation Point]:::seg

    srcA --> segB
    segB --> colC
    colC --> trfD

    mrfE --> bioG
    mrfE --> recI

    colC --> lndH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Examiners:** The figure illustrates the **material flow** from each source (S1–S10) through **segregation, collection, transfer, processing, and final disposal**. Each source contributes a different **material fraction** (organic, recyclable, hazardous, inert) which is **routed differently** in the flow diagram — emphasizing the **core engineering rationale** for source-wise classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain any six sources of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six major sources of MSW are:

1. **Residential / Domestic (S1):** Kitchen waste, paper, plastics, glass from households. Largest contributor (~50–60 %).
2. **Commercial / Mercantile (S2):** Waste from shops, offices, hotels, restaurants — high in paper and packaging.
3. **Institutional (S3):** Waste from schools, colleges, government offices, religious places.
4. **Construction & Demolition (S4):** Concrete, bricks, wood, metal from construction sites.
5. **Industrial (S5):** Process sludge, fly ash, slag from manufacturing units.
6. **Biomedical (S7):** Anatomical waste, sharps, contaminated plastics from hospitals — needs special handling.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Key: Award **0.5 mark per correctly named source** with a one-line description.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Define solid waste. Mention any four characteristics of MSW.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Solid waste is any discarded, abandoned, or rejected material arising from human and animal activities in solid, semi-solid, or contained gaseous form, which is intended for disposal (per MSW Rules 2000).

**Four characteristics of MSW:**
1. **Heterogeneous mixture** — contains organic, inorganic, recyclable, and inert fractions.
2. **High moisture content** — typically 40–60 % in Indian MSW.
3. **Low calorific value** — 1000–1500 kcal/kg (compared to coal ~4000 kcal/kg).
4. **Variable composition** — depends on source (urban vs rural, season, income level).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Key: **1 mark for definition**, **2 marks for four characteristics (0.5 each)**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 1 internal choice questions are of **14 marks total**, typically split into **7 + 7 marks** sub-parts. Two alternative choices are provided below.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the sources of solid waste in detail. Explain the characteristics and typical composition of waste from **Residential** and **Commercial** sources.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A city has a population of **2,50,000** and generates MSW at the rate of **0.45 kg/capita/day**. The loose density is **180 kg/m³** and compacted density is **720 kg/m³**. Calculate:
  (i) Total daily waste generated (in tonnes).
  (ii) Volume of waste in loose and compacted state.
  (iii) Volume Reduction Factor (VRF).
  (iv) Number of compactor trucks of **5 m³ capacity** needed if each makes **2 trips per day**.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Classification, Residential & Commercial Sources [7 Marks]

**Classification of Sources of Solid Waste:**

Solid waste sources are classified into **10 categories** (S1–S10) per the KTU syllabus:

1. **S1 – Residential**
2. **S2 – Commercial**
3. **S3 – Institutional**
4. **S4 – Construction & Demolition**
5. **S5 – Industrial**
6. **S6 – Municipal Services**
7. **S7 – Biomedical**
8. **S8 – Agricultural**
9. **S9 – Mining & Mineral**
10. **S10 – E-Waste**

**Characteristics & Composition of Residential Waste (S1):**

- **Major contributor** to MSW stream (~50–60 % by mass).
- **Composition (by mass):**
  - Organic / Kitchen waste: **40–60 %**
  - Paper & Cardboard: **10–15 %**
  - Plastics, Glass, Metals: **10–15 %**
  - Inerts (dust, debris): **10–15 %**
  - Hazardous household waste: **1–5 %**
- **Moisture Content:** High — **40–60 %**
- **Treatment:** Composting, Anaerobic Digestion, Vermicomposting.

**Characteristics & Composition of Commercial Waste (S2):**

- Generated from offices, shops, hotels, restaurants, markets.
- **Dominant fractions:**
  - Paper (in offices): **60–80 %** of total.
  - Food waste (in hotels/restaurants): **70–80 % organic**.
  - Packaging plastics: **10–20 %**.
- **Low moisture** compared to residential if from offices; **high moisture** if from restaurants.
- **Treatment:** Paper recycling, on-site biogas (for hotels), Material Recovery Facility (MRF).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Population, $P_n = 2{,}50{,}000$
- Per capita generation, $q = 0.45 \text{ kg/capita/day}$
- Loose density, $\rho_L = 180 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Compacted density, $\rho_C = 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Compactor capacity, $V_{truck} = 5 \text{ m}^3$
- Trips per day per truck, $n = 2$

**(i) Total Daily Waste Generated:**

$$
W_{day} = q \times P_n = 0.45 \times 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
W_{day} = 1{,}12{,}500 \text{ kg/day} = 112.5 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**(ii) Volume of Waste:**

*Loose Volume:*

$$
V_L = \dfrac{W_{day}}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{1{,}12{,}500}{180} = 625 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

*Compacted Volume:*

$$
V_C = \dfrac{W_{day}}{\rho_C} = \dfrac{1{,}12{,}500}{720} = 156.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**(iii) Volume Reduction Factor (VRF):**

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_C} = \dfrac{625}{156.25} = 4.0
$$

Alternative check:

$$
\text{VRF} = \dfrac{\rho_C}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{720}{180} = 4.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iv) Number of Compactor Trucks:**

Total compacted volume to be lifted per day = **156.25 m³/day**.

Volume lifted by **one truck in one day**:

$$
V_{1 truck} = V_{truck} \times n = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

Therefore, **number of trucks**:

$$
N_{trucks} = \dfrac{V_C}{V_{1 truck}} = \dfrac{156.25}{10} = 15.625
$$

Since we cannot have a fraction of a truck, we **round up**:

$$
\boxed{N_{trucks} = 16 \text{ trucks/day}}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 1 Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the nature and management challenges of **Biomedical** and **E-Waste** as sources of solid waste. Highlight relevant rules in India.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential colony has **500 households** with an average of **4 persons per household**. The waste generation rate is **0.50 kg/capita/day**. If the organic fraction is **55 %**, the recyclable fraction is **30 %**, and the inert fraction is **15 %**:
  (i) Calculate the **total daily waste** and the **mass of each fraction**.
  (ii) If organic waste is composted with a **volume reduction of 80 %** in composting (density of compost = 700 kg/m³), determine the **volume of compost produced per day**.
  (iii) How many **days** will the composting process take if the pit capacity is **5 m³**?

---

#### Solution to (a) — Biomedical and E-Waste [7 Marks]

**Biomedical Waste (S7):**

- **Origin:** Hospitals, clinics, pathology labs, veterinary facilities, blood banks.
- **Categories (per BMWM Rules 2018):**
  - **Yellow** — Anatomical, soiled, chemical waste.
  - **Red** — Contaminated recyclable plastics.
  - **White (Puncture-proof)** — Sharps, needles, scalpels.
  - **Blue** — Glassware, metallic implants.
- **Generation Rate:** **0.5 – 2.0 kg/bed/day**.
- **Hazards:** Infectious (HIV, Hepatitis B), cytotoxic, radioactive.
- **Treatment:** Incineration (for pathological waste), autoclaving (for culture & stocks), shredding & recycling (for plastics after disinfection).
- **Rules:** **Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016 (Amended 2018)** mandate segregation at source, bar-coded tracking, and **no co-mingling with MSW**.

**E-Waste (S10):**

- **Origin:** Discarded computers, mobile phones, TVs, batteries, electrical appliances.
- **Generation Rate (India):** ~**3.2 million tonnes/year** (2021 estimate).
- **Toxic Constituents:** Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Mercury (Hg), BFRs (Brominated Flame Retardants), Beryllium (Be).
- **Hazards:** Soil & groundwater contamination via leachate; respiratory illness from informal recycling.
- **Rules:** **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** mandate **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)**, collection centers, and **target-based回收 (recycling) goals** for producers.
- **Management Strategy:** Formal dismantling → **Hydrometallurgical / Pyrometallurgical** recovery of precious metals (Au, Ag, Pd, Cu) → Safe disposal of residue.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Households = 500
- Persons/household = 4
- Total population = 500 × 4 = **2000 persons**
- Per capita generation, $q = 0.50 \text{ kg/person/day}$
- Organic = 55 %, Recyclable = 30 %, Inert = 15 %
- Composting volume reduction = 80 %
- Compost density, $\rho_{compost} = 700 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Pit capacity = 5 m³

**(i) Total Daily Waste & Mass of Each Fraction:**

*Total daily mass:*

$$
W_{day} = q \times P_n = 0.50 \times 2000 = 1000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

*Mass of each fraction:*

$$
W_{organic} = 1000 \times 0.55 = 550 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
W_{recyclable} = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
W_{inert} = 1000 \times 0.15 = 150 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Volume of Compost Produced:**

Mass of organic waste = **550 kg/day**.

Since **80 % volume reduction** occurs during composting, **20 %** of the original volume remains as compost.

*Step 1 — Original volume of organic waste:*

Assume loose density of organic waste ≈ **400 kg/m³** (standard for kitchen waste).

$$
V_{organic} = \dfrac{550}{400} = 1.375 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

*Step 2 — Volume after 80 % reduction:*

$$
V_{compost} = V_{organic} \times (1 - 0.80) = 1.375 \times 0.20 = 0.275 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Check using compost density:**

$$
V_{compost} = \dfrac{W_{organic} \times 0.20}{\rho_{compost}} = \dfrac{550 \times 0.20}{700} = 0.157 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two values differ because the **loose organic density** and **final compost density** are not the same. The second value (using compost density) is more accurate and accepted in KTU solutions. We will use:

$$
\boxed{V_{compost} = 0.157 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}
$$

**(iii) Days to Fill the Composting Pit:**

$$
\text{Days} = \dfrac{V_{pit}}{V_{compost}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ m}^3}{0.157 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}} \approx 31.85 \text{ days}
$$

Rounding up to ensure the pit is not over-filled:

$$
\boxed{\text{Time to fill pit} \approx 32 \text{ days}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Most students forget to convert **tonnes → kg** or **m³ → litres**, costing **1–2 marks** easily.
> 2. **Rounding rule** — For *number of trucks/containers*, always **round UP** (ceiling function), never round down. A fraction of a truck cannot operate!
> 3. **Formula substitution** — In VRF problems, write **both forms** ($V_L/V_C$ and $\rho_C/\rho_L$) for **self-verification** and earn the **method mark** even if arithmetic has a slip.
> 4. **Definition marks** — KTU examiners allot **1 mark** purely for the **correct formal definition** of solid waste. Do not skip this in long answers.
> 5. **Composition percentages** — Memorize the **typical range** (e.g., organic = 40–60 % in residential MSW) rather than a single number — this shows analytical depth and earns **full marks** in descriptive parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sources of Solid Wastes (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Solid waste = any discarded, abandoned, rejected, or residue material from human/animal activities, in **non-free-flowing** form.
- **Classification:** **10 standard sources** (S1–S10): Residential, Commercial, Institutional, C&D, Industrial, Municipal Services, Biomedical, Agricultural, Mining, E-Waste.
- **Residential (S1):** Largest MSW contributor (~50–60 %). High organic (40–60 %) → ideal for composting & biogas.
- **Commercial (S2):** High paper & packaging fraction. Restaurant waste is highly organic.
- **Institutional (S3):** Includes schools, offices, religious places. **Kerala special:** heavy flower waste during festivals.
- **C&D (S4):** Mostly inert — concrete, bricks, tiles. 30 % of total urban waste by volume.
- **Industrial (S5):** Hazardous + non-hazardous. Governed by **HOWM Rules, 2016**.
- **Municipal Services (S6):** Street sweepings, silt, STP sludge. Added on top of domestic.
- **Biomedical (S7):** 4-bin segregation (Yellow, Red, White, Blue). Governed by **BMWM Rules, 2016 (Amended 2018)**. **Never co-mingle with MSW.**
- **Agricultural (S8):** Crop residue, manure, coir, husk. Highly biodegradable. **Kerala-specific**: coconut, areca, rubber waste.
- **Mining (S9):** Tailings, overburden, red mud. Causes **Acid Mine Drainage (AMD)**.
- **E-Waste (S10):** Phones, computers, batteries. Governed by **E-Waste Rules, 2022**. Contains Pb, Cd, Hg, BFRs.
- **Per Capita Generation Formula:** $q = W / (P_n \times t)$ → typical Indian value = **0.5 kg/capita/day**.
- **VRF Formula:** $\text{VRF} = V_L / V_C = \rho_C / \rho_L$ → typical MSW VRF = **3 to 4**.
- **Indian Standard References:** **MSW Rules 2000**, **BMWM Rules 2016/2018**, **E-Waste Rules 2022**, **HOWM Rules 2016**, **CPCB Guidelines**.
- **Kerala-Specific Initiatives:** **Suchitwa Mission**, **Haritha Keralam Mission**, **Kerala State Waste Management Policy (2022)**, **Clean Kerala Mission**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** *"RCC IMA BAME"* → **R**esidential, **C**ommercial, **C**onstruction, **I**nstitutional, **M**unicipal, **A**gricultural, **B**iomedical, **A**nd **M**ining **E**-waste — covers all 10 sources in order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature and Characteristics - Types of Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nature and Characteristics - Types of Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Solid Waste

As per **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT861)** and standard Environmental Engineering nomenclature, **Solid Waste** can be defined as:

> **Solid Waste** is any unwanted or discarded material that is not a liquid or a gas, generated from human activities, industrial operations, agricultural processes, or institutional activities, and has lost its original utility or value to the owner.

According to the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** (and subsequent amendments), solid waste includes *"any solid or semi-solid material which is discarded from residential, commercial, institutional, or industrial establishments."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course PECHT861 explicitly focuses on **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** as the primary subject, with industrial, biomedical, and hazardous wastes treated as specialized streams. For board examinations, give maximum weightage to MSW characteristics.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Byproduct of Living"

Imagine you are cooking in a kitchen, packing lunch for school, or repairing a bicycle. Every activity produces something you cannot use anymore — vegetable peels, plastic wrappers, broken spokes. **Solid waste is simply the "footprint of human life."** Just as a body sheds dead cells, society sheds the materials it consumes. The challenge of Solid Waste Management (SWM) is essentially deciding *what to do with the shed skin of civilization.*

- **Producer** → consumes resources and generates waste.
- **Nature** → has cycles (water cycle, carbon cycle) to handle its own waste.
- **Humans** → must design *engineered cycles* (reduce → reuse → recycle → dispose) to handle theirs.

### 1.3 Categorical Overview of Solid Waste Types

Solid wastes are broadly classified into the following streams:

| # | Type | Typical Examples | Generators |
|---|------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Food waste, paper, plastics, glass | Households, offices, streets |
| 2 | **Industrial Solid Waste** | Slag, fly ash, red mud, chemicals | Factories, refineries |
| 3 | **Biomedical/Hospital Waste** | Needles, blood-soiled cotton, pharmaceuticals | Hospitals, labs, clinics |
| 4 | **Hazardous Waste** | Pesticides, heavy metals, solvents | Industries, chemical plants |
| 5 | **Electronic Waste (E-Waste)** | Mobile phones, batteries, PCBs | Households, IT industries |
| 6 | **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** | Concrete, bricks, steel scrap | Construction sites |
| 7 | **Agricultural Waste** | Crop residues, animal dung | Farms, agro-industries |
| 8 | **Radioactive Waste** | Spent fuel rods, contaminated materials | Nuclear plants, hospitals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):**
> MSW is the everyday waste collected by the municipal authority from residential, commercial, institutional, and street-cleaning operations. It is also called *urban solid waste* or *trash*. **It is the single largest fraction handled by SWM systems globally.**

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic forms the **foundation** for every subsequent module — collection, transportation, processing, and disposal. Board questions frequently test:
1. Classification of waste by **source** (most frequent).
2. Physical characteristics like **moisture content, density, calorific value**.
3. Chemical composition of MSW (**organic vs. inorganic split**).
4. Differences between **MSW, hazardous, and biomedical waste**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Solid Waste — Three Reference Axes

Solid waste is classified along **three orthogonal axes** that KTU examiners commonly test:

#### A. Classification by Source (Most Common in Boards)

1. **Residential / Domestic Waste** — Generated from households.
2. **Commercial Waste** — From shops, hotels, restaurants, offices.
3. **Institutional Waste** — From schools, colleges, government offices.
4. **Street Sweeping Waste** — Road dust, leaves, litter.
5. **Construction & Demolition Waste** — Excavated material, debris.
6. **Industrial Waste** — Process-specific residues.
7. **Biomedical Waste** — From healthcare facilities.
8. **Hazardous Waste** — Toxic, reactive, flammable, corrosive.

#### B. Classification by Type (Physical Nature)

- **Organic (Biodegradable)** — Food waste, yard waste, paper.
- **Recyclable (Non-Biodegradable but recoverable)** — Plastics, glass, metals.
- **Inert** — Construction debris, soil.
- **Hazardous** — Toxic chemicals, medical sharps.

#### C. Classification by Hazard Potential (Critical for Rules)

| Category | Hazard Level | Examples | Disposal Method |
|----------|--------------|----------|-----------------|
| **General Waste** | Low | Paper, food | Landfill, composting |
| **Inert Waste** | Negligible | Concrete, soil | Sanitary landfill |
| **Hazardous Waste** | High | Chemicals, e-waste | Secured landfill, incineration |

### 2.2 Characteristics of Solid Waste — The 4 Pillars

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists the following characteristics for evaluation:

#### **Pillar 1: Physical Characteristics**

1. **Density (kg/m³)** — Mass per unit volume. Crucial for vehicle design.
2. **Moisture Content (%)** — Mass of water per unit mass of wet waste.
3. **Particle Size & Distribution** — Affects compaction behavior.
4. **Permeability** — Important for leachate flow in landfills.
5. **Compactibility** — Volume reduction under applied pressure.

#### **Pillar 2: Chemical Characteristics**

1. **Proximate Analysis** — Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash.
2. **Ultimate Analysis** — %C, %H, %O, %N, %S, %Ash.
3. **Calorific Value (kJ/kg or kcal/kg)** — Energy content.
4. **pH** — Acidic / Alkaline nature.
5. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — For organic fraction.

#### **Pillar 3: Biological Characteristics**

1. **Biodegradability** — Rate of decomposition by microbes.
2. **C/N Ratio** — Carbon-to-Nitrogen ratio; **ideal: 25–35:1** for composting.
3. **Presence of Pathogens** — Especially in biomedical waste.

#### **Pillar 4: Engineering Properties**

1. **Compressibility** — Under landfill overburden.
2. **Shear Strength** — Slope stability of landfills.
3. **Hydraulic Conductivity** — Leachate flow rate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are tested frequently. Memorize the unit conventions.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Symbols} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
\text{Moisture Content} & MC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 & W_w=\text{wet wt}, W_d=\text{dry wt} & \% \\
\hline
\text{Density} & \rho = \dfrac{M}{V} & M=\text{mass}, V=\text{volume} & \text{kg/m}^3 \\
\hline
\text{Per Capita Waste} & q = \dfrac{\text{Total Waste}}{\text{Population} \times \text{Days}} & q = \text{generation rate} & \text{kg/capita/day} \\
\hline
\text{Energy Content} & CV = \dfrac{\text{Heat released}}{\text{Mass burnt}} & \text{Calorific Value} & \text{kJ/kg} \\
\hline
\text{Volume Reduction} & VR = \dfrac{V_i - V_f}{V_i} \times 100 & V_i, V_f = \text{initial, final vol} & \% \\
\hline
\text{C/N Ratio} & C/N = \dfrac{\text{\% Carbon}}{\text{\% Nitrogen}} & \text{ratio (dimensionless)} & - \\
\hline
\text{Bulk Density} & \rho_b = \dfrac{M_{loose}}{V_{container}} & \text{loose/uncompacted} & \text{kg/m}^3 \\
\hline
\end{array}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute values in formulas. Board evaluators in KTU 2024 mark **schemes explicitly deduct marks** for unit inconsistencies.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Moisture Content** determines whether **incineration** (needs < 50% MC) or **composting** (needs 50–60% MC) is appropriate.
- **Calorific Value** dictates the energy recovery potential through **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** plants.
- **Density** governs **collection truck design** and frequency.
- **C/N Ratio** decides if **compost produced** will be stable and marketable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Moisture Content Calculation

The moisture content (MC) of a solid waste sample is determined gravimetrically by oven-drying at **103 °C – 105 °C** for 24 hours.

**Starting Relationship (Definition):**
$$
MC = \frac{\text{Mass of Water}}{\text{Total Mass of Wet Sample}} \times 100
$$

**Step 1:** The mass of water present is the difference between the **wet mass** and the **dry mass** (after evaporation of water).

$$
\text{Mass of Water} = W_w - W_d
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this into the defining equation.

$$
MC = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Worked Numerical (KTU-style):

> A wet MSW sample weighs **2.50 kg**. After oven drying at 105 °C for 24 hours, it weighs **1.20 kg**. Calculate the moisture content.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_w &= 2.50 \text{ kg} \\
W_d &= 1.20 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mass of water} &= W_w - W_d = 2.50 - 1.20 = 1.30 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC &= \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.30}{2.50} \times 100 \\
&= 52.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since **MC > 50%**, this waste is unsuitable for direct incineration; **biological treatment** (composting/anaerobic digestion) is preferred.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Per Capita Waste Generation Rate

**Definition:** Per capita waste generation (q) is the average amount of waste produced per person per day.

**Step 1:** Identify input variables.

$$
\text{Total Waste Generated} = M \;\; (\text{kg/day})
$$
$$
\text{Population served} = P \;\; (\text{people})
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the definition.

$$
q = \frac{M}{P} \;\; [\text{kg/capita/day}]
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical Example (typical KTU question):

> A town with population **5,00,000** generates **250 tonnes** of MSW per day. Compute the per capita generation. Comment on the design of collection vehicles.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 250 \text{ tonnes/day} = 250{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
P &= 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ people} \\
q &= \frac{M}{P} = \frac{250{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.50 \;\; \text{kg/capita/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This value (0.5 kg/capita/day) is typical for **Tier-2 Indian cities**. For a collection truck with capacity **5 tonnes**, it would serve approximately:

$$
N_{trips} = \frac{250 \text{ tonnes/day}}{5 \text{ tonnes/trip}} = 50 \text{ trips/day}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Composition Analysis (Proximate Basis)

The **proximate analysis** of MSW sums to **100%** and partitions the waste into four engineering fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moisture Content} + \text{Volatile Matter (VM)} + \text{Fixed Carbon (FC)} + \text{Ash} = 100 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Determine moisture content by oven drying.
**Step 2:** Determine volatile matter by ignition at **950 °C** in a covered crucible (loss in mass minus moisture).
**Step 3:** Determine ash by ignition at **750 °C** in an open crucible.
**Step 4:** Fixed Carbon by difference:

$$
FC = 100 - (MC + VM + \text{Ash})
$$

**Worked Example:**

> A proximate analysis of MSW gives: MC = 45%, VM = 38%, Ash = 12%. Find Fixed Carbon.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC &= 100 - (45 + 38 + 12) \\
&= 100 - 95 \\
&= 5 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Composition of MSW in Indian Cities

| Component | % by Weight (Typical Indian MSW) | Nature | Treatment Route |
|-----------|----------------------------------|--------|-----------------|
| **Organic (food + yard)** | 40 – 60 % | Biodegradable | Composting / AD |
| **Paper** | 6 – 12 % | Recyclable | Paper recycling |
| **Plastics** | 8 – 15 % | Non-bio, recyclable | Recycling / WtE |
| **Glass** | 2 – 5 % | Inert, recyclable | Glass industry |
| **Metals** | 1 – 3 % | Recyclable | Smelting |
| **Textile / Rubber** | 2 – 5 % | Variable | Reuse / WtE |
| **Inerts (soil, stone)** | 10 – 30 % | Non-reactive | Sanitary landfill |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For Indian cities, organic fraction is **40–60%**. For Western/developed cities, it is only **25–35%**. This is a frequently tested comparison.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Classification of Solid Waste — Hierarchical Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Solid Waste] --> B[Municipal Solid Waste MSW]
    A --> C[Industrial Solid Waste]
    A --> D[Biomedical Waste]
    A --> E[Hazardous Waste]
    A --> F[Electronic Waste E-Waste]
    A --> G[Construction and Demolition Waste]
    A --> H[Agricultural Waste]
    A --> I[Radioactive Waste]
    B --> B1[Residential Domestic]
    B --> B2[Commercial]
    B --> B3[Institutional]
    B --> B4[Street Sweeping]
    C --> C1[Non Hazardous Industrial]
    C --> C2[Hazardous Industrial]
    D --> D1[Infectious]
    D --> D2[Pathological]
    D --> D3[Sharps]
    D --> D4[Pharmaceutical]
    D --> D5[Chemical]
    E --> E1[Ignitable]
    E --> E2[Corrosive]
    E --> E3[Reactive]
    E --> E4[Toxic]
    F --> F1[Large Appliances]
    F --> F2[IT Equipment]
    F --> F3[Mobile Phones]
    F --> F4[Batteries]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Sources of MSW

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Population Center] --> R[Residential Areas]
    P --> CM[Commercial Zones]
    P --> IN[Institutional Buildings]
    P --> ST[Streets and Public Spaces]
    P --> CN[Construction Sites]
    R --> W[Collected MSW Stream]
    CM --> W
    IN --> W
    ST --> W
    CN --> W
    W --> T[Transfer Station]
    T --> PR[Processing Plant]
    T --> LF[Sanitary Landfill]
```
