topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) management, **Sampling and Analysis** refers to the systematic collection, preservation, and characterization of representative waste fractions to determine their physical, chemical, and biological properties. These properties form the foundational database required for designing collection systems, treatment plants, sanitary landfills, incinerators, and resource recovery units.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECHT861, Module 1 specifically addresses the *quantitative* and *qualitative* characterization of solid waste, which is the **first and most critical step** in any integrated solid waste management (ISWM) hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master the standard methodologies prescribed by agencies such as the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, **ASTM**, and the **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)** for sampling and characterization of MSW.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing medicine, you collect blood samples, urine samples, and run lab tests. The patient's health cannot be treated based on guesswork — you need *data*.

Solid waste management operates on exactly the same principle:

- **The Waste** is the "patient."
- **The Sampling** is the act of collecting a small but representative portion (like drawing blood).
- **The Analysis** is the lab test that reveals what the waste is made of (its "blood report").

Without proper sampling, engineers cannot answer critical questions:
- How much waste is generated per day?
- Is the waste biodegradable (wet) or non-biodegradable (dry)?
- How much energy can be recovered through incineration?
- Will the waste leachate contaminate groundwater if landfilled?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A common misconception is that "more sample = better accuracy." In reality, what matters is **representativeness** — the sample must accurately reflect the entire waste stream in composition and proportion. A poorly collected 100 kg sample is worse than a properly collected 1 kg sample.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Solid Waste Characterization

The following standard parameters are universally used in solid waste characterization:

- **Moisture Content (MC):** Typically 20% to 60% for Indian MSW (high during monsoon).
- **Volatile Solids (VS):** Usually 40% to 60% of dry weight for mixed MSW.
- **Calorific Value (CV):** Ranges from **800 to 2000 kcal/kg** for Indian MSW (lower than Western MSW).
- **Bulk Density:** **300 to 500 kg/m³** for loose, uncompacted MSW in Indian conditions.
- **pH of Leachate:** Generally **5.5 to 7.5** for fresh MSW.
- **C:N Ratio (Carbon to Nitrogen):** Typically **20:1 to 30:1** for mixed organic MSW.

### 1.4 Importance in Engineering Practice

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, sampling and analysis data directly feeds into:
1. **Design of composting plants** (requires C:N ratio, moisture, VS).
2. **Design of biogas/anaerobic digesters** (requires biodegradable fraction).
3. **Design of incinerators** (requires calorific value, moisture content).
4. **Landfill design** (requires leachate characterization, bulk density).
5. **Policy decisions** by municipal corporations regarding waste segregation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Objectives of Sampling (KTU High-Yield Point)

The primary objectives of solid waste sampling are:

1. **To determine the quantity** of waste generated (per capita waste generation rate).
2. **To determine the composition** (physical components like paper, plastic, food waste, glass, metals).
3. **To determine the characteristics** (physical, chemical, biological).
4. **To assess the variability** of waste over time and space.
5. **To obtain data** for designing treatment and disposal systems.

### 2.2 Types of Sampling Methods

The methodology of sampling solid waste is broadly classified into:

#### A. Grab Sampling (Single / Spot Sampling)
- A single portion of waste is collected at one specific time and location.
- **Advantages:** Quick, simple, low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Not representative of temporal variations; only reflects that moment.
- **Use:** Initial surveys, spot checks, when the waste stream is relatively homogeneous.

#### B. Composite Sampling
- Multiple grab samples are collected at different times/locations and then physically mixed to form a single representative sample.
- **Advantage:** Better representativeness over time.
- **Disadvantage:** Loses information about temporal variability.

#### C. Quartering Technique
- A large sample is spread uniformly on a clean, flat surface (circular or square).
- Divided into four equal quarters.
- Two opposite quarters are retained; the other two are discarded.
- The retained portion is mixed and re-quartered until a manageable sample size is achieved.
- **Standard Reference:** Used extensively in CPCB protocols for MSW characterization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction Ratio in Quartering:** The minimum sample size after quartering must be at least **1 kg per 100 kg of original sample** to maintain representativeness for heterogeneous Indian MSW.

#### D. Composite Incinerator Sample
- Used specifically for determining calorific value.
- Sample must be representative of the waste that would actually be fed to an incinerator (i.e., after removal of non-combustibles like glass, metals, and inert materials).

### 2.3 Sample Preservation and Storage

Solid waste samples, once collected, must be preserved to prevent biological/chemical changes:

- **Temperature:** Store at **4°C ± 2°C** if analysis is delayed beyond 24 hours.
- **Containers:** Use **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** or **glass containers** with airtight lids.
- **Time Limits:**
  - Moisture content: Analyze within **24 hours**.
  - Volatile solids: Analyze within **7 days**.
  - Calorific value: Air-dry the sample and analyze as soon as practical.

### 2.4 Physical Characterization of MSW

Physical characterization includes:

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Range (Indian MSW) |
|-----------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Bulk Density** | Mass per unit volume of waste as collected | 300–500 kg/m³ |
| **Moisture Content** | Weight loss on drying at 103–105°C | 20–60% |
| **Particle Size** | Sieve analysis for size distribution | Variable |
| **Component Density** | Density of individual components (paper, plastic, etc.) | 50–1500 kg/m³ |

### 2.5 Chemical Characterization

Chemical analysis covers:

- **Proximate Analysis:** Moisture, Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), Ash Content.
- **Ultimate Analysis:** Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S), Chlorine (Cl).
- **Calorific Value:** Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV).
- **Leachate Analysis:** pH, COD, BOD, TDS, heavy metals.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear repeatedly in KTU University Examinations.**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $\text{Moisture Content (\%)} = \dfrac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1} \times 100$ | $W_1$ = initial wet weight; $W_2$ = oven-dry weight (at 103–105°C for 24 h) | % |
| $\text{Volatile Solids (\%)} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_3)}{W_2} \times 100$ | $W_3$ = weight after ignition at 550°C for 1 h | % |
| $\text{Ash Content (\%)} = \dfrac{W_3}{W_2} \times 100$ | Mineral residue after combustion | % |
| $\text{Fixed Carbon (\%)} = 100 - (\% \text{VM} + \% \text{Ash})$ | By difference | % |
| $\text{Bulk Density} = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | $M$ = mass; $V$ = volume of container | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $\text{Calorific Value (LHV)} = \text{HHV} - 6 \times (9H + M)$ | Approx. correction where H = %Hydrogen, M = %Moisture | $\text{kcal/kg}$ |
| $\text{Per Capita Waste} = \dfrac{\text{Total Waste Collected}}{\text{Population} \times \text{Days}}$ | MSW generation rate | $\text{kg/capita/day}$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Composting Plant Design:** Moisture content (must be 50–60% optimum) and C:N ratio (25:1 to 30:1 optimum) are derived from sample analysis.
- **Incinerator Design:** Calorific value > 1500 kcal/kg indicates waste can sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel.
- **Landfill Sizing:** Bulk density determines compaction and landfill airspace requirements.
- **Leachate Treatment:** Leachate COD and BOD guide the selection of treatment processes (e.g., activated sludge vs. membrane bioreactor).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Moisture Content Determination

**Principle:** Moisture content is determined gravimetrically by measuring the weight loss of a sample after oven drying to a constant weight.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Weigh an empty, clean, dry crucible. Let its mass be $W_c$.

$$W_c = \text{mass of empty crucible (g)}$$

**Step 2:** Place a representative portion of the freshly collected waste sample into the crucible.

**Step 3:** Weigh the crucible with the wet sample immediately.

$$W_1 = \text{mass of crucible} + \text{wet sample (g)}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the mass of the wet sample alone.

$$W_{\text{wet}} = W_1 - W_c$$

**Step 5:** Place the crucible in a hot air oven at **103°C to 105°C** for **24 hours** (or until constant weight is achieved).

**Step 6:** Remove the crucible, cool it in a **desiccator** to prevent moisture reabsorption from ambient air.

**Step 7:** Weigh the crucible with the dried sample.

$$W_2 = \text{mass of crucible} + \text{dry sample (g)}$$

**Step 8:** Calculate the mass of the dry sample.

$$W_{\text{dry}} = W_2 - W_c$$

**Step 9:** Apply the moisture content formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Moisture Content (\%)} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{(W_1 - W_c) - (W_2 - W_c)}{W_1 - W_c} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_c} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

Given: $W_c = 25.40 \text{ g}$, $W_1 = 78.65 \text{ g}$, $W_2 = 49.20 \text{ g}$ (after oven drying).

Compute:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_1 - W_c &= 78.65 - 25.40 = 53.25 \text{ g} \\
W_2 - W_c &= 49.20 - 25.40 = 23.80 \text{ g} \\
\text{MC (\%)} &= \dfrac{53.25 - 23.80}{53.25} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{29.45}{53.25} \times 100 \\
&= 55.31 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

This result (≈55%) is characteristic of fresh Indian MSW, especially during monsoon or post-monsoon seasons.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Volatile Solids and Ash Content

**Principle:** After moisture removal, the dry sample is combusted in a muffle furnace at **550°C ± 50°C** for **1 hour**. Organic matter burns off (volatilizes), leaving behind inorganic ash.

**Step 1:** Use the dry sample from the moisture determination step (mass = $W_2 - W_c$).

**Step 2:** Place the crucible in a muffle furnace at 550°C for 60 minutes.

**Step 3:** Cool the crucible in a desiccator to prevent moisture pickup.

**Step 4:** Weigh the crucible with the ash.

$$W_3 = \text{mass of crucible} + \text{ash (g)}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the ash mass.

$$W_{\text{ash}} = W_3 - W_c$$

**Step 6:** Calculate Volatile Solids:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VS (\%)} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{dry}} - W_{\text{ash}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{(W_2 - W_c) - (W_3 - W_c)}{W_2 - W_c} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{W_2 - W_3}{W_2 - W_c} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate Ash Content:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ash (\%)} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{ash}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{W_3 - W_c}{W_2 - W_c} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8:** Calculate Fixed Carbon (by difference):

$$\text{FC (\%)} = 100 - \text{VS (\%)} - \text{Ash (\%)}$$

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Calorific Value Correction (HHV to LHV)

The Higher Heating Value (HHV) is measured in a **bomb calorimeter**, where the water vapor from combustion is condensed and the latent heat is recovered.

In real-world incinerators, flue gas exits at high temperatures, and water vapor escapes uncondensed. Therefore, the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** is the practically usable energy.

**Standard Correction Formula:**

$$\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 6 \times (9H + M)$$

Where:
- HHV = Higher Heating Value (kcal/kg)
- HHV = percent hydrogen in the waste
- $M$ = percent moisture in the waste
- $6$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (approximately 6 kcal/% per kg in this empirical form)

> [!NOTE]
> Some references use **5.85** instead of **6** as the latent heat factor. Both are accepted approximations in KTU examinations; state the assumption clearly.

**Numerical Example:**

Given: HHV = 3200 kcal/kg, H = 6%, M = 40%.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHV} &= 3200 - 6 \times (9 \times 6 + 40) \\
&= 3200 - 6 \times (54 + 40) \\
&= 3200 - 6 \times 94 \\
&= 3200 - 564 \\
&= 2636 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

Since this value (2636 kcal/kg) is significantly lower than the HHV (3200 kcal/kg), it confirms why high-moisture waste is unsuitable for incineration without pre-drying.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison

| Parameter | Method | Standard Reference | Temperature | Duration |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|-------------|----------|
| Moisture Content | Oven Drying | IS 9234, ASTM D2216 | 103–105°C | 24 h |
| Volatile Solids | Muffle Furnace Ignition | IS 9235, ASTM E872 | 550°C | 1 h |
| Ash Content | Muffle Furnace | IS 9235 | 550–600°C | 1–2 h |
| Calorific Value (HHV) | Bomb Calorimeter | IS 1350, ASTM D5865 | Adiabatic | — |
| Bulk Density | Weighed Container | ASTM E1109 | Ambient | Immediate |
| pH | pH Meter (Leachate) | APHA 4500-H+ | 25°C | Immediate |
| COD | Dichromate Reflux | APHA 5220-B | Reflux 2 h | 2 h |
| BOD | Winkler / Respirometric | APHA 5210-B | 20°C | 5 days |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Sampling Workflow for Solid Waste

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Waste Source and Type] --> B[Define Sampling Objective]
    B --> C[Select Sampling Method]
    C --> D[Collect Grab Samples from Multiple Locations]
    D --> E[Composite Sample Formation]
    E --> F[Quartering for Size Reduction]
    F --> G[Sub-Sample Division for Different Tests]
    G --> H[Physical Analysis]
    G --> I[Chemical Analysis]
    G --> J[ Biological Analysis]
    H --> K[Moisture, Bulk Density, Particle Size]
    I --> L[Proximate, Ultimate, Calorific Value]
    J --> M[BOD, COD, Biodegradability]
    K --> N[Data Compilation and Reporting]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Engineering Design Inputs]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Analytical Test Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Representative Solid Waste Sample] --> B[Physical Tests]
    A --> C[Chemical Tests]
    A --> D[ Biological Tests]
    B --> B1[Moisture Content]
    B --> B2[Volatile Solids]
    B --> B3[Ash Content]
    B --> B4[Fixed Carbon]
    B --> B5[Bulk Density]
    B --> B6[Particle Size Distribution]
    C --> C1[pH of Leachate]
    C --> C2[Calorific Value HHV and LHV]
    C --> C3[Ultimate Analysis C H N S O]
    C --> C4[COD]
    C --> C5[Heavy Metals]
    D --> D1[BOD at 20 degrees C for 5 days]
    D --> D2[Biogas Yield Potential]
    D --> D3[Microbial Count]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Quartering Procedure (Visual Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large Composite Sample on Flat Surface] --> B[Mix Thoroughly to Homogenize]
    B --> C[Form Circular or Square Heap]
    C --> D[Divide into 4 Equal Quarters]
    D --> E[Retain 2 Opposite Quarters]
    D --> F[Discard 2 Opposite Quarters]
    E --> G[Re-mix Retained Sample]
    G --> H{Sample Size Still Large?}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I[Final Representative Sample for Analysis]
    F --> J[Discarded Portion]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Sample Collection to Design Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[MSW Collection Vehicle Arrives] --> P2[Weigh Total Load]
    P2 --> P3[Random Grab Sampling at Multiple Points]
    P3 --> P4[Composite Sample of 50 to 100 kg]
    P4 --> P5[Quartering to Reduce to 5 to 10 kg]
    P5 --> P6[Sub-sample A for Moisture VS Ash]
    P5 --> P7[Sub-sample B for Calorific Value]
    P5 --> P8[Sub-sample C for Leachate Tests]
    P6 --> Q1[Physical Characterization Data]
    P7 --> Q2[Energy Recovery Data]
    P8 --> Q3[Environmental Impact Data]
    Q1 --> R[Integrated Database for Design]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    R --> S[Composting Plant or Incinerator or Landfill Design]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Moisture Content in MSW. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content in Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) is defined as the weight loss observed when a representative waste sample is dried in a hot air oven at a temperature of **103°C to 105°C** until a constant weight is achieved, expressed as a percentage of the original wet weight of the sample.

$$\text{MC (\%)} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_c} \times 100$$

Where $W_c$, $W_1$, $W_2$ are masses of empty crucible, crucible + wet sample, and crucible + dry sample respectively. For Indian MSW, moisture content typically ranges from **20% to 60%**, depending on season and waste type.

#### Question 2: What is the difference between Grab Sampling and Composite Sampling? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
**Grab Sampling** involves collecting a single portion of waste at one specific time and location. It is quick, inexpensive, and useful for preliminary or spot assessments, but it does not capture temporal variability.

**Composite Sampling** involves collecting multiple grab samples at different times and/or locations and physically mixing them into a single representative sample. It provides better representativeness over time and is the preferred method for detailed engineering characterization of MSW, especially when variability is high.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **forget to subtract the crucible mass** when calculating dry/wet/ash weights, leading to incorrect percentages. Always show the full equation.
> - In calorific value problems, students often **confuse HHV and LHV**. Remember: HHV is measured in the bomb calorimeter; LHV is the practically usable value in real incinerators.
> - Avoid mixing units. Use **kg** or **g** consistently throughout a calculation.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of sampling solid waste. Discuss the quartering technique with a neat sketch. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Solid waste sampling methods include:

1. **Grab Sampling:** A single portion collected at one time/location. Fast and economical but not representative of variability.

2. **Composite Sampling:** Multiple grabs combined into one sample. Better representativeness but loses temporal resolution.

3. **Quartering Technique:** Used to reduce large samples to manageable, representative sizes without losing proportionality. The procedure is:
   - Spread the composite sample uniformly on a clean flat surface.
   - Divide into four equal quarters.
   - Retain two diagonally opposite quarters; discard the other two.
   - Mix the retained portion thoroughly.
   - Repeat the quartering process until the desired sample size (typically 5–10 kg) is obtained.
   - This method ensures that each component fraction retains its original proportion.

4. **Composite Incinerator Sample:** A specific preparation where non-combustibles (glass, metals) are excluded, mimicking actual incinerator feed.

**(b)** A solid waste sample collected from a municipal bin has the following data: empty crucible weight = 32.50 g; crucible + wet sample = 124.75 g; after oven drying at 105°C, crucible + dry sample = 72.40 g. Calculate the moisture content. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.
$W_c = 32.50 \text{ g}$, $W_1 = 124.75 \text{ g}$, $W_2 = 72.40 \text{ g}$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of the wet sample.
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{wet}} &= W_1 - W_c \\
&= 124.75 - 32.50 \\
&= 92.25 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the mass of the dry sample.
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{dry}} &= W_2 - W_c \\
&= 72.40 - 32.50 \\
&= 39.90 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the moisture content formula.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MC (\%)} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{92.25 - 39.90}{92.25} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{52.35}{92.25} \times 100 \\
&= 56.75 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The moisture content of the sample is **56.75%**, which is high and characteristic of fresh organic-rich Indian MSW, particularly during the monsoon season.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks):** [Alternative Module Internal Choice]

**(a)** What is meant by Proximate Analysis of solid waste? List its components and explain how Volatile Solids are determined. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Proximate Analysis** is a chemical characterization technique that determines four key parameters of solid waste: **Moisture Content, Volatile Solids (VS), Fixed Carbon (FC), and Ash Content**.

The components are:
- **Moisture Content (MC):** Water present in waste, removed at 103–105°C.
- **Volatile Solids (VS):** Organic matter that combusts at 550°C.
- **Ash Content:** Inorganic residue left after combustion.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Calculated by difference: FC = 100 − (VS + Ash).

**Determination of Volatile Solids:**

**Step 1:** Take the oven-dried sample (from moisture determination).

**Step 2:** Place in a muffle furnace at **550°C ± 50°C** for **1 hour**.

**Step 3:** Cool in a desiccator to prevent moisture absorption.

**Step 4:** Weigh the residue (ash).

**Step 5:** Apply:

$$\text{VS (\%)} = \dfrac{W_{\text{dry}} - W_{\text{ash}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$$

**(b)** A waste sample has HHV of 2800 kcal/kg, hydrogen content of 5%, and moisture content of 45%. Calculate the Lower Heating Value (LHV). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** HHV = 2800 kcal/kg, H = 5%, M = 45%.

**Formula:**
$$\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 6 \times (9H + M)$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHV} &= 2800 - 6 \times (9 \times 5 + 45) \\
&= 2800 - 6 \times (45 + 45) \\
&= 2800 - 6 \times 90 \\
&= 2800 - 540 \\
&= 2260 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The LHV of 2260 kcal/kg indicates that the waste has sufficient energy content for **self-sustaining combustion in an incinerator** (typical threshold: 1500 kcal/kg). However, the high moisture (45%) means pre-drying may be beneficial to improve combustion efficiency.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation**

- **Sampling is the first and most critical step** in solid waste management. Without representative sampling, all downstream design is unreliable.
- **Grab Sampling** = one shot, one place. **Composite Sampling** = multiple shots mixed. **Quartering** = size reduction technique.
- **Moisture Content** is determined by oven drying at **103–105°C for 24 hours** until constant weight.
- **Volatile Solids** are determined by ignition in a **muffle furnace at 550°C for 1 hour**.
- **Ash Content** is the inorganic residue after combustion; **Fixed Carbon** is calculated by difference.
- **HHV** is measured in a bomb calorimeter; **LHV** is calculated using the empirical correction formula.
- **Indian MSW** typically has high moisture (20–60%), moderate calorific value (800–2000 kcal/kg), and bulk density of 300–500 kg/m³.
- **C:N Ratio** of 25:1 to 30:1 is optimum for composting; derived from ultimate analysis.
- **Preservation rule:** Store samples at **4°C ± 2°C** if analysis is delayed beyond 24 hours; use HDPE containers.
- **Standard references** to memorize for KTU exams: **IS 9234, IS 9235, IS 1350, ASTM D2216, ASTM E872, APHA Standard Methods**.
- Always show **crucible mass subtraction** in MC/VS/Ash calculations — examiners deduct marks for incomplete equations.
- **Quartering** retains two opposite quarters and discards the other two; repeat until 5–10 kg final sample is obtained.
- **Units consistency** is critical: do not mix kg and g; convert all to one unit before substituting.
- For incinerator feed: **LHV > 1500 kcal/kg** indicates self-sustained combustion without auxiliary fuel.
- The **bulk density** of waste affects landfill airspace calculations and compaction requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Solid Wastes - Energy Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Solid Wastes - Energy Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Content (Calorific Value)** of solid waste is defined as the total amount of heat energy released when a unit mass of waste material undergoes complete combustion with oxygen under standardized laboratory conditions, expressed typically in **kJ/kg**, **MJ/kg**, **kcal/kg**, or **BTU/lb**. In the context of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) management, energy content is the single most decisive physico-chemical parameter that determines whether a given waste stream is amenable to **energy recovery through thermal treatment** processes such as incineration, refuse-derived fuel (RDF) combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, or anaerobic digestion.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the energy content is broadly classified into two principal categories:

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** or **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)**: The total heat released assuming all water vapor produced during combustion is condensed back to liquid form, thus recovering the latent heat of vaporization of water.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** or **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**: The heat released assuming the water vapor remains in the gaseous state (no latent heat recovery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Characteristics of Solid Wastes):**
> Energy content is a **physical characteristic** of solid waste that governs the design of incinerators, mass-burn systems, RDF plants, and the economic feasibility of Waste-to-Energy (WtE) projects. It is a function of the **proximate composition** (moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash) and **ultimate composition** (carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur) of the waste.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Solid Waste as a Hidden Battery"**
>
> Imagine your kitchen waste mixed with garden leaves as a **partially charged chemical battery**. When you burn dry wood, it releases tremendous heat — that is the "stored electrical energy" in the battery being rapidly discharged. However, if the waste is **wet (high moisture)**, it behaves like a **drained battery** — burning it is difficult, inefficient, and most of the energy is wasted in evaporating the water rather than producing useful heat.
>
> The **energy content** is the **charge level** of this "waste battery." Engineers measure it to decide: *"Is this waste worth burning for energy, or should it be composted/landfilled?"*
>
> - **Dry paper, plastics, dry leaves** = Fully charged battery (High energy content, 15–20 MJ/kg)
> - **Food waste, wet kitchen waste** = Drained battery (Low energy content, 3–6 MJ/kg)
> - **Wood, textiles, rubber** = Half-charged battery (Medium energy content, 10–18 MJ/kg)

## 1.3 Why Energy Content is a Critical Design Parameter

The energy content determines:

1. **Type of thermal treatment** suitable (incineration vs. composting vs. landfilling).
2. **Auxiliary fuel requirements** (does the waste need co-firing with coal/biomass?).
3. **Plant throughput and incinerator sizing**.
4. **Economic viability** of waste-to-energy projects.
5. **Emission characteristics** (higher energy content → higher flame temperatures → higher NOₓ).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Threshold for Autogenous Combustion:**
> For municipal solid waste to burn **without auxiliary fuel support**, its calorific value should be at least **1200 kcal/kg (≈ 5.0 MJ/kg)**. Indian municipal waste, due to high moisture (40–60%) and high inert content, often falls **below** this threshold, which is why most Indian WtE plants use **refuse-derived fuel (RDF)** prepared by removing moisture and inerts.

## 1.4 Typical Energy Content Values of Common Waste Components

| Waste Component | Energy Content (MJ/kg, dry basis) |
|---|---|
| Plastics (mixed) | 30 – 40 |
| Rubber / Tyres | 25 – 35 |
| Paper / Cardboard | 15 – 18 |
| Textiles (cotton) | 16 – 18 |
| Wood / Yard waste (dry) | 14 – 18 |
| Food waste (dry) | 12 – 18 |
| Leather | 17 – 20 |
| Garden waste (wet) | 6 – 10 |
| Food waste (wet, as discarded) | 3 – 5 |
| Inert (glass, metal, ash) | ≈ 0 |
| Typical Indian MSW (as generated) | 4 – 8 |
| Typical European / US MSW | 9 – 13 |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of Energy Content Measurement

The energy content of solid waste is determined experimentally using an **adiabatic bomb calorimeter** under standard conditions (ASTM D5865, IS 1350). The procedure involves:

1. A known mass of waste (≈ 1 g) is pelletized and placed inside a sealed stainless-steel "bomb."
2. The bomb is pressurized with **pure oxygen (≈ 25–30 atm)**.
3. The sample is ignited electrically.
4. The released heat is absorbed by a known mass of water surrounding the bomb.
5. The temperature rise of water is precisely measured.
6. The calorific value is calculated as:

$$Q = \frac{m_w \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T + C_{bomb} \cdot \Delta T}{m_{sample}}$$

where:
- $m_w$ = mass of water (kg)
- $c_w$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K)
- $C_{bomb}$ = heat capacity of bomb calorimeter (kJ/K)
- $m_{sample}$ = mass of waste sample (kg)

## 2.2 Energy Content from Ultimate Analysis (Dulong's Equation)

When experimental determination is not feasible, the calorific value can be **estimated** from the ultimate (elemental) composition of waste using **Dulong's empirical formula**:

$$HHV \; (\text{MJ/kg}) = 0.338 \cdot C + 1.428 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \cdot S$$

where $C$, $H$, $O$, and $S$ are the mass percentages of **carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur** respectively on a dry, ash-free basis.

## 2.3 Energy Content from Proximate Analysis

For quick field estimates, the calorific value can be approximated from the **proximate composition**:

$$HHV \; (\text{kcal/kg}) \approx 81.4 \cdot C_{fix} + 344.6 \cdot VM - 43 \cdot M - 25 \cdot A$$

where:
- $C_{fix}$ = % Fixed Carbon
- $VM$ = % Volatile Matter
- $M$ = % Moisture
- $A$ = % Ash

## 2.4 Higher vs. Lower Heating Value Relationship

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \cdot \left(\frac{9 \cdot H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right) \cdot 1000 \; (\text{in kJ/kg})$$

$$LHV = HHV - 5.85 \cdot \left(\frac{9 \cdot H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right) \; (\text{in MJ/kg})$$

where $H$ = % hydrogen, $M$ = % moisture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **2.442 MJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C. The term $\frac{9H}{100}$ accounts for water formed from hydrogen combustion (since 1 g of H produces 9 g of H₂O).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bomb Calorimeter Heat Release | $Q = \frac{m_w c_w \Delta T + C_{bomb} \Delta T}{m_{sample}}$ | kJ/kg | Experimental method |
| 2 | Dulong's Formula (Ultimate Analysis) | $HHV = 0.338C + 1.428\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095S$ | MJ/kg | Most accurate empirical estimate |
| 3 | LHV from HHV | $LHV = HHV - 2.442\left(\frac{9H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right) \times 10^{-3}$ | MJ/kg | Standard correction |
| 4 | Proximate Analysis Approximation | $HHV \approx 81.4 C_{fix} + 344.6\,VM - 43\,M - 25\,A$ | kcal/kg | Field-level estimation |
| 5 | Stoichiometric Oxygen for Combustion | $O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ | kg O₂/kg waste | For combustion air calculations |
| 6 | Stoichiometric Air | $Air = 4.312 \cdot \left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O\right)$ | kg air/kg waste | Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass |
| 7 | Energy Recovery Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{LHV_{waste} \times m_{waste}}{E_{aux} + LHV_{waste} \times m_{waste}} \times 100$ | % | For WtE plant analysis |
| 8 | Threshold for Autogenous Combustion | $HHV_{min} \geq 5.0 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ (1200 kcal/kg) | MJ/kg | Design rule of thumb |
| 9 | Moisture Correction to HHV | $HHV_{as-fired} = HHV_{dry} \times (1 - M/100) - 2.442 \cdot M/100$ | MJ/kg | As-received basis |
| 10 | Energy from Methane (Anaerobic Digestion) | $E_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8$ | MJ/m³ | 1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 35.8 MJ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Incinerator Design**: The calorific value determines the thermal load (MW) of the combustion chamber, refractory design, and waste residence time.
2. **Co-firing Decisions**: Wastes with HHV < 5 MJ/kg are typically co-fired with coal/biomass; wastes with HHV > 12 MJ/kg may need temperature control to avoid refractory damage.
3. **RDF Quality Grading**: RDF pellets are graded (e.g., IS 17614, CEN 343) based on calorific value — Class 1 RDF > 15 MJ/kg.
4. **Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery**: The methane yield from anaerobic digestion of waste is predictable from organic carbon content, which is linked to calorific value.
5. **Carbon Credit Accounting**: Energy recovery displaces fossil fuel use, generating carbon credits under CDM/Verra protocols.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dulong's Equation (Theoretical Foundation)

Dulong's formula is derived from the principle that the heat of combustion of an organic compound is the sum of the **heats of combustion of its constituent elements** minus the heat absorbed in converting any pre-existing oxygen into water. For each element per unit mass:

- **Carbon** → CO₂ releases **33.8 MJ/kg** of C
- **Hydrogen** → H₂O releases **142.8 MJ/kg** of H
- **Sulfur** → SO₂ releases **9.5 MJ/kg** of S

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Oxygen Correction:**
> If a fuel contains pre-existing oxygen, that oxygen is assumed to be **already combined with hydrogen** (as in cellulose, sugars, alcohols). Since 1 kg of "O" can convert 1/8 kg of H to H₂O (because 2H + O → H₂O, molar ratio 2:1 = 2:16 = 1:8), the effective hydrogen available for energy release is reduced by O/8.
>
> The **effective hydrogen** for combustion is therefore: $H_{eff} = H - \frac{O}{8}$

Thus, by the law of additivity of bond energies:

$$HHV = 0.338 \cdot C + 1.428 \cdot H_{eff} + 0.095 \cdot S$$

$$\boxed{HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S \; \text{MJ/kg}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LHV–HHV Relationship

When hydrogen in the fuel burns, the water formed is initially in the vapor state. If we cool this vapor and condense it (HHV condition), we recover the **latent heat of vaporization**, $L_v = 2{,}442$ kJ/kg = **2.442 MJ/kg of water**.

Per 1 kg of waste, the mass of water formed is:
- From hydrogen combustion: $\frac{9H}{100}$ kg (since 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O)
- From pre-existing moisture: $\frac{M}{100}$ kg

Total water formed per kg of waste = $\frac{9H + M}{100}$ kg

Heat recovered only in HHV (not in LHV) = $2.442 \times \frac{9H + M}{100}$ MJ/kg

Therefore:

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \cdot \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right) \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right) \; \text{MJ/kg}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — HHV from Ultimate Analysis

**Problem:** A sample of municipal solid waste has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis):
- Carbon (C) = 45.2%
- Hydrogen (H) = 6.1%
- Oxygen (O) = 32.0%
- Nitrogen (N) = 1.4%
- Sulfur (S) = 0.5%
- Ash (A) = 14.8%

Calculate the **HHV** using Dulong's formula.

**Step 1 — Identify the input parameters:**

$$C = 45.2, \quad H = 6.1, \quad O = 32.0, \quad S = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dulong's formula:**

$$HHV = 0.338 \cdot C + 1.428 \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \cdot S$$

**Step 3 — Compute the carbon contribution:**

$$0.338 \times 45.2 = 15.278 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective hydrogen:**

$$H_{eff} = 6.1 - \frac{32.0}{8} = 6.1 - 4.0 = 2.1 \; \%$$

**Step 5 — Compute the hydrogen contribution:**

$$1.428 \times 2.1 = 2.999 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the sulfur contribution:**

$$0.095 \times 0.5 = 0.0475 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Sum all contributions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 15.278 + 2.999 + 0.0475 \\
&= 18.324 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{HHV \approx 18.32 \; \text{MJ/kg} \; (\approx 4{,}378 \; \text{kcal/kg})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is typical of dried, RDF-grade waste with low inert content. It indicates that this waste is **highly suitable for incineration** with no auxiliary fuel required.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — LHV Calculation

Continuing from Example 1, suppose the waste has a **moisture content of 25%** (as-fired basis). Calculate the LHV.

**Step 1 — Note the HHV from previous example:**

$$HHV = 18.32 \; \text{MJ/kg} \; \text{(dry basis)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply moisture correction to obtain HHV on as-fired basis:**

$$HHV_{as-fired} = HHV_{dry} \times \left(1 - \frac{M}{100}\right) - \frac{2.442 \times M}{100}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{as-fired} &= 18.32 \times (1 - 0.25) - \frac{2.442 \times 25}{100} \\
&= 18.32 \times 0.75 - 0.6105 \\
&= 13.74 - 0.6105 \\
&= 13.13 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the LHV using the HHV-to-LHV relationship:**

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values** (H = 6.1% on dry basis; converting hydrogen to as-fired basis is usually small, so use H ≈ 6.1%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV &= 18.32 - 2.442 \times \frac{(9 \times 6.1) + 25}{100} \\
&= 18.32 - 2.442 \times \frac{54.9 + 25}{100} \\
&= 18.32 - 2.442 \times 0.799 \\
&= 18.32 - 1.951 \\
&= 16.37 \; \text{MJ/kg (dry, LHV basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — LHV on as-fired basis:**

$$LHV_{as-fired} = 16.37 \times (1 - 0.25) = 12.28 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{LHV_{as-fired} \approx 12.28 \; \text{MJ/kg} \; (\approx 2{,}933 \; \text{kcal/kg})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the *dry* waste has a high calorific value, the as-fired LHV drops to ~12 MJ/kg because 25% of the mass is water, which absorbs energy to evaporate. This is why Indian MSW (40–60% moisture) typically shows LHV < 5 MJ/kg as fired.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Combustion Air Requirement

**Problem:** For the waste in Example 1, calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen and air requirement**.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric oxygen formula:**

$$O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3} \cdot C + 8 \cdot H + S - O \; \text{(in mass of O₂ per 100 kg waste)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (per 100 kg dry waste):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{req} &= \frac{8}{3}(45.2) + 8(6.1) + 0.5 - 32.0 \\
&= 120.53 + 48.8 + 0.5 - 32.0 \\
&= 137.83 \; \text{kg O}_2 \; / \; 100 \; \text{kg waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Per kg of waste:**

$$O_2^{req} = 1.378 \; \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg waste}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric air** (air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass):

$$Air = \frac{1.378}{0.232} = 5.94 \; \text{kg air / kg waste}$$

**Step 5 — In volumetric terms** (at STP, air density ≈ 1.293 kg/m³):

$$V_{air} = \frac{5.94}{1.293} = 4.60 \; \text{m}^3 \; \text{air / kg waste}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{O_2^{req} = 1.378 \; \text{kg/kg}, \quad Air_{stoch} \approx 5.94 \; \text{kg/kg} \; (\approx 4.6 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **30–50% excess air** is supplied in real incinerators to ensure complete combustion, so actual air supply is typically 7.5–9 kg/kg waste.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Energy Content vs. Treatment Route

| Energy Content (LHV, MJ/kg, as-fired) | Treatment Suitability | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| < 2.0 | Composting / Landfilling only | Too wet, too low energy |
| 2.0 – 5.0 | Landfill with gas recovery | Marginal for thermal |
| 5.0 – 8.0 | Incineration with auxiliary fuel | Typical Indian MSW |
| 8.0 – 12.0 | Stand-alone incineration (mass burn) | Good quality waste |
| 12.0 – 15.0 | High-efficiency WtE (e.g., fluidized bed) | Excellent energy recovery |
| > 15.0 | RDF / Refuse-derived fuel combustion | Pelleted, dried waste |
| > 25.0 | Co-firing with coal in cement kilns | Industrial-grade waste |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Content Determination Methods

The energy content (calorific value) of solid waste can be determined through three principal routes, as shown in the block-level functional architecture below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Municipal Solid Waste Sample]) --> preNode{Pre-treatment}
    preNode -->|Drying, Grinding, Pelletizing| labNode[Bomb Calorimeter]
    preNode -->|Drying, Grinding| ultNode[Ultimate Analysis]
    preNode -->|Drying, Charring| proxNode[Proximate Analysis]

    labNode --> bombNode[Bomb Combustion with Pure O2 at 30 atm]
    bombNode --> tempRiseNode[Measure Temperature Rise of Water]
    tempRiseNode --> expNode[Experimental HHV from Calorimeter Equation]

    ultNode --> elemNode[Determine C, H, N, S, O Percentages]
    elemNode --> dulongNode[Apply Dulongs Empirical Formula]
    dulongNode --> compHHVNode[Computed HHV from Ultimate Analysis]

    proxNode --> proxDataNode[Determine Moisture, VM, FC, Ash]
    proxDataNode --> proxEstNode[Apply Proximate-Based Approximation]
    proxEstNode --> compHHV2Node[Approximate HHV from Proximate Analysis]

    expNode --> compareNode{Compare and Validate}
    compHHVNode --> compareNode
    compHHV2Node --> compareNode

    compareNode --> lhvNode[Convert HHV to LHV]
    lhvNode --> treatNode{Treatment Route Selection}

    treatNode -->|LHV greater than 12| route1Node[Incineration or WtE Plant]
    treatNode -->|LHV 5 to 12| route2Node[Co-firing or Mass Burn with Aux Fuel]
    treatNode -->|LHV less than 5| route3Node[Composting or Landfilling]

```

The energy content of waste is the **driving force** for the entire Waste-to-Energy (WtE) recovery chain. The diagram below illustrates the role of energy content at each stage of the recovery process.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT861, Module 1]**
**Define Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of solid waste. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** is the total amount of heat released per unit mass of waste when it undergoes complete combustion, assuming that all the water vapor formed during combustion (both from hydrogen combustion and from pre-existing moisture) is condensed back to liquid form, thus recovering the **latent heat of vaporization**.

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** is the total amount of heat released per unit mass of waste during complete combustion, assuming that the water vapor formed remains in the **gaseous phase** and the latent heat of vaporization is **not recovered**.

- **Relationship:**

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right) \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

where $H$ = % hydrogen (dry basis), $M$ = % moisture content. *(3 marks: 1+1+1)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECHT861, Module 1]**
**List the typical energy content (calorific value) of (i) mixed plastics, (ii) food waste (as discarded), and (iii) typical Indian municipal solid waste.**

**Model Answer:**

| Material | Energy Content (LHV) |
|---|---|
| (i) Mixed plastics | **30 – 40 MJ/kg** (dry) |
| (ii) Food waste (as discarded, wet) | **3 – 5 MJ/kg** |
| (iii) Typical Indian MSW (as generated) | **4 – 8 MJ/kg** |

The wide variation in Indian MSW is due to its high moisture (40–60%) and high inert (ash, sand) content. *(3 marks: 1+1+1)*

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in three ways: (1) **forgetting the moisture correction** when applying Dulong's formula (the formula gives HHV on a dry basis; if the question gives "as-fired" data, you must correct it), (2) **unit mismatch** between kcal/kg and MJ/kg (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ), and (3) **ignoring the (9H/100) term** in the LHV calculation. Also, when the problem gives "as-fired" ultimate analysis, **do not** plug it directly into Dulong's equation without considering moisture — convert hydrogen content to dry basis first.

## 5.3 MODEL SOLUTIONS — PART B

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Bomb Calorimeter Procedure (7 marks)

**Principle:** A known mass of waste is burned in a sealed, oxygen-filled steel "bomb" surrounded by a known mass of water. The heat released raises the water temperature, from which the calorific value is calculated.

**Apparatus:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sampleNode[Dried and Pelletized Waste Sample 1 g] --> inside[Bomb Vessel]
    inside --> ignition[Ignition Wires and Fuse]
    inside --> oxy[Pressurized Pure Oxygen 30 atm]
    inside --> jacket[Water Jacket 2 L]
    jacket --> thermo[Precision Thermometer +/- 0.001 deg C]
    jacket --> stirrer[Stirrer for Uniform Mixing]
    jacket --> outNode[Measure Temperature Rise dT]

    style inside fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style jacket fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style outNode fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

**Procedure:**

1. **Sample preparation**: A representative sample (≈ 1 g) of waste is dried in an oven at 105 °C, ground, and pressed into a pellet.
2. **Bomb loading**: The pellet is placed in the combustion crucible, and the ignition wire is connected to the electrodes.
3. **Oxygen charging**: The bomb is sealed and filled with **pure oxygen at 25–30 atm** pressure.
4. **Assembly**: The bomb is placed inside a known mass of water (typically 2,000 g) in a calorimeter bucket fitted with a stirrer and a precision thermometer (± 0.001 °C resolution).
5. **Ignition**: An electric current is passed through the fuse wire, igniting the sample inside the pressurized oxygen atmosphere.
6. **Combustion**: Complete combustion occurs rapidly (1–3 minutes). The released heat is absorbed by the water and bomb assembly.
7. **Temperature measurement**: Initial and final equilibrium temperatures of the water are recorded, and $\Delta T$ is computed.
8. **Calculation:**

$$HHV = \frac{(m_w \cdot c_w + C_{bomb}) \cdot \Delta T}{m_{sample}} \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

9. **Correction**: Acid correction (for HNO₃ and H₂SO₄ formed from N and S) and fuse-wire correction are applied.

**Standard:** The method follows **ASTM D5865** and **IS 1350** (for solid fuels, also applicable to waste).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given (dry basis):** C = 38.5, H = 5.4, O = 28.6, N = 1.1, S = 0.4, Ash = 26.0, M(as-fired) = 30%

**(i) HHV on dry basis using Dulong's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{dry} &= 0.338\,C + 1.428\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S \\
&= 0.338 \times 38.5 + 1.428 \times \left(5.4 - \frac{28.6}{8}\right) + 0.095 \times 0.4 \\
&= 13.013 + 1.428 \times (5.4 - 3.575) + 0.038 \\
&= 13.013 + 1.428 \times 1.825 + 0.038 \\
&= 13.013 + 2.606 + 0.038 \\
HHV_{dry} &= 15.66 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) HHV on as-fired basis** (correcting for moisture):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{as-fired} &= HHV_{dry} \times \left(1 - \frac{M}{100}\right) - \frac{2.442 \times M}{100} \\
&= 15.66 \times (1 - 0.30) - \frac{2.442 \times 30}{100} \\
&= 15.66 \times 0.70 - 0.7326 \\
&= 10.962 - 0.7326 \\
HHV_{as-fired} &= 10.23 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) LHV on as-fired basis:**

First, calculate LHV on dry basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{dry} &= HHV_{dry} - 2.442 \times \frac{(9H + 0)}{100} \\
&= 15.66 - 2.442 \times \frac{9 \times 5.4}{100} \\
&= 15.66 - 2.442 \times 0.486 \\
&= 15.66 - 1.187 \\
LHV_{dry} &= 14.47 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, correct for moisture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{as-fired} &= LHV_{dry} \times (1 - 0.30) - \frac{2.442 \times 30}{100} \\
&= 14.47 \times 0.70 - 0.7326 \\
&= 10.129 - 0.7326 \\
LHV_{as-fired} &= 9.40 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment on incineration suitability:**

The as-fired LHV of this waste is **9.40 MJ/kg (≈ 2,247 kcal/kg)**, which is **above the autogenous combustion threshold of 5.0 MJ/kg (1,200 kcal/kg)** but **below the optimum 12 MJ/kg** for high-efficiency WtE.

**Conclusion:** The waste **can be incinerated without auxiliary fuel support**, but for efficient energy recovery, it is advisable to either:
- **Co-fire** with a higher calorific waste (RDF or coal), or
- **Pre-treat** (mechanical drying, RDF preparation) to improve its calorific value. *(7 marks: 2+2+2+1)*

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Factors Affecting Energy Content (7 marks)

**Major Factors:**

1. **Moisture Content (M):** This is the **most influential factor** in MSW. High moisture (e.g., Indian MSW: 40–60%) drastically reduces LHV because energy is consumed in evaporating water. The relationship is:

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right)$$

A 10% increase in moisture can reduce LHV by **0.5–1.0 MJ/kg**.

2. **Proportion of Combustibles (Volatile Matter + Fixed Carbon):** Higher organic content (food, paper, plastics) → higher energy. Inerts (ash, glass, metal) contribute zero energy.

3. **Plastic and Rubber Content:** Plastics (HHV 30–40 MJ/kg) are the **single highest energy contributors** in MSW. Even 5–10% plastics in the mix can raise the waste's calorific value significantly.

4. **Hydrogen-to-Carbon Ratio:** Hydrogen-rich waste (plastics, oil, fats) releases more energy per unit mass and produces more water vapor.

5. **Ultimate Composition (C, H, O content):** Higher C and H with lower O (since O is already "oxidized") give higher calorific value (Dulong's equation).

6. **Particle Size and Density:** Affect combustion kinetics, not gross calorific value, but influence practical energy recovery rate.

**Moisture and Autogenous Combustion Threshold:**

For waste to burn **without auxiliary fuel**, the heat released during combustion of the dry fraction must exceed the heat required to:
- Evaporate the moisture content
- Heat the combustion products to flame temperature (~ 850–1,000 °C)
- Heat the inert (ash) fraction

A simple rule of thumb gives the threshold:

$$LHV_{as-fired} \geq 1{,}200 \; \text{kcal/kg} \approx 5.0 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

Waste with LHV < 5 MJ/kg is termed "energy negative" and requires auxiliary fuel.

**Derivation of HHV–LHV Relationship:** (as in Section 3.2 above)

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \left(\frac{9H + M}{100}\right) \; \text{MJ/kg}$$

#### Part (b) — Average Energy Content and Daily Energy Potential (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate the dry mass fraction for each component:**

$$\text{Dry mass fraction} = 1 - \frac{\text{Moisture}}{100}$$

| Component | Mass % | Moisture (%) | Dry Mass Fraction | LHV (MJ/kg, dry) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paper | 12 | 6 | 0.94 | 16.5 |
| Plastics | 8 | 2 | 0.98 | 35.0 |
| Food waste | 45 | 70 | 0.30 | 17.0 |
| Yard waste | 15 | 55 | 0.45 | 15.0 |
| Glass & metal | 8 | 0 | 1.00 | 0 |
| Textiles | 4 | 10 | 0.90 | 18.0 |
| Others | 8 | 20 | 0.80 | 12.0 |

**Step 2 — Calculate LHV on as-fired basis for each component** (energy retained per kg of wet waste):

$$LHV_{wet} = LHV_{dry} \times (1 - M/100) - 2.442 \times M/100$$

*(For inert components, LHV is zero.)*

| Component | Calculation | LHV_wet (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Paper | $16.5 \times 0.94 - 2.442 \times 0.06$ | $15.51 - 0.147 = 15.36$ |
| Plastics | $35.0 \times 0.98 - 2.442 \times 0.02$ | $34.30 - 0.049 = 34.25$ |
| Food waste | $17.0 \times 0.30 - 2.442 \times 0.70$ | $5.10 - 1.709 = 3.39$ |
| Yard waste | $15.0 \times 0.45 - 2.442 \times 0.55$ | $6.75 - 1.343 = 5.41$ |
| Glass & metal | 0 | 0 |
| Textiles | $18.0 \times 0.90 - 2.442 \times 0.10$ | $16.20 - 0.244 = 15.96$ |
| Others | $12.0 \times 0.80 - 2.442 \times 0.20$ | $9.60 - 0.488 = 9.11$ |

**Step 3 — Calculate the weighted average LHV of the as-generated waste:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{LHV} &= \sum w_i \times LHV_{wet,i} \\
&= (0.12)(15.36) + (0.08)(34.25) + (0.45)(3.39) \\
&\quad + (0.15)(5.41) + (0.08)(0) + (0.04)(15.96) + (0.08)(9.11) \\
&= 1.843 + 2.740 + 1.526 + 0.812 + 0 + 0.638 + 0.729 \\
\overline{LHV} &= 8.29 \; \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the total daily energy potential:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{daily} &= \text{Total mass per day} \times \overline{LHV} \\
&= 500 \times 10^3 \; \text{kg/day} \times 8.29 \; \text{MJ/kg} \\
&= 4.145 \times 10^6 \; \text{MJ/day} \\
&= 4{,}145 \; \text{GJ/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Results:**

$$\boxed{\overline{LHV}_{as-fired} \approx 8.29 \; \text{MJ/kg}, \quad E_{daily} \approx 4{,}145 \; \text{GJ/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The energy potential of 4,145 GJ/day from 500 TPD of MSW is substantial. With a typical WtE conversion efficiency of 25–30% (electrical), this can generate approximately **1,000–1,200 GJ/day of electricity** (≈ 12–14 MW continuous), enough to power a small township.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Energy content (calorific value)** is the most critical design parameter in MSW thermal management; it determines the suitability of waste for incineration, co-firing, or biological treatment.
- [x] **HHV** assumes water vapor is condensed (latent heat recovered); **LHV** assumes water remains as vapor (no latent heat recovery). LHV is always **less than** HHV.
- [x] **Dulong's equation** ($HHV = 0.338C + 1.428(H - O/8) + 0.095S$) is the most widely used empirical formula for estimating HHV from ultimate analysis on a **dry basis**.
- [x] The **moisture correction** is essential: $HHV_{as-fired} = HHV_{dry}(1 - M/100) - 2.442 \times M/100$.
- [x] **Threshold for autogenous combustion** = **1,200 kcal/kg (≈ 5 MJ/kg)**. Below this, auxiliary fuel is required.
- [x] **Indian MSW** has LHV of **4–8 MJ/kg** as-generated (high moisture); **European/US MSW** has **9–13 MJ/kg** (drier, more paper/plastic).
- [x] The factor **2.442 MJ/kg** is the **latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C** (2,442 kJ/kg).
- [x] **9H/100** in the LHV formula represents the mass of water formed from hydrogen combustion (per kg of waste), since 1 g H → 9 g H₂O.
- [x] **Plastics** are the **highest energy contributors** (30–40 MJ/kg) in MSW; **glass and metal** contribute **zero** energy.
- [x] **Stoichiometric air** for waste combustion ≈ 4.31 × O₂ required; practical incinerators use **30–50% excess air**.
- [x] **RDF preparation** (drying + shredding + screening) raises the LHV of MSW from ~5 MJ/kg to **15–20 MJ/kg**, making it suitable for high-efficiency WtE.
- [x] The **bomb calorimeter** method (ASTM D5865 / IS 1350) is the standard experimental technique; Dulong's equation is the standard empirical estimation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chemical Content** of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) refers to the quantitative determination of the elemental and compound-level composition of waste materials, expressed through standardized analytical procedures such as **Proximate Analysis**, **Ultimate Analysis**, and **Calorific Value Determination**. It provides the fundamental chemical fingerprint of waste that dictates its treatment pathway — be it incineration, composting, anaerobic digestion, landfilling, or recycling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT861 — Module 1):**
> Chemical content of solid waste is assessed using **four pillars**:
> 1. **Proximate Analysis** — Moisture, Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), Ash
> 2. **Ultimate Analysis** — C, H, O, N, S, Cl (elemental %)
> 3. **Calorific Value** — Energy potential in kJ/kg or kcal/kg
> 4. **Chemical Composition** — Organic fractions (food, paper), inorganic fractions (glass, metal)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of chemical content analysis as the **"dietary nutrition label" of garbage**. Just like a food packet tells you the percentages of proteins, fats, carbs, and calories per 100 g, the chemical content of MSW tells engineers:

- **How much "burnable fuel"** is locked inside the waste (volatile matter, carbon, hydrogen) → tells us if incineration is feasible.
- **How much "useless residue"** remains after burning (ash) → tells us how often we need to clean incinerator grates.
- **How much "free water"** is present (moisture) → tells us if we must dry the waste before thermal treatment.
- **How much "plant food"** (N, P, K) is in biodegradable waste → tells us if composting will yield a good fertilizer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Memory Trick:**
> **"M-V-F-C"** = **M**oisture, **V**olatile Matter, **F**ixed Carbon, **C**ombustible ash (raw ash) — the four pillars of Proximate Analysis. Add **"C-H-O-N-S-Cl"** (Ultimate) and you have the complete chemical DNA of solid waste.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical MSW Range (India) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content | M | 40 – 60 | % wet basis |
| Volatile Matter | VM | 40 – 50 | % dry basis |
| Fixed Carbon | FC | 5 – 10 | % dry basis |
| Ash Content | A | 25 – 40 | % dry basis |
| Carbon | C | 15 – 25 | % dry basis |
| Hydrogen | H | 1.5 – 3.5 | % dry basis |
| Nitrogen | N | 0.5 – 1.5 | % dry basis |
| Sulphur | S | 0.1 – 0.5 | % dry basis |
| Calorific Value (CV) | Q | 800 – 1500 | kcal/kg |
| C/N Ratio | C/N | 25 – 50 | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Chemical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Proximate Analysis (Composition by Behaviour)

A **functional** rather than elemental analysis. It classifies waste into four operationally-defined fractions based on how each behaves under controlled heating:

1. **Moisture (M)** — Weight loss when heated to **105 °C** for 1 hour.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** — Weight loss (excluding moisture) when ignited at **950 °C ± 20 °C** for 7 minutes in a closed crucible.
3. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — Combustible residue left after VM removal, lost upon further strong heating.
4. **Ash (A)** — Incombustible inorganic residue remaining after complete combustion.

$$\text{M + VM + FC + Ash} = 100\% \text{ (of the waste sample)}$$

### 2.1.2 Ultimate Analysis (Composition by Elements)

Determines the **elemental weight percentages** of the organic fraction using a CHNS-O analyzer:

- **C** (Carbon) — Main energy carrier.
- **H** (Hydrogen) — Contributes high energy; produces water vapour on combustion.
- **O** (Oxygen) — Already bound in waste; reduces net CV.
- **N** (Nitrogen) — Causes NOx emissions; valuable for composting.
- **S** (Sulphur) — Causes SO₂ emissions, corrosion, acid rain.
- **Cl** (Chlorine) — Causes dioxins/furans; corrodes boiler tubes.

## 2.2 Why Each Parameter Matters — The "Why" Behind Every Step

| Parameter | Engineering Decision It Drives |
|---|---|
| **High Moisture** | Pre-drying needed before incineration → higher plant cost |
| **High Volatile Matter** | Excellent for pyrolysis & gasification; supports combustion |
| **High Ash** | Slag formation; grate wear; reduces effective CV |
| **High Carbon** | High calorific value → efficient incineration |
| **High Hydrogen** | Boosts CV significantly (each 1% H ≈ 4× contribution of 1% C) |
| **High Oxygen** | **Reduces** CV — oxygen is already "pre-oxidized" |
| **High Nitrogen** | Good for compost (C/N ≈ 30 ideal); bad for thermal (NOx) |
| **High Sulphur/Chlorine** | Pollution control equipment mandatory (scrubbers, filters) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Proximate Sum | $M + VM + FC + A = 100$ | % (wet basis) | Mass balance check |
| 2 | Moisture (Wet Basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Lab drying at 105 °C |
| 3 | Moisture (Dry Basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Engineering design |
| 4 | Dulong's Formula (CV) | $Q = 337\,C + 1418\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 93\,S$ | kcal/kg | Predicted CV |
| 5 | Dulong's Formula (SI) | $Q = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ | kJ/kg | Predicted CV |
| 6 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_2 = \dfrac{32}{12}C + \dfrac{32}{4}H + \dfrac{32}{32}S - O$ | kg O₂/kg waste | Combustion air |
| 7 | Theoretical Air | $\text{Air}_{th} = \dfrac{O_2}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg waste | Combustion system |
| 8 | Actual Air (% excess) | $\text{Air}_{act} = \text{Air}_{th}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\%e}{100}\right)$ | kg air/kg waste | Burner design |
| 9 | C/N Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{\%\,C}{\%\,N}$ | dimensionless | Composting |
| 10 | Combustion Air Volume | $V = \dfrac{O_2}{0.21}$ | Nm³ air/kg waste | Duct sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient Memory Aid:**
> Carbon needs **8/3 kg O₂** per kg C. Hydrogen needs **8 kg O₂** per kg H. Sulphur needs **1 kg O₂** per kg S. The numbers **8, 4, 32** in the denominators come from molar masses: 12 (C), 2 (H₂), 32 (S), and 32 (O₂).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incinerator Plant Design** — CV dictates fuel input; moisture dictates waste-receiving pit sizing.
- **Composting Plant Design** — C/N ratio dictates co-substrate blending (e.g., mixing sewage sludge with sawdust).
- **Landfill Biogas Prediction** — Ultimate analysis + empirical methane yield equations (IPCC method).
- **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) Manufacturing** — Proximate analysis selects high-VM, low-ash fractions.
- **Pollution Control Sizing** — S and Cl content dictates flue-gas cleaning train design (electrostatic precipitator + scrubber + bag filter).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Dulong's Formula (Calorific Value)

The Dulong-Berthelot equation is **empirically derived** from heats of combustion of pure elements. The energy released per kg of waste arises from oxidation of C, H, and S:

### Step 1 — Energy from Carbon Combustion
$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H = -32{,}800 \text{ kJ/kg C}$$
**Logic:** Each kg of carbon releases **32,800 kJ** on complete oxidation to CO₂.

### Step 2 — Energy from Hydrogen Combustion
$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad \Delta H = -143{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg H}_2$$
Per kg of **elemental H** (i.e., per kg of H₂ × 1, since 1 kg H₂ contains 1 kg H atoms): energy released = **1,43,000 kJ/kg H**.

### Step 3 — Correction for Bound Oxygen
Some H is already **pre-oxidized** in the waste (e.g., in cellulose, sugars, water). The pre-bound H has effectively already "burned" its fuel value. Per kg of bound O, **1/8 kg of H** is pre-bound (since 2H + O → H₂O implies 2 kg H binds with 16 kg O, so 1 kg O binds 1/8 kg H).

Hence the **net effective hydrogen** is:

$$H_{\text{net}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8}$$

### Step 4 — Energy from Sulphur Combustion
$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H \approx -9{,}250 \text{ kJ/kg S}$$

### Step 5 — Combining All Contributions
Adding per kg of waste (using mass fractions C, H, O, S in decimal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{net}} &= 32800 \cdot C + 143000 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9250 \cdot S \\
&\text{(in kJ/kg, when C, H, O, S are mass fractions)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In **percentages** (as commonly reported in ultimate analysis, i.e., C% = C × 100), we divide each coefficient by 100:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{net}} &= \frac{32800}{100}\,C_{\%} + \frac{143000}{100}\left(H_{\%} - \frac{O_{\%}}{8}\right) + \frac{9250}{100}\,S_{\%} \\
Q_{\text{net}} &= 328\,C_{\%} + 1430\left(H_{\%} - \frac{O_{\%}}{8}\right) + 92.5\,S_{\%} \quad \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by **4.184 to convert kJ → kcal** (since 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ), we obtain the textbook form:

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = 78.4\,C_{\%} + 342\left(H_{\%} - \frac{O_{\%}}{8}\right) + 22.1\,S_{\%} \quad \text{kcal/kg}
$$

> **Note on Coefficients:** The widely-used rounded coefficients in Indian engineering textbooks are **337, 1418, 93** (kcal/kg), which are slightly higher due to using **Gross (Higher) Calorific Value** with latent heat of water condensation recovered.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Dulong's Formula (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** A sample of MSW has the following ultimate analysis on dry basis:
C = 24.0%, H = 3.0%, O = 18.0%, N = 0.5%, S = 0.3%, Ash = 38.0%.
**Estimate the calorific value using Dulong's formula.**

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify elements contributing to CV → C, H, O, S.
**Step 2:** Apply Dulong's equation:

$$
Q = 337 \times C + 1418 \times \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 93 \times S
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 337 \times 24.0 + 1418 \times \left(3.0 - \frac{18.0}{8}\right) + 93 \times 0.3 \\
&= 8088 + 1418 \times (3.0 - 2.25) + 27.9 \\
&= 8088 + 1418 \times 0.75 + 27.9 \\
&= 8088 + 1063.5 + 27.9 \\
Q &= 9179.4 \text{ kcal/kg (on dry basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since the analysis is on **dry basis**, the predicted CV is **9179 kcal/kg dry waste**. This is typical of well-dried combustible MSW; wet waste with 50% moisture would only deliver **≈ 4590 kcal/kg as-fired** — below the **1000 kcal/kg** threshold for autogenous (self-sustaining) incineration in most Indian plants.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Stoichiometric Air Requirement

**Problem:** For the same MSW (C=24, H=3, S=0.3, O=18 %), calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen and theoretical air** required for complete combustion.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of waste:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\,\text{req}} &= \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - O_{\text{available}} \\
&= 2.667 \times 24 + 8 \times 3 + 1 \times 0.3 - 18 \\
&= 64.0 + 24.0 + 0.3 - 18.0 \\
O_{2,\,\text{req}} &= 70.3 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ / 100 kg waste} \\
&= 0.703 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical air (air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass):

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{th}} = \frac{0.703}{0.232} = 3.03 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

**Step 3:** Actual air with 100 % excess (typical for MSW incinerators):

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 3.03 \times (1 + 1.00) = 6.06 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Chemical Content of MSW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MSW SAMPLE COLLECTION"] --> B["SAMPLE PREPARATION AND QUARTERING"]
    B --> C["CHEMICAL CONTENT ANALYSIS"]

    C --> D["PROXIMATE ANALYSIS"]
    C --> E["ULTIMATE ANALYSIS"]
    C --> F["CALORIFIC VALUE TEST"]
    C --> G["CHEMICAL COMPOSITION"]

    D --> D1["MOISTURE M at 105 DEG C"]
    D --> D2["VOLATILE MATTER VM at 950 DEG C"]
    D --> D3["FIXED CARBON FC BY DIFFERENCE"]
    D --> D4["ASH A AT 815 DEG C"]

    E --> E1["CARBON C PERCENT"]
    E --> E2["HYDROGEN H PERCENT"]
    E --> E3["NITROGEN N PERCENT"]
    E --> E4["SULPHUR S PERCENT"]
    E --> E5["OXYGEN O BY DIFFERENCE"]
    E --> E6["CHLORINE Cl PERCENT"]

    F --> F1["BOMB CALORIMETER MEASURED"]
    F --> F2["DULONG PREDICTED"]

    G --> G1["ORGANIC FOOD PAPER TEXTILE"]
    G --> G2["INORGANIC GLASS METAL SAND"]

    D1 --> H["DESIGN DECISIONS"]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    D4 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    E4 --> H
    E5 --> H
    E6 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H

    H --> H1["INCINERATOR FEASIBILITY"]
    H --> H2["COMPOSTING C by N RATIO"]
    H --> H3["BIOGAS POTENTIAL"]
    H --> H4["POLLUTION CONTROL SIZING"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Treatment Method from Chemical Content

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START: SAMPLE ANALYSIS COMPLETE"] --> Q1{"MOISTURE M GREATER THAN 50 PERCENT"}

    Q1 -- YES --> P["PRE DRYING NEEDED BEFORE ANY TREATMENT"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"C BY N RATIO BETWEEN 25 AND 35"}

    Q2 -- YES --> COM["ROUTE TO COMPOSTING OR ANAEROBIC DIGESTION"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"CALORIFIC VALUE GREATER THAN 1000 KCAL PER KG"}

    Q3 -- YES --> Q4{"CHLORINE LESS THAN 0.5 PERCENT AND S LESS THAN 0.3 PERCENT"}
    Q3 -- NO --> LF["ROUTE TO SANITARY LANDFILL WITH LEACHATE CONTROL"]

    Q4 -- YES --> INC["ROUTE TO MASS BURN INCINERATION WITH ENERGY RECOVERY"]
    Q4 -- NO --> RDF["ROUTE TO RDF MANUFACTURING AND CO COMBUSTION IN CEMENT KILN"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular node represents an analytical output or a process unit. Each diamond is a binary decision point. The flowchart maps **chemical content → engineering treatment pathway**, which is the core outcome CO1 (Apply) of Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between Proximate Analysis and Ultimate Analysis of solid waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Mass fractions of Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash | Elemental composition: C, H, O, N, S, Cl |
| **Principle** | Controlled heating (thermal fractionation) | Chemical oxidation (CHNS analyzer) |
| **Output** | Operational categories | Elemental weight % |
| **Use** | Quick check of combustibility | Detailed combustion & emission calculations |
| **Standards** | ASTM D3172, IS 1350 | ASTM D5373, ASTM D4239 |

*(1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for table distinguishing, 1 mark for any standard/use)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Why is the C/N ratio an important chemical parameter in solid waste management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- C/N ratio represents the balance between **carbon (energy source)** and **nitrogen (protein source)** for microbial activity during composting (1 mark).
- The **optimum range is 25–35:1**; at this ratio, microbial decomposition proceeds at maximum rate without nutrient limitation or ammonia toxicity (1 mark).
- **If C/N > 35:** composting is slow (carbon excess, nitrogen becomes limiting).
- **If C/N < 20:** nitrogen excess → ammonia release, odour, and potential nitrate leaching (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the proximate and ultimate analysis of municipal solid waste in detail. List the standard laboratory methods/apparatus used for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Proximate Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- **Moisture (M):** Determined by oven-drying a known weight of waste at **105 °C for 1 hour** until constant weight.
   $$M = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$$
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** The dried sample is heated in a **closed crucible at 950 °C ± 20 °C for 7 minutes**. Weight loss (excluding moisture) = VM.
- **Ash (A):** The residual sample is heated in an **open crucible at 815 °C ± 10 °C for 1 hour** until constant weight = Ash content.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Determined by difference: $FC = 100 - (M + VM + A)$.
- **Apparatus:** Muffle furnace (max 1200 °C), desiccator, analytical balance (0.1 mg), silica/nickel crucibles, tongs.

**Ultimate Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- **Carbon (C) and Hydrogen (H):** Determined by **combustion of the sample in a tube furnace at 1350 °C** with pure O₂; C is trapped as CO₂ and H as H₂O, both measured gravimetrically or by IR/thermal conductivity detector (CHNS-O Analyzer).
- **Nitrogen (N):** Determined by the **Kjeldahl method** (digestion with conc. H₂SO₄ + catalyst → ammonia distillation → titration) or **Dumas method** (high-temperature combustion + TCD).
- **Sulphur (S):** Determined by **Eschka method** (fusion with Eschka mixture, precipitation as BaSO₄) or by **IR detection** in modern CHNS analyzers.
- **Oxygen (O):** Determined **by difference**: $O = 100 - (C + H + N + S + \text{Ash})$.
- **Chlorine (Cl):** Determined by **oxygen bomb combustion** followed by **ion chromatography** or **Mohr's titration** (AgNO₃ + K₂CrO₄ indicator).
- **Standards:** ASTM D5373, ASTM D4239, IS 1350 (Part III).

**(b) The ultimate analysis of an MSW sample (dry basis) is as follows: C = 22.5%, H = 3.2%, O = 20.0%, N = 0.6%, S = 0.4%, Ash = 40.0%. Estimate the calorific value using Dulong's formula. If the actual moisture content is 45% (wet basis), determine the calorific value on a "as-fired" basis. Comment on whether autogenous incineration is possible. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Dulong's Formula (dry basis):** (3 marks)

$$
Q_{dry} = 337\,C + 1418\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 93\,S
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{dry} &= 337 \times 22.5 + 1418 \times \left(3.2 - \frac{20.0}{8}\right) + 93 \times 0.4 \\
&= 7582.5 + 1418 \times (3.2 - 2.5) + 37.2 \\
&= 7582.5 + 1418 \times 0.7 + 37.2 \\
&= 7582.5 + 992.6 + 37.2 \\
Q_{dry} &= 8612.3 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Conversion to as-fired basis (account for moisture):** (2.5 marks)

Moisture wet basis (W) = 45 % → for **100 kg wet waste**, dry mass = 55 kg.

CV per kg wet waste:

$$
Q_{as\text{-}fired} = Q_{dry} \times (1 - W) = 8612.3 \times 0.55 = 4736.8 \text{ kcal/kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An additional **latent heat loss** of ~9 × W × 100 ≈ 9 × 45 = 405 kcal/kg occurs due to water evaporation, but the simple dilution formula above is sufficient for KTU exams. The corrected (net) CV ≈ **4330 kcal/kg** if evaporation losses are subtracted.

**Step 3: Comment on Autogenous Incineration:** (1.5 marks)

- The "as-fired" CV ≈ **4330–4740 kcal/kg** is **well above the autogenous threshold of 1000 kcal/kg** (some sources use 1200 kcal/kg).
- **Autogenous incineration is POSSIBLE** — the waste will sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel support.
- The high ash (40 %) will, however, reduce effective heat release and may necessitate **frequent ash removal** and possibly a **fluidised bed** design rather than a moving grate.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) Define calorific value. Explain the experimental determination of calorific value using a Bomb Calorimeter. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
- Calorific value (CV) is the amount of heat liberated by the **complete combustion of unit mass (1 kg) of a fuel/waste** with oxygen, under specified conditions. It is expressed in **kcal/kg** (or kJ/kg).
- **Higher (Gross) CV (GCV):** Includes latent heat recovered from condensation of water vapour formed during combustion.
- **Lower (Net) CV (NCV):** Excludes this latent heat — water leaves as vapour.

**Bomb Calorimeter — Construction (2.5 marks):**
- **Bomb (Combustion Chamber):** Thick-walled stainless-steel pressure vessel (~3–5 atm rated) with two electrodes and an ignition wire.
- **Crucible:** Small quartz/metal cup holding the waste sample inside the bomb.
- **Water Jacket (Calorimeter):** The bomb is immersed in a known mass (W) of water inside an insulated copper calorimeter.
- **Outer Jacket:** A second air-gap/water jacket provides thermal insulation.
- **Stirrer, Thermometer (Beckmann, ±0.01 °C):** For uniform temperature reading.
- **Oxygen Cylinder + Ignition Circuit:** To charge the bomb to ~25–30 atm O₂.

**Experimental Procedure (2.5 marks):**
1. Weigh ~1 g of dried, pelletized waste into the crucible.
2. Tie a cotton thread (~10 cm) to the ignition wire, with one end touching the sample (helps ignition).
3. Charge bomb with **O₂ to 25–30 atm**; check for leaks by submerging in water.
4. Place bomb in the calorimeter; add a known mass (W kg) of water at temperature T₁.
5. Connect ignition circuit, stir continuously, and record temperature every 30 s.
6. Ignite; record the **maximum temperature T₂** reached.
7. Continue stirring for 5 min to obtain the corrected temperature rise, **ΔT = T₂ − T₁**.
8. Calculate CV using:

$$
Q = \frac{(W + w)\,C_p\,\Delta T - \text{(corrections for fuse wire, acid formation, etc.)}}{m}
$$

where *m* is sample mass, *w* is water equivalent of the bomb, *C_p* is specific heat of water (≈ 1 kcal/kg·°C).

**Sources of Error (1 mark):**
- Heat loss to surroundings (minimized by water jacket).
- Incomplete combustion (rare, but verified by washing bomb for unburnt carbon).
- Acid formation (HNO₃ from N) — requires empirical correction of 0.0015 °C per g of N.

**(b) Discuss the significance of Sulphur (S) and Chlorine (Cl) content in MSW for thermal treatment plants. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sulphur Content (3.5 marks):**
- **Emission of SO₂:** Combustion converts S → SO₂, contributing to **acid rain** and PM₂.₅ formation (sulphates).
- **Corrosion:** SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃, leading to **acidic dew-point corrosion** of boiler tubes, economizers, and air-preheaters (especially at 80–120 °C metal temperature).
- **Regulatory Limit:** Indian CPCB GSR 593(E) (2018) sets MSW incinerator SO₂ emission limit at **50 mg/Nm³**.
- **Control:** Wet lime/NaOH scrubbers, dry sorbent injection (NaHCO₃), semi-dry scrubbers.
- **Design Threshold:** S > 0.5 % generally requires additional scrubbing capacity.

**Chlorine Content (3.5 marks):**
- **Dioxins & Furans:** Cl + organic matter + 300 °C window → formation of **polychlorinated dibenzo-p-dioxins (PCDDs) and dibenzofurans (PCDFs)** — the most toxic chemicals in MSW incinerator emissions.
- **PCBs & Chlorobenzenes:** Secondary pollutants from incomplete combustion.
- **HCl Emission:** Cl → HCl gas → corrosive to ductwork, fans, bag filters (especially polyester bags).
- **Control:** (i) Rapid quench of flue gas from 400 °C to <200 °C within 1 s (dioxin window bypass); (ii) Activated carbon injection; (iii) Bag filters; (iv) Wet scrubbers.
- **Regulatory Limit (India):** HCl < 50 mg/Nm³; PCDDs/PCDFs < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- **Design Threshold:** Cl > 0.5 % typically triggers a **dedicated air-pollution control (APC) train** and may force selection of **co-combustion in cement kilns** (where high Cl wastes are typically avoided in primary MSW units).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the O/8 correction in Dulong's Formula** — Maximum 1 mark penalty, but it changes the answer by ~100–200 kcal/kg.
> 2. **Confusing wet-basis vs dry-basis moisture** — Always state the basis clearly; examiners award 1 mark for this.
