# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hydraulic Conductivity Constraint for a Clay Liner

A **compacted clay liner (CCL)** must satisfy a maximum allowable seepage rate. The KTU/regulatory design procedure is:

**Given:**
- Hydraulic conductivity of clay, $K_c = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s
- Liner thickness, $L = 0.6$ m
- Head of leachate over the liner, $h = 0.3$ m
- Cross-sectional area, $A = 1$ m² (unit area basis)

**Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic gradient**

$$
i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{0.3 \text{ m}}{0.6 \text{ m}} = 0.5
$$

This is the dimensionless gradient driving flow downward through the clay.

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law to compute the discharge velocity**

$$
v = K_c \cdot i = (1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (0.5)
$$

$$
v = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to more intuitive units (liters per hectare per day)**

For $1$ m² of liner area:

$$
v = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s/day} = 4.32 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{day per m}^2
$$

$$
= 4.32 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L/day per m}^2 = 43.2 \text{ mL/day per m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Extrapolate to a hectare of landfill base**

For an area $A_h = 10{,}000$ m²:

$$
Q_{hectare} = 4.32 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L/m}^2/\text{day} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{hectare} \approx 432 \text{ L/day per hectare}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A properly compacted clay liner at $K = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s limits seepage to less than 500 L/day per hectare — a small but non-zero flow, which is why a **leachate collection system above the liner** is essential.

## 3.2 Derivation of Travel Time for a Contaminant Parcel

This derivation is frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems on "Control of leachate movement."

**Given:**
- Distance from liner to water table, $L_w = 5$ m
- Hydraulic gradient, $i = 0.05$ (downward)
- Effective porosity of natural subsoil, $n_e = 0.30$
- Hydraulic conductivity, $K = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute the Darcy (discharge) velocity**

$$
v = K \cdot i = (1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (0.05)
$$

$$
v = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the seepage velocity (real velocity of contaminant)**

$$
v_s = \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the travel time to the water table**

$$
\tau = \frac{L_w}{v_s} = \frac{5 \text{ m}}{1.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
\tau = 3.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years**

$$
\tau = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}}{31{,}536{,}000 \text{ s/year}} \approx 9.51 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 9.5-year travel time is the **theoretical minimum** assuming no sorption, no dispersion retardation, and continuous flow. In reality, sorption onto clay particles and dilution in the aquifer extend the actual breakthrough time by factors of 10–100, which provides a measure of natural attenuation safety.

## 3.3 Derivation: Saturated Flow Through a Compacted Liner (Dupuit–Forchheimer)

For a saturated liner of length $L$, with the leachate head $h$ above it and zero head at the base, the flow per unit width is given by **Dupuit's equation**:

$$
q = K \cdot \frac{h^2}{2L}
$$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1** — Consider a vertical column of the saturated liner. The hydraulic gradient at a depth $z$ below the leachate surface is:

$$
i(z) = -\frac{dh}{dz}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply Darcy's Law locally:

$$
q = K \cdot i \cdot h(z) = -K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dz}
$$

**Step 3** — Separate variables and integrate from $z = 0$ (where $h = h_0$, the leachate head) to $z = L$ (where $h = 0$ at the base):

$$
\int_0^L q \, dz = \int_{h_0}^{0} -K \cdot h \, dh
$$

**Step 4** — Solve the right side:

$$
q \cdot L = K \cdot \frac{h_0^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5** — Rearrange to get the Dupuit–Forchheimer result:

$$
q = K \cdot \frac{h_0^2}{2L}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply to a typical design case**

With $K = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s, $h_0 = 0.3$ m, $L = 0.6$ m:

$$
q = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot \frac{(0.3)^2}{2 \cdot 0.6} = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot \frac{0.09}{1.2}
$$

$$
q = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.075) = 7.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m width}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result agrees dimensionally with the unit-area discharge computed earlier ($v = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m/s) within the same order of magnitude, confirming the consistency of Darcy-based approaches.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Liner Systems

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** that examiners love to test.

| Property | Compacted Clay Liner (CCL) | Geomembrane (HDPE) | Composite Liner (GCL + GM) |
|:---------|:--------------------------:|:------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Hydraulic conductivity $K$ | $\leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s | Practically impermeable (only defects allow flow) | $\leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s (effective) |
| Typical thickness | 0.6 – 1.2 m | 1.5 – 2.5 mm | 0.6 m clay + 1.5 mm GM |
| Construction complexity | Moderate (moisture/density control) | High (welding of seams critical) | High (both components) |
| Chemical resistance | Good for most inorganics; poor for some organics | Excellent for most chemicals | Excellent (combined) |
| Vulnerability to puncture | Low | Moderate–High (sharp objects) | Low (clay cushions GM) |
| Cost | Low–Moderate | Moderate | High |
| KTU-recommended? | **Yes** (with GM above for composite) | **Yes** (as a component) | **Strongly Yes (best practice)** |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Components of a Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS)

| Component | Function | Typical Design Parameter |
|:----------|:---------|:------------------------:|
| Granular drainage layer (sand/gravel) | Conveys leachate to collection pipes | Hydraulic conductivity $\geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s; thickness $\geq 0.3$ m |
| Perforated HDPE collection pipes | Gathers leachate to sumps | Slope $\geq 1$% ; spacing $\leq 6$ m |
| Filter/Geotextile | Prevents clogging of drainage layer | Apparent opening size (AOS) $\leq 0.21$ mm |
| Sump and pump station | Removes collected leachate | Designed for peak storm event (e.g., 25-yr, 24-hr) |
| Leak detection layer (in double-liner systems) | Detects breaches between primary and secondary liners | $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Leachate Generation, Movement, and Control

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **flow of leachate from generation to control** in a modern engineered landfill. It deliberately avoids physical-drawing limitations and emphasizes functional interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall and Snowmelt Entering Landfill Surface] --> B[Waste Mass Percolation Zone]
    C[Inherent Moisture in Waste] --> B
    D[Decomposition Water from Microbial Activity] --> B
    E[Surface or Groundwater Infiltration] --> B
    B --> F[Leachate Saturated Zone Inside Refuse]
    F --> G[Primary Leachate Collection and Removal System LCRS]
    G --> G1[Perforated HDPE Pipes]
    G1 --> G2[Granular Drainage Layer]
    G2 --> G3[Sump and Pump Station]
    G3 --> H[Leachate Treatment Plant]
    H --> I[Treated Effluent Discharge or Reuse]
    F --> J[Primary Liner System]
    J --> J1[Compacted Clay Liner CCL]
    J --> J2[Geomembrane GM]
    J --> J3[Geosynthetic Clay Liner GCL]
    J1 --> K[Leak Detection Layer LDL]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Secondary Liner System]
    L --> M[Native Subsoil Aquitard]
    M --> N[Groundwater Monitoring Wells]
    N --> O[Compliance Verification and Reporting]
    P[Final Cover System] --> A
    P1[Top Vegetative Layer] --> P
    P2[Drainage Layer] --> P
    P3[Barrier Layer Low Permeability] --> P
    P4[Gas Collection Layer] --> P
    P5[Foundation Layer] --> P
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Sources of moisture (**A, C, D, E**) feed the percolation zone (**B**).
- The **LCRS** (collection system) **prevents leachate head from building up** on the liner.
- The **liner system** (**J1, J2, J3**) provides a low-permeability barrier.
- The **leak detection layer (K)** sits between the primary and secondary liners, flagging any breach.
- The **final cover system (P, P1–P5)** minimizes rainfall infiltration into the closed landfill, dramatically reducing long-term leachate generation.

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Landfill Liner to Groundwater — A Mermaid Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Leachate Head Builds Over Liner] --> S2[Step 2: Seepage Through CCL via Darcy Flow]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Possible Geomembrane Defect]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Composite Liner Slows Further]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Leak Detection Layer Signals Alarm]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Secondary Liner Delays Contaminant Travel]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Aquitard Provides Natural Attenuation]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Monitoring Wells Verify Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Selecting a Liner Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Design Question] --> Q2{Is the Waste Hazardous?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Use Single Composite Liner: GM over CCL or GCL]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Double Composite Liner with Leak Detection]
    Q3 --> Q5[Verify K less than 1 times 10 to the negative 9 m per s]
    Q4 --> Q6[Verify K less than 1 times 10 to the negative 11 m per s]
    Q5 --> Q7[Design LCRS and Final Cover]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8[End: Engineered Landfill Ready for Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "describe the control of leachate movement," always structure the answer around **THREE barriers**: (1) **minimization of generation** (cover system + water balance), (2) **collection** (LCRS — keep leachate head low), and (3) **containment** (liner system). Examiners reward this logical sequence with full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define leachate. List any four characteristics of young (acidogenic) landfill leachate.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

Leachate is the aqueous liquid that has percolated through solid waste in a landfill, carrying dissolved and suspended contaminants, formed primarily by the infiltration of rainwater through the refuse mass.

Characteristics of young (acidogenic) leachate:

1. **Low pH** (typically **5.0 to 6.0**) due to accumulation of short-chain volatile fatty acids.
2. **Very high BOD₅** ranging from **10,000 to 50,000 mg/L** and proportionally high COD (**BOD/COD ratio > 0.5**).
3. **High concentrations of volatile organic acids (VFAs)** and ammoniacal nitrogen.
4. **High concentrations of heavy metals** (Cd, Pb, Zn) because of the low pH and high solubility under acidic conditions.
5. **Dark color and foul odor** from dissolved organics and hydrogen sulfide.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "leachate is dirty water" — a **vague answer that loses 1 mark**. You must use the formal definition involving *percolation through solid waste* and list **specific numeric characteristics** (pH, BOD range) to score the full 3 marks.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State Darcy's Law. What is the role of hydraulic conductivity in controlling leachate movement through a landfill liner?**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

Darcy's Law states that the volumetric flow rate of water through a saturated porous medium is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient and the cross-sectional area:

$$
Q = -K \cdot i \cdot A
$$

The negative sign indicates flow occurs from high to low head.

**Role of hydraulic conductivity ($K$):**

- It quantifies the ease with which leachate moves through the liner soil.
- For a **compacted clay liner**, $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s is mandated, ensuring seepage is negligible.
- For a **composite liner** (geomembrane over clay), the *effective* $K$ drops to $\leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s, providing near-zero advective flow.
- $K$ is therefore the **single most important design parameter** in liner engineering — it directly determines the seepage rate of contaminants to the subsurface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Standard:** In a 14-mark question, KTU convention is **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, with internal choice within each sub-part. The model answer below is for **Question A**. An independent **Question B** is provided as the alternative.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **engineered liner system** used in modern municipal solid waste landfills. List the materials used and the design value of hydraulic conductivity for each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A compacted clay liner of thickness **0.9 m** is placed beneath a municipal solid waste landfill. The leachate head above the liner is **0.45 m** and the hydraulic conductivity of the clay is **$1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s**. Compute:
- (i) The hydraulic gradient.
- (ii) The Darcy velocity of seepage.
- (iii) The volume of leachate seeping per hectare per day.
- (iv) The travel time for a contaminant to traverse the liner (assume effective porosity $n_e = 0.4$). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Engineered Liner System (Bottom-Up Description):**

1. **Subgrade (Prepared native soil)** — Smoothed and compacted to provide a uniform base. Slope: 2–3% toward sumps. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Secondary Composite Liner (if double-liner system used)**
   - Compacted clay liner (CCL): $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s, thickness $\geq 0.6$ m. *(0.5 mark)*
   - Geomembrane (HDPE, 1.5–2.5 mm) above the clay. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Leak Detection Layer (LDL)** — Granular drainage layer with $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, typically 0.3 m of sand, with perforated pipes to convey any leakage to a sump. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Primary Composite Liner**
   - Geosynthetic Clay Liner (GCL) or additional CCL: $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s effective. *(0.5 mark)*
   - HDPE geomembrane (1.5–2.5 mm) on top — the primary barrier. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS)**
   - Geotextile filter (prevents fines clogging drainage layer).
   - Granular drainage layer: $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, thickness 0.3 m.
   - Perforated HDPE collection pipes (slope $\geq 1$%, spacing 6 m).
   - Sump and pump station. *(2 marks total for LCRS components)*

6. **Protective layer** — often a geotextile or sand cushion above the LCRS to prevent damage from waste placement. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **Refuse** placed above, with daily and intermediate cover. *(0.5 mark)*

**Materials & Hydraulic Conductivity Table (asked explicitly):**

| Liner Component | Material | Design $K$ |
|:----------------|:---------|:----------:|
| Compacted Clay Liner (CCL) | Natural or amended clay, compacted at optimum moisture | $\leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s |
| Geomembrane (HDPE) | High-Density Polyethylene sheet, 1.5–2.5 mm | Essentially impermeable; defects dominate |
| Geosynthetic Clay Liner (GCL) | Bentonite sandwiched in geotextiles | $\leq 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s |
| Composite Liner (GM over CCL) | Both acting together | $\leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s effective |

*(1 mark for the table)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**

- Liner thickness, $L = 0.9$ m
- Leachate head, $h = 0.45$ m
- Hydraulic conductivity, $K = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s
- Effective porosity, $n_e = 0.4$
- Area, $A_h = 1$ hectare $= 10{,}000$ m²

**Sub-question (i): Hydraulic gradient** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
i = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{0.45}{0.9} = 0.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}
$$

**Sub-question (ii): Darcy velocity** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
v = K \cdot i = (1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (0.5) = 7.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Sub-question (iii): Volume per hectare per day** *(2 marks)*

$$
Q_{per\,m^2} = v = 7.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m}^2
$$

Converting to daily:

$$
Q_{per\,m^2} = 7.5 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 86{,}400 = 6.48 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{day per m}^2
$$

$$
= 0.0648 \text{ L/day per m}^2
$$

For 1 hectare:

$$
Q_{hectare} = 0.0648 \text{ L/m}^2/\text{day} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 648 \text{ L/day per hectare}
$$

**Sub-question (iv): Travel time through the liner** *(2 marks)*

Seepage velocity:

$$
v_s = \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-10}}{0.4} = 1.875 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}
$$

Travel time:

$$
\tau = \frac{L}{v_s} = \frac{0.9}{1.875 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.8 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
\tau = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{8}}{31{,}536{,}000} \approx 15.2 \text{ years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert from Darcy velocity to **seepage velocity** in part (iv). The Darcy velocity is the *apparent* velocity across the full cross-section, but water actually travels faster through the pores. The seepage velocity is what determines the *real* contaminant travel time. Many students lose 1 mark here.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **factors affecting the generation of leachate** in a sanitary landfill. How does the **age of the landfill** influence leachate characteristics? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the components and function of a **Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS)**. What design considerations ensure that the leachate head above the liner does not exceed **0.3 m**? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Factors Affecting Leachate Generation:**

1. **Climatic factors** — precipitation (intensity, frequency, duration), temperature, evapotranspiration, wind. *(1 mark)*
2. **Waste characteristics** — composition (organic content), moisture content, density, particle size. *(1 mark)*
3. **Landfill design** — type of cover, liner system, drainage, slope. *(1 mark)*
4. **Operational practices** — daily cover, intermediate cover, liquid waste acceptance, compaction. *(1 mark)*
5. **Site hydrogeology** — depth to water table, soil permeability beneath. *(1 mark)*

**Influence of Landfill Age (2 marks):**

- **Young landfill (0–5 years, acidogenic phase):** Anaerobic acidogenesis dominates. Short-chain fatty acids accumulate, giving **low pH (5–6), very high BOD (10,000–50,000 mg/L), high COD, and high heavy-metal solubility**. The BOD/COD ratio is **> 0.5**.
- **Mature landfill (10+ years, methanogenic phase):** Methanogenic archaea consume the VFAs and produce methane and carbon dioxide. pH rises to **7–8**, BOD drops to **< 1000 mg/L**, COD persists (humic substances), and heavy metals precipitate as hydroxides/sulfides. The BOD/COD ratio falls to **< 0.1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for control:** The liner and treatment system must be designed for the **worst-case** (young) leachate, which is highly acidic and metal-rich. Mature leachate is more biodegradable but harder to treat for recalcitrant organics.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Components of LCRS:**

1. **Granular drainage blanket** (sand/gravel, 0.3 m thick, $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s). *(1 mark)*
2. **Perforated HDPE collection pipes** (slope $\geq 1$%, spacing $\leq 6$ m). *(1 mark)*
3. **Geotextile filter** above drainage layer to prevent clogging. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Sump and submersible pump** at the low point. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Inspection risers and cleanouts** for maintenance. *(0.5 mark)*

**Design Considerations to Keep Leachate Head $\leq 0.3$ m:** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Pipe slope $\geq 1$%** to ensure gravity-driven flow.
- **Pipe spacing $\leq 6$ m** to limit drainage path length.
- **Drainage layer $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s** for high transmissivity.
- **Pump capacity** designed for the **peak leachate generation rate** (e.g., 25-year, 24-hour storm event), not the average.
- **Regular cleaning** of pipes and sumps to prevent clogging from biofilms or precipitates.
- **Inlet protection** with geotextile and gravel filters to prevent fines migration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state "design the pump to handle the leachate." This is too vague. You must specify the **design storm** (25-year/24-hour or 100-year) and justify the pipe slope and spacing. Marks are deducted for omitting the **peak event** criterion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Leachate & Control)**

- **Definition:** Leachate = water + dissolved/suspended contaminants from waste; not "dirty water" alone.
- **Sources:** Rainfall (largest), inherent moisture, decomposition water, intrusion.
- **Water balance:** $L = P - R - ET - \Delta S - SW$ — always state the units of mm/year.
- **Young leachate:** Low pH (5–6), high BOD (10,000–50,000 mg/L), high metals, BOD/COD > 0.5.
- **Mature leachate:** pH 7–8, low BOD, persistent COD (humics), BOD/COD < 0.1.
- **Darcy's Law:** $Q = -K i A$; seepage velocity $v_s = v/n_e$.
- **Critical gradient:** $i_c = (G_s - 1)/(1+e)$ — controls heaving/piping.
- **Compacted clay liner (CCL):** $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s, thickness $\geq 0.6$ m.
- **Geomembrane (HDPE):** 1.5–2.5 mm; near-zero advective flow.
- **Composite liner:** GM over CCL/GCL; effective $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s.
- **LCRS components:** drainage layer, perforated pipes, geotextile filter, sump, pump.
- **Leachate head design criterion:** $\leq 0.3$ m above the liner.
- **LCRS pipe design:** Slope $\geq 1$%, spacing $\leq 6$ m, drainage layer $K \geq 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s.
- **Final cover:** Vegetative + drainage + barrier + gas + foundation layers; minimizes $L$ after closure.
- **Travel time equation:** $\tau = L \cdot n_e / (K \cdot i)$ — used in groundwater impact assessment.
- **Three pillars of control:** (1) **Minimization** (cover), (2) **Collection** (LCRS), (3) **Containment** (liners).
- **Regulatory benchmarks:** $K \leq 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s (single liner); $\leq 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s (composite).
- **Real-world case:** Love Canal — illustrates the consequence of inadequate leachate control.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** Always present your answer in **three logical parts** — *Generation → Movement → Control*. Examiners reward structured answers; structure carries marks even when content is imperfect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Movement and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gas Movement and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Landfill Gas (LFG) Movement** refers to the bulk transport of gaseous decomposition products (primarily **CH₄** and **CO₂**, with trace H₂S, NH₃, VOCs, and water vapor) from their point of generation within the refuse mass of a **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) landfill** through the porous waste matrix to the atmosphere or to a gas collection infrastructure.

**Landfill Gas Control** is the engineered set of design, operational, and monitoring practices used to (i) contain LFG within the landfill boundary, (ii) extract LFG at controlled rates for energy recovery or flaring, and (iii) prevent uncontrolled migration that may cause explosions, vegetation stress, odor nuisance, or greenhouse gas emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of **mass transfer in porous media** and **environmental process engineering**. It is directly tested under Module 2 of PECHT861 and links upstream to "Landfill Bioreactor Concepts" and downstream to "Leachate Management."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge" Model

Imagine a **sponge soaked in coloured water** sitting on a table.

- The **sponge** = the compacted waste mass (porous medium).
- The **coloured water** = the landfill gas trapped in pore spaces.
- **Tilting the sponge** = creates a **pressure gradient**, forcing water to flow out (this is **Darcy-type advection**).
- **Heat from a hair dryer** = increases gas pressure and molecular motion (this is **convection + diffusion**).
- **Placing a straw inside and sucking** = mimics an **active gas extraction well**.
- **Wrapping the sponge in plastic** = mimics a **geomembrane cover** restricting surface emission.

In a real landfill, gas movement is governed by the same three drivers:
1. **Pressure gradients** (ΔP) — the dominant driver.
2. **Concentration gradients** (ΔC) — cause diffusive transport.
3. **Buoyancy / thermal gradients** — natural convection since LFG is warm and less dense than air.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| LFG generation rate constant | k | 0.02 – 0.35 | yr⁻¹ |
| Methane fraction in LFG | x_CH₄ | 0.40 – 0.60 | – |
| Waste porosity | n | 0.40 – 0.60 | – |
| Permeability of waste | k_perm | 10⁻¹² – 10⁻⁸ | m² |
| Gas pressure (typical) | P | 0 – 2,000 | Pa (gauge) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Favorite:** Always state the **typical composition** of LFG — **CH₄ (40–60%)** and **CO₂ (30–50%)**, balance N₂ + trace gases. This single line scores 2 marks in any KTU Part-A.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Engineering-Relevant

- **Safety:** Uncontrolled CH₄ migration causes explosions (e.g., Loscoe, UK 1986).
- **Climate:** CH₄ has a GWP of **~28–36** over 100 years (IPCC AR6); capture mitigates GHG.
- **Energy:** 1 million tonnes of MSW can yield **~1–4 MW** of electricity via LFG-to-energy.
- **Regulatory:** India's **SWM Rules 2016 (amended 2020)** mandate LFG capture at landfills > 5 MW capacity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Breakdown of Gas Movement

Landfill gas moves through the refuse mass via **three simultaneous, superimposed mechanisms**. Each contributes differently depending on site conditions.

### Mechanism A — Pressure-Driven (Advective) Flow
- **Why:** Decomposition produces gas faster than it can diffuse; internal pressure builds above atmospheric.
- **How:** Gas flows from high-pressure zones (interior) to low-pressure zones (atmosphere, extraction well, or side slope) following **Darcy's Law for compressible fluids**.
- **Dominant in:** Active, deep landfills with low-permeability cover.

### Mechanism B — Molecular Diffusion (Fickian)
- **Why:** Concentration gradients exist between the gas-rich waste interior and the gas-lean cover soil or ambient air.
- **How:** Gas molecules migrate from high to low concentration, governed by **Fick's First Law**.
- **Dominant in:** Covered, low-generation cells where pressure gradients are negligible.

### Mechanism C — Convection (Buoyancy-Driven)
- **Why:** LFG is warm (~30–50 °C) and rich in CO₂ and CH₄, both denser than air per mole but warmer overall, creating a density differential.
- **How:** Warm gas rises through any available high-permeability pathway (cracks, gravel trenches, side slopes).
- **Dominant in:** Older, well-settled cells with chimney-effect pathways.

## 2.2 Governing Equation — Darcy's Law for Compressible Gas Flow

For an **isothermal, ideal gas** flowing through a homogeneous porous medium, mass flux per unit area is:

$$J = -\frac{k_{eff}}{\mu} \cdot \rho_g \cdot \frac{dP}{dL}$$

Where:
- $J$ = mass flux of gas, $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$
- $k_{eff}$ = effective permeability of waste, $\text{m}^{2}$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of gas, $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\rho_g$ = gas density, $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}$
- $dP/dL$ = pressure gradient, $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}$

For **volumetric flow rate** at standard conditions, the integrated form becomes:

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{\pi \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot T_{sc}}{\mu \cdot P_{sc} \cdot T \cdot \ln(R_2/R_1)}$$

This is the **radial flow equation** used to design **vertical gas extraction wells** — a high-yield KTU formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Application | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = -k_{eff} A \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$ | Darcy's Law (laminar gas flow) | $Q$ = volumetric flow, $k_{eff}$ = permeability, $\mu$ = viscosity | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $J_D = -D_{eff} \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (diffusion) | $J_D$ = diffusive flux, $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity, $C$ = concentration | $\text{kg}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$ |
| 3 | $Q_{sc} = \dfrac{\pi k_{eff}(P_1^2 - P_2^2) T_{sc}}{\mu P_{sc} T \ln(R_2/R_1)}$ | Radial flow to extraction well | $P_1,P_2$ = pressures at well and radius of influence, $R_1,R_2$ = radii | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $G(t) = k \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | First-order LFG generation (Scholl Canyon) | $G(t)$ = generation rate, $L_0$ = methane potential, $k$ = rate constant | $\text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ |
| 5 | $V_{CH_4}(t) = L_0 \cdot M \cdot (1 - e^{-kt})$ | Cumulative methane yield | $M$ = mass of waste, $L_0$ = methane generation potential | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 6 | $P_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_w} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_g}\right)^{-1}$ | Capillary pressure in waste (2-phase flow) | $P_c$ = capillary pressure, $\sigma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = pore radius | Pa |
| 7 | $R_{cap} = \dfrac{k_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Permeation rate per unit area | $R_{cap}$ = gas capture rate | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $t_{50} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life of gas generation | $t_{50}$ = time to 50% generation | yr |
| 9 | $\eta_{collection} = \dfrac{Q_{extracted}}{Q_{generated}} \times 100\%$ | Collection efficiency | $\eta$ = efficiency | % |
| 10 | $V_{air,LEL} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{0.05}$ | Volume of air needed to reach LEL (5%) | LEL = Lower Explosive Limit | $\text{m}^3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme has a strong bias toward **derivation-based Part-B questions** on Equation 1 and Equation 3. Memorize the assumptions (ideal gas, isothermal, steady-state, homogeneous) — they account for ~3 marks of follow-up.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where Formula/Concept is Used |
|---|---|
| **LFG-to-Energy plant design** | Equations 4 & 5 to size engines/turbines |
| **Gas extraction well spacing** | Equation 3 to determine radius of influence |
| **Landfill cover design** | Equation 2 to estimate diffusive surface emissions |
| **Explosion hazard assessment** | Equation 10 to determine safe venting volumes |
| **Compliance reporting (CPCB)** | Equation 9 to demonstrate ≥ 60% collection efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Darcy's Law for Compressible Gas (Mass Balance Approach)

### Starting Point — Microscopic Momentum Balance
For a single pore channel of radius $r_p$, gas flow is governed by the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow:

$$q_{pore} = -\frac{\pi r_p^4}{8 \mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

### Step 1 — Geometric Summation Over a Representative Elementary Volume (REV)
The REV contains $N$ pore channels per unit cross-section, where $N$ is related to porosity $n$ and pore density. Summing over the REV and dividing by total area $A$ gives the **Darcy flux** (volumetric flux per unit total area):

$$v_D = -\frac{n \cdot \langle r_p^2 \rangle}{8 \mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

### Step 2 — Define Permeability
The intrinsic permeability $k_{eff}$ is defined to absorb the geometric term:

$$k_{eff} = \frac{n \cdot \langle r_p^2 \rangle}{8}$$

This is the **Kozeny-Carman-type** geometric definition. Substituting:

$$v_D = -\frac{k_{eff}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

This is **Darcy's Law for incompressible (liquid) flow** at the macroscopic scale.

### Step 3 — Conversion to Compressible Gas Form Using Ideal Gas Law
For an ideal gas at constant temperature:

$$\rho_g = \frac{P \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T}$$

For a **mass flow rate per unit area** (true flux, independent of local pressure):

$$\dot{m} = \rho_g \cdot v_D = -\frac{k_{eff}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{P M_w}{R T} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

### Step 4 — Express as $P \cdot dP$ Form
Using the chain rule identity $P \cdot dP = \frac{1}{2} d(P^2)$:

$$\dot{m} = -\frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} \cdot \frac{d(P^2)}{dx}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Over a Linear Control Volume of Length L
For 1-D flow from $P_1$ (at $x=0$) to $P_2$ (at $x=L$):

$$\dot{m} \int_0^L dx = -\frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} d(P^2)$$

$$\dot{m} \cdot L = -\frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} (P_2^2 - P_1^2)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Volumetric Flow at Standard Conditions
At standard conditions $(P_{sc}, T_{sc})$, using $\dot{m} = \rho_{sc} Q_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc} M_w}{R T_{sc}} Q_{sc}$:

$$\frac{P_{sc} M_w}{R T_{sc}} Q_{sc} \cdot L = \frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} (P_1^2 - P_2^2)$$

Cancelling $M_w$ and $R$, and solving for $Q_{sc}$:

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot T_{sc}}{2 \mu \cdot P_{sc} \cdot T \cdot L}$$

This is the **linear 1-D compressible gas flow equation** — the parent of Equation 3 in the cheat sheet (which becomes the radial form when $A = 2\pi r L$ is integrated from $R_1$ to $R_2$).

### Verification Check
Units check:
- $[k_{eff}] = \text{m}^2$
- $[P^2] = \text{Pa}^2$
- $[\mu] = \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- Numerator: $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{Pa}^2 \cdot \text{K}$
- Denominator: $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} \cdot \text{Pa} \cdot \text{K} \cdot \text{m} = \text{Pa}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{K} \cdot \text{m}$
- Result: $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LFG Generation Rate (Scholl Canyon Model, IPCC 2006)

The **Scholl Canyon first-order decay model** assumes:
- Methane generation follows first-order kinetics.
- $L_0$ = total methane generation potential (m³ CH₄ per Mg waste).
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (yr⁻¹).

### Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation
The instantaneous methane generation rate per unit mass of waste is proportional to the **remaining methane potential**:

$$\frac{dG}{dt} = -k \cdot (G_\infty - G)$$

Where $G_\infty = L_0$ is the asymptotic total yield.

### Step 2 — Solve the ODE
With initial condition $G(0) = 0$:

$$G(t) = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-kt})$$

This is the **cumulative methane yield** at time $t$ (Equation 5 in cheat sheet).

### Step 3 — Differentiate to Get the Rate
Differentiating $G(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dG}{dt} = L_0 \cdot k \cdot e^{-kt}$$

This is the **instantaneous generation rate** (Equation 4 in cheat sheet).

### Step 4 — Peak Generation Time
To find when generation is maximum, set $d^2G/dt^2 = 0$:

$$\frac{d^2G}{dt^2} = -L_0 k^2 e^{-kt} = 0$$

This is satisfied only at $t \to \infty$ — meaning **maximum rate occurs at $t = 0$**, and it decays exponentially. Hence, the **half-life of generation** is:

$$t_{50} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}$$

For $k = 0.05\,\text{yr}^{-1}$, $t_{50} = 13.86$ years.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A landfill has $M = 500{,}000$ Mg of waste, $L_0 = 60\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{Mg}$, and $k = 0.04\,\text{yr}^{-1}$. Compute cumulative generation and instantaneous rate at $t = 10$ yr.

### Solution
**Cumulative methane generated at $t = 10$ yr:**

$$G(10) = 60 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.04 \times 10}) = 60 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.4})$$

$$= 60 \cdot (1 - 0.6703) = 60 \cdot 0.3297 = 19.78\,\text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$$

**Total cumulative volume:**

$$V_{CH_4} = 19.78 \times 500{,}000 = 9.89 \times 10^6\,\text{m}^3$$

**Instantaneous rate at $t = 10$ yr:**

$$\frac{dG}{dt}\bigg\vert_{10} = 60 \times 0.04 \times e^{-0.4} = 2.4 \times 0.6703 = 1.608\,\text{m}^3/\text{Mg/yr}$$

**Total instantaneous flow rate:**

$$Q_{inst} = 1.608 \times 500{,}000 = 8.04 \times 10^5\,\text{m}^3/\text{yr} = 2{,}203\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** At $t = 10$ yr, ~33% of methane has been produced. A 2,200 m³/day flow can support a **~0.5 MW electricity generation** plant assuming 35% electrical efficiency and 50% methane content. This is a typical KTU Part-B numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Landfill Gas Collection System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents a **functional architecture flow** of a typical engineered LFG collection and treatment system, mapping component interactions from generation to end-use or safe disposal.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GENERATION["GENERATION ZONE - Inside Waste Mass"]
        wasteMass["A1: Decomposing MSW Mass"] --> microbio["A2: Anaerobic Microbial Activity"]
        microbio --> lfgProd["A3: LFG Generation - CH4 plus CO2 plus Trace Gases"]
    end

    subgraph MOVEMENT["MOVEMENT ZONE - Through Porous Medium"]
        lfgProd --> pressure["B1: Pressure Driven Advection"]
        lfgProd --> diffTrans["B2: Molecular Diffusion"]
        lfgProd --> buoyCon["B3: Buoyancy Convection"]
        pressure --> wellhead["B4: Convergence at Extraction Well"]
        diffTrans --> soilCover["B5: Diffusive Loss to Cover Soil"]
        buoyCon --> sideMig["B6: Lateral Migration to Side Slope"]
    end

    subgraph COLLECTION["COLLECTION ZONE - Engineered Capture"]
        wellhead --> wellField["C1: Vertical Extraction Well Network"]
        wellField --> header["C2: Condensate Knockout and Header Piping"]
        sideMig --> perimeter["C3: Perimeter Gas Migration Trenches"]
        perimeter --> header
    end

    subgraph TREATMENT["TREATMENT ZONE - Processing and Disposition"]
        header --> blower["D1: Blower or Vacuum Pump - Vacuum 0 to minus 50 kPa"]
        blower --> moisture["D2: Moisture and Particulate Removal"]
        moisture --> siloxane["D3: Siloxane and H2S Scrubbing"]
        siloxane --> splitNode["D4: Flow Distribution Node"]
        splitNode --> flareUnit["D5: Enclosed Flare - Thermal Destruction"]
        splitNode --> engineUnit["D6: Internal Combustion Engine - Electricity"]
        splitNode --> boilerUnit["D7: Boiler - Steam and Heat"]
    end

    subgraph MONITORING["MONITORING AND CONTROL ZONE"]
        monitorSys["E1: Continuous Gas Analyzer - CH4 CO2 O2 H2S"]
        monitorSys --> bgcSystem["E2: Blower and Wellfield SCADA Control"]
        bgcSystem --> wellField
        monitorSys --> alarmSys["E3: LEL Alarms and Auto Shutoff"]
        alarmSys --> flareUnit
    end

    soilCover --> emissions["F1: Fugitive Surface Emissions - Quantified via Flux Chamber"]
    emissions --> monitorSys
```

## 4.2 Component Interaction Matrix

| From → To | Function | Engineering Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Generation → Movement | Pressure built up drives flow | Permeability $k_{eff} > 10^{-12}$ m² required for viable extraction |
| Movement → Collection | Wellfield captures advected gas | Well spacing 30–90 m; vacuum 0.5–5 kPa typical |
| Collection → Treatment | Removes contaminants | H₂S < 200 mg/m³; siloxanes < 10 mg/m³ for engines |
| Treatment → Atmosphere | Flare destroys CH₄ | Combustion efficiency ≥ 99% (per CPCB) |
| Monitoring → Control | Feedback loop | Trigger flare at CH₄ < 30% or O₂ > 2% in extracted gas |

## 4.3 Typical LFG Well Construction Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    surface["Surface - Top of Landfill"] --> seal["Bentonite or Concrete Surface Seal"]
    seal --> casing["PVC Well Casing - 100 to 150 mm Dia"]
    casing --> perfZone["Perforated Section - 1 to 3 m Slotted PVC"]
    perfZone --> wasteBody["Decomposing Waste Body"]
    perfZone --> gravelPack["Gravel Pack Around Perforations"]
    gravelPack --> wasteBody
    casing --> wellheadValve["Wellhead Valve and Flow Control"]
    wellheadValve --> vacuumLine["Vacuum Line to Header"]
    wellheadValve --> samplePort["Gas Sampling Port"]
    wellheadValve --> pressureGauge["Vacuum Pressure Indicator"]
    wasteBody --> condensate["Condensate Collection at Base"]
    condensate --> drain["Drain to Leachate Sump"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT861]**
State the typical composition of landfill gas. Why is methane considered the most hazardous component, and what is its lower explosive limit (LEL) in air?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Landfill gas (LFG) typically contains **methane (CH₄): 40–60%**, **carbon dioxide (CO₂): 30–50%**, with the balance being nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen sulfide, ammonia, and trace VOCs.

Methane is the most hazardous component because:
- It is **highly flammable** and forms explosive mixtures with air in the range **5–15% by volume** in air.
- The **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) is 5%**; above this, an ignition source can cause detonation.
- It is an odourless, colourless gas, making leaks undetectable without instrumentation.
- It is a potent greenhouse gas with **GWP ≈ 28–36** over 100 years (IPCC AR6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Composition 1 mark + hazard reason 1 mark + LEL value 1 mark.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT861]**
Distinguish between **active** and **passive** landfill gas collection systems. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Active System | Passive System |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Mechanical blower / vacuum pump applying suction | Natural pressure differential & buoyancy |
| Gas flow rate | High, controlled | Low, weather-dependent |
| Capital cost | High (blowers, flare, piping) | Low (vents, gravel trenches) |
| End use | Energy recovery / high-destruction flaring | Venting to atmosphere (legacy landfills) |
| Example | **Vertical extraction well with flare/CHP** | **Passive vent stack with gravel chimney** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition + example of active 1.5 marks, passive 1.5 marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECHT861]**

**(a)** Derive the **1-D form of Darcy's Law for compressible gas flow** through a porous medium, starting from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for a single pore. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vertical gas extraction well of radius $R_1 = 0.15$ m is screened through a saturated waste layer of permeability $k_{eff} = 5 \times 10^{-12}$ m² and effective thickness $H = 8$ m. The vacuum applied at the wellhead is such that $P_1 = 85$ kPa absolute. The radius of influence extends to $R_2 = 25$ m, where the gas pressure equals atmospheric $P_2 = 101.325$ kPa. The dynamic viscosity of LFG is $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas temperature $T = 308$ K, and standard conditions are $P_{sc} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{sc} = 288.7$ K. Calculate the **volumetric flow rate of LFG extracted at standard conditions** using the radial flow equation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark):
   - Isothermal, steady-state flow
   - Ideal gas behaviour
   - Homogeneous, isotropic porous medium
   - Laminar flow (low Reynolds number)
   - Single-phase gas flow (negligible liquid interference)

2. **Hagen-Poiseuille for single pore** (1 mark):

$$q_{pore} = -\frac{\pi r_p^4}{8 \mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

3. **Geometric averaging over REV** (1 mark):

$$v_D = -\frac{k_{eff}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

4. **Apply ideal gas law** $\rho_g = PM_w / RT$ to obtain mass flux (1 mark):

$$\dot{m} = \frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} \cdot \frac{d(P^2)}{dx}$$

5. **Integrate over length L from P₁ to P₂** (1 mark):

$$\dot{m} \cdot L = \frac{k_{eff} M_w}{2 \mu R T} (P_1^2 - P_2^2)$$

6. **Convert to standard volumetric flow** (2 marks):

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot T_{sc}}{2 \mu \cdot P_{sc} \cdot T \cdot L}$$

(For radial flow to a well, replace A = 2πrL and integrate, leading to the logarithmic form.)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the radial flow equation (2 marks):

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{\pi \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (P_1^2 - P_2^2) \cdot T_{sc}}{\mu \cdot P_{sc} \cdot T \cdot \ln(R_2/R_1)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values (1 mark):

- $P_1^2 = (85{,}000)^2 = 7.225 \times 10^9$ Pa²
- $P_2^2 = (101{,}325)^2 = 1.0267 \times 10^{10}$ Pa²
- $P_1^2 - P_2^2 = 7.225 \times 10^9 - 1.0267 \times 10^{10} = -3.042 \times 10^9$ Pa²
- (Negative sign indicates flow **toward** the well, which is correct for a vacuum well.)
- $\ln(R_2/R_1) = \ln(25/0.15) = \ln(166.67) = 5.116$
- $T_{sc}/T = 288.7 / 308 = 0.9373$

**Step 3:** Plug in (2 marks):

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-12}) \times (3.042 \times 10^9) \times 0.9373}{(1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (101{,}325) \times 5.116}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator (1 mark):

$$\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.042 \times 10^9 \times 0.9373 = 4.482 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator (1 mark):

$$1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 101{,}325 \times 5.116 = 6.221 \times 10^{0} = 6.221$$

**Step 6:** Final answer (1 mark):

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{4.482 \times 10^{-2}}{6.221} = 7.20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{sc} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 622\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Spots:**
> 1. **Sign convention:** Students often write $(P_2^2 - P_1^2)$ and get a **negative flow**. Always re-phrase as "magnitude of flow rate toward the well."
> 2. **Unit conversion:** $P$ must be in **Pa (absolute)**, not bar or kPa without squaring. $k_{eff}$ must be in **m²**, not Darcy ($1$ Darcy $= 9.87 \times 10^{-13}$ m²).
> 3. **Forgetting $T_{sc}/T$:** Many students set $T = T_{sc}$ and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Using $\ln$ base confusion:** Ensure natural log, not $\log_{10}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **three mechanisms of landfill gas movement** (advection, diffusion, convection) in detail. For each, state the driving force, the governing equation, and the conditions under which it dominates. (7 marks)

**(b)** A new landfill receives $L_0 = 80\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{Mg}$ with rate constant $k = 0.06\,\text{yr}^{-1}$. Total waste input is $M = 250{,}000$ Mg. Compute:
   (i) Cumulative methane yield at $t = 5$ yr and $t = 20$ yr.
   (ii) Instantaneous generation rate at $t = 10$ yr in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$.
   (iii) Half-life of gas generation $t_{50}$.
   (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 3 Mechanisms (7 marks):**

1. **Advection (Pressure-Driven) — 2.5 marks**
   - Driving force: Pressure gradient $\Delta P$ (Pa/m).
   - Governing equation: $v = -\frac{k_{eff}}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$ (Darcy's Law).
   - Dominant in: Active, deep landfills with high gas generation rates and low-permeability covers.
   - Real example: Gas migration toward an extraction well.

2. **Molecular Diffusion (Concentration-Driven) — 2.5 marks**
   - Driving force: Concentration gradient $dC/dx$ (kg/m⁴).
   - Governing equation: $J_D = -D_{eff} \frac{dC}{dx}$ (Fick's First Law).
   - Dominant in: Cells after peak generation; surface emissions through cover soil.
   - Real example: Diffusive CH₄ loss through a clay cap.

3. **Convection (Buoyancy-Driven) — 2 marks**
   - Driving force: Density difference between warm LFG and ambient air.
   - Governing equation: $v_{conv} = C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g \Delta\rho h}{\rho_{avg}}}$ (Stack-effect approximation).
   - Dominant in: Old, well-settled cells with high-permeability chimney pathways.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Cumulative yield (3 marks):**

At $t = 5$ yr:
$$G(5) = 80 \times (1 - e^{-0.06 \times 5}) = 80 \times (1 - e^{-0.3}) = 80 \times 0.2592 = 20.74\,\text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$$

Total: $20.74 \times 250{,}000 = 5.18 \times 10^6\,\text{m}^3$

At $t = 20$ yr:
$$G(20) = 80 \times (1 - e^{-0.06 \times 20}) = 80 \times (1 - e^{-1.2}) = 80 \times 0.6988 = 55.91\,\text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$$

Total: $55.91 \times 250{,}000 = 1.398 \times 10^7\,\text{m}^3$

**(ii) Instantaneous rate at $t = 10$ yr (2 marks):**

$$\frac{dG}{dt}\bigg\vert_{10} = 80 \times 0.06 \times e^{-0.6} = 4.8 \times 0.5488 = 2.634\,\text{m}^3/\text{Mg/yr}$$

Total instantaneous rate:
$$= 2.634 \times 250{,}000 = 658{,}500\,\text{m}^3/\text{yr} = 1{,}804\,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iii) Half-life (2 marks):**

$$t_{50} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.06} = 11.55\,\text{years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Spots:**
> 1. **Mixing up cumulative and instantaneous** — using $G(t)$ when rate is asked (or vice versa). Marks deducted: 2.
> 2. **Forgetting the multiplier $M$** — answering per-Mg instead of total. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** for yr → day (multiply by 365.25, not 360). Marks deducted: 1.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Master These:**

- **LFG composition:** CH₄ = 40–60%, CO₂ = 30–50%, balance N₂ + trace.
- **Three movement mechanisms:** Advection (ΔP), Diffusion (ΔC), Convection (buoyancy).
- **Darcy's Law for gas:** $Q_{sc} = \frac{k_{eff} A (P_1^2 - P_2^2) T_{sc}}{2 \mu P_{sc} T L}$ — memorize with units.
- **Radial flow to well:** $Q_{sc} = \frac{\pi k_{eff} (P_1^2 - P_2^2) T_{sc}}{\mu P_{sc} T \ln(R_2/R_1)}$ — must use **natural log**.
- **Scholl Canyon Model:** $G(t) = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$; Rate = $L_0 k e^{-kt}$.
- **Half-life:** $t_{50} = \ln 2 / k$.
- **LEL of CH₄:** 5% in air; UEL = 15%.
- **GWP of CH₄:** ~28–36 (IPCC AR6).
- **Active vs. Passive collection:** Active uses blower + flare/CHP; passive uses vents/chimneys.
- **Well spacing:** 30–90 m typical; vacuum 0.5–5 kPa; well radius 0.10–0.20 m.
- **Collection efficiency target:** ≥ 60% as per CPCB/SWM Rules 2016.
- **Units to remember:** $k_{eff}$ in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s, $P$ in Pa (absolute), $Q$ in m³/s.
- **Conversion:** 1 Darcy = $9.87 \times 10^{-13}$ m².
- **CPCB mandate:** Mandatory LFG capture for landfills > 5 MW gas generation potential.
- **Standard conditions:** $P_{sc} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{sc} = 288.7$ K (15 °C).
- **Explosion disaster references:** Loscoe (UK, 1986) and Skellingsted (Denmark, 1991).
- **Flow regime assumption:** Laminar (Re < 1) for accurate Darcy application in waste.
- **Cover soil roles:** Clay mineral layer restricts advection; sand/gravel layer collects for venting.
- **Monitoring parameters:** CH₄, CO₂, O₂, H₂S, flow rate, static pressure, temperature.
- **Engine protection:** Siloxanes < 10 mg/m³, H₂S < 200 mg/m³ to prevent corrosion.
- **Fugitive emissions quantification:** Surface flux chamber method.
- **End-of-pipe options:** Enclosed flare (≥ 99% destruction), IC engine, gas turbine, boiler.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Operation of Landfills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design and Operation of Landfills ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Sanitary Landfill (KTU Definition):** A sanitary landfill is an engineered facility for the **disposal of solid waste** on land in a manner that protects **public health and the environment** by confining the waste to the smallest practical volume, compacting it to the highest practical density, and covering it with a layer of compacted soil or approved alternative material at the end of each day (daily cover) and at the end of each cell (intermediate cover). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *PECHT861 – Solid Waste Management*, landfill design refers to the systematic engineering process of **planning, sizing, configuring, and constructing** a controlled waste containment facility that integrates barrier systems, drainage networks, gas management infrastructure, and operational protocols to ensure long-term environmental safety. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Engineered Sandwich"** > Imagine making a multi-layered sandwich. A landfill is conceptually similar: > - **Bread (Top):** The final cover system (cap) that prevents rain ingress. > - **Filling (Middle):** Compacted waste in daily cells (like layers of filling). > - **Bread (Bottom):** The composite liner system that prevents leachate escape. > - **Tomato (Drainage):** Leachate collection pipes and drainage layer. > - **Sides (Sealant):** The perimeter wall preventing lateral leakage. > Without the structural engineering, the "sandwich" (waste) leaks "juice" (leachate) everywhere, contaminating groundwater, soil, and air. The landfill is essentially a **scientifically engineered sandwich that traps pollution** instead of food. ### 1.3 Classification of Landfills Landfills are classified based on the type of waste they receive: | Landfill Type | Acceptable Waste | Regulatory Standard | |---------------|------------------|---------------------| | **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) Landfill** | Household, commercial, institutional non-hazardous waste | **MSW Rules 2016 (India)** | | **Hazardous Waste Landfill** | Industrial hazardous waste (ignitable, corrosive, reactive, toxic) | **Hazardous Waste Rules 2016** | | **Industrial (Non-Hazardous) Landfill** | Inert industrial waste (ash, slag, construction debris) | State Pollution Control Board norms | | **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Landfill** | Concrete, bricks, wood, metals | Local municipal byelaws | ### 1.4 Landfill vs. Open Dumping (KTU Distinction) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Open Dump:** Uncontrolled waste disposal site. No liner, no leachate collection, no gas management, no compaction, no daily cover. Pollutes soil, water, and air freely. > **Sanitary Landfill:** Engineered facility with engineered liner, leachate and gas collection systems, daily cover, compaction, and long-term monitoring. ### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Engineering Standards - **Standard Compaction Density of MSW:** **400 to 700 kg/m³** (after compaction in landfill cell) - **Loose Density of MSW (in collection vehicle):** **80 to 150 kg/m³** - **Hydraulic Conductivity (k) of Compacted Clay Liner (CCL):** ≤ **1 × 10⁻⁹ m/s** - **Hydraulic Conductivity of Geomembrane (HDPE):** Practically impermeable (k ≈ **1 × 10⁻¹⁴ m/s**) - **Landfill Slope (working face):** **3:1 (horizontal:vertical)** to **4:1** for stability - **Final Cover Slope:** Minimum **3% to 5%** to promote drainage - **Daily Cover Thickness:** **150 mm to 300 mm** of compacted soil - **Final Cover Thickness:** **600 mm to 1200 mm** of compacted soil layer ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of an Engineered Landfill A sanitary landfill consists of **six essential components**, each serving a critical engineering function: 1. **Sub-base Preparation** — Compacted native soil or bedrock foundation, graded to prevent ponding beneath the liner. 2. **Liner System (Bottom Barrier)** — Prevents leachate migration into groundwater. May be single (geomembrane alone), double (CCL + GMB), or composite. 3. **Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS)** — Perforated pipes in a granular drainage layer (typically 300 mm thick) that collect contaminated liquid and pump it out for treatment. 4. **Leachate Detection, Collection, and Removal System (LDCRS)** — Secondary system between primary and secondary liners to detect leaks and pump them out. 5. **Landfill Gas (LFG) Collection System** — Vertical and horizontal wells/pipes extracting methane (CH₄) and carbon dioxide (CO₂) for flaring or energy recovery. 6. **Final Cover System (Cap)** — Multi-layer cap installed upon closure to minimize infiltration and gas escape. ### 2.2 Design Considerations — The "Why" and "How" > [!NOTE] > **Why each component matters:** > - **Liner** → Environmental protection (EPA / CPCB mandate) > - **Leachate collection** → Prevents build-up of head (pressure) on liner > - **Gas collection** → Prevents CH₄ explosion hazard & greenhouse gas emission > - **Daily cover** → Controls vectors (flies, rats, birds), odors, wind-blown litter > - **Final cover** → Long-term stability against erosion & infiltration ### 2.3 Site Selection Criteria The selection of a landfill site is governed by **environmental, geological, hydrological, and socio-economic** factors: | Criterion | Recommended Standard | |-----------|---------------------| | Distance from airport | > **10 km** to avoid bird-strike hazard | | Distance from river/lake/reservoir | > **200 m** (preferably > **500 m**) | | Distance from habitation | > **500 m** from nearest dwelling | | Groundwater table depth | > **2 m** below liner base | | Distance from flood plain | Above 100-year flood level | | Soil type | Low permeability clay preferred (k < **10⁻⁷ m/s**) | | Geological stability | Away from seismic zones, fault lines, karst terrain | | Site area | Sufficient for **5 to 15 years** of operation + buffer | | Access | Good road connectivity (12 m wide minimum) | ### 2.4 Liner System Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Composite Liner (Most Common in Modern Landfills):** > A composite liner combines a **Geomembrane (GMB)** with a **Compacted Clay Liner (CCL)** to leverage the strengths of both. The GMB is virtually impermeable but vulnerable to puncture; the CCL has self-healing capacity due to swelling of clay particles. Together, they provide redundant protection. **Standard Configuration (Bottom to Top):** 1. Prepared subgrade (compacted to ≥ 95% Proctor density) 2. **CCL** — 600 mm compacted clay, k ≤ 1 × 10⁻⁹ m/s 3. **GMB (HDPE)** — 1.5 mm to 2.5 mm thick textured High-Density Polyethylene 4. **Geotextile cushion** — 200 g/m² to prevent GMB puncture 5. **Granular drainage layer** — 300 mm gravel, k ≥ 1 × 10⁻³ m/s 6. **Perforated HDPE leachate collection pipes** — 100 mm to 150 mm dia, spaced 30–60 m ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $V = \dfrac{M}{\rho_{comp}}$ | Landfill volume required, where M = total waste mass, $\rho_{comp}$ = compacted density | m³ | | $V_c = L \times B \times H$ | Cell volume (L = length, B = width, H = height of daily cell) | m³ | | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | Leachate generation (C = runoff coefficient, I = rainfall intensity, A = area) | m³/day | | $L_g = P - ET - SR$ | Water balance: leachate = Precipitation - Evapotranspiration - Surface Runoff | mm/year | | $k = \dfrac{Q \times L}{A \times h \times t}$ | Hydraulic conductivity (Darcy's law) | m/s | | $\rho_c = \dfrac{M_{loose}}{V_{compacted}}$ | Compaction ratio, typically **3:1 to 4:1** | — | | $A_{cell} = \dfrac{W_d}{\rho_{comp} \times H_{cell}}$ | Daily cell area for given daily waste | m² | | $A_{landfill} = \dfrac{P_{annual}}{h \times \rho_{comp}}$ | Total land area needed for n years | m² | | $S = \dfrac{\gamma \times H}{\tan \phi}$ | Factor of safety for slope stability (simplified) | — | | $V_{CH_4} = 0.4 \times M_{waste} \times L_0 \times e^{-kt}$ | Methane generation (LandGEM / IPCC first-order decay model) | m³/year | | $P_{sw} = \gamma_w \times h_w$ | Hydrostatic pressure from leachate head on liner | kPa | | $FS = \dfrac{\sum R}{\sum D}$ | Factor of safety = Sum of resisting forces / Sum of driving forces | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to design a landfill, students often forget to include the **daily cell volume** calculation. Always compute: (i) Daily waste arriving, (ii) Daily cell area, (iii) Daily cell volume, (iv) Active face area, and (v) Total landfill footprint. ### 2.6 Landfill Operations — Phases A landfill project has **four operational phases**: 1. **Pre-Construction Phase** — Site investigation, hydrogeological study, EIA, regulatory approvals, design. 2. **Construction Phase** — Earthworks, liner installation, leachate and gas infrastructure. 3. **Operation Phase** — Waste reception, weighing, daily cell construction, compaction, daily cover, gas extraction, leachate management. 4. **Closure & Post-Closure Phase** — Installation of final cap, monitoring for **15 to 30 years** post-closure. ### 2.7 Landfill Gas (LFG) Generation and Composition > [!NOTE] > **Landfill Gas Composition (Typical):** > - **Methane (CH₄):** **40% – 60%** by volume > - **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** **30% – 50%** by volume > - **Nitrogen (N₂):** **2% – 5%** > - **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S):** **0% – 1%** (responsible for odor) > - **Trace gases:** VOCs, siloxanes, mercury, vinyl chloride Methane is **21 times** more potent than CO₂ as a greenhouse gas (IPCC AR4). Hence, gas collection is both a safety and climate-change mitigation measure. ### 2.8 Landfill Stability Analysis The factor of safety (FS) of a landfill slope must satisfy: $$FS \geq 1.3 \text{ (during construction)}, \quad FS \geq 1.5 \text{ (long-term, post-closure)}$$ Stability considerations include: - **Translational slope failure** (sliding along liner interface — interface shear strength governs) - **Rotational failure** (deep-seated) - **Seismic loading** (pseudo-static coefficient) - **Phreatic surface** location (excess leachate head reduces stability) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Sample Problem: Landfill Sizing Calculation (KTU Board Style) > **Problem Statement:** > A city generates **500 tonnes/day** of MSW. The waste density in collection vehicles is **120 kg/m³**, and the compaction ratio is **3:1**. The proposed landfill will be operated for **15 years**. Design the landfill using a **trench method**, with each cell being **4 m wide × 3 m deep × 50 m long**. Calculate: > (a) Compacted waste density. > (b) Daily cell volume required. > (c) Number of cells per day. > (d) Daily cell footprint (length × width). > (e) Total landfill volume (excluding cover material). > (f) Total landfill footprint assuming an average height of 15 m. --- ### 3.2 Solution with Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation **Given Data:** - $M_d = 500 \text{ t/day} = 500{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$ - $\rho_{loose} = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Compaction ratio = 3:1 (i.e., volume is reduced to 1/3) - Operating period $T = 15 \text{ years}$ - Cell dimensions: $L_c = 50 \text{ m}, W_c = 4 \text{ m}, H_c = 3 \text{ m}$ --- #### Part (a) — Compacted Waste Density **Step 1:** The compaction ratio (C) of 3:1 means that 3 units of loose volume become 1 unit of compacted volume. $$\rho_{compacted} = C \times \rho_{loose}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values: $$\rho_{compacted} = 3 \times 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\rho_{compacted} = 360 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ > **Verification against KTU standard:** This lies within the KTU acceptable compacted MSW density range of **400 to 700 kg/m³** (slightly low; a higher compaction ratio of ~4:1 would bring it to 480 kg/m³, which is more realistic). The student should note this discrepancy in viva-voce. --- #### Part (b) — Daily Cell Volume Required **Step 1:** Daily waste volume in compacted state: $$V_{daily} = \dfrac{M_d}{\rho_{compacted}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$V_{daily} = \dfrac{500{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{360 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ $$V_{daily} = 1388.89 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 3:** Since one cell must accommodate one day's waste: $$\boxed{V_{cell} = 1388.89 \text{ m}^3 \approx 1389 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$ --- #### Part (c) — Number of Cells per Day **Step 1:** Volume of one pre-defined cell: $$V_{one\_cell} = L_c \times W_c \times H_c$$ $$V_{one\_cell} = 50 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$$ $$V_{one\_cell} = 600 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Number of cells required per day: $$N_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{daily}}{V_{one\_cell}}$$ $$N_{cells} = \dfrac{1388.89}{600}$$ $$N_{cells} = 2.315$$ **Step 3:** Round up (cannot have partial cells in practice): $$\boxed{N_{cells} \approx 3 \text{ cells per day}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** Practically, the operator should slightly increase the cell dimensions or operate 3 cells/day with the third being partially filled. --- #### Part (d) — Daily Cell Footprint **Step 1:** If 3 cells are constructed per day at the given dimensions: $$A_{cell\_each} = L_c \times W_c = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Total daily footprint (3 cells side-by-side): $$A_{daily} = 3 \times 200 = 600 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{A_{daily} = 600 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}}$$ --- #### Part (e) — Total Landfill Volume (15 Years) **Step 1:** Total days in 15 years (assuming 365 days/year, continuous operation): $$N_{days} = 15 \times 365 = 5475 \text{ days}$$ **Step 2:** Total compacted waste volume: $$V_{total} = V_{daily} \times N_{days}$$ $$V_{total} = 1388.89 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 5475 \text{ days}$$ $$V_{total} = 7{,}604{,}167 \text{ m}^3 \approx 7.6 \text{ million m}^3$$ **Step 3:** Convert to a more readable unit: $$V_{total} = 7.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 7.6 \text{ Mm}^3$$ $$\boxed{V_{total} \approx 7.6 \text{ million m}^3}$$ > **Note:** This excludes daily cover, intermediate cover, and final cover soil volumes. For a complete KTU answer, add **15% to 20%** extra volume for cover materials. --- #### Part (f) — Total Landfill Footprint **Step 1:** Average height of landfill mound $H_{avg} = 15 \text{ m}$. **Step 2:** Required footprint: $$A_{landfill} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{H_{avg}}$$ $$A_{landfill} = \dfrac{7.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3}{15 \text{ m}}$$ $$A_{landfill} = 506{,}667 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Convert to hectares (1 hectare = 10,000 m²): $$A_{landfill} = \dfrac{506{,}667}{10{,}000} = 50.67 \text{ ha}$$ $$\boxed{A_{landfill} \approx 50.7 \text{ hectares}}$$ > **Engineering Comment:** Add a **20% buffer** for access roads, leachate lagoons, weighbridge, and office area → Effective land required ≈ **60 hectares**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Parameters Summary | Parameter | Calculated Value | KTU Acceptable Range | Verdict | |-----------|------------------|----------------------|---------| | Compacted density | 360 kg/m³ | 400 – 700 kg/m³ | Slightly low (use C=4) | | Daily cell volume | 1389 m³ | — | OK | | Cells per day | 3 | 1 – 5 typical | OK | | Daily footprint | 600 m²/day | 400 – 800 m²/day | OK | | Total volume (15 yr) | 7.6 × 10⁶ m³ | Depends on city size | OK | | Total footprint | 50.7 ha | 40 – 100 ha | OK | | Cell slope | 3:1 | 3:1 to 4:1 | OK | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Landfill Cross-Section (Schematic Block Flow) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **vertical cross-section of a modern sanitary landfill**, showing all six essential engineering components and their layered arrangement. ```mermaid graph TD A[Final Cover System
Top Cap Layer]:::cover B[Top Soil Layer 150 mm
Vegetation Support]:::cover C[Geosynthetic Drainage Layer
Evapotranspiration Barrier]:::cover D[Compacted Clay Barrier 600 mm
Low Permeability k less than 10 power minus 9 m per s]:::cover E[Geosynthetic Clay Liner GCL
Bentonite Composite]:::cover F[Geomembrane HDPE 1.5 to 2.5 mm
Primary Hydraulic Barrier]:::liner G[Geotextile Cushion 200 g per sq m
Puncture Protection]:::liner H[Granular Drainage Layer 300 mm
Leachate Collection]:::leach I[Perforated HDPE Pipes 100 to 150 mm dia
Leachate Removal]:::leach J[Compacted Waste Mass
MSW with Daily Cover]:::waste K[Leachate Detection Layer
LDCRS Sand 300 mm]:::leach L[Secondary Geomembrane 1.5 mm
Backup Hydraulic Barrier]:::liner M[Compacted Clay Liner CCL 600 mm
Secondary Mineral Barrier]:::liner N[Geotextile Cushion]:::liner O[Prepared Subgrade
Native Compacted Soil]:::subgrade P[Groundwater Table
Minimum 2 m Below Liner]:::subgrade A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L L --> M M --> N N --> O O --> P classDef cover fill:#d4e157,stroke:#827717,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef liner fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leach fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef waste fill:#8d6e63,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef subgrade fill:#a1887f,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Landfill Operational Block Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR R1[Waste Collection Vehicles]:::input R2[Weighbridge and Tipping]:::process R3[Active Tipping Face]:::process R4[Compaction Unit Bulldozer]:::process R5[Daily Cell Construction]:::process R6[Daily Cover 150 mm Soil]:::process R7[Leachate Collection System]:::output R8[Leachate Treatment Plant]:::output R9[Landfill Gas Collection Wells]:::output R10[Gas Flare or Energy Plant]:::output R11[Final Cover Closure]:::terminal R12[Post Closure Monitoring 15 to 30 yr]:::terminal R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R4 R4 --> R5 R5 --> R6 R5 --> R7 R7 --> R8 R5 --> R9 R9 --> R10 R6 --> R11 R11 --> R12 classDef input fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef terminal fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Leachate Generation Water Balance Schematic ```mermaid graph TD P[Precipitation P]:::inflow ET[Evapotranspiration ET]:::outflow SR[Surface Runoff SR]:::outflow INF[Infiltration]:::process LCH[Leachate Generated]:::process SC[Soil Cap Absorption]:::outflow SW[Soil Water Storage SW]:::process P --> INF P --> SR P --> ET INF --> LCH INF --> SC SC --> SW SW --> ET classDef inflow fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outflow fill:#bcaaa4,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Landfill Site Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD S1[Start Site Selection]:::start S2[Environmental Criteria
Distance from water, habitation, airport]:::process S3[Geotechnical Criteria
Soil permeability, slope, GW depth]:::process S4[Hydrological Criteria
Drainage, flood plain]:::process S5[Socio Economic Criteria
Land cost, public acceptance]:::process S6[Regulatory Compliance
EIA clearance, SPCB consent]:::process S7{All Criteria Met}:::decision S8[Site Approved]:::success S9[Reject and Resurvey]:::fail S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 -- Yes --> S8 S7 -- No --> S9 classDef start fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef success fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** > **Differentiate between a sanitary landfill and an open dump yard. Mention any three engineering features of a sanitary landfill.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **sanitary landfill** is an *engineered* facility for waste disposal that incorporates environmental protection systems, whereas an **open dump** is a *uncontrolled* waste disposal site without any engineered safeguards. | Feature | Sanitary Landfill | Open Dump | |---------|-------------------|-----------| | Liner system | Provided (CCL + GMB) | Absent | | Leachate collection | Engineered LCRS | None; leachate seeps to groundwater | | Daily cover | 150–300 mm soil applied daily | Not done | | Compaction | Mechanical (bulldozer/compactor) | None or minimal | | Gas management | LFG collection and flaring | Methane escapes uncontrolled | | Public health risk | Low | High (vectors, fire, odor) | *Three engineering features of a sanitary landfill (1 mark each):* 1. **Engineered composite liner** (geomembrane + compacted clay) to prevent leachate migration. 2. **Leachate collection and removal system (LCRS)** with perforated pipes and granular drainage. 3. **Landfill gas collection system** with vertical/horizontal wells for methane extraction and flaring. 4. **Daily soil cover** to control vectors, odor, and windblown litter. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** > **List the six essential components of a modern sanitary landfill. State the function of the geomembrane.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **six essential components** of a modern sanitary landfill are: 1. **Sub-base** (prepared foundation) 2. **Liner system** (composite barrier) 3. **Leachate collection and removal system (LCRS)** 4. **Leachate detection, collection, and removal system (LDCRS)** (in double-lined systems) 5. **Landfill gas collection system** 6. **Final cover system (cap)** **Function of the Geomembrane (1 mark):** The geomembrane is a synthetic liner (typically 1.5 to 2.5 mm thick High-Density Polyethylene) that acts as the **primary hydraulic barrier**, preventing leachate from infiltrating into the underlying soil and groundwater. It has hydraulic conductivity on the order of **1 × 10⁻¹⁴ m/s**, making it virtually impermeable. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- > **Module 2 — Question Choice (ESE Pattern)** #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **(a)** Describe the engineering components of a modern sanitary landfill with the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram. Explain the functions of the **liner system**, **leachate collection system**, and **final cover system**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A city generates **800 tonnes/day** of MSW with a loose density of **150 kg/m³**. Assuming a compaction ratio of **4:1**, design the landfill for **20 years** of operation. Each daily cell has dimensions of **5 m wide × 3 m deep × 60 m long**. Calculate: > (i) Compacted waste density > (ii) Daily cell volume required > (iii) Number of cells per day > (iv) Total landfill volume > (v) Total landfill footprint assuming an average landfill height of 18 m **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Components of a Sanitary Landfill (7 marks):** A modern sanitary landfill consists of the following engineering components, arranged from top to bottom: **1. Final Cover System (1 mark):** Installed upon closure, it consists of (top to bottom): - Top soil layer (150 mm) for vegetation - Geosynthetic drainage layer - Compacted clay barrier (k ≤ 10⁻⁹ m/s) - Geosynthetic clay liner (GCL) **Function:** Prevents rainwater infiltration, minimizes gas escape, controls erosion, and provides aesthetic integration. **2. Waste Mass (1 mark):** Compacted MSW placed in daily cells of 3–4 m thickness, with **150–300 mm of daily cover soil** applied at the end of each operating day. **3. Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS) (1 mark):** - 300 mm granular drainage layer (sand/gravel, k ≥ 10⁻³ m/s) - Perforated HDPE pipes (100–150 mm dia, spaced 30–60 m) - Side slopes ≥ 2% to ensure flow toward sumps - Sumps and pumps to remove collected leachate **Function:** Maintains leachate head on liner < 300 mm; conveys leachate to treatment plant. **4. Liner System (Primary) (1.5 marks):** - 600 mm compacted clay liner (CCL) with k ≤ 10⁻⁹ m/s - 1.5 to 2.5 mm HDPE geomembrane - Geotextile cushion to prevent puncture **Function:** Acts as the primary barrier preventing leachate migration to groundwater. **5. Secondary Liner & LDCRS (1 mark):** - 300 mm sand detection layer with perforated pipes - Second geomembrane and CCL below **Function:** Detects any leakage through primary liner and conveys it for removal. **6. Sub-base & Groundwater (0.5 marks):** - Compacted native subgrade - Groundwater table must be **at least 2 m below the bottom of the liner** **Cross-sectional diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw a vertical cross-section showing all six layers in sequence from top to bottom, similar to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.1. Label all components with their thicknesses.)* --- **(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):** **Given:** - $M_d = 800 \text{ t/day} = 800{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$ - $\rho_{loose} = 150 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $C = 4:1$ - $T = 20 \text{ years}$ - Cell: $L_c = 60 \text{ m}, W_c = 5 \text{ m}, H_c = 3 \text{ m}$ **Step 1: Compacted Density (1 mark)** $$\rho_{compacted} = C \times \rho_{loose} = 4 \times 150 = 600 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2: Daily Cell Volume (1 mark)** $$V_{daily} = \dfrac{M_d}{\rho_{compacted}} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{600} = 1333.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 3: Number of Cells per Day (1.5 marks)** $$V_{one\_cell} = 60 \times 5 \times 3 = 900 \text{ m}^3$$ $$N_{cells} = \dfrac{1333.33}{900} = 1.48 \approx 2 \text{ cells/day}$$ **Step 4: Total Landfill Volume (1.5 marks)** $$N_{days} = 20 \times 365 = 7300 \text{ days}$$ $$V_{total} = 1333.33 \times 7300 = 9{,}733{,}333 \text{ m}^3 \approx 9.73 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 5: Total Footprint (2 marks)** $$A_{landfill} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{H_{avg}} = \dfrac{9.73 \times 10^6}{18} = 540{,}741 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_{landfill} \approx 54.1 \text{ hectares}$$ | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $\rho_{compacted}$ | 600 kg/m³ | | $V_{daily}$ | 1333.33 m³ | | $N_{cells}$ | 2 per day | | $V_{total}$ | 9.73 × 10⁶ m³ | | $A_{landfill}$ | 54.1 ha | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** > **(a)** Explain in detail the **site selection criteria** for a sanitary landfill. Discuss the **environmental, geotechnical, and socio-economic** factors that govern site selection. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the generation, composition, and management of **landfill gas (LFG)**. Explain the **landfill gas collection system** with a neat sketch. How is methane utilized for **energy recovery**? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Site Selection Criteria (7 marks):** The selection of a landfill site is a multi-criteria decision governed by environmental protection, engineering feasibility, and public acceptance. **1. Environmental Criteria (2 marks):** - Distance from airport: > **10 km** (bird-strike hazard) - Distance from river/lake/reservoir: > **200 m**, preferably > **500 m** - Distance from habitation: > **500 m**, ideally > **1 km** - Not in notified forest, wetland, or eco-sensitive zone - Not in environmentally protected areas (wildlife sanctuaries, coastal regulation zone) **2. Geotechnical & Hydrogeological Criteria (2.5 marks):** - Soil permeability: low; **k < 10⁻⁷ m/s** for natural soil - Groundwater table depth: > **2 m** below proposed liner base - Geology: away from karst terrain, fault lines, seismic zones (Seismic Zone III or less preferred) - Slope: gentle, **< 15%**; flat or rolling terrain preferred - Bedrock depth: at least 5 m below liner - Foundation: stable, non-collapsible, non-expansive **3. Hydrological Criteria (1 mark):** - Site must be above **100-year flood level** - Adequate drainage to prevent waterlogging - Surface water diversion channels around the perimeter **4. Socio-Economic & Operational Criteria (1 mark):** - Land cost and availability - Public acceptance (NIMBY effect is common) - Road connectivity (12 m wide access road) - Distance from waste generation center: < **50 km** preferred - Site area sufficient for **15–20 years** of operation + buffer zone - Approach route should not pass through dense residential areas **5. Regulatory Criteria (0.5 mark):** - Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) clearance - Consent from State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) - Compliance with MSW Rules 2016 > **Decision:** The site must satisfy **ALL** criteria; if any one is violated, the site is rejected. --- **(b) Landfill Gas — Generation, Composition & Management (7 marks):** **1.
