| pH | **6.5 – 8.5** | Below 6 → fungal dominance, slow; Above 8.5 → NH₃ volatilization |
| Particle size | **10 – 50 mm** | Small → low FAS; Large → low surface area |
| O₂ concentration | **> 5% (10–18% ideal)** | Below 5% → anaerobic switch, odor (H₂S, mercaptans) |
| Bulk density | **350 – 550 kg/m³** | Indicator of compaction |
| C:P ratio | **75 : 1 to 150 : 1** | Low → P limitation |

### Calculation of C/N for Blended Waste

$$ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{blend}} = \frac{\sum m_i C_i}{\sum m_i N_i} $$

where **mᵢ** = mass of component *i*, **Cᵢ** = carbon fraction of *i*, and **Nᵢ** = nitrogen fraction of *i*.

---

## 2.3 Methods of Aerobic Composting

KTU expects you to know **all** of the following:

### (a) Windrow Composting
- Waste is piled in long rows (**windrows**) of triangular or trapezoidal cross-section.
- Periodically turned by **front-end loader** or **windrow turner**.
- **Turn frequency:** 3–10 days during active phase.
- **Disadvantage:** Odor, land-intensive, slow.

### (b) Aerated Static Pile (ASP)
- Pile sits on a **network of perforated aeration pipes** connected to a **blower**.
- Negative pressure (suction) or positive pressure (blowing) modes.
- **No turning required** → reduced labor and odor.
- **Drawback:** Channeling of air along pile edges.

### (c) In-Vessel Composting
- Composting inside an **enclosed reactor** (drum, silo, or tunnel).
- Continuous feed → continuous discharge.
- **Higher capital cost**, but **faster, odor-controlled**, and occupies less land.
- Examples: **Taiko Press, Eweson, Herhof systems**.

### (d) Vermicomposting
- Uses **epigeic earthworms** (*Eisenia foetida*, *Eudrilus eugeniae*).
- **Temperature:** 15–30 °C (mesophilic only).
- **Best for:** Kitchen waste, food waste, paper, but **not** for meat, dairy, oily waste.
- **Final product:** **Vermicompost** (rich in N, P, K, humic acid, and plant growth hormones).

---

## 2.4 Anaerobic Digestion — Four Biochemical Stages (HIGH-YIELD)

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **Reaction:** Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) → **monomers** (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids).
- **Enzymes:** **Cellulase, protease, lipase, amylase**.
- **Rate-limiting for:** Cellulosic waste (MSW, lignocellulosics).
- **Microbes:** *Clostridium*, *Streptococcus*, *Bacteroides*.

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- Monomers → **short-chain volatile fatty acids (VFAs)** + **alcohols** + **H₂** + **CO₂**.
- VFAs: acetic, propionic, butyric, valeric acids.
- Microbes: facultative anaerobes.

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis
- VFAs + alcohols → **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** + **H₂** + **CO₂**.
- Critical: H₂ partial pressure must be kept **low (< 10⁻⁴ atm)** by methanogens to keep this reaction thermodynamically favorable.

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **Two pathways:**
  1. **Acetoclastic:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (≈ 70% of CH₄)
  2. **Hydrogenotrophic:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (≈ 30%)
- **Microbes:** Strict anaerobes — *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*, *Methanosarcina*.
- **Doubling time:** 4–6 days — **slowest stage**, rate-limiting for soluble substrates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often claim acidogenesis is rate-limiting. The correct answer depends on substrate: **hydrolysis limits cellulosic MSW; methanogenesis limits already-solubilized waste (sludge)**.

---

## 2.5 Types of Anaerobic Digesters

| Type | Operating Mode | Key Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Batch** | All feedstock loaded once | Simple, used in labs/pilot |
| **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** | Single stage, fully mixed | Standard for sewage sludge |
| **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** | Unmixed, longitudinal flow | Good for fibrous waste |
| **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** | Biomass retained as granular blanket | High-rate, used for wastewater |
| **KVIC Floating Drum** | Indian rural design, gas collection in inverted drum | Household scale (1–6 m³) |
| **Janata / Fixed Dome** | Underground masonry, no moving parts | Low-cost, durable |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — these equations recur every KTU exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ideal C/N blend | $(C/N)_{blend} = \frac{\sum m_i C_i}{\sum m_i N_i}$ | Mass-weighted average |
| 2 | C/N after digestion (rule of thumb) | $C/N_{final} \approx (C/N)_{initial} / 2$ | For N loss via NH₃ |
| 3 | **Buswell Stoichiometry** (general) | $C_nH_aO_b + (n - a/4 - b/2)H_2O \rightarrow (n/2 - a/8 + b/4)CO_2 + (n/2 + a/8 - b/4)CH_4$ | Theoretical CH₄ yield |
| 4 | Methane yield per kg VS | $Y_{CH_4} = (n/2 + a/8 - b/4) \times 22.4 \text{ L/CH}_4$ | At STP, per mole substrate |
| 5 | Biogas energy content | $E = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ | Calorific value of pure CH₄ |
| 6 | Theoretical CH₄ (kg COD basis) | $0.35 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 \text{ per kg COD removed}$ | McCarty equation |
| 7 | **Volatile Solids Reduction (VSR)** | $VSR(\%) = \frac{VS_{in} - VS_{out}}{VS_{in}} \times 100$ | Typical 40–70% in MSW digesters |
| 8 | Volumetric methane production | $V_{CH_4} = VSR \times VS_{in} \times 0.35$ | m³ CH₄ per m³ reactor |
| 9 | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | $HRT = V/Q$ | V = digester volume, Q = flow |
| 10 | Solids Retention Time (SRT) | $SRT = V \cdot X / (Q \cdot X_e)$ | For CSTR with biomass X |
| 11 | Free Air Space (FAS) | $FAS(\%) = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{particle}}$ | Porosity of pile |
| 12 | Composting time (rule) | $t_{compost} = \frac{C/N_{initial} - C/N_{final}}{k \cdot rate}$ | Empirical kinetic model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** AD is the cornerstone of **waste-to-energy** policy in India (SATAT scheme by MoPNG, 2018). The composted/digested output links directly to the **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0** and the **National Biogas Programme**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — C/N Ratio of a Blended Substrate (7-Mark Favourite)

**Problem (KTU-typical):** A composting facility receives **500 kg/day** of municipal organic waste with **C/N = 40 : 1** and **200 kg/day** of nitrogen-rich sewage sludge with **C/N = 10 : 1**. Calculate the **C/N ratio of the blend**. Will the blend compost efficiently?

### Step 1 — List Given Quantities

| Stream | Mass (mᵢ) | C/N Ratio | %C (assume 50% C by dry mass for organics) | %N |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MSW organics | 500 kg | 40 | 50 % | 1.25 % |
| Sewage sludge | 200 kg | 10 | 50 % | 5.00 % |

> We will use the **mass-weighted C and N** method.

### Step 2 — Calculate Carbon Content of Each Stream

For MSW:
$$ C_{MSW} = 500 \times 0.50 = 250 \text{ kg C} $$

For sludge:
$$ C_{sludge} = 200 \times 0.50 = 100 \text{ kg C} $$

### Step 3 — Calculate Nitrogen Content of Each Stream

For MSW (from C/N = 40):
$$ N_{MSW} = \frac{C_{MSW}}{40} = \frac{250}{40} = 6.25 \text{ kg N} $$

For sludge (from C/N = 10):
$$ N_{sludge} = \frac{C_{sludge}}{10} = \frac{100}{10} = 10.0 \text{ kg N} $$

### Step 4 — Apply the Blending Formula

$$ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{blend} = \frac{\sum m_i C_i}{\sum m_i N_i} = \frac{C_{MSW} + C_{sludge}}{N_{MSW} + N_{sludge}} $$

$$ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{blend} = \frac{250 + 100}{6.25 + 10.0} = \frac{350}{16.25} $$

$$ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{blend} = 21.54 \approx 21.5 : 1 $$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **21.5 : 1 is BELOW the ideal 25–35 : 1 range.** This means **excess nitrogen** will be released as **ammonia (NH₃)** during composting → **odor problem** and **nitrogen loss** from final compost.
> **Remedy:** Mix in a **bulking agent (sawdust, dry leaves, straw, C/N ≈ 500 : 1)** to raise the blend's C/N back into the optimum window.

### Step 6 — Remedy Calculation (Optional Bonus Point)

To bring blend to target C/N = 30, required sawdust (assume C = 50%, N = 0.1%, so C/N = 500):

$$ \frac{350 + 0.5 x}{16.25 + 0.001 x} = 30 $$

Solving:
$$ 350 + 0.5x = 487.5 + 0.03x \Rightarrow 0.47x = 137.5 \Rightarrow x \approx 292.6 \text{ kg sawdust/day} $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Theoretical Methane Yield Using the Buswell Equation (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** Estimate the theoretical **biogas volume** (CH₄ + CO₂) and **% methane** from the anaerobic digestion of **1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)**. Take **buswell molecular formula as basis**.

### Step 1 — Write the Buswell Equation for Glucose (n=6, a=12, b=6)

$$ C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 $$

### Step 2 — Compute Water Coefficient

$$ n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0 $$

> Since this is **0**, no water is consumed or produced — glucose is self-balanced.

### Step 3 — Compute CO₂ Coefficient

$$ \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0 $$

### Step 4 — Compute CH₄ Coefficient

$$ \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3.0 $$

### Step 5 — Final Stoichiometric Equation

$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2 $$

> **Verification:** C: 6 = 3 + 3 ✓ | H: 12 = 12 ✓ | O: 6 = 6 ✓

### Step 6 — Convert to Moles from 1 kg Glucose

Molecular weight of glucose = **180 g/mol**.

$$ \text{Moles of glucose} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = 5.556 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 7 — Compute Moles of Each Gas

$$ n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667 \text{ mol} $$

$$ n_{CO_2} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 8 — Convert to Volume at STP (22.4 L/mol)

$$ V_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 22.4 \text{ L} = 373.33 \text{ L} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{CO_2} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ V_{biogas} = 0.747 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per kg glucose} $$

### Step 9 — Methane Percentage

$$ \% CH_4 = \frac{0.373}{0.747} \times 100 = 50\% $$

> Glucose → **50% CH₄ / 50% CO₂ biogas** — a textbook-perfect result.

### Step 10 — Energy Content

$$ E = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 0.373 \times 35.8 = 13.35 \text{ MJ per kg glucose} $$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — VSR and Biogas Yield for Real MSW

**Problem:** A **100 m³ CSTR digester** processes **10 tonnes/day** of MSW with **VS = 80%** (wet basis). Volatile solids reduction (VSR) achieved is **55%**. Find:
1. Daily methane production
2. Daily energy generation (kWh)

### Step 1 — Daily VS Input

$$ VS_{in} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.80 = 8000 \text{ kg VS/day} $$

### Step 2 — Mass of VS Destroyed

$$ VS_{destroyed} = 8000 \times 0.55 = 4400 \text{ kg VS/day} $$

### Step 3 — CH₄ Yield (McCarty: 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD; for VS use 0.4 m³/kg VS as average)

$$ V_{CH_4} = 4400 \times 0.4 = 1760 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day} $$

### Step 4 — Energy Content (35.8 MJ/m³ of CH₄)

$$ E_{MJ} = 1760 \times 35.8 = 63{,}008 \text{ MJ/day} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)

$$ E_{kWh} = \frac{63{,}008}{3.6} = 17{,}502 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 17.5 \text{ MWh/day} $$

### Step 6 — Power Equivalent

$$ \text{Power} = \frac{17{,}502}{24} \approx 729 \text{ kW continuous} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This 100 m³ plant can **electrify ~500 Kerala households** (assuming 1.5 kW per home) — illustrating the engineering scale-up relevance of these formulas.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a guaranteed 7-mark question** in some form. Memorize the contrasts.

| Parameter | Aerobic Composting | Anaerobic Digestion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Oxygen required | Yes (O₂ supplied) | No (O₂ excluded) |
| End products | Compost (humus), CO₂, H₂O, heat | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate |
| Energy balance | **Exothermic** (heat lost) | **Net energy positive** (biogas captured) |
| Decomposition speed | Fast (3–6 weeks for active) | Slower (15–40 days HRT) |
| C/N tolerance | Narrow (25–35 : 1) | Wider (15–45 : 1) |
| Moisture range | 50–60 % | 80–95 % (wet process) |
| Greenhouse gas | CO₂ (biogenic) | CH₄ (25× CO₂ GWP if leaked) |
| Pathogen kill | Excellent (55–65 °C) | Moderate (needs pasteurization step) |
| Odor potential | Low (with proper O₂) | High (H₂S, VFA, NH₃) |
| Capital cost | Low (windrow) to Medium (in-vessel) | High (digesters, gas handling) |
| Energy output | None (some heat recovery) | High (electricity, heat, CNG) |
| Footprint | Large (windrow) | Compact (closed reactor) |
| Operator skill | Low to Medium | High (microbiology sensitive) |
| Final use of product | Soil conditioner, fertilizer | Biogas = fuel; digestate = fertilizer |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define aerobic composting. State any two parameters that affect its efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerobic composting** is the controlled biological decomposition of organic solid waste by **aerobic microorganisms** in the presence of **free oxygen (O₂)**, yielding **CO₂, H₂O, heat**, and a humus-rich **compost**.

**Two parameters affecting efficiency:**

1. **C/N Ratio (optimum 25 : 1 to 35 : 1):** A low C/N causes nitrogen loss as ammonia; a high C/N limits microbial growth due to nitrogen starvation.

2. **Moisture Content (50–60%):** Below 45%, microbial activity halts due to lack of solute transport. Above 65%, water fills pore spaces, displaces oxygen, and creates anaerobic pockets producing odor (H₂S, mercaptans).

*(Any other correct parameter: pH 6.5–8.5, O₂ > 5%, particle size 10–50 mm, temperature 55 °C in thermophilic phase.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion with the major end product of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Process | Major End Product |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Hydrolysis | Polymers → Monomers | Amino acids, sugars, fatty acids |
| 2. Acidogenesis | Monomers → VFAs | Acetate, propionate, butyrate, H₂, CO₂ |
| 3. Acetogenesis | VFAs → Acetate | Acetic acid, H₂, CO₂ |
| 4. Methanogenesis | Acetate / H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ | **Methane (CH₄)** |

---

## 📗 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose THIS or Question B

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **factors affecting aerobic composting** (C/N ratio, moisture, pH, temperature, particle size, O₂, porosity). Mention the **role of thermophilic phase** in pathogen destruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipality generates **800 kg/day** of segregated organic waste with **C/N = 45 : 1** and **150 kg/day** of paper waste with **C/N = 170 : 1**. Calculate the **C/N ratio of the blend**. Comment on whether composting will proceed efficiently. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (a)

**1. C/N Ratio (3 marks' worth of detail)**
- Ideal: **25 : 1 to 35 : 1**.
- If C/N < 20 → **excess N** released as NH₃ → odor + N loss.
- If C/N > 40 → microbes are **N-starved** → slow composting.
- Corrective action: blend with sawdust / dry leaves (high C) or sewage sludge (high N).

**2. Moisture Content**
- **50–60%** by weight.
- Maintains **water film** for microbial nutrient transport.
- Excess water → displaces O₂ in pores → anaerobic conditions, leachate.

**3. pH**
- Optimal **6.5 – 8.5**.
- Falls to 4.5–5.0 during early acidogenesis; rises to 8.0–8.5 during ammonia release.

**4. Temperature (with thermophilic phase)**
- Mesophilic 0–40 °C → Thermophilic 40–65 °C → Cooling → Curing.
- **Thermophilic phase is critical** because it destroys **pathogens (Salmonella, E. coli, helminth ova)** and **weed seeds** within **3–5 days of sustained > 55 °C** — this is mandated under **Schedule II of SWM Rules 2016**.

**5. Particle Size**
- **10 – 50 mm** optimum.
- Smaller particles → larger surface area → faster, but pore clogging.
- Larger particles → better aeration, but slow microbial access.

**6. Oxygen / Aeration**
- Maintain **O₂ > 5%** (ideally 10–18%) in pile atmosphere.
- Below 5% → anaerobic switch → H₂S, CH₄, organic acids → odor + slow.

**7. Free Air Space (FAS / Porosity)**
- **≥ 30%** required.
- Maintained by adding bulking agents (wood chips, straw).

#### 📝 Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Carbon content of each stream**

| Stream | Mass (kg) | C/N | Approx %C | kg C | kg N |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Organic waste | 800 | 45 | 50% | 400 | 8.89 |
| Paper waste | 150 | 170 | 46% | 69 | 0.41 |

(Using %C by dry mass assumption: organic = 50% C, paper = 46% C.)

**Step 2 — Compute N from C and C/N**

For organic waste:
$$ N_{org} = \frac{C_{org}}{C/N} = \frac{400}{45} = 8.89 \text{ kg} $$

For paper:
$$ N_{paper} = \frac{69}{170} = 0.41 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Apply Blend Formula**

$$ \left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_{blend} = \frac{400 + 69}{8.89 + 0.41} = \frac{469}{9.30} = 50.43 : 1 $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

> The blend C/N of **50.4 : 1** is **well above the ideal 25–35 range**. Composting will be **slow** due to nitrogen starvation. **Remedy:** Add a **nitrogen-rich amendment** (urea, sewage sludge, poultry manure) or reduce paper fraction.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Buswell equation**, derive the **theoretical methane yield** for complete anaerobic digestion of **1 kg of acetic acid (CH₃COOH)**. State the **% of CH₄** in the resulting biogas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **aerobic composting** and **anaerobic digestion** with respect to **energy recovery, end products, microbial ecology, footprint, and suitability for Kerala's MSW**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Identify n, a, b for Acetic Acid**

Acetic acid: **CH₃COOH = C₂H₄O₂**
- n = 2, a = 4, b = 2

**Step 2 — Apply Buswell Equation**

$$ C_2H_4O_2 + (n - a/4 - b/2)H_2O \rightarrow (n/2 - a/8 + b/4)CO_2 + (n/2 + a/8 - b/4)CH_4 $$

Water coefficient:
$$ n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(no water required)} $$

CO₂ coefficient:
$$ \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 $$

CH₄ coefficient:
$$ \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = 1 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 $$

**Step 3 — Balanced Reaction**

$$ CH_3COOH \longrightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 $$

**Step 4 — Yield from 1 kg Acetic Acid**

Molecular weight = **60 g/mol**

$$ n_{acetic} = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.667 \text{ mol} $$

CH₄ moles = 16.667 mol → Volume at STP:
$$ V_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 22.4 \text{ L} = 373.3 \text{ L} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 5 — % Methane**

Biogas = 1 mol CH₄ + 1 mol CO₂ = 2 mol total
$$ \%CH_4 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50\% $$

> **Answer:** Theoretical CH₄ yield ≈ **0.373 m³ per kg acetic acid**, biogas contains **50% CH₄ and 50% CO₂**. This matches the well-known fact that **acetoclastic methanogenesis** is the dominant methane-producing pathway in digesters.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to (b) — Comparison

| Criterion | Aerobic Composting | Anaerobic Digestion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy recovery** | Negligible; heat is lost to atmosphere. | **Positive — biogas can generate electricity, heat, or CNG.** |
| **End products** | Compost (humus), CO₂, H₂O, heat. | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate slurry. |
| **Microbial ecology** | Aerobic bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi; broad temperature range. | Strict anaerobes, Archaea, sensitive to O₂; narrow optimal conditions. |
| **Footprint** | Land-intensive (windrow requires large area). | Compact (closed reactor saves land). |
| **Suitability for Kerala** | Excellent for **kitchen waste, market waste, coir pith, and plantation leaf litter**. | Highly suitable — Kerala has **wet organic waste (high moisture)** which favors wet AD. |
| **Synergy** | Digestate from AD can be further **composted** to produce a stable soil conditioner. |  |

> **Concluding remark for KTU:** A **hybrid plant** (Anaerobic Digestion of wet organic fraction → Aerobic Composting of digestate) is the **best integrated strategy** for Kerala's MSW, especially under the **SMART SWM initiative by Suchitwa Mission**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hydrolysis and acidogenesis:** Many students merge them into one stage. Examiner expects them **separately**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in C/N calculation:** Writing "C/N = 21.5" without the ": 1" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Buswell equation mistakes:** Swapping CO₂ and CH₄ coefficients is the most common error. **Mnemonic:** "n/2 − a/8 + b/4" is CO₂; the **mirror** gives CH₄.
> 4. **Citing C/N = 30 as a single value:** Examiner expects the **range 25 : 1 to 35 : 1**.
> 5. **Missing pathogen destruction angle:** Forgetting to mention **SWM Rules 2016 / Schedule II / FCO standards** in the thermophilic phase explanation loses application marks.
> 6. **Omitting temperature vs. moisture interplay:** The 50–60% moisture window **must** be linked to the **thermophilic temperature**; otherwise, dry pockets and wet pockets are not understood.
> 7. **Saying "AD is anaerobic digestion" without elaborating methanogens:** Always specify the **Archaea domain** — examiners expect modern taxonomy.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Aerobic Composting** = **O₂ present**, exothermic, end product is **compost (humus)**.
- ✅ **Anaerobic Digestion** = **O₂ absent**, net energy positive, end products are **biogas + digestate**.
- ✅ Ideal **C/N = 25 : 1 to 35 : 1**; ideal **moisture = 50–60%**; ideal **pH = 6.5–8.5**; ideal **O₂ ≥ 5%**.
- ✅ Four phases of composting: **Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Curing**.
- ✅ Four stages of AD: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- ✅ **Buswell Equation** is the master stoichiometry — memorize the CH₄ coefficient: **n/2 + a/8 − b/4**.
- ✅ **Methane yield ≈ 0.35 m³ per kg COD destroyed** (McCarty).
- ✅ **Acetoclastic methanogenesis** contributes ~**70%** of total CH₄ in digesters.
- ✅ **Hydrolysis** is rate-limiting for **cellulosic MSW**; **methanogenesis** is rate-limiting for **soluble substrates**.
- ✅ **Thermophilic phase (40–65 °C)** is essential for **pathogen destruction** (SWM Rules 2016 compliance).
- ✅ **Vermicomposting** uses **Eisenia foetida** at **mesophilic range (15–30 °C)**.
- ✅ Kerala's MSW is **high-moisture, high-organic** → **AD is preferred**; complement with aerobic composting of digestate.
- ✅ **KVIC floating-drum** and **Janata fixed-dome** are common Indian biogas plant designs.
- ✅ Biogas composition: **CH₄ 50–70%** and **CO₂ 30–40%**; energy content **18–26 MJ/m³**.
- ✅ Methane is **25× more potent than CO₂** as a greenhouse gas → prevent leakage from AD systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas Plant Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biogas Plant Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biogas Plant Operation** refers to the engineered, microbiologically-mediated controlled decomposition of organic solid waste (biomass, municipal solid waste, animal manure, agricultural residue, food waste) under **anaerobic conditions** (absence of free oxygen) in a sealed, airtight reactor called a **digester**, producing a combustible gas mixture termed **biogas** (composed primarily of **Methane (CH₄, 50–70%)** and **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂, 30–40%)**), along with a nutrient-rich liquid effluent (**digestate**) and a stabilized solid fraction (**bio-slurry/digestate cake**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Biogas plant operations span feedstock preparation, digester loading regimes, microbiological kinetics, process parameter control (pH, temperature, HRT, OLR, C/N ratio), gas collection, and digestate management. The phenomenon is governed by the **Buswell Equation** and standard anaerobic digestion stoichiometry.

## 1.2 The Four-Stage Microbiological Cascade (AD Process)

The conversion of complex organics to methane proceeds through four sequential trophic groups of microorganisms:

| Stage | Microbial Group | Input Substrate | Output Product | Optimum pH |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|------------|
| 1. **Hydrolysis** | Hydrolytic bacteria | Polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) | Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| 2. **Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic (fermentative) bacteria | Monomers | Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂ | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| 3. **Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria | VFAs, alcohols | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | 6.0 – 7.0 |
| 4. **Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate, H₂ + CO₂ | CH₄ + CO₂ | 6.5 – 7.8 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach as a Living Biogas Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a biogas plant as an **industrial-scale cow's rumen**. Just as a cow swallows grass, ferments it in four stomach chambers (without oxygen), and burps out methane, a biogas digester:
> - **Receives organic waste** (the "grass") via a feed inlet.
> - **Seals it in an oxygen-free tank** (the "rumen").
> - **Bacteria digest it** in four stages over 30–40 days.
> - **Releases biogas** (the "burp") captured for cooking/electricity.
> - **Excretes nutrient-rich slurry** (the "manure") used as biofertilizer.
>
> The major difference: A cow's body temperature (**38–39°C**) is mesophilic — and most biogas plants deliberately operate in this **mesophilic range (30–40°C)** because methane-producing archaea thrive there.

## 1.4 Key Process Parameters (Examination Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Operational Constants** (must memorize):
> - **Optimum pH for methanogens:** **6.5 – 7.5** (critical — drop below 6.2 causes VFA accumulation and digester "souring").
> - **Optimum C:N ratio of feedstock:** **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** (ideal **25:1**).
> - **Mesophilic operating temperature:** **30 – 40°C** (typical **35°C**).
> - **Thermophilic operating temperature:** **50 – 60°C** (typical **55°C**) — faster kinetics but energy-intensive.
> - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) for municipal waste:** **30 – 40 days**.
> - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** **1.0 – 3.0 kg VS/m³/day** for solid waste digesters.
> - **Biogas calorific value:** **18 – 25 MJ/m³** (≈ 5000 – 7000 kcal/m³).
> - **Methane density (STP):** **0.717 kg/m³**.
> - **Theoretical methane yield (Buswell):** **0.25 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Biogas Plant

A functional biogas plant integrates **five engineering subsystems**:

1. **Feedstock Preparation Unit** — Shredding (particle size 5–10 mm), mixing, dilution to total solids (TS) of **8–12%**.
2. **Digester (Reactor Vessel)** — Airtight, insulated, mixed/unnixed reactor (continuous stirred tank reactor — CSTR, plug flow, or upflow anaerobic sludge blanket — UASB).
3. **Gas Collection & Storage** — Floating gas holder, fixed dome, or gasholder; pressure regulators; H₂S scrubber (iron oxide / activated carbon).
4. **Digestate Management** — Liquid slurry storage pond; solid-liquid separation; composting of separated solids.
5. **Utilization / End-Use** — Burner, generator (CHP), purification to biomethane (CO₂/H₂S removal via water scrubbing, PSA, or membrane separation).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Four Stages

### Stage 1: Hydrolysis (Rate-Limiting for Cellulosic Wastes)
- **Why it matters:** Complex polymers (cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, lipids) have molecular weights of **10⁴–10⁶ Da** — too large for microbial transport.
- **How it works:** Exo-enzymes (cellulases, proteases, lipases) secreted by hydrolytic bacteria cleave polymers into soluble monomers.
- **Rate-limiting factor:** For lignocellulosic MSW, hydrolysis is the **slowest stage**; for readily biodegradable food waste, methanogenesis is rate-limiting.

### Stage 2: Acidogenesis
- **Why:** Monomers are fermented by *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides* into VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, and gases.
- **Hazard:** A sudden organic overload (e.g., festival waste spike) triggers rapid acidogenesis → VFA spike → **pH crash below 6.2** → methanogen inhibition → "sour digester."

### Stage 3: Acetogenesis
- **Syntrophic acetogens** (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*) oxidize VFAs to acetate, H₂, and CO₂.
- **Critical thermodynamics:** Propionate oxidation is endergonic under standard conditions (ΔG°' = +76.1 kJ/mol) — requires H₂ partial pressure kept **below 10⁻⁴ atm** by methanogens (interspecies hydrogen transfer).

### Stage 4: Methanogenesis
- **Two pathways:**
  - **Acetoclastic (≈ 70% of CH₄):** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (catalyzed by *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*).
  - **Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30%):** 4H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O (catalyzed by *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the 14-mark question:**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Buswell Equation** (general) | $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$ | — | Stoichiometric prediction of CH₄:CO₂ ratio |
| 2 | **Methane yield (Buswell for carbohydrate CnH₂nOn)** | $B_0 = \dfrac{22.4 \times n}{12n + 2n + 16n} \times 0.4$ | m³ CH₄/kg VS | Theoretical yield: 0.4 m³ CH₄/kg glucose |
| 3 | **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $OLR = \dfrac{M_{feed} \times VS\%}{V_{digester} \times t_{HRT}}$ | kg VS/m³·day | Sizing of digester volume |
| 4 | **Hydraulic Retention Time** | $HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{Q_{feed}}$ | days | Determines feed rate |
| 5 | **Biogas yield (empirical)** | $Y_{biogas} = 0.37 \times (\%VS/100) \times COD_{feed}$ | m³/kg COD | COD-based yield estimation |
| 6 | **Methane fraction correction** | $Y_{CH_4} = Y_{biogas} \times (\%CH_4 / 100)$ | m³ CH₄/kg | Design of gas holder |
| 7 | **Digester volume** | $V = \dfrac{M_{waste/day} \times VS\%}{OLR \times \rho_{slurry}}$ | m³ | Plant sizing |
| 8 | **Energy recovery** | $E = Y_{CH_4} \times 35.8 \times 0.9$ | MJ/kg VS | CHP design (η = 35.8 MJ/m³ CH₄ × 90% conversion) |
| 9 | **C/N ratio adjustment** | $C/N_{mix} = \dfrac{C_1 W_1 + C_2 W_2}{N_1 W_1 + N_2 W_2}$ | — | Feedstock blending |
| 10 | **Souring indicator** | $VFA/Alkalinity > 0.4$ | — | Process stability warning |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Electrification:** Decentralized 1–10 m³ KVIC plants provide cooking fuel and lighting to **millions of Indian households** under the National Biogas Programme (target: 50 lakh plants).
- **Municipal Solid Waste:** Large UASB/CSTR plants (e.g., 1000 TPD Okhla, Delhi) generate **5–8 MW electricity** from landfill-diverted waste.
- **Climate Co-Benefit:** CH₄ has **Global Warming Potential (GWP) = 28–36** vs CO₂ — capturing it offsets **6.5–7.0 tonnes CO₂-equivalent per tonne MSW treated**.
- **Circular Economy:** Digestate returns N-P-K to soil, replacing synthetic fertilizers (saves **40–60 kg CO₂-eq/tonne fertilizer**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Buswell Equation for Generic Substrate

### Step 0: Setup
Consider a generic organic substrate with molecular formula $C_n H_a O_b$. Under complete anaerobic digestion, the reaction yields only CH₄ and CO₂ (since H₂S, NH₃ are trace).

The generic equation template is:

$$C_n H_a O_b + x\,H_2O \rightarrow y\,CH_4 + z\,CO_2$$

### Step 1: Carbon Balance
Each carbon atom in the substrate ends up in either CH₄ or CO₂. So:

$$n = y + z$$

### Step 2: Hydrogen Balance
Each H₂O contributes 2 H atoms; each CH₄ contains 4 H atoms. Substrate contributes 'a' H atoms.

$$a + 2x = 4y$$

### Step 3: Oxygen Balance
Substrate has 'b' O atoms; H₂O contributes 'x' O atoms; CO₂ contains 2 O atoms.

$$b + x = 2z$$

### Step 4: Solve the Three-Equation System

From Step 1: $z = n - y$

Substitute into Step 3:

$$b + x = 2(n - y) \implies x = 2n - 2y - b$$

Substitute into Step 2:

$$a + 2(2n - 2y - b) = 4y \implies a + 4n - 4y - 2b = 4y$$

$$8y = 4n + a - 2b \implies y = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}$$

Then:

$$z = n - y = n - \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}$$

And:

$$x = 2n - 2y - b = 2n - 2\left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) - b = -\dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}$$

$$x = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}$$

### Step 5: Final Buswell Equation

$$\boxed{C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check — Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)
Plug n=6, a=12, b=6:

- $y = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$ mol CH₄
- $z = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$ mol CO₂
- $x = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$ (no water needed — glucose is already oxidized enough)

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Theoretical yield of CH₄ from glucose = 3 mol CH₄/mol glucose = 0.373 m³ CH₄/kg glucose (at STP).**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Biogas Plant Sizing (Digester Volume)

### Given
- Daily waste input: $W$ kg/day
- Volatile Solids (VS) content: $V_s$ (fraction)
- Operating HRT: $\tau$ days
- Slurry density: $\rho$ kg/m³ (typical 1000 kg/m³)
- OLR target: $L$ kg VS/m³·day

### Step 1: Express Daily VS Loading
$$M_{VS} = W \times V_s \quad \text{[kg VS/day]}$$

### Step 2: Apply OLR Definition
$$L = \dfrac{M_{VS}}{V \times \tau} \quad \text{[kg VS/m³·day]}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Digester Volume

$$V = \dfrac{M_{VS}}{L \times \tau} = \dfrac{W \times V_s}{L \times \tau} \quad \text{[m³]}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
A community generates **W = 500 kg/day food waste**, **VS = 85%**, design OLR = **2.0 kg VS/m³·day**, HRT = **30 days**.

$$V = \dfrac{500 \times 0.85}{2.0 \times 30} = \dfrac{425}{60} = 7.08 \, m^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 7.1 \, m^3 \text{ digester volume required}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Methane Energy Recovery

### Given
- Daily VS destroyed: $M_{VS,dest}$ kg/day
- Specific methane yield: $B_0$ m³ CH₄/kg VS (typical 0.25–0.40)
- Methane calorific value: $LHV_{CH_4} = 35.8$ MJ/m³
- CHP electrical efficiency: $\eta_{el} = 35\%$

### Step 1: Daily Methane Volume

$$Q_{CH_4} = M_{VS,dest} \times B_0 \quad \text{[m³ CH₄/day]}$$

### Step 2: Daily Chemical Energy

$$E_{chem} = Q_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4} \quad \text{[MJ/day]}$$

### Step 3: Daily Electrical Output

$$P_{el} = \dfrac{E_{chem} \times \eta_{el}}{24 \times 3600} \quad \text{[MW]}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
- $M_{VS,dest} = 425$ kg/day (from above)
- $B_0 = 0.30$ m³/kg VS
- $\eta_{el} = 0.35$

$$Q_{CH_4} = 425 \times 0.30 = 127.5 \, m^3/day$$

$$E_{chem} = 127.5 \times 35.8 = 4564.5 \, MJ/day$$

$$P_{el} = \dfrac{4564.5 \times 0.35}{86400} = 0.0185 \, MW \approx 18.5 \, kW$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Comparing Digester Configurations

| Parameter | KVIC (Floating Drum) | Deenabandhu (Fixed Dome) | Janata Model | CSTR (Industrial) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Capital cost | Medium | Low | Very low | High |
| Gas pressure | **Constant** (drum weight) | Variable (rising dome) | Low / none | Regulated by compressor |
| **Gas storage** | Built-in (drum) | External gasholder | None (low pressure) | External gasholder |
| Suitability for MSW | Limited (dung) | Limited (dung) | Limited (dung) | **Best for MSW** |
| HRT typical | 30–40 days | 40–55 days | 55–60 days | 15–25 days |
| Gas leakage | Low | Moderate (masonry joints) | High | Very low (steel) |
| Operating pressure | **40–80 cm WC** | 80–120 cm WC | Atmospheric | 200–500 cm WC |
| Indian context | **Most common rural** | Most common for subsidies | Outdated | Urban plants |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the term "Hydraulic Retention Time" (HRT) as applied to biogas plant operations. State the typical HRT range for mesophilic digestion of municipal solid waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** HRT is the average time the input slurry resides inside the anaerobic digester, defined mathematically as the ratio of the digester working volume to the daily feed/flow rate:
$$HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester} \, [m^3]}{Q_{feed} \, [m^3/day]} \quad \text{[days]}$$
- **Typical range (1 mark):** For mesophilic (30–40°C) digestion of MSW, HRT is **30–40 days**. For high-solid batch systems, it can extend to **40–60 days**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**State the four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion. What is the optimum pH for the methanogenic stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Hydrolysis** — polymers broken down to monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis** — monomers fermented to volatile fatty acids.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs converted to acetate and H₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — acetate and H₂ converted to CH₄ and CO₂.
- **Optimum pH for methanogens:** **6.5 – 7.5** (ideal ≈ **7.0**). Below 6.2, "souring" occurs.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select ONE)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Digester Design & Sizing Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of the **Buswell Equation**, derive the stoichiometric relationship for complete anaerobic digestion of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆). Calculate the theoretical volume of methane produced at STP from 1 kg of glucose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community generates **800 kg/day of mixed food waste** with volatile solids content of **80%** and moisture of **75%**. Design a mesophilic anaerobic digester operating at HRT of **35 days** and OLR of **2.5 kg VS/m³·day**. Estimate the daily methane production and electrical energy recoverable at 35% CHP efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Buswell Equation for Glucose (7 marks):**

Step 1: Substitute n = 6, a = 12, b = 6 into the Buswell template:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + \left(6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4}\right)CO_2$$

Step 2: Simplify each coefficient:

- Water: $6 - 3 - 3 = 0$
- CH₄: $3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$ mol
- CO₂: $3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$ mol

Step 3: Final balanced equation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Step 4: Calculate CH₄ volume at STP from 1 kg glucose:

Moles of glucose in 1 kg:

$$n_{glucose} = \dfrac{1000}{180} = 5.556 \, mol$$

Moles of CH₄ produced (3:1 stoichiometry):

$$n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.67 \, mol$$

Volume at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L):

$$V_{CH_4} = 16.67 \times 22.4 = 373.3 \, L = 0.373 \, m^3 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Step 5: Theoretical yield:

$$\boxed{Y_{CH_4} = 0.373 \, m^3 \, CH_4 \text{ per kg glucose} \quad \text{[1 mark]}}$$

Step 6: Stoichiometric interpretation (CH₄:CO₂ = 1:1 → 50% CH₄ content, as observed in real digesters for sugar-rich waste). **[1 mark]**

**Part (b) — Digester Sizing (7 marks):**

Step 1: Daily VS input:

$$M_{VS} = 800 \times 0.80 = 640 \, kg \, VS/day \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 2: Required digester volume (using OLR = 2.5 kg VS/m³·day):

$$V = \dfrac{M_{VS}}{OLR \times HRT} = \dfrac{640}{2.5 \times 35} = \dfrac{640}{87.5} = 7.31 \, m^3 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 3: Add 20% headspace for gas storage:

$$V_{total} = 1.20 \times 7.31 = 8.77 \, m^3 \approx 9 \, m^3 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 4: Daily methane production (assuming 80% VS destruction and Buswell-type yield 0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS):

$$Q_{CH_4} = 640 \times 0.80 \times 0.30 = 153.6 \, m^3 \, CH_4/day \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 5: Chemical energy content:

$$E_{chem} = 153.6 \times 35.8 = 5499 \, MJ/day \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 6: Electrical power output (η = 0.35):

$$P_{el} = \dfrac{5499 \times 0.35}{86400} = 0.0223 \, MW = 22.3 \, kW \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Step 7: Annual energy production:

$$\boxed{E_{annual} = 22.3 \times 24 \times 365 = 195{,}228 \, kWh/year \approx 195 \, MWh/year \quad \text{[1 mark]}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting the **20% headspace** in digester sizing → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using **HRT = 35 days** but applying the formula incorrectly as $V = M_{VS} \times HRT$ instead of dividing by OLR → loses 1.5 marks.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not converting the **molar volume** (22.4 L/mol at STP) — students often incorrectly use 24.5 L (which is NTP) → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 4:** For **Buswell**, students forget to specify **n, a, b** values clearly before substitution → loses 1 mark for "lack of work shown."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Process Control & Operational Failure Analysis [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** in detail with their characteristic microorganisms, substrates, and products. Why is hydrolysis considered the rate-limiting step for lignocellulosic wastes? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digester operating at **35°C (mesophilic)** suddenly shows a **pH drop from 7.2 to 5.8**, with **VFA accumulation to 8000 mg/L** and **methane content falling to 25%**. Diagnose the failure, identify the root cause, and recommend a **step-by-step operational recovery protocol**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Four Stages of AD (7 marks):**

**Stage 1: Hydrolysis (2 marks)**
- **Microorganisms:** Hydrolytic/fermentative bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Cellulomonas*).
- **Substrates:** Complex polymers — cellulose, hemicellulose, starch, proteins, lipids.
- **Products:** Soluble monomers — sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids, glycerol.
- **Key enzymes:** Cellulases, amylases, proteases, lipases.

**Stage 2: Acidogenesis (1.5 marks)**
- **Microorganisms:** Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bifidobacterium*).
- **Substrates:** Monomers from Stage 1.
- **Products:** Volatile fatty acids (acetic, propionic, butyric, valeric), alcohols, H₂, CO₂.

**Stage 3: Acetogenesis (1.5 marks)**
- **Microorganisms:** Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*).
- **Substrates:** VFAs, alcohols.
- **Products:** Acetate, H₂, CO₂.
- **Critical:** Syntrophic relationship with methanogens to keep H₂ partial pressure low (interspecies hydrogen transfer).

**Stage 4: Methanogenesis (1.5 marks)**
- **Microorganisms:** Methanogenic archaea (e.g., *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*, *Methanobacterium*).
- **Substrates:** Acetate (70%) and H₂ + CO₂ (30%).
- **Products:** CH₄ + CO₂.

**Why hydrolysis is rate-limiting for lignocellulosic waste (0.5 marks):**
Lignocellulose is recalcitrant due to the **crystalline cellulose-hemicellulose-lignin matrix**. Aerobic/anaerobic microbes lack efficient ligninases; the slow breakdown of cellulose (k ≈ 0.1/day) becomes the bottleneck. Pre-treatments (thermal, mechanical, enzymatic) accelerate hydrolysis.

**Part (b) — Failure Diagnosis & Recovery (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Diagnosis (2 marks)**
- **pH 5.8** is far below the methanogenic optimum (6.5–7.5).
- **VFA at 8000 mg/L** is alarmingly high (safe < 2000 mg/L).
- **CH₄ drop to 25%** confirms methanogen inhibition.
- **Diagnosis: Digester "souring" / acid crash.**

**Step 2: Root Cause (1.5 marks)**
- **Most likely cause:** Sudden organic overload (e.g., festival waste spike, industrial food waste addition) or hydraulic overloading.
- Result: Acidogens outpace methanogens → VFA accumulation → pH crash → methanogenic archaea (slow growers, generation time 3–5 days) are washed out or inhibited.

**Step 3: Step-by-Step Recovery Protocol (3.5 marks)**

| Step | Action | Rationale |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **STOP feeding** the digester immediately | Prevents further VFA build-up |
| 2 | **Reduce OLR by 50%** when feeding resumes | Gives methanogens time to recover |
| 3 | **Add alkalinity** — dose NaHCO₃ or Ca(OH)₂ to raise pH to 6.8–7.0 | Buffers VFA acidity |
| 4 | **Increase mixing intensity** (gentle, no foam) | Improves mass transfer; releases trapped H₂ |
| 5 | **Enhance H₂ consumption** — improve HRT | Promotes hydrogenotrophic methanogens |
| 6 | **Consider bio-augmentation** with commercial methanogen consortia | Accelerates recovery |
| 7 | **Monitor daily** — pH, VFA, alkalinity, gas composition | Stability indicators |
| 8 | **Resume normal OLR** only when VFA < 2000 mg/L and pH > 6.8 for 5 consecutive days | Confirms recovery |
| 9 | **Implement VFA/Alkalinity ratio alarm at > 0.4** | Future early warning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students write only "stop feeding" without giving a **complete recovery protocol** (loses up to 2.5 marks).
> - **Common mistake 2:** Confusing **acidogenesis** (Stage 2) with **acetogenesis** (Stage 3) — VFA accumulation is a Stage 2/3 imbalance, not Stage 4. Examiners check if the student can localize the failure.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not explaining the **interspecies hydrogen transfer** concept (0.5–1 mark loss).
> - **Common mistake 4:** Failing to use the **VFA/Alkalinity ratio** — this is the *de facto* industry stability indicator and is a high-value KTU term.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Memorize List for Biogas Plant Operations (Module 3):**

- ✅ **Four stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- ✅ **Buswell Equation** with n, a, b substitution — must derive for any CₙHₐOᵦ.
- ✅ **Glucose Buswell:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 3CH₄ + 3CO₂ → **0.373 m³ CH₄/kg**.
- ✅ **Optimum pH (methanogens):** **6.5–7.5**; C/N ratio: **20–30:1**.
- ✅ **Mesophilic:** 30–40°C; **Thermophilic:** 50–60°C.
- ✅ **HRT** = V/Q; **OLR** = M_VS / (V·τ).
- ✅ **Digester types:** KVIC (floating drum), Deenabandhu (fixed dome), Janata, CSTR/UASB.
- ✅ **Souring indicator:** VFA/Alkalinity > 0.4; pH < 6.2.
- ✅ **Methane calorific value:** **35.8 MJ/m³**; biogas calorific value: **18–25 MJ/m³**.
- ✅ **Major Indian biogas schemes:** National Biogas Programme (50 lakh plants target), SATAT scheme (CBG from waste).
- ✅ **Co-benefit:** CH₄ GWP = 28–36 → offsets **6.5–7.0 tCO₂-eq per tMSW**.
- ✅ **Digester design headspace:** add **20%** to working volume.
- ✅ **Slurry TS optimum:** **8–12%**; particle size: **5–10 mm**.

topics.title: Incineration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Incineration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Incineration** is a controlled, high-temperature thermal treatment process in which combustible solid waste is oxidized (burned) in the presence of excess air (or oxygen) to convert it into incombustible residues (ash, slag), heat energy, and flue gases. The principal objective is **volume reduction (typically 80–90%)** and **mass reduction (typically 65–75%)** of municipal solid waste (MSW), along with energy recovery (in modern waste-to-energy plants).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):** *“Incineration is the combustion of organic and combustible fraction of solid waste under controlled conditions to reduce its volume, mass, and toxicity, while recovering energy in the form of steam, hot water, or electricity.”*

The three engineering essentials governing any incinerator are collectively called the **“3-T Rule”**:

| Parameter | Typical Range (MSW) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | **850 °C – 1200 °C** | Ensures destruction of pathogens, dioxins/furans precursors |
| **Time (Residence)** | **1 – 2 seconds** (for flue gases at ≥ 850 °C) | Ensures complete combustion of volatiles |
| **Turbulence** | High mixing intensity | Ensures O₂–fuel contact |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of incineration like a **"controlled bonfire inside a sealed, engineered furnace."** When you burn a pile of leaves in your backyard, smoke (unburnt hydrocarbons), ash, and foul gases are released — this is *uncontrolled* combustion. An incinerator, on the other hand, is like a **scientifically designed high-tech oven** that:

1. **Dries** the waste (evaporates moisture).
2. **Burns** the volatile gases at 850–1200 °C.
3. **Burns** the fixed carbon in the residue.
4. **Cools and cleans** the flue gas before letting it out the stack.

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world analogy: Just as a pressure cooker cooks food faster and more cleanly than an open pot, an incinerator burns waste faster, hotter, and with air-pollution control — turning "trash" into "ash + energy."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Standards)

- **Stoichiometric O₂ demand for C → CO₂:** $1\ \text{kmol O}_2 / 1\ \text{kmol C}$
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of typical MSW:** **8 – 12 MJ/kg** (≈ 2000 – 2800 kcal/kg)
- **Auxiliary fuel support** (e.g., diesel) required if waste LHV < **5 MJ/kg**
- **Excess air required:** **80 – 150 %** of stoichiometric
- **Flue gas residence time at ≥ 850 °C:** **≥ 2 seconds** (statutory)
- **Particulate emission limit (Indian CPCB):** **< 50 mg/Nm³** for new plants

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Combustion Sequence

A solid-waste incinerator goes through **four sequential zones** along the grate:

1. **Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C):** Moisture evaporates using radiant + convective heat from the flame above.
2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (200 – 600 °C):** Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are driven off as combustible gases (CH₄, H₂, CO, tars).
3. **Combustion (Volatiles Burning) Zone (600 – 1000 °C):** Volatiles mix with secondary air and ignite.
4. **Char Burn-out Zone (800 – 1200 °C):** Fixed carbon (residual char) reacts with oxygen: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 3-T Rule matters:** If the temperature is < 850 °C, dioxins (PCDD/Fs) form in the cooling flue gas. If residence time < 2 s, incomplete combustion (CO, soot) occurs. If turbulence is low, fuel–air mixing fails → unburnt hydrocarbons escape.

## 2.2 Types of Incinerators (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Capacity | Key Feature | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass-Burn Incinerator** | > 200 TPD | Refuse charged **as-received** (no preprocessing) | Large cities |
| **Modular / Starved-Air Incinerator** | 5 – 100 TPD | Two chambers: primary (pyrolysis, starved air) + secondary (oxidation) | Small towns, hospitals |
| **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) Incinerator** | 50 – 1000 TPD | Waste pre-shredded & dried to make pellets/fluff | Industrial boilers, cement kilns |
| **Fluidized-Bed Combustor (FBC)** | 20 – 500 TPD | Waste burned in suspended bed of hot sand | Hazardous waste, sludge |
| **Rotary Kiln** | 1 – 250 TPD | Inclined rotating cylinder | Hazardous, biomedical waste |
| **Hearth Furnace** | 1 – 20 TPD | Fixed grate, batch-fed | Biomedical, lab waste |

## 2.3 Combustion Stoichiometry — The Chemistry

General oxidation of a waste component $C_x H_y O_z N_a S_b Cl_c$ with oxygen:

$$C_x H_y O_z N_a S_b Cl_c + n\ O_2 \rightarrow x\ CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\ H_2 O + \frac{a}{2}\ N_2 + b\ SO_2 + c\ HCl$$

**Stoichiometric oxygen per kg of waste (simplified, dry basis):**

$$O_2^{stoich}\ (\text{kg/kg waste}) = \frac{32}{12}\cdot C + \frac{32}{4}\cdot H + \frac{32}{32}\cdot S - \frac{32}{32}\cdot O$$

$$= 2.667\,C + 8.0\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O$$

where C, H, S, O are the mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur, and Oxygen in the waste.

**Stoichiometric air (theoretical air):**

$$A_{th} = \frac{O_2^{stoich}}{0.232} = 4.312 \cdot O_2^{stoich}$$

**Actual air supplied (with excess air $e$, expressed as decimal, e.g., 0.80 for 80 %):**

$$A_{actual} = A_{th}\,(1 + e)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | Carbon oxidation | — |
| 2 | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2 O$ | Hydrogen oxidation | — |
| 3 | $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$ | Sulphur oxidation | — |
| 4 | $O_2^{stoich} = 2.667C + 8H + S - O$ | Theoretical O₂ demand (per kg dry waste) | kg O₂ / kg waste |
| 5 | $A_{th} = O_2^{stoich}\, /\, 0.232$ | Theoretical air (air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass) | kg air / kg waste |
| 6 | $A_{actual} = A_{th}(1+e)$ | Actual air with excess air fraction $e$ | kg air / kg waste |
| 7 | $LHV = 33{,}850\,C + 144{,}200\,(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 9410\,S$ | Dulong's Equation for LHV | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{Q_{input}}$ | Combustion efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\text{CO}_2\% = \dfrac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]}\times 100$ | Combustion completeness indicator | % |
| 10 | $\tau = V_{gas} / Q_{gas}$ | Gas residence time in combustion chamber | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why avoid `|` symbols:** In LaTeX inside markdown tables, the `|` symbol breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $C \vert x$ as $C \,\vert\, x$ or just $\lvert C \rvert$ for absolute value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incineration is critical in **land-constrained megacities** (e.g., Mumbai, Singapore, Tokyo) where landfill space is scarce. Modern **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** plants combine incineration with steam-turbine generators to produce 0.5 – 1.0 MWh of electricity per ton of MSW. Advanced systems also include **air-pollution control (APC)** trains: electrostatic precipitator (ESP) → wet scrubber → activated-carbon injection for mercury/dioxin capture — and **bottom-ash recovery** for road construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand for a Generic Fuel

**Given:** A waste sample has the following ultimate analysis (mass basis, dry):
- C = 0.45, H = 0.06, O = 0.30, N = 0.015, S = 0.005, Ash = 0.07
- Moisture content (wet basis) = 0.25

**Step 1 — Write the oxidation reactions (per kg of each element, mass basis):**

For Carbon: 1 kg C requires $\dfrac{32}{12}$ kg O₂ to form CO₂.

For Hydrogen: 1 kg H requires $\dfrac{32}{4} = 8$ kg O₂ to form H₂O.

For Sulphur: 1 kg S requires $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1$ kg O₂ to form SO₂.

**Step 2 — Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel (it self-oxidizes):**

$$O_2^{stoich} = \frac{32}{12}C + 8H + \frac{32}{32}S - \frac{32}{32}O$$

$$O_2^{stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{stoich} &= 2.667 \times 0.45 + 8 \times 0.06 + 1.0 \times 0.005 - 1.0 \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 1.200 + 0.480 + 0.005 - 0.300 \\[4pt]
&= 1.385\ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical air demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{th} &= \frac{O_2^{stoich}}{0.232} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.385}{0.232} \\[4pt]
&= 5.969\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual air with 100 % excess air ($e = 1.0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{actual} &= A_{th}\,(1 + e) \\[4pt]
&= 5.969 \times (1 + 1.0) \\[4pt]
&= 11.938\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic note:** Mass of air per kg O₂ is $1/0.232 = 4.31$ because air is **23.2 % O₂ by mass and 21 % by volume**. The factor $1/0.232$ converts the oxygen demand into an equivalent air demand.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dulong's Formula for Lower Heating Value (LHV)

**Given:** Same ultimate analysis as above. Find LHV in kJ/kg of dry waste.

**Step 1 — Dulong's empirical equation (mass basis):**

$$LHV = 33{,}850\,C + 144{,}200\,\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9410\,S\ \ [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV &= 33{,}850 \times 0.45 + 144{,}200 \times \left(0.06 - \frac{0.30}{8}\right) + 9410 \times 0.005 \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}232.5 + 144{,}200 \times (0.06 - 0.0375) + 47.05 \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}232.5 + 144{,}200 \times 0.0225 + 47.05 \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}232.5 + 3{,}244.5 + 47.05 \\[4pt]
&= 18{,}524.05\ \text{kJ/kg of dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Adjust for moisture (latent heat loss due to water in fuel):**

Each kg of moisture consumes ~2450 kJ to vaporize. With 0.25 kg water per kg wet waste (and dry fraction = 0.75):

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{wet} &= LHV_{dry} \times 0.75 - 2450 \times 0.25 \\[4pt]
&= 18{,}524 \times 0.75 - 612.5 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}893 - 612.5 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}281\ \text{kJ/kg of wet waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real interpretation:** $LHV \approx 13.3$ MJ/kg falls in the **typical MSW range (8–14 MJ/kg)**, indicating that the waste is **self-sustaining** (no auxiliary fuel required if $LHV > 5$ MJ/kg).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Combustion Efficiency from Flue-Gas Composition

**Given:** Dry flue gas analysis: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 7 %, CO = 1000 ppm (0.1 %), N₂ = balance.

**Step 1 — Combustion efficiency (simplified Siegert formula):**

$$\eta_{combustion} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100\ \%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (note: convert both to %):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{12}{12 + 0.1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12}{12.1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 99.17\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** CO₂ is the desired end-product; CO indicates incomplete combustion. A high CO₂ / (CO₂ + CO) ratio implies > 99 % combustion efficiency, which is the KTU benchmark for well-designed incinerators.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mass-Burn Plant Capacity vs. Air Flow

| Plant capacity (TPD) | LHV (MJ/kg) | Stoich. air (kg/kg) | Actual air (kg/kg) | Air flow (Nm³/h) | Stack gas flow (Nm³/h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 10 | 5.97 | 11.94 | ~ 50,000 | ~ 60,000 |
| 500 | 10 | 5.97 | 11.94 | ~ 250,000 | ~ 300,000 |
| 1000 | 10 | 5.97 | 11.94 | ~ 500,000 | ~ 600,000 |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define incineration. State the 3-T rule and its significance in incinerator design.**

**Model Answer:**

Incineration is the controlled thermal oxidation of combustible solid waste at high temperature (850–1200 °C) in the presence of excess air to reduce volume, mass, and recover energy.

The 3-T rule specifies the three design parameters governing combustion quality:

1. **Temperature:** Combustion chamber temperature should be maintained at **≥ 850 °C** (preferably 1000–1200 °C) to ensure destruction of pathogens and prevention of dioxin formation.
2. **Time:** Flue gas residence time at this temperature should be **≥ 2 seconds** to ensure complete oxidation of CO and unburnt hydrocarbons.
3. **Turbulence:** Adequate mixing between fuel and air must be ensured through proper grate design, secondary air injection, and chamber geometry.

Significance: Maintaining the 3-T rule ensures > 99 % combustion efficiency, minimizes CO and dioxin emissions, and satisfies CPCB statutory norms. *(3 marks: 1 def + 2 for 3-T explanation)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Mass-Burn and RDF-fired incinerators.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mass-Burn | RDF-Fired |
|---|---|---|
| Feed form | As-received MSW (no preprocessing) | Pre-shredded, dried, densified pellets/fluff |
| Capacity | Large (> 200 TPD) | Medium to large (50–1000 TPD) |
| Moisture tolerance | Handles 40–55 % moisture | Requires waste dried to < 20 % moisture |
| Capital cost | High (grate + refractory) | Lower (boiler-grade fuel) |
| Auxiliary fuel | Often required in cold start | Seldom required |
| Heat value input | Variable (5–12 MJ/kg) | Stable (15–20 MJ/kg) |
| Application | Urban municipal plants | Cement kilns, captive power plants |

*(3 marks: comparison + key distinguishing feature)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass (dry basis): C = 42 %, H = 6 %, O = 32 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 7.5 %, Moisture = 30 % (wet basis). Calculate:
- (i) Stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of dry waste.
- (ii) Theoretical air required per kg of dry waste.
- (iii) Actual air required when excess air is 120 %.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the LHV of the above waste using Dulong's formula. State whether the waste is self-sustaining in an incinerator.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a)

**(i) Stoichiometric oxygen:**

Using $O_2^{stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1.0\,S - 1.0\,O$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2^{stoich} &= 2.667 \times 0.42 + 8 \times 0.06 + 1.0 \times 0.01 - 1.0 \times 0.32 \\[4pt]
&= 1.120 + 0.480 + 0.010 - 0.320 \\[4pt]
&= 1.290\ \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical air:**

$$
A_{th} = \frac{1.290}{0.232} = 5.560\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
$$

**(iii) Actual air at 120 % excess ($e = 1.20$):**

$$
A_{actual} = 5.560 \times (1 + 1.20) = 5.560 \times 2.20 = 12.232\ \text{kg air / kg dry waste}
$$

#### Part (b)

**LHV (Dulong, dry basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{dry} &= 33{,}850 \times 0.42 + 144{,}200 \times \left(0.06 - \frac{0.32}{8}\right) + 9410 \times 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 144{,}200 \times (0.06 - 0.04) + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 144{,}200 \times 0.02 + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 14{,}217 + 2{,}884 + 94.1 \\[4pt]
&= 17{,}195.1\ \text{kJ/kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LHV (wet basis, accounting for 30 % moisture, 0.30 kg water per kg wet waste, dry fraction = 0.70):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{wet} &= 17{,}195 \times 0.70 - 2450 \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}036.5 - 735 \\[4pt]
&= 11{,}301.5\ \text{kJ/kg wet waste} \approx 11.30\ \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $LHV_{wet} = 11.30$ MJ/kg **> 5 MJ/kg**, the waste is **self-sustaining** and does not require auxiliary fuel support. *(7 marks: 4 for LHV dry + 2 for wet + 1 for conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **mass-burn incinerator** with energy recovery. List its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Air Pollution Control (APC)** train in a modern WtE plant. Mention the typical Indian CPCB emission norms for PM, SO₂, NOx, HCl, and dioxins.

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### Part (a)

- **Sketch:** Feed hopper → inclined grate furnace (drying, pyrolysis, combustion, burnout zones) → secondary chamber → waste-heat boiler → ESP → scrubber → stack; bottom-ash conveyor.
- **Working:** MSW charged onto grate; primary air enters from below; secondary air injected above the bed; flue gas at 1000 °C passes to boiler generating 40 bar, 400 °C steam; steam drives turbine-generator; bottom ash quenched and metal-recovered.
- **Advantages:** No preprocessing; high volume reduction (90 %); generates electricity; suitable for large urban plants.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost; sensitive to waste composition; produces toxic fly ash requiring secure landfill.

#### Part (b)

- **APC Train:** SNCR (Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction for NOx) → dry lime injection (HCl/SO₂) → activated carbon (Hg, dioxins) → baghouse filter (PM) → wet scrubber polish → continuous emissions monitoring system (CEMS).
- **Typical CPCB Norms (WtE plants):**
  - PM < **30 mg/Nm³**
  - SO₂ < **200 mg/Nm³**
  - NOx < **400 mg/Nm³**
  - HCl < **50 mg/Nm³**
  - Dioxins/Furans < **0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³**
  - CO < **50 mg/Nm³** (24-h average)

*(7 marks: 3 sketch + 2 working + 2 APC explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in Dulong's formula** — students often forget the negative term $-O/8$ for H. The correct form is $144{,}200 \times (H - O/8)$, not $144{,}200 \times H + 144{,}200 \times O/8$. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 2. **Using 0.21 instead of 0.232 for air composition** — air is 23.2 % O₂ by *mass* and 21 % by *volume*. Wrong choice → wrong answer. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 3. **Excess air formula** — excess air is the air *in excess of stoichiometric*, so actual air = theoretical × (1 + e/100). If a student writes (1 + e) but e is given as percent, the value is 100× wrong. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversion from dry to wet LHV** — KTU board expects the *final answer in MJ/kg of wet waste*, not dry. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 5. **No sketch in mass-burn question** — board examiners in KTU deduct up to **2 marks** if the diagram is missing or unlabeled.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Incineration** = controlled thermal oxidation; 80–90 % volume reduction.
- **3-T Rule:** Temperature (≥ 850 °C), Time (≥ 2 s gas residence), Turbulence (good mixing).
- **Six main types:** Mass-burn, Modular, RDF-fired, FBC, Rotary Kiln, Hearth.
- **Stoichiometric O₂:** $O_2^{stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + S - O$ (kg O₂ / kg dry waste).
- **Theoretical air:** $A_{th} = O_2^{stoich}\, /\, 0.232$ (use **0.232 for mass, 0.21 for volume**).
- **Actual air with excess %:** $A_{actual} = A_{th} \times (1 + e/100)$.
- **Dulong's LHV:** $33{,}850\,C + 144{,}200\,(H - O/8) + 9410\,S$ in **kJ/kg dry**.
- **Self-sustaining waste:** $LHV_{wet} > 5$ MJ/kg → no auxiliary fuel needed.
- **Combustion efficiency indicator:** $\eta = [\text{CO}_2] / ([\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]) \times 100\ \%$.
- **CPCB emission limits (WtE):** PM < 30, SO₂ < 200, NOx < 400, HCl < 50, CO < 50 mg/Nm³; Dioxins < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- **Residues:** bottom ash (~25 % by mass, recyclable in road base), fly ash (~3–5 %, hazardous, secure landfill).
- **Energy recovery:** 1 ton MSW → ~ 0.5–1.0 MWh electricity in modern WtE plants.
- **Auxiliary fuel** is required during cold start or for low-LHV waste (< 5 MJ/kg).
- **Excess air** of 80–150 % is the KTU standard range for MSW incinerators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Municipal Incinerator** is an engineered thermal treatment facility in which **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is subjected to controlled, high-temperature combustion (typically **850 °C to 1200 °C**) in the presence of excess air, with the primary objectives of:

1. **Volume reduction** (typically 80–90 % by volume, 70 % by weight)
2. **Mass reduction** of combustible fraction
3. **Energy recovery** (through waste-heat boilers producing steam/electricity)
4. **Pathogen destruction** in the residual ash
5. **Detoxification** of certain organic hazardous components

The process converts the chemical energy locked in the organic fraction of refuse into usable thermal energy, while inorganic residues are collected as bottom ash and air-pollution-control (APC) residues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A municipal incinerator is a compartmentalized thermal reactor in which heterogeneous solid waste is dried, ignited, pyrolyzed, and combusted in successive zones over moving or stationary grates, using stoichiometrically controlled excess air (150–200 %), to achieve complete oxidation of organics with residence times of 1–2 hours and flue-gas residence in the secondary chamber of at least 2 seconds at ≥ 850 °C."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of an **incinerator as a precision-engineered, three-stage oven** — like a baker's oven but with far stricter airflow, temperature, and timing controls.

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | What Happens Inside |
|-------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Drying Zone** | A towel left on a sunny clothesline | Moisture in wet waste evaporates at **100 °C – 200 °C** |
| **Combustion Zone (Primary)** | Charcoal grill with bellows | Volatiles burn over the **grate** at **600 °C – 900 °C** |
| **Combustion Zone (Secondary)** | A turbo afterburner | Remaining gases + fly-ash get oxidized at **≥ 850 °C** for **≥ 2 s** |

A common student misconception is to compare incineration to open burning. The critical difference is **the Three T's of Combustion**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three T's (Mandated by EU Directive 2000/76/EC & CPCB India):**
> 1. **Temperature** ≥ 850 °C (1100 °C if waste > 10 % halogens)
> 2. **Turbulence** — swirling, well-mixed flow in secondary chamber
> 3. **Time** — flue-gas residence ≥ **2 seconds** post-last air injection

Without any one of these, the products of incomplete combustion (PICs) like **dioxins, furans, CO, and PAHs** escape — and the unit is just an expensive "smoke machine".

---

## 1.3 Classification of Municipal Incinerators (KTU Modular Hierarchy)

Incinerators are classified by the **method of waste feeding** and **combustion technology**:

### A. By Combustion Configuration
1. **Mass-Burn Incinerators** — Mixed MSW fed directly without pre-processing
2. **Modular / Shop-Fabricated Incinerators** — Pre-assembled units for 5–100 TPD
3. **RDF-Fired Incinerators** — Burn Refuse-Derived Fuel pellets
4. **Fluidized-Bed Combustors (FBC)** — Sand-bed suspension; rare for raw MSW
5. **Rotary Kiln Incinerators** — Used for hazardous waste; secondary for MSW

### B. By Grate Type
- **Reciprocating Grate** (most common — Martin, Von Roll, KKK)
- **Rolling Grate**
- **Traveling Grate**
- **Rotary Kiln (no grate)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Metrics (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR):** 350,000 – 600,000 kcal/hr·m²
> - **Furnace Volume Heat Release:** 100,000 – 200,000 kcal/hr·m³
> - **Excess Air:** 100 % – 150 % (sometimes 200 %)
> - **Residence Time on Grate:** 30 – 90 minutes
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Mass-Burn Incinerator

A modern mass-burn incinerator is a **multi-compartment reactor** performing five sequential physico-chemical operations on the moving mass of refuse:

### 2.1.1 The Five Functional Zones (Grate Trajectory)

The refuse travels along the inclined reciprocating grate and undergoes:

**Zone 1 — Drying Zone (15–25 % of grate length)**
- Temperature: **100 °C – 250 °C**
- Energy from radiant furnace walls + recycled flue gas
- Moisture (typically 20–50 % of MSW) evaporates
- Equation: H₂O(l) → H₂O(g); Latent Heat ≈ **540 kcal/kg**

**Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (30–40 % of grate length)**
- Temperature: **250 °C – 600 °C**
- In absence of oxygen on lower surface, organics crack into **volatiles** (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars) leaving **char**
- This is *endothermic* — needs external heat

**Zone 3 — Ignition / Combustion Zone (25–35 % of grate length)**
- Temperature: **600 °C – 1000 °C**
- Volatiles + char burn with **overfire air (OFA)** injected through nozzles
- Most exothermic region — peak temperature here

**Zone 4 — Burnout / Ash Cooling Zone (15–20 % of grate length)**
- Temperature: **1000 °C – 400 °C**
- Residual carbon oxidized; ash cools before discharge into the **wet ash extractor** (water bath)

**Zone 5 — Secondary Combustion Chamber (SCC) / Post-Combustion Chamber**
- Volume designed for **≥ 2 s** gas residence at **≥ 850 °C**
- Burns out residual CO, soot, VOCs, dioxin precursors
- Precedes the waste-heat boiler

---

## 2.2 Material & Energy Balances — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion Reactions

The general oxidation reactions governing municipal solid waste combustion are:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -94{,}052 \text{ kcal/kmol}$$

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -57{,}798 \text{ kcal/kmol}$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -70{,}960 \text{ kcal/kmol}$$

For each 1 kg of the elements C, H, S, the theoretical O₂ required (in kg) is:

| Element | Theoretical O₂ (kg/kg) | Source of O₂ in MSW Credit |
|---------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Carbon (C) | **2.667** | — |
| Hydrogen (H) | **8.000** | — |
| Sulphur (S) | **1.000** | — |
| Oxygen (O) | − 0.667 (Deduct) | O already in waste |

The mass ratio of air to O₂ is **1 / 0.232 = 4.31** (since dry air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass).

### 2.2.2 Theoretical Air Requirement (Stoichiometric)

$$A_{th} = \left[ \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - \frac{2}{3}O \right] \times \frac{100}{23.2} \quad \text{(kg air / kg waste)}$$

> Where C, H, S, O are mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur, Oxygen in the waste on a dry basis.

### 2.2.3 Actual Air Supply (with Excess Air)

$$A_{actual} = A_{th} \times \left( 1 + \frac{EA}{100} \right)$$

Where **EA** = excess air percentage (typically **80 % – 150 %** for MSW).

### 2.2.4 Combustion Air Distribution (Critical KTU Concept)

Total air is split into:

1. **Primary (Underfire) Air** — fed *below* the grate, through the grate bars
   - Provides O₂ for char burnout in the lower bed
   - Quantity: **40 – 60 %** of total air
   - Pre-heated to **150 °C – 250 °C** using waste-heat boiler

2. **Secondary (Overfire) Air** — injected *above* the waste bed
   - Burns off volatiles, ensures mixing
   - Quantity: **40 – 60 %** of total air
   - Velocity: **20 – 50 m/s** for turbulence
   - Tangential injection → swirling pattern

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers ~70 % of numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Value |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ per kg waste | $O_{2,th} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - \frac{2}{3}O$ | kg O₂ / kg waste | 1.0 – 1.8 |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_{th} = O_{2,th} \times \frac{100}{23.2} = 4.31 \cdot O_{2,th}$ | kg air / kg waste | 4.3 – 7.7 |
| 3 | Actual Air (with EA %) | $A_{act} = A_{th} \left( 1 + \frac{EA}{100} \right)$ | kg air / kg waste | 8 – 14 |
| 4 | Flue gas from combustion | $G = \left( A_{act} + 1 \right) - \text{Ash}$ (approx) | kg flue gas / kg waste | 10 – 14 |
| 5 | Heat Release Rate | $Q = \dot{m}_{waste} \times \text{GCV}$ | kcal/hr or kW | — |
| 6 | Grate Heat Release Rate | $GHRR = \frac{Q}{A_{grate}}$ | kcal/hr·m² | 3.5×10⁵ – 6×10⁵ |
| 7 | Furnace Volume Heat Release | $FVHR = \frac{Q}{V_{furnace}}$ | kcal/hr·m³ | 1×10⁵ – 2×10⁵ |
| 8 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = \frac{CO_2}{CO_2 + CO} \times 100$ | % | > 99 % |
| 9 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta_B = \frac{\text{Steam enthalpy output}}{\text{Heat input from waste}} \times 100$ | % | 65 – 85 % |
| 10 | Plant Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \eta_c \times \eta_B \times 0.85$ | % | 55 – 70 % |
| 11 | Wet vs Dry MSW | $\text{GCV}_{dry} = \text{GCV}_{wet} \left( \frac{100-M}{100} \right)$ | kcal/kg | 800 – 2200 |
| 12 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR = \frac{A_{act}}{1}$ | kg air / kg waste | 6 – 12 |
| 13 | Energy from 1 TPD MSW (GCV ≈ 1500 kcal/kg) | $E = 1000 \times 1500 = 1.5 \times 10^6$ | kcal/day | — |
| 14 | Equivalent kWh from steam | $kWh = \frac{\dot{m}_{steam}(h_g - h_f)}{860}$ | kWh | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Incineration is not an academic curiosity — it is deployed at scale globally:

- **Denmark**: ~54 % of MSW incinerated (with energy recovery) — supplies district heating to **>1.2 million households**
- **Singapore**: Tuas Nexus plant — 5,400 TPD with **122 MW** power output
- **India**: Timarpur-Okhla plant (Delhi, 1950 TPD) — generates **16 MW**
- **KTU Context**: Kerala's only large-scale incinerator at **Brahmapuram, Kochi** — recently retrofitted with air-pollution control after NGT directives
