# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Numerical):** Marks are lost when students forget to convert moisture to dry-basis, neglect the "O in waste" term, or use volumetric ratios instead of mass ratios. Follow every step below meticulously.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Stoichiometric Air Calculation (Standard 7-Mark KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A municipal waste has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, mass %):

$$\text{C} = 32.5\%, \quad \text{H} = 4.2\%, \quad \text{O} = 22.8\%, \quad \text{N} = 0.5\%, \quad \text{S} = 0.3\%, \quad \text{Ash} = 12.5\%$$

The remaining balance is moisture. Calculate:
- (a) Stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of dry waste
- (b) Stoichiometric air required per kg of dry waste
- (c) Actual air required if 100 % excess air is used

### Step 1 — Verify Ultimate Analysis

Sum of constituents: $32.5 + 4.2 + 22.8 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 12.5 = 72.8\%$

Remaining = $100 - 72.8 = 27.2\%$ is **moisture**.

### Step 2 — Identify the Four Reactive Elements

In stoichiometry, only **C, H, S** consume O₂. **Oxygen present in waste** reduces the external O₂ demand. **Nitrogen** passes through unchanged (as N₂ in flue gas).

So we operate on: **C = 32.5 %**, **H = 4.2 %**, **S = 0.3 %**, **O = 22.8 %**

### Step 3 — Compute O₂ Demand per kg Dry Waste

The general expression (per kg dry waste, mass fractions converted to decimals):

$$\text{O}_{2,th} = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H + \frac{32}{32}S - O_{present}$$

Substituting (with mass fractions as decimals, e.g., C = 0.325):

$$O_{2,th} = (2.667 \times 0.325) + (8.0 \times 0.042) + (1.0 \times 0.003) - 0.228$$

Evaluating each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1 (Carbon)}: \quad 2.667 \times 0.325 &= 0.8668 \text{ kg O}_2 \\
\text{Term 2 (Hydrogen)}: \quad 8.0 \times 0.042 &= 0.3360 \text{ kg O}_2 \\
\text{Term 3 (Sulphur)}: \quad 1.0 \times 0.003 &= 0.0030 \text{ kg O}_2 \\
\text{Sum of demands} &= 0.8668 + 0.3360 + 0.0030 = 1.2058 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Less Oxygen in waste} &= - 0.2280 \text{ kg} \\
\hline
\text{Net O}_{2,th} &= 0.9778 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry waste}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Part (a) Answer: } O_{2,th} = 0.978 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg dry waste}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Stoichiometric Air

$$A_{th} = O_{2,th} \times \frac{1}{0.232}$$

Because dry air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass. Thus:

$$A_{th} = 0.9778 \times 4.310 = 4.215 \text{ kg air / kg dry waste}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Part (b) Answer: } A_{th} \approx 4.21 \text{ kg air / kg dry waste}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply 100 % Excess Air

$$A_{act} = A_{th} \times \left(1 + \frac{100}{100}\right) = 4.215 \times 2 = 8.43 \text{ kg air / kg dry waste}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Part (c) Answer: } A_{act} = 8.43 \text{ kg air / kg dry waste}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Check:** With 100 % EA, $A_{act} = 2 \times A_{th}$. If a student reports 12 kg/kg, they probably used volumetric (1/0.21) instead of mass basis (1/0.232). **Lose 1 mark instantly.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Heat Release Rate & Grate Sizing (7-Mark KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A city generates **300 tonnes/day** of MSW with an average **as-fired GCV = 1100 kcal/kg** and moisture content **35 %**. The incinerator operates **24 hours/day** with **90 % availability** of the grate. Design:

- (a) Total heat release rate (kcal/hr)
- (b) Required grate area, assuming GHRR = 4.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m²
- (c) Furnace volume if FVHR = 1.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m³

### Step 1 — Compute Effective Daily Operating Hours

$$t_{eff} = 24 \times 0.90 = 21.6 \text{ hr/day}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Burn Rate (kg/hr)

$$\dot{m}_{waste} = \frac{300 \times 1000}{21.6} = 13{,}888.9 \text{ kg/hr}$$

### Step 3 — Total Heat Release Rate

$$Q = \dot{m}_{waste} \times \text{GCV} = 13{,}888.9 \times 1100$$

$$Q = 1.528 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

Converting to MW (using 1 kcal/hr = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kW):

$$Q = 1.528 \times 10^7 \times 1.163 \times 10^{-3} = 17{,}768 \text{ kW} \approx 17.77 \text{ MW}_{\text{th}}$$

### Step 4 — Required Grate Area

$$A_{grate} = \frac{Q}{GHRR} = \frac{1.528 \times 10^7}{4.5 \times 10^5} = 33.96 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 5 — Required Furnace Volume

$$V_{furnace} = \frac{Q}{FVHR} = \frac{1.528 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^5} = 101.87 \text{ m}^3$$

### Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Daily waste | m | 300 | TPD |
| Mass burn rate | $\dot{m}$ | 13,889 | kg/hr |
| Heat release rate | Q | 1.528 × 10⁷ | kcal/hr |
| Thermal power | P | 17.77 | MW_th |
| Grate area | $A_{grate}$ | 33.96 | m² |
| Furnace volume | $V_{furnace}$ | 101.87 | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Note:** A square grate of side $\sqrt{33.96} = 5.83$ m is non-standard. Designers typically use **rectangular grates** of, say, 6 m × 6 m = 36 m² for fabrication convenience and slightly higher thermal margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Excess Air on Combustion

This is a **favourite 7-mark question** — "Discuss the effect of excess air on combustion temperature and energy recovery."

| Excess Air | Combustion Temperature | Energy Recovery | CO Emissions | Dioxin Risk |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------|--------------|-------------|
| 50 % | Highest (~1400 °C) | Highest (η_B ↑) | Higher (incomplete mixing) | Medium |
| 100 % (optimal) | ~1100 °C | High | Low | Low |
| 150 % | ~950 °C | Moderate | Low | Low |
| 200 % | ~800 °C | Low (heat lost in N₂) | Low | Higher (below 850 °C) |

**Key Insight:** The trade-off is between **combustion completeness** (favours high EA) and **adiabatic flame temperature** (favours low EA). The optimum is **80 – 120 %** for MSW.

---

## 3.4 Energy Balance of a Waste-to-Energy Plant

$$\underbrace{\dot{m}_{waste} \times \text{GCV}}_{\text{Energy In}} = \underbrace{\dot{m}_{steam}(h_g - h_{fw})}_{\text{Useful Steam}} + \underbrace{Q_{flue\,gas}}_{\text{Sensible heat loss}} + \underbrace{Q_{radiation}}_{\text{Wall loss}} + \underbrace{Q_{ash}}_{\text{Ash loss}}$$

Typical distribution for a 1000 TPD plant:

| Loss Component | Share |
|----------------|-------|
| Useful steam (electrical + thermal) | 70 – 80 % |
| Flue gas sensible heat | 10 – 15 % |
| Wall radiation | 1 – 3 % |
| Bottom ash sensible + unburnt C | 2 – 5 % |
| Other (convection, blowdown) | 1 – 2 % |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Mass-Burn Incinerator Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire process flow of a mass-burn municipal incinerator from waste reception to flue-gas emission. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `n1`, `n2`); labels contain raw uppercase text only, with no markdown tags.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[n1 Waste Reception] --> B[n2 Storage Pit]
    B --> C[n3 Grab Crane and Mixing]
    C --> D[n4 Refuse Feed Hopper]
    D --> E[n5 Hydraulic Ram Feeder]
    E --> F[n6 Combustion Grate]
    F --> G[n7 Primary Combustion Chamber]
    G --> H[n8 Secondary Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[n9 Waste Heat Boiler]
    I --> J[n10 Steam Turbine and Generator]
    J --> K[n11 Electrostatic Precipitator]
    K --> L[n12 Wet Scrubber for Acid Gases]
    L --> M[n13 Bag Filter for Particulates]
    M --> N[n14 ID Fan and Stack]
    F --> O[n15 Bottom Ash Conveyor]
    O --> P[n16 Wet Ash Extractor]
    P --> Q[n17 Ash Disposal Site]
    K --> R[n18 Fly Ash Silo]
    R --> S[n19 APC Residue Stabilization]
    S --> T[n20 Secure Landfill]
    A1[n21 Primary Air Blower] --> F
    A2[n22 Secondary Air Fan] --> H
    A3[n23 Flue Gas Recirculation] --> G
    A4[n24 Auxiliary Fuel Burner] --> H
    A5[n25 Waste Water Treatment Plant] --> L
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Description:**

1. **n1–n5: Waste Reception Train** — Trucks tip MSW into a 3–5 day storage pit. The grab crane mixes and homogenizes the waste. A hydraulic ram pushes refuse onto the grate at controlled rates (typically 1–2 tonnes per stroke).
2. **n6–n8: Combustion Train** — The reciprocating grate has 3–6 rows that oscillate to advance waste. Underfire air (primary) enters from below; overfire air (secondary) is injected tangentially above the bed in the secondary chamber at velocities of 20–50 m/s.
3. **n9–n10: Energy Recovery Train** — Hot flue gases (850–1100 °C) pass through a water-tube waste-heat boiler, generating steam at 40–60 bar and 400–500 °C, which drives a turbine-generator producing 0.5–2 MWh per tonne of waste.
4. **n11–n14: Air Pollution Control Train** — Sequential treatment: ESP removes 99 % of particulates → wet scrubber neutralizes HCl/SO₂ → bag filter captures residual dust → ID fan pushes clean gas up the stack.
5. **n15–n20: Residue Handling Train** — Bottom ash is quenched, ferrous metals magnetically separated, and the remainder sent for road sub-base or landfill. Fly ash is stabilized with cement/lime and disposed as hazardous waste.
6. **n21–n25: Auxiliary Systems** — Air blowers, Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR) for temperature control, auxiliary burners for startup, and WWTP for scrubber blowdown.

---

## 4.2 Zonal Layout Schematic on the Grate (Textual Diagram)

```
  +---------------------------------------------------+  -->  HOT END (Flue gas to SCC)
  |   Drying    |  Pyrolysis    |   Ignition   | Burnout |
  |   Zone      |   Zone        |   Zone       |  Zone    |
  |   100-250°C |  250-600°C    |   600-1000°C | 1000-400°C|
  |   15-25 %   |   30-40 %     |   25-35 %    |  15-20 %  |
  +---------------------------------------------------+
  ^                                               ^
  |                                               |
  PRIMARY AIR (40-60% total)               ASH DISCHARGE
  (from below grate)                      (to ash extractor)
```

The reciprocal motion of grate bars moves waste from left to right through each zone. The **peak temperature** occurs in the **ignition zone** where both primary and secondary air participate.

---

## 4.3 Subsystem Cross-Reference Matrix

| Subsystem | Input | Process | Output | Key Equipment |
|-----------|-------|---------|--------|---------------|
| Waste Handling | Mixed MSW | Storage, mixing | Homogenized feed | Storage pit, grab crane |
| Combustion | Feed MSW + Air | Drying→Pyrolysis→Burning | Hot flue gas + ash | Reciprocating grate, OFA nozzles |
| Energy Recovery | Hot flue gas (1000 °C) | Heat exchange | Steam (40–60 bar) | Waste-heat boiler, superheater |
| Power Generation | High-pressure steam | Expansion | Electricity | Steam turbine, alternator |
| Air Pollution Control | Raw flue gas | Particulate + gas removal | Clean flue gas (<50 mg/Nm³ dust) | ESP, scrubber, bag filter |
| Residue Management | Bottom ash + fly ash | Quenching, separation, stabilization | Inert ash + metals | Wet extractor, magnetic separator |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the "Three T's" of combustion as applied to municipal incinerators. Why is each critical for pollution control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Three T's are the minimum operating conditions mandated by EU Directive 2000/76/EC and the Indian CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) for safe MSW incineration:

1. **Temperature (T) ≥ 850 °C** in the secondary combustion chamber
2. **Turbulence (T)** — High mixing; ensures volatiles meet O₂
3. **Time (t) ≥ 2 seconds** — Flue-gas residence time after the last air injection

**Why critical:** Below 850 °C, organic compounds (especially **dioxins and furans**) do not thermally decompose. Without turbulence, unburnt CO and VOCs escape. Without residence time, combustion is incomplete. Together, the Three T's ensure **> 99.9 % destruction of organics** and **dioxin emissions < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³**, the legal limit.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between primary (underfire) and secondary (overfire) air in a mass-burn incinerator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Air (Underfire) | Secondary Air (Overfire) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Location** | Below the grate | Above the waste bed |
| **Quantity** | 40 – 60 % of total air | 40 – 60 % of total air |
| **Function** | Burns out fixed carbon (char) in the bed | Oxidizes volatiles rising from bed |
| **Temperature** | Pre-heated to 150 – 250 °C | Ambient or warm; high velocity |
| **Injection method** | Through grate bars | Tangential / overfire nozzles |
| **Drives process** | Char combustion, ash cooling | Volatile oxidation, mixing |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A municipal waste stream has the following composition (mass %, as-fired basis):**

$$\text{C} = 25.4\%, \quad \text{H} = 3.3\%, \quad \text{O} = 17.8\%, \quad \text{N} = 0.4\%, \quad \text{S} = 0.2\%, \quad \text{Ash} = 9.5\%, \quad \text{Moisture} = 43.4\%$$

**The plant operates with 100 % excess air and incinerates 200 tonnes/day of waste over 20 effective hours.**

**(a) Compute the stoichiometric and actual air requirements (kg air per kg of as-fired waste).** (7 marks)

**(b) Design the grate area and furnace volume, and discuss the factors governing grate selection.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify elements and convert to mass fractions (per kg as-fired):**
C = 0.254, H = 0.033, S = 0.002, O = 0.178, N = 0.004, Ash = 0.095, Moisture = 0.434

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg as-fired waste:**

$$O_{2,th} = \frac{8}{3}(0.254) + 8(0.033) + (0.002) - (0.178)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{2,th} &= 0.6773 + 0.2640 + 0.0020 - 0.1780 \\
&= 0.7653 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg as-fired waste}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric Air:**

$$A_{th} = 0.7653 \times \frac{1}{0.232} = 0.7653 \times 4.310 = 3.299 \text{ kg air / kg waste}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Air at 100 % EA:**

$$A_{act} = 3.299 \times (1 + 1.00) = 6.598 \text{ kg air / kg waste}$$

$$\boxed{A_{th} = 3.30 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad A_{act} = 6.60 \text{ kg/kg as-fired waste}}$$

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Assume GCV of as-fired MSW** (using empirical Dulong formula or typical KTU value):
$$GCV = 337C + 1418\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 92.2S - 23.7M \text{ (kcal/kg, dry basis)}$$

$$GCV_{dry} = 337(0.254) + 1418(0.033) - 1418(0.178/8) + 92.2(0.002) - 23.7(0.434/0.566 \text{ not used})$$

Converting to **dry basis** (dividing by 0.566 since 56.6 % is dry matter):

$$C_{dry} = 0.254/0.566 = 0.4488, \quad H_{dry} = 0.033/0.566 = 0.0583, \quad O_{dry} = 0.178/0.566 = 0.3145$$

$$GCV_{dry} = 337(0.4488) + 1418\left(0.0583 - \frac{0.3145}{8}\right) + 92.2(0.002/0.566)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
GCV_{dry} &= 151.2 + 1418(0.0583 - 0.0393) + 0.326 \\
&= 151.2 + 1418(0.019) + 0.326 \\
&= 151.2 + 26.94 + 0.326 \\
&= 178.47 \text{ kcal/kg dry}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to as-fired GCV:**

$$GCV_{af} = 178.47 \times 0.566 = 101.0 \text{ kcal/kg} \quad \text{(low — typical of high-moisture Indian MSW)}$$

If typical Indian value is taken as **1100 kcal/kg as-fired** (per KTU reference), use that for sizing.

**Step 3 — Mass burn rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{waste} = \frac{200 \times 1000}{20} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat release:**

$$Q = 10{,}000 \times 1100 = 1.10 \times 10^7 \text{ kcal/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Grate area (assuming GHRR = 4.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m²):**

$$A_{grate} = \frac{1.10 \times 10^7}{4.5 \times 10^5} = 24.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Furnace volume (assuming FVHR = 1.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m³):**

$$V_{furnace} = \frac{1.10 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^5} = 73.3 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Factors governing grate selection (to list 5):**
1. **Moisture content** — high moisture (e.g., 40–50 %) demands long drying zone → **reciprocating grate** with 25 % drying zone
2. **CV variability** — wide CV range needs long residence time → rolling/reciprocating
3. **Particle size and inert content** — high inert demands robust grate bars
4. **Plant capacity** — > 250 TPD → mass-burn with reciprocating grate; < 50 TPD → modular
5. **Capital cost and local fabrication** — Von Roll/Martin grates are imported; indigenous KKK type preferred in India

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the components of a modern Mass-Burn Municipal Incinerator with Energy Recovery. Explain the role of each component.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain in detail the Air Pollution Control (APC) system used in a modern incinerator. List the typical emission limits for particulates, SO₂, HCl, NOₓ, CO, and dioxins.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

A modern mass-burn incinerator with energy recovery (Waste-to-Energy plant) comprises the following major components:

**1. Waste Reception and Storage**
A reinforced concrete pit of 3–5 days storage capacity. The pit depth (typically 8–15 m) allows easy truck dumping. A **bridge-type grab crane** with clamshell bucket (capacity 4–8 m³) transfers waste to the feed hopper. The crane operator manually mixes waste to homogenize moisture and CV.

**2. Refuse Feed System**
A **feed hopper** with inclined chute (to prevent bridging of bulky waste) feeds refuse onto the grate via a **hydraulic ram feeder** that advances the bed in controlled strokes. The feed rate is variable (typically 1–2 tonnes per stroke) and may be modulated by **load cell weight** feedback to maintain a constant heat release.

**3. Combustion Grate**
A **reciprocating grate** (Martin/Von Roll/KKK type) made of cast iron or refractory-lined steel bars, tilted at 15–25° from horizontal. The bars move alternately forward and backward, slowly conveying waste uphill. The grate is divided into **drying, pyrolysis, ignition, and burnout zones**. Air-cooled grate bars prevent overheating.

**4. Primary Combustion Chamber**
Located above the grate, lined with refractory bricks (chamotte, SiC, or castable). Receives primary air from below the grate through the grate bars. The waste undergoes drying, devolatilization, and char combustion.

**5. Secondary Combustion Chamber (SCC)**
A refractory-lined chamber downstream of the primary chamber. Receives secondary (overfire) air injected tangentially at high velocity (20–50 m/s), creating swirling turbulence. Sized for **≥ 2 second residence time at ≥ 850 °C**. Burns out residual CO, VOCs, soot.

**6. Waste-Heat Boiler (WHB)**
Water-tube boiler with superheater, evaporator, and economizer banks. Generates steam at 40–60 bar, 400–500 °C. Flue gas cools from ~1000 °C to ~200 °C.

**7. Steam Turbine-Generator**
Steam expands in a multi-stage turbine coupled to an alternator, producing 0.5–2 MWh per tonne of waste. Condensate is recycled.

**8. Air Pollution Control (APC) System**
Detailed in part (b).

**9. Residue Handling System**
Bottom ash falls into a wet extractor (water bath), is conveyed out, magnetically separated for ferrous metals, eddy-current separated for non-ferrous, and the remainder is sent to monofill/road sub-base. Fly ash from ESP/bag filter is collected, conditioned, and disposed to secure landfill.

**10. Chimney/Stack**
Self-supporting steel stack (60–120 m tall) with continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) for online opacity, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, O₂, and dust.

*(Neat block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Air Pollution Control (APC) Train in a Modern MSW Incinerator:**

Modern incinerators employ a **multi-stage dry/semi-dry/wet** system. The typical sequence is:

**Stage 1 — Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**
- Removes 99 % of fly-ash particles by charging them and collecting on oppositely-charged plates
- Operates at 30–60 kV; flue gas temperature 150–200 °C
- Outlet dust load: ~50–100 mg/Nm³

**Stage 2 — Spray Dryer Absorber (SDA) — for Acid Gases**
- Slaked lime (Ca(OH)₂) slurry is atomized into hot flue gas
- Reacts with HCl, SO₂ to form CaCl₂, CaSO₃, CaSO₄
- Operates at 130–180 °C

**Stage 3 — Bag Filter (Fabric Filter)**
- Captures remaining particulates + reaction products from SDA
- Uses PTFE or P84 filter bags (heat-resistant)
- Outlet dust: < 10 mg/Nm³
- Polishing stage for high-grade filtration

**Stage 4 — Wet Scrubber (Optional, for higher HCl/SO₂ removal)**
- Counter-current water/NaOH spray tower
- Removes residual HCl, SO₂, heavy metals
- Generates wastewater sent to **on-site Waste Water Treatment Plant (WWTP)**

**Stage 5 — Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) — for NOₓ**
- Ammonia (NH₃) injected into flue gas passing over catalyst bed
- 4 NH₃ + 4 NO + O₂ → 4 N₂ + 6 H₂O
- Operates at 200–300 °C
- Reduces NOₓ to < 70 mg/Nm³

**Stage 6 — Activated Carbon Injection (for Dioxins & Mercury)**
- Carbon absorbs dioxins, furans, and mercury vapour
- Doses 0.5–2 kg/tonne of waste
- Captured in bag filter

**Emission Limits (EU Directive 2000/76/EC and CPCB India, 11 % O₂ basis):**

| Pollutant | EU Limit (mg/Nm³) | Indian CPCB Limit (mg/Nm³) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Particulates (dust) | 10 | 50 |
| HCl | 10 | 50 |
| SO₂ | 50 | 200 |
| NOₓ | 200 | 400 |
| CO | 50 | 100 |
| TOC (Total Organic Carbon) | 10 | 20 |
| HF | 1 | 4 |
| Hg | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| Cd + Tl | 0.05 | 0.1 |
| Heavy metals (sum of 6) | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| **Dioxins & Furans** | **0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³** | **0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.232 vs 0.21 confusion** (mass vs volume O₂ fraction) — 1 mark lost
> 2. **Not converting to dry basis** when moisture is given separately — 2 marks lost
> 3. **Confusing GHRR with FVHR** — students interchange them — 1 mark lost
> 4. **Writing "incinerator = burner"** without mentioning the 5 zones — 1–2 marks lost
> 5. **Forgetting the 2 s / 850 °C** legal requirement for SCC — 1 mark lost
> 6. **Skipping the energy recovery block** in the diagram — 1 mark lost
> 7. **Emission limits in wrong units** (ppm vs mg/Nm³) — 1 mark lost
> 8. **Not stating that air pollution control has multiple stages** — 1 mark lost

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Revision):**

### Definition & Classification
- [x] Incinerator = compartmentalized thermal reactor for MSW
- [x] Five types: **Mass-burn, Modular, RDF-fired, Fluidized Bed, Rotary Kiln**
- [x] Mass-burn dominates for cities > 250 TPD

### The Five Zones on the Grate
- [x] **Drying (100–250 °C) → Pyrolysis (250–600 °C) → Ignition (600–1000 °C) → Burnout (1000–400 °C) → SCC (≥ 850 °C)**
- [x] **Three T's: Temperature, Turbulence, Time** (2 s, 850 °C) — CPCB mandate

### Air System
- [x] **Primary (underfire) air = 40–60 %** of total, from below grate, pre-heated
- [x] **Secondary (overfire) air = 40–60 %** of total, from above, tangential injection
- [x] **Excess air = 100–150 %** typical for MSW

### Key Formulas (Top Priority)
- [x] $O_{2,th} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ (kg O₂ / kg waste)
- [x] $A_{th} = O_{2,th} / 0.232 = 4.31 \cdot O_{2,th}$
- [x] $A_{act} = A_{th} \times (1 + EA/100)$
- [x] $Q = \dot{m} \times GCV$ (Heat release rate)
- [x] $A_{grate} = Q / GHRR$ (GHRR = 4.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m²)
- [x] $V_{furnace} = Q / FVHR$ (FVHR = 1.5 × 10⁵ kcal/hr·m³)

### Design Metrics
- [x] Volume reduction 80–90 %; mass reduction ~70 %
- [x] Grate residence 30–90 min; SCC gas residence ≥ 2 s
- [x] Energy output 0.5–2 MWh per tonne of waste

### Air Pollution Control Sequence
- [x] **ESP → SDA → Bag Filter → SCR → Carbon Injection** (typical modern plant)
- [x] Dioxin limit: **0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³**
- [x] Dust limit: **10 mg/Nm³ (EU) / 50 mg/Nm³ (India)**

### Common Pitfalls
- [x] Always use **mass basis (0.232)** for air calculation
- [x] Always state excess air as **% of stoichiometric**
- [x] Always state grate area in **m²** and FVHR in **m³**
- [x] Never forget to mention **energy recovery** in the answer
- [x] Always cite **CPCB / EU Directive** when stating emission limits

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grate (Combustion Grate / Incinerator Grate):** A refractory-lined, air-permeable hearth or platform forming the floor of a combustion chamber of a *mass-burn incinerator*, on which Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) is progressively dried, ignited, combusted, and reduced to ash, while primary combustion air is supplied from beneath through air nozzles. The grate simultaneously supports the waste bed, transports it through the furnace, and agitates it for better burnout.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PECHT861 – Solid Waste Management, Module 3 (Thermal Treatment & Incineration)**, a grate is the **heart of a moving-bed mass-burn system** for MSW, Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF), and other heterogeneous solid wastes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Grate like a moving BBQ rack on a conveyor belt, but in reverse orientation.**
>
> Imagine a *barbecue grill* in your backyard: charcoal sits on metal bars, hot air rises from below, ash falls through the gaps, and you use tongs to flip the food. A combustion grate is the **industrial-scale, mechanized version**:
> - The "metal bars" are water-cooled cast-iron or alloy tines (called **grate bars** or **fire bars**).
> - The "tongs" are replaced by a mechanized reciprocating or rotating motion that automatically turns and advances the waste bed.
> - The "hot air from below" is **primary combustion air**, precisely metered to dry, ignite, and burn the waste.
> - The "conveyor" moves the burning waste from the charging end (feed chute) to the ash discharge end.

Just like a barbecue must keep turning food for *even cooking*, a grate must continually agitate MSW because waste is a wildly heterogeneous fuel — wet paper, plastic, garden clippings, food waste, demolition debris — all mixed in random proportions.

## 1.3 Role of the Grate in the Incineration Process

A grate performs **five simultaneous functions** during combustion:

| S.No. | Function | Description |
|:-----:|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Transport** | Mechanically conveys waste from feed hopper to ash discharger |
| 2 | **Agitation** | Turns and mixes the bed for uniform burnout and exposure to air |
| 3 | **Air Distribution** | Delivers primary combustion air uniformly through the waste bed |
| 4 | **Combustion Zoning** | Creates distinct zones: drying → ignition → combustion → burnout |
| 5 | **Ash Removal** | Discharges bottom ash, clinker, and inert residues at the end |

## 1.4 Position in the Mass-Burn Incineration Train

The grate is sandwiched between upstream and downstream unit operations:

> **[ Feed Hopper / Ram Feeder ]** → **GRATE (Combustion Chamber)** → **[ Secondary Combustion Chamber / Post-Combustion Zone ]** → **[ Heat Recovery Boiler ]** → **[ Air Pollution Control ]**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Combustion Grates

KTU examiners expect a *clear taxonomy*. Grates are classified along three axes:

### (A) Based on Motion / Movement

| Type | Motion | Application |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Stationary (Fixed) Grate** | No motion; manual poking | Small-scale, batch operations (< 1 TPD) |
| **Moving/Traveling Grate** | Continuous belt of cast-iron links | Continuous medium-scale plants |
| **Reciprocating Grate** | Alternate back-and-forth stroke | Most common for MSW (Martin, Von Roll) |
| **Rocking Grate** | Slow rotational rocking around axis | Older designs, drying of sludge |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Rotating cylindrical drum | Hazardous waste, sludges, RDF |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Bed of sand kept suspended in air | Uniform, shredded waste, RDF |

### (B) Based on Cooling Mechanism

| Type | Cooling Medium | Advantages |
|------|----------------|------------|
| **Air-Cooled Grate** | Forced ambient air | Simpler; pre-heats combustion air |
| **Water-Cooled Grate** | Circulating water in cast tines | Withstands higher temperatures (> 1100 °C); protects metal from slagging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Modern MSW incinerators > 5 TPD almost universally use **water-cooled reciprocating grates** because they tolerate the highly variable, often sticky and clinker-forming nature of Indian and global MSW.

### (C) Based on Inclination

| Type | Angle to Horizontal | Application |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Horizontal / Stepped** | 0° – 15° | Uniform, shredded waste |
| **Inclined** | 15° – 30° | Most MSW plants |
| **Steeply Inclined** | > 30° | High-moisture waste |

## 2.2 Combustion Zones on the Grate

A modern grate physically partitions the fire bed into **four sequential zones**, typically demarcated by under-grate air compartments (zones I–IV):

| Zone | Location on Grate | Process | Temperature (°C) | Air Fraction |
|:----:|:-----------------:|---------|:-----------------:|:------------:|
| **I – Drying** | Front 25–30 % length | Moisture evaporation, water release | 200 – 600 | 25 – 30 % |
| **II – Ignition / Pyrolysis** | 25–50 % | Volatiles released, char begins forming | 500 – 900 | 20 – 25 % |
| **III – Combustion / Burnout** | 50–80 % | Char oxidation, peak flame | 900 – 1200 | 30 – 40 % |
| **IV – Ash Cooling** | Last 20 % | Residual burnout, ash quenching | 600 – 800 | 10 – 15 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The under-grate air is **not equally distributed** across the grate length — it is **zone-controlled** by dampers and fans to match the stoichiometric demand of each zone. Mis-zoning is the most common cause of incomplete burnout and high CO emissions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **frequently tested** for numerical problems (3–7 mark sub-parts).

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|---------|-------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | $q_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{A_{grate}}$ | **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR)** — heat liberated per unit grate area | $\text{MJ/m}^{2}\cdot\text{h}$ or $\text{kW/m}^{2}$ |
| 2 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{L_{grate}}{v_{grate}}$ | **Residence time** of waste on grate | minutes or hours |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{air,\,stoich} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste}}{0.232} \times \left( C + 0.375 S - 0.267 O \right)$ | Stoichiometric air (by mass) for combustible analysis | Nm³/h |
| 4 | $\dot{V}_{air,\,actual} = n \times \dot{V}_{air,\,stoich}$ | Actual air with excess ratio $n \approx 1.6 - 2.2$ | Nm³/h |
| 5 | $A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{q_{grate,\,design}}$ | Grate area required for a given waste throughput | m² |
| 6 | $\eta_{burnout} = 1 - \dfrac{L_{LOI}}{100}$ | Combustion efficiency via Loss-On-Ignition of ash | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{primary} = (0.4 \text{ to } 0.7) \times \dot{V}_{air,\,actual}$ | Primary (under-grate) air share | Nm³/h |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{secondary} = \dot{V}_{air,\,actual} - \dot{Q}_{primary}$ | Secondary (over-fire) air injected in the post-combustion zone | Nm³/h |
| 9 | $\tau_{stroke} = \dfrac{L_{stroke}}{v_{avg,\,forward}}$ | Time between forward strokes of a reciprocating grate | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU design values to memorize:**
> - GHRR for MSW: **250 – 500 kW/m²** (≈ 0.9 – 1.8 GJ/m²·h)
> - Excess air ratio: **1.6 – 2.2**
> - Primary air share: **40 – 70 %** of stoichiometric
> - Grate residence time: **30 – 90 minutes** (depends on waste calorific value and grate load)
> - Grate load (specific waste load): **150 – 400 kg/m²·h**
> - Under-grate air velocity through tine gaps: **5 – 15 m/s**
> - Maximum allowable under-grate velocity to prevent fly-ash entrainment: **< 1.5 m/s**

## 2.4 Design Variables of a Reciprocating Grate

A *reciprocating grate* consists of several rows of cast-iron grate bars arranged laterally. Each row is fixed to a movable carrier that undergoes a slow forward stroke (advancing the waste) followed by a rapid backward stroke (which leaves the waste in place, creating agitation). The grate geometry is defined by:

- **Bar length** $L_b$ (1.0 – 1.5 m)
- **Bar width** $w_b$ (50 – 100 mm)
- **Bar spacing / gap** $g_b$ (8 – 15 mm) — controls air distribution and ash fall-through
- **Effective air opening ratio** $\alpha = \dfrac{g_b}{g_b + w_b} \approx 0.10 - 0.20$
- **Stroke length** $s$ (100 – 300 mm)
- **Stroke frequency** $f$ (2 – 8 strokes/min)
- **Forward velocity** $v_f = s \times f$ (typical: 1 – 5 m/h)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Grate Area from Heat Release Rate

The **design of grate area** is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU examinations. The complete derivation is shown below.

**Given Data:**
- Mass feed rate of waste: $\dot{m}_{waste}$ in $\text{kg/h}$
- Net Calorific Value (NCV) of waste: $NCV$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$
- Design Grate Heat Release Rate: $q_{grate,\,design}$ in $\text{kW/m}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Convert mass feed rate to heat release rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV \quad [\text{kJ/h}]
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to SI power units (kW).**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 3 — Equate total heat release to the product of grate area and design GHRR.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = A_{grate} \times q_{grate,\,design}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for grate area.**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{q_{grate,\,design}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600 \times q_{grate,\,design}} \quad [\text{m}^{2}]
$$

**Step 5 — Choose a rectangular layout.** Let $L_{grate}$ and $W_{grate}$ be length and width.

$$
A_{grate} = L_{grate} \times W_{grate}
$$

A typical aspect ratio: $\dfrac{L_{grate}}{W_{grate}} \approx 1.5 - 3.0$.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **[Worked Example 3.1]**
> A municipal incinerator must process **12 tonnes/hour** of MSW with an average **NCV of 9000 kJ/kg**. The design **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR) is 350 kW/m²**. Compute (a) the total heat release, (b) the required grate area, and (c) suitable dimensions if the length-to-width ratio is 2:1.

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{waste} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $NCV = 9000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $q_{grate,\,design} = 350 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- $L_{grate} : W_{grate} = 2 : 1$

**(a) Total heat release:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 9000}{3600} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{8}}{3600} = 30{,}000 \text{ kW} = 30 \text{ MW}
$$

**(b) Required grate area:**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{350} = 85.71 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(c) Suitable dimensions:**

$$
L_{grate} \times W_{grate} = 85.71
$$

Let $W_{grate} = x$, then $L_{grate} = 2x$.

$$
2x^{2} = 85.71 \implies x^{2} = 42.86 \implies x = 6.55 \text{ m}
$$

Therefore:
$$
W_{grate} \approx 6.5 \text{ m}, \quad L_{grate} \approx 13.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Check GHRR:**

$$
q_{grate,\,actual} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{6.5 \times 13.1} = 352.3 \text{ kW/m}^{2} \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Combustion Air Requirement

**Step 1 — Ultimate analysis of MSW (typical Indian municipal waste).**

| Component | Mass Fraction |
|-----------|:-------------:|
| C (Carbon) | 25 % |
| H (Hydrogen) | 3 % |
| O (Oxygen) | 18 % |
| N (Nitrogen) | 0.5 % |
| S (Sulphur) | 0.2 % |
| Moisture | 40 % |
| Ash | 13.3 % |

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen demand per kg of dry combustible matter.**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg combustible}]
$$

Where $\dfrac{8}{3}$ comes from the molar mass ratio $\dfrac{32 \times 2}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3}$ for $C + O_{2} \to CO_{2}$.

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air demand.**

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{O_{2,\,stoich}}{0.232} \quad [\text{kg air / kg combustible}]
$$

The factor **0.232** is the mass fraction of oxygen in dry air (≈ 23.2 % by mass).

**Step 4 — Actual air with excess ratio $n$.**

$$
A_{actual} = n \times A_{stoich}, \quad n \approx 1.6 - 2.2 \text{ (typically 1.8)}
$$

**Numerical illustration for the MSW above (per kg of dry combustible):**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.25) + 8(0.03) + 0.002 - 0.18 = 0.6667 + 0.24 + 0.002 - 0.18 = 0.7287 \text{ kg O}_2
$$

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{0.7287}{0.232} = 3.14 \text{ kg air / kg combust.}
$$

$$
A_{actual} = 1.8 \times 3.14 = 5.65 \text{ kg air / kg combust.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Indian MSW (which has high moisture and ash), the air requirement on a *wet basis* is much higher — typically 4.5 – 6.5 kg air/kg waste.**

## 3.4 Residence Time and Forward Velocity

For an MSW grate:

$$
t_{res} = \dfrac{L_{grate}}{v_{forward}}
$$

For a reciprocating grate with stroke length $s$ and frequency $f$ (strokes/min):

$$
v_{forward} = \dfrac{s \times f}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}] \quad \text{or} \quad s \times f \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

**Numerical illustration:**

Given $s = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $f = 4 \text{ strokes/min}$, $L_{grate} = 13.1 \text{ m}$:

$$
v_{forward} = 0.25 \times 4 = 1.0 \text{ m/min} = 60 \text{ m/h}
$$

$$
t_{res} = \dfrac{13.1}{1.0} = 13.1 \text{ min}
$$

If residence is too short (< 10 min) → incomplete burnout, high LOI in ash. If too long (> 60 min) → low throughput.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

## 4.1 Schematic of a Mass-Burn MSW Incinerator with Reciprocating Grate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Waste Receipt and Weighbridge] --> B[Feed Hopper]
    B --> C[Ram Feeder]
    C --> D[Zone I Drying 200 to 600 C]
    D --> E[Zone II Ignition 600 to 900 C]
    E --> F[Zone III Combustion 900 to 1200 C]
    F --> G[Zone IV Ash Cooling 600 to 800 C]
    G --> H[Bottom Ash Discharger]
    H --> I[Ash Quench and Convey]
    G --> J[Secondary Combustion Chamber]
    J --> K[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    K --> L[Air Pollution Control ESP Fabric Filter]
    L --> M[Stack Emission]
    subgraph UnderGrateAirSystem
        P1[Primary Air Fan Zone I] -.-> D
        P2[Primary Air Fan Zone II] -.-> E
        P3[Primary Air Fan Zone III] -.-> F
        P4[Primary Air Fan Zone IV] -.-> G
    end
    subgraph OverFireAirSystem
        S1[Secondary Air Fan] -.-> J
        T1[Tertiary Air if any] -.-> J
    end
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Reciprocating Grate Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ReciprocatingGrateAssembly
        G1[Grate Bar Row 1 Drying Section] --> G2[Grate Bar Row 2 Ignition Section]
        G2 --> G3[Grate Bar Row 3 Combustion Section]
        G3 --> G4[Grate Bar Row 4 Burnout Section]
    end
    subgraph DriveSystem
        H1[Hydraulic Cylinders] --> H2[Drive Shaft and Linkage]
        H2 --> G1
        H2 --> G2
        H2 --> G3
        H2 --> G4
    end
    subgraph CoolingCircuit
        W1[Water Inlet 40 C] --> W2[Cooled Grate Bar Network]
        W2 --> W3[Water Outlet 180 to 220 C]
        W3 --> W4[Boiler Feedwater Integration]
    end
    W2 -.-> G1
    W2 -.-> G2
    W2 -.-> G3
    W2 -.-> G4
    subgraph AirPlenum
        Q1[Plenum Chamber I] -.-> G1
        Q2[Plenum Chamber II] -.-> G2
        Q3[Plenum Chamber III] -.-> G3
        Q4[Plenum Chamber IV] -.-> G4
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: Grate vs Rotary vs Fluidized Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ComparisonBlock
        O[Solid Waste Feed] --> P{Selection of Combustion Technology}
        P --> Q[Grate Furnace]
        P --> R[Rotary Kiln]
        P --> S[Fluidized Bed]
        Q --> Q1[Capacity: 1 to 1000 TPD]
        Q --> Q2[Particle Size: As Received, up to 1.5 m]
        Q --> Q3[Moisture Tolerance: Up to 55 percent]
        Q --> Q4[Application: MSW and Refuse Derived Fuel]
        R --> R1[Capacity: 0.5 to 100 TPD]
        R --> R2[Particle Size: Variable, Hazardous]
        R --> R3[Moisture Tolerance: High]
        R --> R4[Application: Hazardous Waste and Sludge]
        S --> S1[Capacity: 5 to 500 TPD]
        S --> S2[Particle Size: Must be Shredded below 50 mm]
        S --> S3[Moisture Tolerance: Moderate below 40 percent]
        S --> S4[Application: Refuse Derived Fuel and Sewage Sludge]
    end
```

## 4.4 Process Flow Matrix of Combustion Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZoneI
        A1[Raw MSW] --> A2[Water Evaporation]
        A2 --> A3[Heated to 200 to 600 C]
    end
    subgraph ZoneII
        A3 --> B1[Volatiles Pyrolysis]
        B1 --> B2[Char Formation]
        B2 --> B3[Flame Stabilization 600 to 900 C]
    end
    subgraph ZoneIII
        B3 --> C1[Char Oxidation]
        C1 --> C2[Peak Exothermic Reaction]
        C2 --> C3[Flame Core 900 to 1200 C]
    end
    subgraph ZoneIV
        C3 --> D1[Residual Burnout]
        D1 --> D2[Ash Discharge]
        D2 --> D3[Bottom Ash 600 to 800 C]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a combustion grate. List its four primary functions. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A combustion grate is a refractory-lined, air-permeable platform on which MSW is progressively burned in a mass-burn incinerator, with primary combustion air supplied from below. Its four primary functions are:

1. **Transport** of waste from feed end to ash discharge end.
2. **Agitation** of the waste bed to expose fresh fuel surfaces.
3. **Distribution** of primary combustion air through the bed.
4. **Combustion zoning** for drying, pyrolysis, combustion, and burnout.

### Q2. Distinguish between stationary and moving grates. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stationary Grate | Moving Grate |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Movement | None — manual poking | Mechanized reciprocation / rotation |
| Capacity | < 1 TPD (batch) | 1 – 1000 TPD (continuous) |
| Labour | High | Low |
| Combustion control | Poor | Excellent (zone-based air) |
| Suitability for MSW | Very limited | Industry standard |
| Examples | Old hospital waste units | Martin, Von Roll, Takuma |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the various types of combustion grates used in MSW incineration and explain the working principle of a **water-cooled reciprocating grate**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An MSW incinerator handles **8 TPH** of waste with NCV = **8500 kJ/kg**. The design **GHRR is 400 kW/m²**. Determine (i) total heat released, (ii) required grate area, and (iii) primary air requirement if the stoichiometric air demand is **4.5 kg air/kg waste** and excess air ratio is **1.8**, with primary air share **60 %**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Grate Classification and Working of Water-Cooled Reciprocating Grate**

**Classification (with brief explanation):**

1. **Stationary (Fixed) Grate** – No motion; used in small batch incinerators.
2. **Traveling Grate** – Continuous cast-iron belt; waste loaded at one end, ash discharged at the other.
3. **Reciprocating Grate** – Rows of bars move forward slowly and backward rapidly to advance and agitate waste. *Industry workhorse.*
4. **Rocking Grate** – Tilts cyclically; older design.
5. **Rotary Kiln** – Rotating refractory-lined cylinder; for hazardous waste.
6. **Fluidized Bed** – Sand bed suspended in air; for shredded waste.

**Working Principle of Water-Cooled Reciprocating Grate:**

- The grate consists of **cast-iron or alloy grate bars** arranged in 4–6 rows. Each row is mounted on a movable carrier driven by a **hydraulic cylinder** or eccentric crank.
- Each cycle has two phases:
  - **Forward stroke** (slow, ~ 10 s): The bar row pushes the waste bed forward by the stroke length (100 – 300 mm).
  - **Backward stroke** (fast, ~ 3 s): The bar row retracts; the waste, held by friction and ash bridges, remains in place, creating an **agitation effect**.
- The bars are **hollow**, with **pressurized water circulating at ~ 4 bar**, entering at ~ 40 °C and exiting at ~ 180 – 220 °C. The water absorbs heat, protecting the metal from slagging (ash melting) and from high-temperature corrosion.
- The heated water is **integrated into the boiler feedwater circuit** (cascade heating), improving overall plant efficiency by **3 – 6 %**.
- Underneath each row, a separate **air plenum** supplies **zone-controlled primary air**.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{waste} = 8 \text{ TPH} = 8000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $NCV = 8500 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $q_{grate,\,design} = 400 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- Stoichiometric air $= 4.5 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$
- Excess air ratio $n = 1.8$
- Primary air share $= 60\%$ of total air

**(i) Total heat released:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dfrac{8000 \times 8500}{3600} = \dfrac{6.8 \times 10^{7}}{3600} = 18{,}888.9 \text{ kW} \approx 18.89 \text{ MW}
$$

**(ii) Required grate area:**

$$
A_{grate} = \dfrac{18{,}888.9}{400} = 47.22 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Primary air requirement:**

Total air per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,actual} = n \times A_{stoich} \times \dot{m}_{waste} = 1.8 \times 4.5 \times 8000 = 64{,}800 \text{ kg/h}
$$

In volumetric terms (at STP, density ≈ 1.293 kg/Nm³):

$$
\dot{V}_{air,\,actual} = \dfrac{64{,}800}{1.293} = 50{,}116 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} \approx 50.12 \times 10^{3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

Primary air (60 %):

$$
\dot{V}_{primary} = 0.60 \times 50{,}116 = 30{,}070 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

Secondary air (40 %):

$$
\dot{V}_{secondary} = 0.40 \times 50{,}116 = 20{,}046 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **four combustion zones** that develop on a moving grate of an MSW incinerator. Discuss how **primary air distribution** is matched to each zone. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A municipal waste stream of **25,000 kg/h** has the following ultimate analysis: C = 22 %, H = 3 %, O = 16 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.2 %, moisture = 45 %, ash = 13.3 %. Calculate (i) stoichiometric air requirement in kg air/kg of dry combustible, and (ii) total actual air supplied if excess air ratio is **1.85**, with **65 % primary air** injected under the grate. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Four Combustion Zones on the Grate**

| Zone | Location | Process | Temperature (°C) | Primary Air % |
|:----:|:--------:|---------|:-----------------:|:------------:|
| **I — Drying** | First 25–30 % length | Moisture evaporation; endothermic | 200 – 600 | 25 – 30 |
| **II — Pyrolysis / Ignition** | 25 – 50 % | Volatiles released; char forms | 500 – 900 | 20 – 25 |
| **III — Combustion / Burnout** | 50 – 80 % | Char oxidation; peak exotherm | 900 – 1200 | 30 – 40 |
| **IV — Ash Cooling** | Last 20 % | Residual burnout; ash discharge | 600 – 800 | 10 – 15 |

**Air Distribution Matching:**

The **under-grate air plenum is divided into 3 – 4 compartments**, each connected to its own damper-controlled fan. The air split is matched to the local stoichiometric demand:

- **Drying zone**: High air demand (to carry away water vapour) but **low oxygen uptake** (combustion hasn't started). Air here is *pre-heated by the grate-cooling circuit* to assist drying.
- **Ignition zone**: Moderate air; primary role is to sustain devolatilization. Temperature rises sharply.
- **Combustion zone**: **Peak air supply** (40 % of total). Char oxidation is the main exothermic reaction.
- **Ash cooling zone**: Air here is *not for combustion* but to **quench the bottom ash**, preventing clinker formation and recovering sensible heat (pre-heated primary air).

> The combined outlet from all plenums is **hot primary air (180 – 250 °C)**, often used as combustion air or for in-bed drying.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Ultimate analysis (dry basis, normalizing 100 % combustible):**

Dry combustible = 100 % − 13.3 % ash = 86.7 % of total waste.
On dry-combustible basis:

- C = 22 / 0.867 = **25.37 %**
- H = 3 / 0.867 = **3.46 %**
- O = 16 / 0.867 = **18.45 %**
- N = 0.5 / 0.867 = **0.58 %**
- S = 0.2 / 0.867 = **0.23 %**

**(i) Stoichiometric oxygen (kg O₂ / kg dry combustible):**

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O
$$

$$
O_{2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}(0.2537) + 8(0.0346) + 0.0023 - 0.1845
$$

$$
= 0.6765 + 0.2768 + 0.0023 - 0.1845 = 0.7711 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg d.c.}
$$

Stoichiometric air:

$$
A_{stoich} = \dfrac{0.7711}{0.232} = 3.324 \text{ kg air/kg dry combust.}
$$

**(ii) Total actual air supplied (on a wet waste basis):**

Dry combustible per kg wet waste = 0.867 kg.
Stoichiometric air per kg wet waste:

$$
A_{stoich,\,wet} = 0.867 \times 3.324 = 2.882 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

Total actual air per hour (with $n = 1.85$):

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,actual} = 1.85 \times 2.882 \times 25{,}000 = 133{,}242.5 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Primary air (65 %):

$$
\dot{m}_{primary} = 0.65 \times 133{,}242.5 = 86{,}608 \text{ kg/h}
$$

In volumetric terms (at STP):

$$
\dot{V}_{primary} = \dfrac{86{,}608}{1.293} \approx 66{,}980 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h} \approx 66.98 \times 10^{3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** mix up dry-combustible basis with wet-waste basis — convert carefully using $(1 - \text{ash fraction})$ and $(1 - \text{moisture fraction})$.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget that stoichiometric oxygen **subtracts the oxygen already present in the fuel** ($O$ term in the formula). Ignoring this gives an answer ~ 25 % too high.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse GHRR (heat per unit grate area) with **specific grate load** (mass per unit grate area per hour) — both have units but mean different things.
> 4. **Do NOT** use **gross calorific value (GCV)** in grate calculations without first converting to **NCV** (subtract latent heat of water vapour — including moisture and combustion-formed water).
> 5. **Do NOT** claim that primary air = 100 % of stoichiometric; in practice, **40 – 70 %** of total air is supplied as primary; the remainder is **secondary/over-fire air**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — "If you remember only 8 things before the exam, remember these:"**

- ☐ A **grate** is the moving combustion hearth of a mass-burn MSW incinerator, performing **transport, agitation, air distribution, zoning, and ash discharge**.
- ☐ The four sequential **combustion zones** are **Drying → Pyrolysis/Ignition → Combustion/Burnout → Ash Cooling**, each with controlled under-grate air share.
- ☐ **Water-cooled reciprocating grates** are the industry standard for MSW due to high temperature tolerance and recovery of sensible heat.
- ☐ The **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR)** formula is $q_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times NCV}{3600 \times A_{grate}}$; typical design value **300 – 500 kW/m²**.
- ☐ Stoichiometric oxygen = $\dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$; divide by **0.232** to get stoichiometric air; multiply by **excess air ratio (1.6 – 2.2)** for actual air.
- ☐ **Primary air share = 40 – 70 %** of total air; injected below the grate.
- ☐ **Secondary / over-fire air = 30 – 60 %**; injected above the waste bed in the post-combustion zone to complete burnout of volatiles (target CO < 50 mg/Nm³).
- ☐ Key performance metrics: **residence time** (30 – 90 min), **specific grate load** (150 – 400 kg/m²·h), **burnout efficiency** (LOI of ash < 5 %), **grate fire temperature** (900 – 1200 °C peak).

> **End of Module 3 — Grates Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Furnaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Furnaces ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **furnace** in the context of solid waste management is a thermally insulated, enclosed combustion chamber engineered to maintain controlled high-temperature oxidation of heterogeneous solid, semi-solid, and gaseous waste streams. The fundamental purpose is the **volume reduction** (typically **80–95%** by volume and **65–80%** by mass), **detoxification** of hazardous constituents, and **energy recovery** from municipal solid waste (MSW), industrial refuse, biomedical waste, and refuse-derived fuel (RDF). As per KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, furnaces are classified as the **primary combustion chamber (PCC)** of an incineration system, distinct from the **secondary combustion chamber (SCC)** which handles off-gases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Furnace" in this module is *not* the metallurgical furnace used for smelting. It specifically refers to the **combustion reactor** designed for **waste-to-energy (WtE)** applications. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a furnace as a **"controlled, high-temperature oven for garbage."** Just as your kitchen oven bakes food by maintaining a precise temperature for a precise time, an industrial waste furnace burns refuse under strictly controlled conditions to: - **Destroy pathogens** (like a pressure cooker sterilizes food). - **Reduce volume** (like burning a pile of paper leaves only a small pile of ash). - **Capture heat** (like a wood stove warms a room, but on an industrial scale, this heat boils water to spin turbines and generate electricity). The key difference from a simple bonfire is **engineering control** — temperature, oxygen, residence time, and mixing are all precisely regulated to ensure complete combustion and prevent the formation of toxic byproducts like **dioxins** and **furans**. ### 1.3 The "3 T's" of Combustion — A Foundational Concept Every well-designed furnace must satisfy the **Three T's of Combustion**: | Parameter | Typical Value (MSW) | Why It Matters | |-----------|---------------------|----------------| | **Temperature** | **850°C – 1200°C** | Ensures destruction of organic toxics | | **Time (Residence)** | **1 – 2 seconds** (in gas phase) | Allows complete oxidation | | **Turbulence** | Reynolds number > **10,000** | Ensures O₂–fuel contact | > [!NOTE] > The **stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio** for typical MSW (approximated as C₆H₁₀O₅) is approximately **5.7 : 1 by mass**, though actual operation requires **100–150% excess air** (i.e., an equivalence ratio of **0.6–0.8**) to guarantee complete combustion. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Furnaces (Combustion Systems) Furnaces used in solid waste management are broadly classified by their **waste-feeding mechanism** and **combustion dynamics**: #### 2.1.1 Mass-Burn Furnaces (Grate-Type) - Waste is fed **as-received** (without pre-processing). - Burns material on a moving or stationary **grate** (stoker). - Sub-types: * **Reciprocating Grate** (most common worldwide) * **Rolling Grate** * **Travelling Grate** - Best suited for **heterogeneous MSW** with moisture content **20–50%**. #### 2.1.2 Rotary Kiln Furnaces - A **refractory-lined, inclined rotating cylinder** (typically 3–8 m diameter, 10–45 m length). - Rotation speed: **0.5–2.5 rpm**; inclination: **1–3 degrees**. - Used for **hazardous waste, biomedical waste, and sludges**. #### 2.1.3 Multiple Hearth Furnaces (MHFs) - Vertical, refractory-lined shaft with **horizontal circular hearths** stacked vertically. - Waste moves **downward by rabble arms**, while air moves **upward or downward** (counter-current or co-current). - Temperature profile: top hearths ~**300°C**, middle ~**700°C**, bottom ~**150°C** (cooling zone). #### 2.1.4 Fluidized Bed Combustors (FBCs) - Waste (often pre-processed) is combusted in a bed of **inert sand particles** (typically silica, 0.5–1.5 mm diameter) fluidized by air. - Operating temperature: **750–900°C** (lower than grate furnaces). - High heat transfer coefficient: **200–600 W/m²·K** (vs. 50–150 for grates). #### 2.1.5 Modular / Starved-Air Furnaces - Small, prefabricated units (1–100 tons/day). - Operate in **starved-air mode** (sub-stoichiometric) on primary grate, then gases are fully oxidized in secondary chamber. - Common for **small communities, hospitals, and rural areas**. ### 2.2 Furnace Design Parameters — The Engineer's Checklist | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |-----------|--------|---------------|------| | Heat Release Rate | HRR | **150 – 500** | kW/m² of grate | | Grate Heat Release Rate | GHRR | **300 – 1000** | kW/m² | | Volumetric Heat Release Rate | VHRR | **100 – 400** | kW/m³ | | Excess Air | EA | **50 – 100** | % | | Furnace Residence Time (gas) | τ | **1 – 3** | seconds | | Flue Gas Velocity | v | **3 – 10** | m/s | | Combustion Efficiency | η_c | **95 – 99.9** | % | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following equations form the **core numerical toolkit** expected in KTU university exams for this module. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $\dot{Q}_{released} = \dot{m}_{waste} \times CV_{waste}$ | Heat released by waste (kW) | | 2 | $GHRR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times CV}{A_{grate}}$ | Grate Heat Release Rate (kW/m²) | | 3 | $VHRR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times CV}{V_{furnace}}$ | Volumetric Heat Release Rate (kW/m³) | | 4 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (dimensionless) | | 5 | $\% \text{ Excess Air} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\phi} - 1\right) \times 100$ | Excess air from equivalence ratio | | 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{furnace}}{\dot{V}_{flue}}$ | Gas residence time (s) | | 7 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{CO_2}{CO_2 + CO} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency (from flue gas) | | 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_c \times \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine}$ | Plant thermal efficiency | | 9 | $\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{waste} \times CV}{10{,}500}$ | Stoichiometric air for MSW (Nm³/s, approx.) | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{ash} = \dot{m}_{waste} \times f_{ash}$ | Ash generation (kg/s); f_ash ≈ 0.20–0.30 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the backbone of **furnace sizing** in real WtE plants. For example, a 500 TPD (tons per day) MSW plant in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram or Njeliyanparambu) would require a grate area of approximately **70–90 m²** using a GHRR of 350 kW/m². ### 2.4 Stoichiometric Combustion Theory For a generalized waste with the empirical formula **CₐHᵦOᵧNδSεClζ**, the stoichiometric oxygen demand (kg O₂ per kg waste) is: $$O_{2,stoich} = \dfrac{32}{12}\left(\dfrac{a}{1}\right) + \dfrac{32}{4}\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) + \dfrac{32}{32}\epsilon - \dfrac{32}{16}\left(\dfrac{\gamma}{1}\right)$$ The corresponding air requirement uses the mass ratio **O₂ : Air ≈ 1 : 4.35** (i.e., air contains 23% O₂ by mass). **For typical MSW (C₆H₁₀O₅):** $$O_{2,stoich} = \dfrac{32}{12}(6) + \dfrac{32}{4}(5) - \dfrac{32}{16}(5) = 16 + 40 - 10 = 46 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg waste}$$ $$\text{Air}_{stoich} = 46 \times 4.35 \approx 200 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$ > [!NOTE] > This means every ton of MSW (when dried) requires **200 kg of stoichiometric air**. With 100% excess air, the actual requirement is **400 kg/ton** of dry waste. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for MSW (C₆H₁₀O₅) **Given:** Municipal solid waste has the empirical formula **C₆H₁₀O₅** (representing cellulose-like material). **Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 2 — Balance carbon atoms:** $$b = 6 \text{ (from 6 C atoms on the left)}$$ **Step 3 — Balance hydrogen atoms:** $$2c = 10 \implies c = 5 \text{ (5 H}_2\text{O molecules)}$$ **Step 4 — Balance oxygen atoms:** Oxygen on RHS = $2(6) + 5 = 17$ atoms. Oxygen from waste = 5 atoms. Therefore, oxygen from air = $17 - 5 = 12$ atoms. Since O₂ contributes 2 oxygen atoms per molecule: $$a = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ moles O}_2$$ **Step 5 — Final balanced equation:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mass basis:** - Molecular weight of C₆H₁₀O₅ = $6(12) + 10(1) + 5(16) = 162$ kg/kmol. - O₂ required = $6 \times 32 = 192$ kg O₂ per kmol of waste. **Step 7 — Specific O₂ demand:** $$O_{2,stoich} = \dfrac{192}{162} = 1.185 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$ **Step 8 — Convert to air demand (air = 23% O₂ by mass):** $$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \dfrac{1.185}{0.23} = 5.15 \text{ kg air/kg waste (mass basis)}$$ Or equivalently: $$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \dfrac{192}{0.21 \times 32} = 28.57 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air/kg waste (volume basis, at 0°C, 1 atm)}$$ > The KTU board often approximates this to **5.7 kg air/kg waste** when including inert N₂ dilution, since air is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume. ### 3.2 Derivation: Furnace Sizing from HRR **Problem Statement:** A WtE plant processes **500 TPD** of MSW with calorific value **2000 kJ/kg**. Design a reciprocating grate furnace assuming a Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR) of **350 kW/m²** and daily operating hours of **24 h**. **Step 1 — Compute waste feed rate:** $$\dot{m}_{waste} = \dfrac{500 \times 1000}{24 \times 3600} = 5.787 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2 — Total heat release rate:** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{waste} \times CV = 5.787 \times 2000 = 11{,}574 \text{ kW} \approx 11.6 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 3 — Required grate area:** $$A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{GHRR} = \dfrac{11{,}574}{350} = 33.07 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Practical sizing (with safety factor 1.4):** $$A_{grate,design} = 33.07 \times 1.4 = 46.3 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Furnace volume (assuming 4 m furnace height):** $$V_{furnace} = 46.3 \times 4 = 185.2 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 6 — Verify volumetric HRR:** $$VHRR = \dfrac{11{,}574}{185.2} = 62.5 \text{ kW/m}^3$$ > [!NOTE] > Since 62.5 kW/m³ is below the typical lower bound (100 kW/m³), this indicates the furnace is **oversized for the heat load** — likely because the volume is dictated by residence time requirements (≥ 2 s for complete combustion) rather than heat release. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Furnace Selection | Waste Type | Moisture | Best Furnace Type | Reason | |------------|----------|-------------------|--------| | Raw MSW (heterogeneous) | 20–50% | **Reciprocating Grate** | Tolerant to size variability | | Hazardous / Industrial | <10% | **Rotary Kiln** | High temperature, long residence | | Sludges (biosolids) | 70–85% | **Multiple Hearth** | Counter-current drying | | Pre-processed RDF | <15% | **Fluidized Bed** | Uniform particle size, high heat transfer | | Biomedical waste | 5–20% | **Rotary Kiln** | Meets WHO/BMW Rules 2016 specs | | Hospital waste (small scale) | 5–20% | **Starved-air modular** | Compact, prefabricated | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mass-Burn Reciprocating Grate Furnace — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Waste Receiving Pit
5-7 days storage] --> A2[Feeding Crane
Grab bucket] A2 --> A3[Feed Hopper
with hydraulic ram] A3 --> A4[Reciprocating Grate
Drying Zone 850C] A4 --> A5[Burning Zone
1000-1200C] A5 --> A6[Combustion Completion Zone
850-900C] A6 --> A7[Ash Discharge
to quench tank] A6 --> A8[Secondary Combustion Chamber
850C for 2 seconds] A8 --> A9[Waste Heat Boiler
generates 4 MPa steam] A9 --> A10[Flue Gas Treatment
Scrubbers and ESP] A10 --> A11[Stack Emission
50 m height minimum] A1 -.secondary air.-> A4 A1 -.secondary air.-> A5 A1 -.secondary air.-> A6 ``` ### 4.2 Rotary Kiln Furnace — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Waste Feed
via chute] --> B2[Rotary Kiln Shell
inclined 2 degrees] B2 --> B3[Kiln Drive Motor
05-25 rpm] B2 --> B4[Primary Burner
auxiliary fuel] B2 --> B5[Secondary Chamber
850C minimum] B5 --> B6[Air Pollution Control
system] B6 --> B7[Bottom Ash Conveyor] B2 --> B7 B3 -.rotational torque.-> B2 B4 -.high temperature flame.-> B2 ``` ### 4.3 The Three T's — Process Control Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB C1[Furnace Input
Waste and Air] --> C2{Temperature
greater than 850C} C2 -- Yes --> C3[Path complete] C2 -- No --> C4[Increase excess air or
add auxiliary fuel] C3 --> C5{Residence Time
greater than 1 sec} C5 -- Yes --> C6[All 3 Ts satisfied] C5 -- No --> C7[Increase furnace volume] C4 --> C2 C7 --> C5 C6 --> C8[Efficient Combustion
low CO low dioxins] ``` ### 4.4 Fluidized Bed Combustor — Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB D1[Pre-processed RDF
size 10-50 mm] --> D2[Screw Feeder] D2 --> D3[Fluidized Sand Bed
850C silica sand] D3 --> D4[Air Distributor Plate
velocity 1-3 m per sec] D4 --> D5[Freeboard Zone
secondary air injection] D5 --> D6[Cyclone Separator
recycle particulates] D6 --> D7[Heat Exchanger Tubes
immersed in bed] D7 --> D8[Ash Discharge
Bottom and cyclone] D4 -.fluidizing air.-> D3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]** **Q1. What is a furnace in the context of solid waste management? Mention its primary functions.** **Model Answer:** A furnace is an enclosed, refractory-lined combustion chamber used to oxidize solid waste at controlled high temperatures (**850–1200°C**). Its primary functions are: 1. **Volume reduction** of waste (80–95% by volume). 2. **Detoxification** of organic and pathogenic components. 3. **Energy recovery** by capturing flue gas heat to generate steam/electricity. It forms the **primary combustion chamber (PCC)** of an incineration system. --- **Q2. State and briefly explain the "Three T's" of combustion.** **Model Answer:** The Three T's govern complete combustion in a furnace: - **Temperature** ≥ **850°C** to destroy organic toxics and pathogens. - **Time** (residence time) ≥ **1–2 seconds** for gas phase to allow complete oxidation of CO to CO₂. - **Turbulence** (Reynolds number > **10,000**) to ensure proper mixing of fuel and oxygen. Failure of any one of the three leads to **incomplete combustion**, producing **CO, soot, dioxins, and furans**. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Classify the various types of furnaces used in solid waste incineration. Discuss the working principle of a **reciprocating grate furnace** with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A municipal corporation processes **300 TPD** of MSW with a calorific value of **1800 kJ/kg**. Calculate (i) the **grate area** required if GHRR = **400 kW/m²**, (ii) the **stoichiometric air** required, and (iii) the **actual air** with **80% excess air**. Take the empirical formula of MSW as C₆H₁₀O₅. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to Q1A(a): **Classification of Furnaces:** 1. **Mass-Burn Furnaces** (Reciprocating, Rolling, Travelling Grate) 2. **Rotary Kiln Furnaces** 3. **Multiple Hearth Furnaces** 4. **Fluidized Bed Combustors** (Bubbling, Circulating) 5. **Modular / Starved-Air Furnaces** **Working Principle of Reciprocating Grate Furnace:** Waste is fed from a hopper onto a series of **inclined, hydraulically-driven grate bars** arranged in a stepped pattern. The grate bars move in a **reciprocating (push-pull) motion** that: - **Dries** the waste on the upper zone (~30% of grate length) using radiation from the burning zone. - **Volatilizes and ignites** the waste in the middle burning zone (~40% of grate length) at **1000–1200°C**. - **Burns out** residual char in the lower burnout zone (~30% of grate length). Primary air is forced **upward through the grate** from below, providing oxygen for combustion while cooling the grate bars. Secondary air is injected into the furnace freeboard to ensure complete combustion of volatile gases. The entire process maintains the **3 T's** principle. --- ##### Model Solution to Q1A(b): **Given:** - $\dot{m}_{waste} = \dfrac{300{,}000}{24 \times 3600} = 3.472$ kg/s - $CV = 1800$ kJ/kg - $GHRR = 400$ kW/m² - Excess air = 80% **Part (i) — Grate Area:** Total heat released: $$\dot{Q} = 3.472 \times 1800 = 6250 \text{ kW}$$ Grate area: $$A_{grate} = \dfrac{6250}{400} = 15.625 \text{ m}^2$$ With a 1.4 safety factor: $A_{design} = 15.625 \times 1.4 = 21.875 \text{ m}^2$. **Part (ii) — Stoichiometric Air:** From the earlier derivation for C₆H₁₀O₅: $$O_{2,stoich} = 1.185 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$ $$\text{Air}_{stoich} = 1.185 / 0.23 = 5.15 \text{ kg air/kg waste (mass basis)}$$ **Part (iii) — Actual Air with 80% Excess:** $$\text{Air}_{actual} = \text{Air}_{stoich} \times (1 + 0.80) = 5.15 \times 1.80 = 9.27 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$ **Final Answer:** Grate area = **21.9 m²**, Stoichiometric air = **5.15 kg/kg**, Actual air = **9.27 kg/kg**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the working of a **rotary kiln furnace** with a diagram. State its advantages and disadvantages. Mention its typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What are the key **design parameters** of a furnace? Derive the expression for **combustion efficiency** based on flue gas analysis. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution to Q1B(a): **Working of Rotary Kiln Furnace:** A rotary kiln consists of a **long, cylindrical, refractory-lined steel shell** rotated slowly on its longitudinal axis (0.5–2.5 rpm) and inclined at **1–3°** from horizontal. Waste is fed from the higher end and moves downward by gravity and rotation. Counter-current air flow from the lower end provides oxygen. **Temperature zones (along the kiln length):** - **Drying zone** (first 25%): 200–400°C, moisture evaporation. - **Combustion zone** (middle 50%): 800–1200°C, volatilization and oxidation. - **Burnout zone** (last 25%): 500–700°C, char oxidation. **Advantages:** - Handles heterogeneous, abrasive, hazardous wastes. - High turbulence due to tumbling action. - Long residence time (1–4 hours) ensures complete destruction. - Good mixing of waste with combustion air. **Disadvantages:** - High capital cost and refractory wear. - Large footprint. - Not suitable for high-moisture waste. - Dust generation is high. **Applications:** Hazardous waste, biomedical waste, sewage sludge, industrial chemical waste. --- ##### Model Solution to Q1B(b): **Key Design Parameters:** 1. **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** — heat generated per unit grate/furnace area. 2. **Excess Air Percentage** — typically 50–100% for MSW. 3. **Grate Heat Release Rate (GHRR)** — usually 200–500 kW/m². 4. **Volumetric Heat Release Rate (VHRR)** — 100–400 kW/m³. 5. **Residence Time (τ)** — gas residence time ≥ 1 s at ≥ 850°C. 6. **Combustion Air Distribution** — primary (under-grate) and secondary (over-fire). 7. **Waste Characteristics** — moisture, CV, density, particle size. 8. **Turndown Ratio** — operational flexibility between 60–110% of design load. **Derivation of Combustion Efficiency (η_c):** The combustion efficiency is determined by measuring CO and CO₂ concentrations in dry flue gas. **Step 1:** Consider a carbon balance on the combustion process. The carbon entering as fuel is distributed between **CO₂** (fully oxidized) and **CO** (incompletely oxidized). **Step 2:** The fraction of carbon fully oxidized to CO₂ is: $$f_{CO_2} = \dfrac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]}$$ **Step 3:** The combustion efficiency is defined as the ratio of heat released by complete combustion to the total heat that would be released if all carbon were fully oxidized. Since oxidation of C → CO₂ releases 393.5 kJ/mol, while C → CO releases only 110.5 kJ/mol: $$\eta_c = \dfrac{f_{CO_2} \times 393.5}{f_{CO_2} \times 393.5 + (1 - f_{CO_2}) \times 110.5} \times 100$$ **Step 4 — Practical engineering approximation:** Since $[CO] \ll [CO_2]$, the simplified form is widely used: $$\boxed{\eta_c = \dfrac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100\%}$$ **Step 5 — Worked Example:** If flue gas shows CO₂ = 10% and CO = 500 ppm = 0.05%: $$\eta_c = \dfrac{10}{10 + 0.05} \times 100 = 99.5\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing furnace vs. incinerator:** A furnace is *one component* of an incinerator (the PCC). Marks are deducted if students treat them as synonyms. > 2. **Units in HRR:** Always include units (kW/m² or kW/m³). Numerical answers without units carry **zero credit** in numerical questions. > 3. **The 1-second rule:** Incomplete mention of "850°C for 1 second" is a common error. The KTU 2024 key specifically requires **both** temperature and time. > 4. **Excess air vs. equivalence ratio:** Students frequently mix up the formulas. Remember: $\phi < 1$ means **lean** (excess air); $\phi > 1$ means **rich** (starved air). > 5. **Combustion efficiency formula:** Writing only $\eta_c = CO_2/CO_2 + CO$ without mentioning the underlying principle of carbon balance loses **2 of 7 marks**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Furnaces (KTU 2024 Module 3)** - [x] A **furnace** = primary combustion chamber (PCC) of an incinerator; refractory-lined, enclosed, controlled. - [x] **Three T's:** Temperature (≥ 850°C), Time (≥ 1–2 s), Turbulence (Re > 10,000). - [x] **Types:** Mass-burn grate, Rotary kiln, Multiple hearth, Fluidized bed, Modular/starved-air. - [x] **Grate HRR:** 300–1000 kW/m²; **Volumetric HRR:** 100–400 kW/m³. - [x] **Excess air** for MSW: typically 50–100% (lean combustion). - [x] **MSW empirical formula:** often approximated as C₆H₁₀O₅ (cellulose basis). - [x] **Stoichiometric air** for C₆H₁₀O₅: **5.15 kg/kg** (mass basis) or **28.6 Nm³/kg** (volume basis). - [x] **Grate area formula:** $A_{grate} = (\dot{m} \times CV) / GHRR$. - [x] **Combustion efficiency:** $\eta_c = [CO_2]/([CO_2] + [CO]) \times 100\%$. - [x] **Furnace residence time:** $\tau = V_{furnace}/\dot{V}_{flue}$ (must be ≥ 1 s). - [x] **Reciprocating grate** is the **most common** for raw MSW worldwide. - [x] **Rotary kiln** is the **gold standard** for hazardous and biomedical waste. - [x] **Fluidized bed** requires **pre-processed** uniform RDF; offers superior heat transfer. - [x] **Multiple hearth** is ideal for **high-moisture sludges** (counter-current drying). - [x] **Dioxins and furans** form below 850°C — hence the **850°C / 2 s** rule in KTU 2024 syllabus. - [x] **Volume reduction:** 80–95%; **Mass reduction:** 65–80% in MSW incineration. - [x] **Auxiliary fuel:** Required during start-up and when waste CV falls below ~1000 kJ/kg. - [x] **Furnace refractory** materials: high-alumina, chrome-magnesia, SiC — chosen for thermal shock resistance. - [x] **Air staging** (primary + secondary air injection) is the **key modern design** feature to control NOₓ and ensure complete combustion. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Furnaces**

topics.title: Pyrolysis of Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis of Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyrolysis** is a **thermochemical decomposition process** in which organic solid waste materials are heated to elevated temperatures (typically **300 °C – 900 °C**) in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with a severely limited oxygen supply — sub-stoichiometric conditions), causing the long-chain hydrocarbon molecules in the waste to break down into smaller, more valuable products such as **pyrolytic char (solid residue)**, **pyrolysis oil / bio-oil (condensable liquids)**, and **non-condensable gases** (CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, and light hydrocarbons).

The word *Pyrolysis* is derived from the Greek words *pyro* (meaning **fire / heat**) and *lysis* (meaning **decomposition / breaking apart**) — literally translating to **"breaking apart by heat."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> Pyrolysis is classified as one of the **Thermochemical Conversion Technologies** for municipal solid waste (MSW), biomedical waste, plastic waste, and tyre waste — alongside **incineration**, **gasification**, and **torrefaction**. It is considered a **cleaner, low-emission alternative to open burning and landfilling** because it does not combust the waste directly and hence produces negligible NOₓ and dioxins when properly controlled.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charcoal Kiln" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Cooking in a Sealed Pressure Cooker:**
> Imagine you are roasting coffee beans in a **completely sealed pressure cooker placed over a stove**. No air (oxygen) enters the cooker from outside. As the temperature rises, the beans do not *burn* with flames — instead, they **slowly darken, release aromatic vapors, and leave behind a black, carbon-rich solid (charcoal-like residue)**. The vapors that escape, when condensed, look like a dark brown liquid (similar to pyrolysis oil), while a portion escapes as combustible gases (similar to syngas).
>
> This is **exactly how pyrolysis works** — except instead of coffee beans, the "feed" is **mixed municipal solid waste, plastics, rubber tyres, or biomass**, and instead of a pressure cooker, an industrial-scale **reactor (pyrolyser)** is used.

### 1.3 Key Process Parameters (Critical for KTU)

- **Operating Temperature:** **300 °C to 900 °C** (Type-dependent)
- **Heating Rate:** **Slow: < 1 °C/s**, **Fast: 10 – 200 °C/s**, **Flash: > 1000 °C/s**
- **Residence Time:** **Seconds (flash)** to **hours (slow)**
- **Oxygen / Air Supply:** **Zero (pure pyrolysis)** or **< stoichiometric (sub-stoichiometric)**
- **Pressure:** Usually **atmospheric (~101.325 kPa)**; sometimes elevated
- **Particle Size of Feed:** **< 50 mm** for uniform heat transfer

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

- **MSW Feed Calorific Value:** **~ 8 – 12 MJ/kg**
- **Pyrolysis Oil (Bio-oil) Yield:** **30 – 70 wt%**
- **Char Yield:** **10 – 35 wt%**
- **Gas Yield:** **10 – 30 wt%**
- **Net Energy Recovery Efficiency:** **60 – 85 %** for modern systems
- **Operating Temperature Range Standard:** **500 °C – 800 °C** (most common industrial window)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Pyrolysis — Step-by-Step Logic

Pyrolysis is fundamentally a **depolymerisation + cracking + recombination** reaction sequence. The process can be broken down into the following sequential sub-steps:

- **Step 1 — Drying (Sensible Heat Phase, T < 200 °C):**
  Moisture and any surface-bound water on the waste are evaporated. This is an **endothermic** physical process; no chemical bonds are broken.

- **Step 2 — Devolatilisation / Primary Pyrolysis (200 °C – 500 °C):**
  Weak C–C and C–H bonds within cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin (and similar polymeric structures in plastics and rubber) begin to break. Volatile tars and condensable vapors are released. Char begins forming.

- **Step 3 — Secondary Reactions (Cracking & Reforming, 500 °C – 800 °C):**
  Primary pyrolysis vapors may further **crack into lighter gases** (H₂, CH₄, CO) if residence time is high or if catalysts are present. Secondary char may form via **repolymerisation (coking)** of heavy tars.

- **Step 4 — Char Carbonisation (T > 700 °C):**
  Residual carbonaceous matter is converted into a **carbon-rich solid char**, often used as a soil amendment, fuel briquette, or activated carbon precursor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Pyrolysis:** Without oxygen, the molecules cannot combust completely. Instead of generating CO₂ + H₂O (as in incineration), they undergo **reductive thermal fragmentation** — preserving much of the chemical energy in the form of **liquid fuels (bio-oil)** and **solid fuel (char)**.
