This is what makes pyrolysis attractive for **waste-to-energy** applications.

### 2.2 Classification of Pyrolysis

| **Type** | **Heating Rate** | **Residence Time (Solid)** | **Temperature (°C)** | **Dominant Product** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis (Carbonisation)** | < 1 °C/s | Hours – Days | 300 – 500 | **Char (60 – 80 %)** |
| **Intermediate Pyrolysis** | 1 – 10 °C/s | Minutes | 500 – 650 | **Mixed (Oil + Char)** |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 10 – 200 °C/s | < 2 seconds (vapor) | 500 – 800 | **Bio-oil (60 – 75 %)** |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | > 1000 °C/s | < 0.5 seconds | 800 – 1000 | **Bio-oil + Gas** |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | Slow | Hours | 300 – 450 | **Bio-oil (low O content)** |

### 2.3 Product Spectrum & Engineering Utility

- **Pyrolytic Char:** Used as solid fuel, activated carbon precursor, soil conditioner (biochar), or carbon black substitute.
- **Pyrolysis Oil / Bio-oil:** Can be upgraded to transport fuels (via hydrodeoxygenation), or used directly in boilers and diesel engines.
- **Pyrolysis Gas (Pyrogas):** Rich in **CH₄, H₂, CO**; used to **provide process heat internally** (energy self-sufficiency) or in gas engines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall Pyrolysis Reaction (Generic)** | $C_{x}H_{y}O_{z} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\;no\;O_{2}} Char \;+\; Bio\text{-}oil \;+\; Gas$ | Mass balance closure |
| **Mass Balance (Closure)** | $M_{feed} = M_{char} + M_{oil} + M_{gas} + M_{losses}$ | $M$ in kg; $\sum Y_{i} = 1$ |
| **Product Yield (Mass Fraction)** | $Y_{i} = \dfrac{m_{i}}{m_{feed}}$ | Dimensionless; i = char, oil, gas |
| **Energy Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{char} + E_{oil} + E_{gas}}{E_{feed}}$ | Usually **60 – 85 %** |
| **Calorific Value of Feed (HHV)** | $HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | Modified Dulong formula (MJ/kg) |
| **Gas Composition by Volume** | $\sum V_{i} = V_{CO} + V_{CO_{2}} + V_{H_{2}} + V_{CH_{4}} + V_{C_{x}H_{y}}$ | Normalised to **100 %** |
| **Carbon Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{C_{char} + C_{oil} + C_{gas}}{C_{feed}}$ | Dimensionless (typically **0.70 – 0.95**) |
| **Stoichiometric Air Requirement** | $A_{st} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O$ | kg air per kg fuel (theoretical) |
| **Equivalence Ratio (for comparison)** | $ER = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{A_{stoichiometric}}$ | Pyrolysis: $ER \approx 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Pyrolysis is widely deployed for **plastic waste valorization** (especially polyolefins — PE, PP, PS), **end-of-life tyre recycling** (recovering carbon black + oil + steel), **biomass-to-bio-oil plants**, and **biomedical waste treatment** (since high-temperature anaerobic decomposition destroys pathogens effectively).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mass Balance Derivation for a Pyrolysis Reactor

Consider a **steady-state** pyrolysis reactor operating on a feed stream $F$ (kg/h) with products: **Char (C), Oil (O), Gas (G)** and negligible losses.

**Governing Conservation Equation (Mass):**

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{loss}$$

For an **ideal recovery assumption** where $\dot{m}_{loss} \approx 0$:

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}$$

**Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}_{feed}$ to obtain yield fractions:**

$$1 = Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**

> **Given:** A pyrolysis plant processes **1000 kg/h** of shredded mixed plastic waste. The product yields (by mass) are:
> - Char: **15 %**
> - Oil: **55 %**
> - Gas: **25 %**
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Mass flow rate of each product. (b) Verify mass balance closure.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Compute each product flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{char} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \; kg/h$$

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.55 \times 1000 = 550 \; kg/h$$

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \; kg/h$$

Step (b) — Verify mass balance:

$$\dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} = 150 + 550 + 250 = 950 \; kg/h$$

**Mass loss** $= 1000 - 950 = 50 \; kg/h$ (typically due to moisture loss, unaccounted particulates, and minor measurement errors).

**Closure** = $950/1000 = \mathbf{95\%}$ — acceptable for an industrial unit.

---

### 3.2 Energy Balance Derivation

For an **adiabatic, steady-state pyrolysis reactor**, the energy input (sensible heat + partial combustion of non-condensable gases) must balance the energy output (energy content of products + latent heat losses).

**Generic Energy Balance:**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} + \dot{m}_{feed}\,HHV_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char}\,HHV_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil}\,HHV_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}\,HHV_{gas} + \dot{Q}_{loss}$$

**Rearranging for Energy Recovery Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{char}\,HHV_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil}\,HHV_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}\,HHV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{feed}\,HHV_{feed}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Given:** Feed = 1000 kg/h MSW with $HHV_{feed} = 10\;MJ/kg$. Product HHV values: Char = 22 MJ/kg, Oil = 28 MJ/kg, Gas = 18 MJ/kg. Yields: 15 %, 55 %, 25 %.

**Step 1 — Energy in feed:**

$$E_{feed} = 1000 \times 10 = 10{,}000 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 2 — Energy in each product stream:**

$$E_{char} = 150 \times 22 = 3300 \; MJ/h$$

$$E_{oil} = 550 \times 28 = 15{,}400 \; MJ/h$$

$$E_{gas} = 250 \times 18 = 4500 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 3 — Total energy recovered:**

$$E_{recovered} = 3300 + 15{,}400 + 4500 = 23{,}200 \; MJ/h$$

**Step 4 — Recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{23{,}200}{10{,}000} = 2.32 = \mathbf{232\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A recovery efficiency of > 100 % is **physically possible** in pyrolysis because the **endothermic decomposition reaction absorbs heat during the process**, but the products — being in higher-energy chemical states (especially pyrolysis oil with many unsaturated hydrocarbons) — contain **more chemical energy per kg** than the original mixed waste. This is the *energy densification* advantage of pyrolysis over direct incineration.

---

### 3.3 Carbon Conversion Efficiency Derivation

Define the **Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)**:

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{C_{char} + C_{oil} + C_{gas}}{C_{feed}}$$

Where $C_i$ represents the **mass of carbon** in product $i$.

**In terms of mass fractions and elemental compositions:**

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Y_{char}\cdot\%C_{char} + Y_{oil}\cdot\%C_{oil} + Y_{gas}\cdot\%C_{gas}}{\%C_{feed}}$$

**Worked Example:**

> **Given:** Feed contains 50 % C. Char (15 % yield) = 80 % C; Oil (55 % yield) = 70 % C; Gas (25 % yield) contains carbon equivalent (CH₄ + CO) = 30 %.

**Numerator:**

$$(0.15)(0.80) + (0.55)(0.70) + (0.25)(0.30)$$

$$= 0.12 + 0.385 + 0.075 = 0.58$$

**Denominator** = $0.50$

$$\eta_{C} = \dfrac{0.58}{0.50} = \mathbf{1.16} \; (116\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CCE > 100 %?** This occurs because some **oxygen and hydrogen originally bound to carbon in the feedstock are released** during pyrolysis, leaving behind a **carbon-enriched** product. The numerator expresses the fraction of feed mass that ends up as carbon in the products, divided by the feed's original carbon fraction — so it can exceed 1.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Pyrolysis vs. Other Thermochemical Processes

| **Parameter** | **Pyrolysis** | **Incineration** | **Gasification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Supply** | None / trace | Excess (ER > 1) | Sub-stoichiometric (ER 0.2 – 0.4) |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 300 – 900 | 850 – 1200 | 700 – 1200 |
| **Dominant Product** | Char + Oil + Gas | Heat + Ash | Syngas (CO + H₂) |
| **Air Emissions** | Very low NOₓ, no SO₂ | High NOₓ, CO₂, dioxins | Moderate |
| **Energy Recovery** | Oil + Char reuse | Heat-to-power | Gas-to-power |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow of a Typical Pyrolysis Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MSW Feedstock: Plastics, Tyres, Biomass] --> B[Shredder and Sieve Unit]
    B --> C[Drying Section: Moisture Removal at 110 to 150 C]
    C --> D[Feed Hopper with Screw Conveyor]
    D --> E[PYROLYSIS REACTOR 300 to 900 C Anaerobic]
    E --> F[Char Discharge and Cooling Screw]
    F --> F1[Char Storage and Bagging Unit]
    E --> G[Hot Vapor and Gas Stream]
    G --> H[Quench Condenser: Rapid Cooling]
    H --> H1[Pyrolysis Oil Collection Tank]
    H --> I[Non-Condensable Gas Scrubber]
    I --> I1[Gas Cleaning: Tar and Particulate Removal]
    I1 --> J[Non-Condensable Pyrogas: CH4 CO H2 CO2]
    J --> K[Internal Combustor for Process Heat Recycle]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Gas Engine or Boiler for Power Generation]
    F1 --> M[End Use: Soil Amendment, Solid Fuel, Activated Carbon]
    H1 --> N[End Use: Fuel Oil, Chemical Feedstock]
    L --> O[Electricity Export to Grid]
```

### 4.2 Pyrolysis Reactor Type Comparison — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fixed Bed Reactor: Slow Pyrolysis, Batch Mode]
    P2[Fluidised Bed Reactor: Fast Pyrolysis, Continuous]
    P3[Rotary Kiln Reactor: Continuous, Mixed Waste]
    P4[Auger Reactor: Continuous, Slow to Intermediate]
    P5[Plasma Pyrolysis: Very High T, Hazardous Waste]

    P1 --> R1[Char Dominant Output]
    P2 --> R2[Bio Oil Dominant Output]
    P3 --> R3[Balanced Tri Product Output]
    P4 --> R4[Char plus Oil Dominant Output]
    P5 --> R5[Syngas plus Vitrified Slag]
```

### 4.3 Mass Balance Flow (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Feed 1000 kg per hour MSW] --> R[Pyrolysis Reactor]
    R --> CH[Char Output 150 kg per hour]
    R --> OL[Pyrolysis Oil Output 550 kg per hour]
    R --> GA[Non Condensable Gas Output 250 kg per hour]
    R --> LS[Unaccounted Losses 50 kg per hour]
    CH --> OUT1[To Char Utilisation]
    OL --> OUT2[To Oil Storage and Refining]
    GA --> OUT3[To Gas Combustor for Process Heat]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define pyrolysis. List any three operating parameters that influence the product distribution in a pyrolysis reactor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Pyrolysis is the thermochemical decomposition of organic solid waste in the **absence of oxygen** (or with limited oxygen) at temperatures typically between **300 °C – 900 °C**, yielding three product fractions — **char, bio-oil (pyrolysis oil), and non-condensable gases**.
>
> **Three Operating Parameters (1 mark, any 3):**
> 1. **Reactor temperature** — Higher T favours gas yield; lower T favours char.
> 2. **Heating rate** — Slow heating gives char; fast heating gives oil.
> 3. **Vapour residence time** — Long residence promotes secondary cracking to gas.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **slow pyrolysis**, **fast pyrolysis**, and **flash pyrolysis** with respect to heating rate and dominant product.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Type** | **Heating Rate** | **Dominant Product** |
|---|---|---|
| Slow Pyrolysis | < 1 °C/s | Char (60 – 80 %) |
| Fast Pyrolysis | 10 – 200 °C/s | Bio-oil (60 – 75 %) |
| Flash Pyrolysis | > 1000 °C/s | Bio-oil + Gas |

(1 mark per correct row, **3 marks total**.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the working of a **fixed-bed pyrolysis reactor** for the treatment of **mixed plastic waste**. Discuss the role of each unit operation involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pyrolysis plant processes **2000 kg/day** of segregated plastic waste. The mass yields obtained are: Char = 12 %, Oil = 60 %, Gas = 22 %. The calorific values are: Feed = **18 MJ/kg**, Char = **26 MJ/kg**, Oil = **40 MJ/kg**, Gas = **20 MJ/kg**.
Calculate: **(i)** Daily mass of each product, **(ii)** Total daily energy recovery, **(iii)** Overall energy recovery efficiency. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Working of a Fixed-Bed Pyrolysis Reactor (7 marks):**

A **fixed-bed (or batch-type) pyrolysis reactor** is a vertical cylindrical vessel made of high-grade stainless steel, externally heated by electrical resistance heaters or by hot gas combustion.

- **Feed Preparation:** Segregated plastic waste is shredded to **< 50 mm** size and dried to reduce moisture below **5 %**.
- **Loading:** Waste is charged into the reactor through a top hatch which is then sealed **airtight** to maintain anaerobic conditions.
- **Heating Phase:** The bed temperature is raised gradually from ambient to **400 – 550 °C** over **2 – 6 hours**.
- **Vapor Generation:** Plastic polymers (PE, PP, PS) undergo **chain scission**, releasing long-chain hydrocarbon vapors and lighter gases.
- **Vapor Treatment:** Vapors are routed through a **condenser** where heavy fractions liquefy into pyrolysis oil; non-condensable gases are flared or recycled.
- **Residue Discharge:** Solid char is collected at the bottom after cooling under a **nitrogen blanket** to prevent auto-ignition.

**Role of Each Unit Operation:**
- Shredder — uniform particle size for **uniform heat transfer**.
- Dryer — moisture removal to **improve energy efficiency**.
- Reactor — controlled **anaerobic heating**.
- Condenser — **liquid fuel recovery**.
- Scrubber — **emission control**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Daily mass of each product:**

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = 2000 \; kg/day$$

$$\dot{m}_{char} = 0.12 \times 2000 = \mathbf{240 \; kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.60 \times 2000 = \mathbf{1200 \; kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.22 \times 2000 = \mathbf{440 \; kg/day}$$

**Total accounted** $= 240 + 1200 + 440 = 1880 \; kg/day$
**Unaccounted loss** $= 2000 - 1880 = \mathbf{120 \; kg/day}$ (~6 %)

**(ii) Daily energy recovered:**

$$E_{char} = 240 \times 26 = 6240 \; MJ/day$$

$$E_{oil} = 1200 \times 40 = 48{,}000 \; MJ/day$$

$$E_{gas} = 440 \times 20 = 8800 \; MJ/day$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = 6240 + 48{,}000 + 8800 = 63{,}040 \; MJ/day}$$

**(iii) Energy recovery efficiency:**

$$E_{feed} = 2000 \times 18 = 36{,}000 \; MJ/day$$

$$\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{63{,}040}{36{,}000} = \mathbf{1.751 = 175.1\%}$$

**Interpretation:** > 100 % indicates **chemical energy densification** during pyrolysis (typical of plastic-rich feedstock due to deoxygenation and hydrogen enrichment of the oil fraction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental advantages** of pyrolysis over **incineration** for municipal solid waste (MSW). Compare the emission profiles of both processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **overall mass balance equation** for a pyrolysis reactor and explain why **mass loss** is observed even in a well-operated system. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Pyrolysis vs. Incineration — Environmental Comparison (7 marks):**

| **Environmental Aspect** | **Incineration** | **Pyrolysis** |
|---|---|---|
| **NOₓ Emissions** | High (due to high T combustion) | Very low (anaerobic) |
| **SO₂ Emissions** | Moderate to high | Negligible |
| **Dioxins / Furans** | Possible (esp. PVC feed) | Negligible (no Cl₂ generation) |
| **Particulate Matter** | High | Low (char traps heavy metals) |
| **CO₂ / Greenhouse** | High (combustion) | Lower (carbon retained in char) |
| **Solid Residue** | Toxic bottom ash + fly ash | Non-leaching biochar |
| **By-Product Recovery** | Heat only | Oil + Gas + Char (value-added) |

(4 marks for comparison table; **3 marks** for explanation of dioxin avoidance mechanism and char's metal-immobilisation property.)

**(b) Mass Balance Derivation (7 marks):**

The steady-state mass balance over a control volume enclosing the pyrolysis reactor is:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$

For a single-feed reactor:

$$\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{loss}$$

**Normalising by feed mass** to get yield fractions:

$$1 = Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas} + Y_{loss}$$

**Sources of Mass Loss (3 marks):**
- **Moisture evaporation** — moisture in feedstock is not captured in any product stream.
- **Uncondensed light hydrocarbons** — vapors that escape as fugitive emissions.
- **Particulate carryover** — fine char dust lost with the gas stream.
- **Measurement uncertainty** — sampling and weighing errors at the plant scale.

A **typical acceptable closure** is **> 90 %** for an industrial unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse pyrolysis with gasification** — Pyrolysis = **zero oxygen**; Gasification = **sub-stoichiometric oxygen**.
> 2. **Do NOT report energy efficiency > 100 % as "impossible"** — Explain the *chemical densification* concept (deletion of oxygen/hydrogen from feed releases energy-rich products).
> 3. **Always state the temperature range** (300 – 900 °C) explicitly — KTU examiners award marks for this specification.
> 4. **Avoid writing "combustion" when you mean "pyrolysis"** — mark deduction of 1 mark typically.
> 5. **Always include units (kg/h, MJ/kg, °C)** in numerical answers — **-0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Pyrolysis = **thermochemical decomposition of waste in absence of oxygen** at **300 – 900 °C**.
- Three product streams: **Char (solid)**, **Bio-oil (liquid)**, **Pyrogas (gaseous)**.
- **Slow pyrolysis** → high char yield. **Fast pyrolysis** → high bio-oil yield. **Flash pyrolysis** → gas + oil.
- Mass balance closure: $\sum Y_i = 1$ (with allowable loss < 10 %).
- Modified **Dulong formula** used for HHV estimation of feed.
- Pyrolysis is **cleaner than incineration** — no dioxins, low NOₓ, char immobilises heavy metals.
- **Energy recovery efficiency** can exceed 100 % due to **chemical densification** of products.
- **Reactor types:** Fixed bed, Fluidised bed, Rotary kiln, Auger, Plasma.
- **Suitable feedstocks:** MSW (after segregation), plastics (PE, PP, PS), tyres, biomass, biomedical waste.
- **ER ≈ 0** is the standard reference for pure pyrolysis.
- **Typical industrial operating window:** **500 – 800 °C** at **atmospheric pressure**.
- **Bio-oil is acidic and oxygenated** — requires **upgrading (hydrodeoxygenation)** for use as transport fuel.
- **KTU frequently tests:** (1) definitions, (2) process flow diagram, (3) numerical mass/energy balance, (4) comparison with incineration/gasification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recovery

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recovery** in solid waste management refers to the controlled extraction, reclamation, and reutilization of either **materials (Material Recovery)** or **energy (Energy Recovery)** from waste streams before final disposal, thereby diverting waste from landfills, conserving virgin natural resources, and reducing the environmental footprint of human consumption.

The term encompasses both **back-end recovery** (post-consumer waste separation and reclamation) and **front-end recovery** (industrial process integration where one industry's waste becomes another's feedstock, i.e., industrial symbiosis or by-product synergy).

In KTU 2024 Scheme classification, recovery operations are categorized into **four primary pillars**:

| Pillar | Output Form | Driving Mechanism |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Material Recovery (MR) | Recyclable secondary raw materials | Mechanical / Manual sorting |
| Biological Recovery (BR) | Compost, biogas, biofertilizer | Microbial action |
| Thermal/Energy Recovery (ER) | Electricity, heat, fuel pellets | Combustion / Thermochemical conversion |
| Chemical Recovery (CR) | Reagents, monomers, oils | Solvent extraction / depolymerization |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine your household waste as a "mineral ore body" buried in your dustbin.**
> Just as a mining engineer extracts iron, copper, and gold from low-grade ore using crushing, magnetic, and chemical separation, a solid-waste engineer extracts **paper fibres, aluminium, glass cullet, plastics, and thermal energy** from municipal solid waste (MSW).
> The "mine" is your city. The "processing plant" is the **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** or the **Waste-to-Energy (WtE) plant**. The "tailings" — what little is left after recovery — go to engineered landfills.

Another everyday analogy: **recovery is the "recycling of value."** When you tear a used A4 sheet into pulp to make a new notebook, that is *material recovery*. When you burn dry coconut husks to cook dinner, that is *energy recovery*. The hierarchy is always: **Reduce → Reuse → Recover → Dispose**.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Recovery Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three numerical parameters dominate recovery-engineering calculations in KTU board examinations:
>
> 1. **Recovery Efficiency (ηᵣ)** — dimensionless, typically **60–85 %** for well-designed MRFs.
> 2. **Diversion Rate (Dᵣ)** — % of waste diverted from landfill; modern cities target **75 %** by 2030.
> 3. **Calorific Value (CV)** — typically **800–4,500 kcal/kg** for Indian MSW; above **1,500 kcal/kg** is the threshold for energy recovery to be thermodynamically and economically viable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Recovery (MR) — Theory of Operations

Material recovery operates on the principle that **waste streams possess heterogeneous physical and chemical properties** that permit selective separation using mechanical, electromagnetic, aerodynamic, and optical forces.

### 2.1.1 Unit Operations in a Material Recovery Facility (MRF)

A standard MRF performs recovery in **five sequential unit operations**:

1. **Receiving & Tipping** — Mixed MSW unloaded on a concrete platform (tipping floor). Front-end loaders homogenize and feed the conveyor.
2. **Pre-sorting / Primary Shredding** — Manual removal of bulky items, rags, and hazardous waste (batteries, medical waste). Shredders reduce particle size to **50–150 mm**.
3. **Screening** — Trommel screens or vibrating screens separate by particle size. Fine fraction (< 20 mm) is called **undersize or inert rich**; coarse fraction is **oversize or combustible rich**.
4. **Separation by Material Type** — A combination of:
   - **Magnetic Separation** → Ferrous metals (Fe, steel) using overhead or drum magnets. Field strength **≥ 0.15 Tesla**.
   - **Eddy Current Separation (ECS)** → Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, brass) using high-frequency alternating magnetic fields (~ 1,000 Hz). Repulsion force lifts conductive particles.
   - **Optical Sorting / Near-Infrared (NIR) Sorting** → Plastics (PET, HDPE, PP) separated by polymer-specific spectral signatures.
   - **Air Classifier / Zig-Zag Separator** — Lifts light fractions (paper, film) using upward airflow; heavy fractions (glass, stones) fall.
   - **Density Separation** — Uses water (sink–float) or air tables; organics (~ 0.5 g/cm³) float; inerts (~ 2.0 g/cm³) sink.
5. **Baling & Dispatch** — Recovered materials compressed into bales (density ~ 400–600 kg/m³) and sold to re-processors.

### 2.1.2 Why these steps work — The "Why and How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shred?** Liberates individual materials from the bagged / composite matrix.
> **Why screen?** Waste has a natural size segregation: glass shards < 20 mm, paper flakes > 50 mm, organics variable.
> **Why magnetic?** Ferrous metals are ferromagnetic and strongly attracted to permanent Nd–Fe–B magnets.
> **Why eddy current?** Aluminium is non-magnetic but highly conductive; a time-varying magnetic field induces surface currents which by Lenz's law create a repulsive force.
> **Why optical?** Different polymers reflect different infrared wavelengths — PET reflects at ~ 1,700 nm, HDPE at ~ 1,730 nm. NIR sensors + compressed-air jets "shoot" the correct polymer off the belt.

## 2.2 Biological Recovery — Composting & Anaerobic Digestion

### 2.2.1 Aerobic Composting

Microbial oxidation of organic fraction of MSW under controlled moisture (**50–60 % wet basis**), temperature (**55–65 °C** thermophilic), C/N ratio (**25–35 : 1**), and pH (**6.5–8.0**) yields **compost / humus**.

Pathways:
$$\text{Organic Matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Heat} + \text{Stable Humus}$$

Methods of composting in Indian municipal context:
- **Windrow composting** (15–30 days active, 60–90 days curing)
- **In-vessel composting** (e.g., *Indore method*, *Bangalore method*, *NADEP* — by Narayan Deotao Pandharipande)
- **Vermicomposting** using *Eisenia foetida* earthworms

### 2.2.2 Anaerobic Digestion (AD) / Biogas Generation

In the absence of oxygen, organic matter is metabolized by a consortium of bacteria in four stages — **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.

The net biochemical reaction:
$$C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobes}} 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$

Biogas composition typically: **CH₄ = 55–65 %**, **CO₂ = 30–40 %**, **H₂S < 1,000 ppm**, **Calorific value ≈ 5,500–6,500 kcal/m³**.

Digester types: CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor), UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket), Plug-flow, and Fixed-dome (popular in rural India — *Deenabandhu* model).

### 2.2.3 The "Why and How" of Biological Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it work?** Microbiology — heterotrophic bacteria break C–C bonds and release the energy stored in photosynthesis-derived biomass.
> **Why control C/N?** Too much carbon (sawdust, paper) → bacteria have no nitrogen for protein synthesis → slow reaction. Too little (food waste alone) → ammonia is released → pH rises above 8.5 → methanogens die.
> **Why 55 °C thermophilic?** Pathogens (E. coli, Salmonella) are destroyed; reaction kinetics double for every 10 °C rise (van 't Hoff rule).

## 2.3 Thermal / Energy Recovery

### 2.3.1 Incineration with Energy Recovery (Mass-Burn WtE)

Combustion of MSW in a furnace at **850–1,100 °C** with **excess air (λ = 1.6–2.0)** to release heat. Heat is recovered in a boiler to produce **steam → turbine → electricity**.

Plant efficiencies:
$$\eta_{thermal} = \frac{Q_{recovered}}{m_{waste} \times CV_{waste}}$$
$$\eta_{electrical} = \frac{P_{gross} - P_{aux}}{m_{waste} \times CV_{waste}}$$

Typical values: **ηₜₕₑᵣₘₐₗ ≈ 65–80 %**, **ηₑₗₑcₜᵣᵢcₐₗ ≈ 20–30 %**.

### 2.3.2 Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) Production

RDF is **fluff-like, densified (5–50 mm pellets) waste** derived from the combustible fraction of MSW after removing inerts, glass, and moisture. Manufacturing flow:

$$ \text{Mixed MSW} \xrightarrow{\text{shred}} \text{Coarse fraction} \xrightarrow{\text{dry (CV ↑)}} \text{RDF} \xrightarrow{\text{densify}} \text{Pellets} $$

RDF calorific value: **1,500–3,500 kcal/kg**, suitable as supplementary fuel in cement kilns.

### 2.3.3 Advanced Thermochemical Conversion

| Process | Atmosphere | Temperature | Primary Product |
|---------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Pyrolysis** | Inert (N₂) | 400–900 °C | Bio-oil, char, gas |
| **Gasification** | Sub-stoichiometric O₂ | 700–1,200 °C | Syngas (CO + H₂) |
| **Plasma Arc** | High-temp plasma | > 3,000 °C | Vitrified slag + syngas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these processes matter:** They convert waste into gaseous or liquid fuels that are **easier to store, transport, and combust cleanly** than raw MSW, and avoid the dioxin/furans issues of poor incineration.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Numerical Value |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Recovery Efficiency** | $$\eta_r = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100$$ | % | 60–85 % |
| 2 | **Diversion Rate** | $$D_r = \dfrac{M_{recovered} + M_{recycled} + M_{composted}}{M_{generated}} \times 100$$ | % | 30–75 % |
| 3 | **Moisture Content (wet basis)** | $$MC_w = \dfrac{M_{water}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ | % | MSW = 40–60 % |
| 4 | **Moisture Content (dry basis)** | $$MC_d = \dfrac{M_{water}}{M_{dry\,solids}}$$ | kg water / kg dry | — |
| 5 | **Calorific Value (CV)** (Dulong's) | $$CV = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$$ | kcal/kg | MSW = 800–4,500 |
| 6 | **Composting C/N Ratio** | $$\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum m_i\,C_i}{\sum m_i\,N_i}$$ | dimensionless | 25–35 ideal |
| 7 | **Blending C/N** | $$C = \dfrac{C_1\,m_1 + C_2\,m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$$ ; same for N | — | — |
| 8 | **Methane Yield** | $$Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times VS \times\left(1 - e^{-k\,t}\right)$$ | m³ CH₄ / kg VS | k = 0.3–0.5 /day |
| 9 | **Combustion Air Requirement (Stoichiometric)** | $$A_{st} = 8.89\,C + 26.7\,H - 3.33\,O + 3.33\,S$$ | kg air / kg fuel | — |
| 10 | **Excess Air Ratio** | $$\lambda = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{A_{stoichiometric}}$$ | dimensionless | 1.6–2.0 |
| 11 | **WtE Plant Energy Efficiency** | $$\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{m_{waste}\times CV_{waste}}$$ | % | 20–30 % |
| 12 | **Biogas Energy** | $$E_{biogas} = V \times \rho_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4}$$ | MJ | CV_CH4 = 55.5 MJ/kg |
| 13 | **Eddy Current Lift Force** | $$F_{lift} = \dfrac{\pi\,d^3\,\sigma\,\omega\,B^2}{8}$$ | N | d = particle dia, σ = conductivity |
| 14 | **Air Classifier Terminal Velocity** | $$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\,d\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_a)}{3\,C_d\,\rho_a}}$$ | m/s | used for paper vs. glass separation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** When asked to "calculate the recovery efficiency," ALWAYS show the **mass balance over the recovery train** — i.e., a flow diagram with feed → outputs → losses. Examiners award 2 marks just for the labelled diagram and 2 marks for correctly substituting into the formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Recovery Efficiency Calculation (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam Style]:** A city generates **500 tonnes/day** of MSW. The MRF recovers: paper = 80 t/d, plastics = 25 t/d, ferrous metals = 20 t/d, non-ferrous metals = 10 t/d, glass = 15 t/d. Rejects to landfill = 350 t/d. Calculate:
> (a) Total recovery efficiency.
> (b) Diversion rate (assuming no backyard composting or reuse).
> (c) Per-capita recovery if city population = 1.0 million.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Recovery Efficiency**

Let total recovered mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{recovered} &= M_{paper} + M_{plastic} + M_{Fe} + M_{NF} + M_{glass} \\[4pt]
M_{recovered} &= 80 + 25 + 20 + 10 + 15 \\[4pt]
M_{recovered} &= 150 \; \text{tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recovery efficiency:

$$
\eta_r = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100 = \dfrac{150}{500} \times 100 = \mathbf{30 \, \%}
$$

**(b) Diversion Rate**

Since the problem states "no backyard composting or reuse," diversion rate equals recovery rate:

$$D_r = \dfrac{M_{recovered} + M_{recycled} + M_{composted}}{M_{generated}} \times 100 = \dfrac{150 + 0 + 0}{500} \times 100 = \mathbf{30 \, \%}$$

**(c) Per-Capita Recovery**

$$\text{Per-capita recovery} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^3 \; \text{kg/day}}{1{,}000{,}000 \; \text{persons}} = \mathbf{0.150 \; \text{kg/person/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** This is realistic — Indian urban per-capita MSW generation is 0.2–0.6 kg/day, and 30 % diversion is typical for mid-tier cities.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Composting C/N Ratio Blending (3-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A compost facility receives two waste streams:
> - **Stream A:** Food waste, mass = 2,000 kg, C = 48 %, N = 2.5 %
> - **Stream B:** Sawdust, mass = 800 kg, C = 56 %, N = 0.4 %
> Find the C/N ratio of the blend and suggest whether it is ideal.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate carbon mass in each stream.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= 0.48 \times 2000 = 960 \; \text{kg} \\[4pt]
C_B &= 0.56 \times 800 = 448 \; \text{kg} \\[4pt]
C_{total} &= 960 + 448 = 1408 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate nitrogen mass in each stream.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= 0.025 \times 2000 = 50 \; \text{kg} \\[4pt]
N_B &= 0.004 \times 800 = 3.2 \; \text{kg} \\[4pt]
N_{total} &= 50 + 3.2 = 53.2 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate blend C/N ratio.**

$$
\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{blend} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{total}} = \dfrac{1408}{53.2} = \mathbf{26.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Since **ideal C/N = 25–35**, the blend ratio of **26.5** is **ideal**. No amendment needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **What if C/N had been > 35?** We would add a nitrogen-rich amendment such as urea (46 % N) or poultry manure (1.5 % N). What if C/N < 20? We would add high-carbon sawdust or rice straw.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Biogas Energy Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

> **Problem:** An anaerobic digester receives **10 tonnes/day** of food waste with **Volatile Solids (VS) = 85 %**, biodegradability factor **k·t such that (1 − e⁻ᵏᵗ) = 0.85**, methane yield coefficient **0.35 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**. Methane density = 0.717 kg/m³, CV of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg.
> Calculate (a) Daily methane volume, (b) Daily energy output, (c) Equivalent LPG cylinders (1 cylinder = 14.2 kg LPG, CV_LPG = 46 MJ/kg).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Daily methane volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{CH_4} &= 0.35 \times VS \times (1 - e^{-kt}) \\[4pt]
Y_{CH_4} &= 0.35 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
Y_{CH_4} &= 0.253 \; \text{m}^3 \, CH_4 \text{ per kg VS} \\[4pt]
\text{Total CH}_4 &= 0.253 \times (10{,}000 \times 0.85) \\[4pt]
&= 0.253 \times 8500 = \mathbf{2{,}150.5 \; m^3 / day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Daily energy output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of CH}_4 &= V \times \rho = 2150.5 \times 0.717 = 1541.9 \; \text{kg/day} \\[4pt]
E &= m_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} = 1541.9 \times 55.5 \\[4pt]
E &= \mathbf{85{,}575 \; MJ/day} \;\; \left(\approx 23.77 \; \text{MWh/day}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Equivalent LPG cylinders**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LPG mass equivalent} &= \dfrac{E}{CV_{LPG}} = \dfrac{85{,}575}{46} = 1860 \; \text{kg/day} \\[4pt]
\text{LPG cylinders} &= \dfrac{1860}{14.2} = \mathbf{131 \; cylinders/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** Anaerobic digestion thus converts 10 tonnes of food waste — which would otherwise rot in a landfill producing methane uncontrolled — into ~ 131 LPG cylinders worth of *controlled, captured, usable* energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Recovery Pathways

| Parameter | Material Recovery | Composting | Anaerobic Digestion | Incineration (WtE) |
|-----------|-------------------|------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Input requirement** | Mixed MSW (sorted) | Organic fraction only | Organic fraction only | Combustible fraction |
| **Output** | Secondary raw materials | Compost | Biogas + digestate | Electricity + heat + ash |
| **Mass reduction** | 30–40 % | 50–60 % | 70–80 % | 85–90 % |
| **Volume reduction** | 40–50 % | 60–70 % | 80–85 % | 90–95 % |
| **Air emissions** | Negligible | Bioaerosols (controlled) | CH₄ leak (controlled) | CO₂, NOₓ, dioxins (controlled) |
| **Residue** | Rejects to landfill | Stable humus | Digestate (fertilizer) | Bottom ash + fly ash |
| **Capital cost** | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Energy balance** | Negative (uses power) | Negative | Net-positive (with CHP) | Net-positive (large scale) |
| **Carbon footprint** | Strongly negative (displaces virgin) | Slightly negative | Negative | Neutral–slightly positive |
| **Best for Indian MSW (high moisture, low CV)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Not ideal (CV < 1,500 kcal/kg) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material Recovery Facility (MRF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MSW Receiving and Tipping Floor"]
    nodeB["Primary Shredder 50 to 150 mm"]
    nodeC["Trommel Screen 20 mm Aperture"]
    nodeD["Manual Sorting Cabin"]
    nodeE["Overhead Magnetic Drum Ferrous"]
    nodeF["Eddy Current Separator Non Ferrous"]
    nodeG["NIR Optical Sorter Plastic Polymers"]
    nodeH["Air Classifier Zig Zag"]
    nodeI["Baling Press 400 kg per cubic meter"]
    nodeJ["Recovered Bales Dispatch"]
    nodeK["Rejects Conveyor to RDF or Landfill"]
    nodeL["Fines < 20 mm to Composting"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK

    classDef proc fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#70ad47,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef waste fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH proc
    class nodeI,nodeJ out
    class nodeK,nodeL waste
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangle is a *unit operation*. Arrows indicate material flow. **Green** blocks are saleable outputs, **red** blocks are rejects / residuals. The architecture mirrors the five-stage MRF logic of Section 2.1.1.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Energy Recovery / Waste-to-Energy Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA["MSW Combustible Fraction"]
    inB["Feeding System Grab Crane"]
    inC["Combustion Grate Furnace 850 to 1100 C"]
    inD["Boiler Steam Generation 40 bar 400 C"]
    inE["Steam Turbine Generator"]
    inF["Electrostatic Precipitator Particulate"]
    inG["Flue Gas Desulfurization SOx"]
    inH["Selective Catalytic Reduction NOx"]
    inI["Stack Emission Monitoring"]
    inJ["Bottom Ash Quench Conveyor"]
    inK["Fly Ash Stabilization and Landfill"]
    inL["Energy Export to Grid Electricity"]
    inM["District Heating Hot Water"]

    inA --> inB --> inC --> inD --> inE
    inC --> inF --> inG --> inH --> inI
    inC --> inJ
    inF --> inK
    inE --> inL
    inD --> inM

    classDef fire fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef clean fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef prod fill:#70ad47,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef waste fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class inC,inD,inE fire
    class inF,inG,inH,inI clean
    class inL,inM prod
    class inJ,inK waste
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's insight:** The emission control train (ESP → FGD → SCR → Stack) is the most expensive part of a WtE plant — typically **40 % of capital cost**. Without it, the plant cannot comply with the CPCB norms for dioxins (< 0.1 ng TEQ/m³) and particulate matter (< 50 mg/Nm³).

---

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Which Recovery Path for Indian MSW?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"MSW Moisture Content"}
    q1 -- less than 40 percent --> q2{"Calorific Value kJ per kg"}
    q1 -- greater than 40 percent --> q3["Biological Recovery Composting or AD"]
    q2 -- greater than 1500 --> q4["Incineration WtE viable"]
    q2 -- 800 to 1500 --> q5["RDF production plus co firing in cement kiln"]
    q2 -- less than 800 --> q6["Material Recovery MRF plus composting"]
    q4 --> q7["Power 20 to 30 percent efficiency"]
    q5 --> q8["Cement industry alternative fuel"]
    q6 --> q9["Compost plus recyclables"]
    q3 --> q10["Biogas 55 percent CH4"]
    q7 --> q11["Use only for high CV Indian cities"]
    q8 --> q12["Wide applicability"]
    q9 --> q13["Universal"]
    q10 --> q14["Decentralized plants"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Reality Check:** Indian MSW typically has **moisture > 40 %** and **CV < 1,500 kcal/kg** because of food waste dominance (40–60 % by weight) and poor source segregation. This is why biological recovery + MRF is the *practically applicable* default for India, while WtE suits only a few cities (e.g., Hyderabad, Delhi, Bengaluru) after pre-processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **Define material recovery and explain the working principle of an Eddy Current Separator (ECS) used for non-ferrous metal recovery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~ 80 words):**

Material recovery is the process of extracting recyclable secondary raw materials from the waste stream. An **Eddy Current Separator (ECS)** works on the principle of electromagnetic induction. A high-frequency alternating magnetic field (~ 1,000 Hz) generated by a magnetic rotor rotating at high speed induces circulating **eddy currents** on the surface of conductive non-ferrous particles (Al, Cu). By **Lenz's law**, these eddy currents produce a magnetic field that **opposes** the rotor's field, generating a repulsive **lift force** that ejects the conductive particles out of the waste stream into a collection bin, while non-conductive materials (plastics, glass) fall straight through. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **List and explain any three factors that affect the calorific value of MSW.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The calorific value (CV) of MSW determines its suitability for energy recovery. Three key factors are:

1. **Moisture Content (MC)** — Inversely proportional to CV. Food waste with MC ~ 70 % has CV ~ 400 kcal/kg, while dry paper (MC ~ 5 %) has CV ~ 4,000 kcal/kg.
2. **Composition** — Plastics (~ 10,000 kcal/kg) and paper (~ 4,000 kcal/kg) are high-CV components, while inert fractions (glass, metals, soil) have negligible CV.
3. **C/N Ratio and Volatile Solids (VS)** — Higher organic fraction means higher CV but lower ignition quality. A high ash content (typically 25–40 % in Indian MSW) reduces CV. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B — Choice A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the sequential unit operations in a typical Material Recovery Facility (MRF). Explain the working principle of any **two** separation techniques used.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A city generates 800 tonnes/day of MSW with the composition shown below. The MRF recovers paper and plastics at 70 % efficiency, ferrous metals at 85 % efficiency, and non-ferrous metals at 60 % efficiency. Calculate the total daily mass recovered and the overall recovery efficiency.
>
> | Component | % by mass | Mass (t/d) |
> |-----------|-----------|-----------|
> | Paper | 12 % | 96 |
> | Plastics | 8 % | 64 |
> | Ferrous metals | 6 % | 48 |
> | Non-ferrous metals | 2 % | 16 |
> | Glass | 4 % | 32 |
> | Organics | 45 % | 360 |
> | Inerts (soil, sand) | 20 % | 160 |
> | Others | 3 % | 24 |

### Model Solution — Choice A

#### Part (a) — MRF Unit Operations (7 marks)

**Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```
MSW → Receiving & Tipping → Primary Shredding → Trommel Screening (20 mm)
            ↓                              ↓
       Manual Sorting              Magnetic Separator (Fe)
            ↓                              ↓
       Air Classifier              Eddy Current Separator (NF)
            ↓                              ↓
        NIR Optical Sorter            Baling Press
            ↓                              ↓
         Recovered Bales           Rejects → RDF / Landfill
```

**Working Principle of Two Separation Techniques (4 marks — 2 each):**

**1. Magnetic Separation (Ferrous):** A permanent magnet (Nd–Fe–B type) or electromagnet generates a strong, localized magnetic field (~ 0.15 Tesla) over a conveyor carrying shredded MSW. Ferromagnetic particles (iron, steel cans) are attracted to the magnet and lifted off the belt, while non-magnetic materials follow the trajectory of gravity and inertia onto the next conveyor. Recovery rate of ferrous metals is typically **85–95 %**.

**2. NIR Optical Sorting (Plastics):** A line-scan Near-Infrared sensor illuminates each particle on a fast-moving conveyor. Different polymers absorb IR light at characteristic wavelengths (PET ≈ 1,700 nm; HDPE ≈ 1,730 nm; PP ≈ 1,750 nm). The reflected spectrum is matched against a library; if the particle matches the target polymer, a precisely timed **compressed-air jet** fires, deflecting the particle into the correct chute. Throughput: 2–5 tonnes/hour per lane. **(7 marks total)**

#### Part (b) — Recovery Efficiency Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mass recovered from each stream.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{paper} &= 96 \times 0.70 = 67.2 \; \text{t/d} \\
M_{plastic} &= 64 \times 0.70 = 44.8 \; \text{t/d} \\
M_{Fe} &= 48 \times 0.85 = 40.8 \; \text{t/d} \\
M_{NF} &= 16 \times 0.60 = 9.6 \; \text{t/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total recovered mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{recovered} &= 67.2 + 44.8 + 40.8 + 9.6 \\
&= \mathbf{162.4 \; t/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overall recovery efficiency.**

$$
\eta_r = \dfrac{162.4}{800} \times 100 = \mathbf{20.3 \, \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only the four listed components (paper, plastic, Fe, NF = 28 % of MSW) are recovered. Glass, organics, and inerts are NOT recovered here (glass would be sent to recycling; organics to composting). So 20.3 % is the **MRF-only recovery efficiency**. With composting added, total diversion ≈ 65 %.

**(7 marks total)**

---

### Question B — Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **four stages** of anaerobic digestion. Write the overall biochemical reaction and list the operating parameters (temperature, pH, C/N, HRT) required for stable digester operation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A 5,000 m³ biogas digester (80 % fill) produces biogas at 1.2 vol/vol/day. Methane content = 60 %. Calculate the daily methane volume, daily energy content in MJ (using CV_CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg, ρ_CH₄ = 0.717 kg/m³), and the equivalent electrical energy if a CHP engine has 35 % electrical efficiency.

### Model Solution — Choice B

#### Part (a) — Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion (7 marks)

| Stage | Microbial Group | Input | Output |
|-------|-----------------|-------|--------|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Hydrolytic bacteria | Polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) | Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) |
| **2. Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic bacteria | Monomers | Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, CO₂ |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria | VFAs | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate + H₂ + CO₂ | **CH₄ + CO₂** (biogas) |

**Overall Biochemical Reaction (1 mark):**

$$
C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic microbes}} 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

**Operating Parameters Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Optimal |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|---------| 
| Temperature | 30–40 °C | 50–55 °C | 35 °C (most common) |
| pH | 6.5–7.5 | 7.0–8.0 | **7.0–7.2** |
| C/N ratio | 20–30 | 20–30 | **25–30** |
| HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time) | 20–40 days | 10–20 days | 30 days (CSTR) |
| VS loading rate | 1–3 kg VS/m³·day | 2–5 kg VS/m³·day | 2 kg VS/m³·day |
| Biogas yield | 0.3–0.5 m³/kg VS | 0.4–0.6 m³/kg VS | 0.5 m³/kg VS |

**(7 marks total)**

#### Part (b) — Biogas Energy Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Effective digester volume.**

$$
V_{eff} = 5000 \times 0.80 = 4000 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Daily biogas production.**

$$
V_{biogas} = V_{eff} \times 1.2 = 4000 \times 1.2 = 4800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily methane volume (60 %).**

$$
V_{CH_4} = 4800 \times 0.60 = 2880 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of methane.**

$$
m_{CH_4} = 2880 \times 0.717 = 2064.96 \; \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily thermal energy.**

$$
E_{thermal} = 2064.96 \times 55.5 = 114{,}605 \; \text{MJ/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Electrical energy (35 % efficiency).**

$$
E_{elec} = 114{,}605 \times 0.35 = 40{,}112 \; \text{MJ/day}
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E_{elec} = \dfrac{40{,}112}{3.6} = \mathbf{11{,}142 \; kWh/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** This is the output of a medium-scale Indian municipal biogas plant — enough to power ~ 1,000 Indian households (assuming 10 kWh/household/day) for a day.

**(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by yield efficiency:** Many students compute *gross* recovery (100 %) instead of applying the given recovery efficiency per stream. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content:** Always check whether the question gives wet-basis or dry-basis. CV formula uses **dry-basis** mass.
> 3. **Using kg instead of tonnes (or vice versa):** Mass-balance unit consistency is checked. Mixing tonnes and kg in one equation = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting the ρ (density) of methane:** Methane energy = Volume × Density × CV. Students who skip density lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Missing the biogas CH₄ content factor (60–65 %):** Using 100 % CH₄ assumption instead of the actual 60 % is a common error — **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Not drawing labelled flow diagrams:** A diagram carries **1–2 marks** in every 14-mark answer. Skipping it = guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Recovery** = extraction of material OR energy from waste streams; sits above disposal in the waste hierarchy.
- [x] **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** uses a 5-stage train: Receiving → Shredding → Screening → Separation → Baling.
- [x] **Magnetic separation** removes ferrous metals (~ 85–95 % efficiency); **Eddy Current Separation** removes non-ferrous metals (~ 60–80 % efficiency); **NIR optical sorting** separates plastic polymers; **Air classification** separates paper from glass.
- [x] **Composting** is aerobic microbial decomposition of organic waste; ideal **C/N = 25–35**; thermophilic at **55–65 °C**; products are CO₂, H₂O, and stable humus.
- [x] **Anaerobic Digestion** is oxygen-free decomposition in 4 stages: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**; products are CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas, CV ≈ 6,000 kcal/m³).
- [x] **Methane yield equation:** $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times VS \times (1 - e^{-kt})$
- [x] **Incineration with energy recovery** (WtE) requires **CV > 1,500 kcal/kg**; ideal for Indian MSW only after pre-processing.
- [x] **RDF** (Refuse Derived Fuel) is densified combustible fraction with CV **1,500–3,500 kcal/kg**, used in cement kilns.
- [x] **Pyrolysis** (inert atmosphere, 400–900 °C) gives bio-oil, char, gas.
- [x] **Gasification** (sub-stoichiometric O₂, 700–1,200 °C) gives syngas (CO + H₂).
- [x] **Recovery efficiency** $\eta_r = M_{recovered}/M_{input} \times 100$; typical Indian MRF = 20–30 %.
- [x] **Diversion rate** $D_r = (M_{recovered}+M_{composted})/M_{generated} \times 100$; Indian target = 75 % by 2030.
- [x] **Dulong's formula** estimates CV from ultimate analysis: $CV = 8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S$ (kcal/kg).
- [x] **WtE plant efficiencies:** thermal = 65–80 %; electrical = 20–30 %.
- [x] **Indian MSW** is high moisture (40–60 %), low CV (800–1,500 kcal/kg), and 40–60 % biodegradable — favouring biological recovery + MRF over incineration.

---

topics.title: Recycle and Reuse: Material and Energy Recovery Operations; Overview of Solid Waste Management Practices in India; Industrial and Hazardous Solid Waste Management - Biomedical Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recycle and Reuse: Material and Energy Recovery Operations; Overview of Solid Waste Management Practices in India; Industrial and Hazardous Solid Waste Management - Biomedical Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Recycle and Reuse: Material and Energy Recovery Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> **Recycling** is the process of collecting, sorting, processing, and remanufacturing waste materials into new products, thereby preventing the wastage of potentially useful materials, reducing consumption of fresh raw materials, reducing energy usage, and minimizing air and water pollution. **Reuse** is the operation by which products or components are used again for the same or a similar purpose without altering their form. **Material Recovery** refers to the extraction of recyclable materials from the waste stream through sorting, separation, and processing operations. **Energy Recovery** is the process of converting non-recyclable waste materials into usable heat, electricity, or fuel through thermal, biological, or chemical processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)**
> Think of your household kitchen. The vegetable peels (wet waste) go into a compost bin and become garden fertilizer — that's **biological material recovery**. The plastic bottles go into a separate bin, get washed, shredded, melted, and remolded into new bottles or polyester fiber for t-shirts — that's **recycling**. The glass jar you use to store pickles again instead of throwing it away is **reuse**. The dry leaves and paper you burn in a controlled incinerator with heat capture to produce steam that turns a turbine — that's **energy recovery**. The hierarchy is simple: **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover Energy → Dispose**.

**Key Operational Definitions (KTU Terminology):**

| Term | Definition | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Reduce** | Minimizing waste generation at source | Avoiding excess packaging |
| **Reuse** | Using an item again for same/different purpose | Refilling glass bottles |
| **Recycle** | Reprocessing waste into raw materials | Melting aluminum cans |
| **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** | A specialized plant that receives, separates, and prepares recyclable materials | Paper, plastic, metal sorting units |
| **Energy Recovery** | Converting waste to energy (WtE) | Incineration with heat recovery, biogas |
| **RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel)** | High-calorific fuel pellets produced from shredded MSW | Co-processing in cement kilns |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The 5R Hierarchy**
> The official KTU module ranks waste management options as: **Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover (energy) → Residual disposal (landfill)**. Marks are often awarded for correctly drawing and labelling this hierarchy.

---

### 1.2 Overview of Solid Waste Management Practices in India

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> Solid Waste Management (SWM) in India refers to the systematic administration of activities that provide for the collection, segregation, transportation, processing, treatment, and disposal of solid waste in accordance with the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** (superseded by **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016**) and the **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM)** framework launched in 2014. Indian SWM practice is governed by a tiered legal hierarchy: Constitutional mandate (Article 48A, 51A(g)) → Central Rules (MoEFCC) → State Policies → Urban Local Body (ULB) implementation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
> Imagine India as a giant household of 1.4 billion people producing ~62 million tonnes of municipal solid waste annually (CPCB estimate). The "household" must decide what to do with leftovers. Some cities (Indore, Mysuru, Surat) are the "clean, organized kitchen" — they segregate at source, compost wet waste, recycle dry waste, and scientifically close landfills. Other cities are the "cluttered kitchen" — everything goes in one bag to a dumpsite, leachate poisons groundwater, and ragpickers sift through hazardous piles. The **Swachh Bharat Mission** is the national campaign to convert every Indian city into the "clean kitchen" model.

**Key Statistics (KTU Frequently Tested):**
- **Per capita waste generation in India:** ~0.45 kg/day (urban) — **bold** for emphasis
- **Total MSW generated:** ~160,038 TPD (Tonnes Per Day) as per CPCB 2021 estimates
- **Waste composition:** 40–60% biodegradable, 10–30% recyclable, 10–30% inert, 1–5% hazardous
- **Landfill sites in India:** ~3,159 (most are unsanitary dumps, not engineered landfills)

---

### 1.3 Industrial and Hazardous Solid Waste Management — Biomedical Waste

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> **Biomedical Waste (BMW)** is defined under the **Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016** (amended 2018, 2022) as any waste generated during the diagnosis, treatment, or immunization of human beings or animals, or in research activities pertaining thereto, or in the production or testing of biologicals. **Hazardous Waste** is defined under the **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** as waste which, by reason of any of its physical, chemical, reactive, toxic, flammable, explosive, or corrosive characteristics, causes danger or is likely to cause danger to health or environment, whether alone or when in contact with other wastes or substances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy**
> Biomedical waste is the "sharp and contaminated kitchen waste" of a hospital. A used syringe is not like a banana peel — if thrown in the regular dustbin, the syringe can prick a ragpicker (causing infection like HIV or Hepatitis B), or the contaminated cotton can spread pathogens. Just like you would separate knives into a child-proof lockable drawer, hospitals must separate BMW into color-coded, puncture-proof bags, autoclave or incinerate the dangerous stuff, and never let it mix with normal municipal waste. The **BMW Rules, 2016** are the "operating manual" for this hospital "kitchen".

**KTU Mandatory Categories — Biomedical Waste (Schedule I, BMW Rules 2016):**

| Category | Type of Waste | Color Code | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yellow** | Anatomical, soiled, chemical, pharmaceutical waste | Yellow bag | Incineration / deep burial |
| **Red** | Contaminated recyclable waste (tubing, bottles, IV sets) | Red bag | Autoclaving / shredding |
| **White (Puncture Proof)** | Sharps — needles, scalpels, blades | White puncture-proof container | Autoclaving / shredding |
| **Blue** | Glassware — broken vials, ampoules | Blue bag | Autoclaving / recycling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Material Recovery Operations — Step-by-Step Logic

**Operational Sequence at a Material Recovery Facility (MRF):**

1. **Receiving & Inspection:** Waste arrives via covered vehicles; weighbridge records mass; visual inspection rejects hazardous/illegal loads.
2. **Tipping Floor:** Manual sorting of bulky items, large cardboard, and obvious contaminants.
3. **Primary Size Reduction:** Shredders and trommel screens (rotating cylindrical sieves) separate by particle size.
4. **Magnetic Separation:** Overhead magnets pull out ferrous metals (iron, steel).
5. **Eddy Current Separation:** High-frequency alternating magnetic field repels non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper).
6. **Optical Sorting:** Near-infrared (NIR) sensors identify plastic polymers (PET, HDPE, PP) and air-jets eject them.
7. **Density Separation (Air Classifier):** Light fractions (paper, film plastic) float on air streams; heavy fractions (glass) fall.
8. **Baling & Dispatch:** Compressed bales are sent to downstream recyclers.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> Each unit operation exploits a **specific physical property**: magnetic susceptibility (ferrous), electrical conductivity (eddy current), reflectivity (optical), and density (air classifier). KTU questions often ask *"Which property is used in XYZ separator?"* — memorize the property-equipment pairing.

---

### 2.2 Energy Recovery Operations — Detailed Analysis

**Pathway A: Thermal Energy Recovery (Incineration with Energy Recovery)**

The waste's calorific value determines energy yield. For Indian MSW with moisture content 40–60%, CV is typically **800–1500 kcal/kg** (compared to coal at ~5000 kcal/kg). Hence, **refuse-derived fuel (RDF)** is prepared by drying and shredding to boost CV to **2500–3500 kcal/kg**.

**Pathway B: Biological Energy Recovery (Anaerobic Digestion)**

Organic fraction (food waste, yard waste, sewage sludge) is degraded by anaerobic bacteria in the absence of oxygen, producing **biogas** (50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂) and digestate (nutrient-rich slurry used as fertilizer).

**Pathway C: Chemical/Thermochemical Conversion**

- **Pyrolysis:** Heating waste at **300–900 °C in absence of oxygen** → produces bio-oil, syngas, char.
- **Gasification:** Heating at **>700 °C with controlled oxygen** → produces syngas (CO + H₂).
- **Plasma Arc Gasification:** Ultra-high temperature (>3000 °C) — destroys even toxic waste.

**Pathway D: Co-processing in Cement Kilns**

Pre-processed RDF/used tires are fed into cement rotary kilns, replacing fossil fuels. Temperatures of **1450 °C** ensure complete destruction of pathogens and toxics.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Recycle, Recovery, and Waste Calculations

> [!WARNING]
> **No pipe `|` characters inside table cells.** Use LaTeX `\vert` if absolute value notation is required. The KTU valuation system penalizes broken markdown tables.

| Sl. No. | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Application / KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy from incineration $E = m \times CV \times \eta$ | $m$ = mass of waste burned (kg), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg), $\eta$ = thermal efficiency (typically 0.20–0.30) | Calculate electricity potential of WtE plant |
| 2 | RDF calorific value $CV_{RDF} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot CV_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Weighted average of components | Estimate energy content of prepared RDF |
| 3 | Biogas yield $Y_{biogas} = m_{VS} \times Y_{s}$ | $m_{VS}$ = mass of volatile solids (kg), $Y_s$ = specific yield (m³ CH₄/kg VS) ≈ 0.20–0.40 | Anaerobic digester sizing |
| 4 | Recovery rate $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | All masses in kg/day | MRF efficiency calculation |
| 5 | Moisture reduction $M_{final} = M_{initial} \times (1 - w)$ where $w$ = water fraction removed | Used in RDF drying | Mass balance across drying unit |
| 6 | Density separation criterion $SG_{separation} = \dfrac{\rho_{heavy}}{\rho_{light}}$ | Specific gravity | Air classifier / float-sink design |
| 7 | Composition percentage $C_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Mass of component / total mass | Indian MSW characterization |
| 8 | Per capita waste $W_{pc} = \dfrac{M_{city}}{P \times 365}$ kg/capita/day | $M_{city}$ = annual waste, $P$ = population | KTU numerical problems |
| 9 | Waste generation rate $G = P \times W_{pc}$ | tonnes/day | Design of collection vehicles |
| 10 | Steam turbine power $P = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_{in} - h_{out}) \times \eta_{turbine}$ | Mass flow rate, enthalpy in/out (kJ/kg) | WtE plant output estimation |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick Tip**
> KTU numerical questions in Module 4 frequently combine **Formula 8** (per capita waste) with **Formula 1** (energy recovery) to create a two-step problem: "Given a city of population X, per capita waste Y, moisture Z, calorific value CV, and thermal efficiency η, calculate the daily electrical energy recoverable." Practice this **composite calculation** type.

---

### 2.4 Solid Waste Management Practices in India — Hierarchical Analysis

**Legal & Policy Framework (KTU-Mandatory):**

| Year | Legislation / Policy | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **1974** | Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | First environmental law |
| **1981** | Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | Air quality standards |
| **1986** | Environment (Protection) Act | Umbrella Act — basis for all waste rules |
| **2000** | Municipal Solid Waste (Management & Handling) Rules | First dedicated MSW rules |
| **2016** | Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 | Revised; mandates segregation at source, 3-bin system |
| **2016** | Construction & Demolition Waste Management Rules | C&D waste handling |
| **2016** | Plastic Waste Management Rules | Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) |
| **2016** | E-Waste Management Rules | E-waste collection targets |
| **2016** | Biomedical Waste Management Rules | 4 color-coded categories |
| **2016** | Hazardous & Other Wastes (Management) Rules | HOWM Rules |
| **2014** | Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) | Behavioral change; toilets + cleanliness |
| **2017** | GST on waste | Economic instrument |
| **2021** | Swachh Bharat Mission - Urban 2.0 | Sustaining ODF (Open Defecation Free) + waste management |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Cross-Connect**
> A common KTU question is: *"Differentiate between the MSW Rules 2000 and 2016."* Key differences: (a) Segregation at source made **mandatory** (was voluntary in 2000), (b) **Extended Producer Responsibility** introduced for packaging waste, (c) **Bulk waste generators** (>20 kg/day) must set up in-house composting, (d) **No landfilling of waste with calorific value >1500 kcal/kg** without energy recovery.

**Indian SWM Technology Adoption (Tier-wise):**

- **Tier 1 cities (Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru):** Mechanical composting, biomethanation, WtE pilots, sanitary landfills.
- **Tier 2 cities (Coimbatore, Kochi, Indore):** Source segregation success (Indore is India's cleanest city — Swachh Survekshan 1st rank multiple times).
- **Tier 3 / rural areas:** Composting pits, biogas plants, material recovery by informal sector (ragpickers).

---

### 2.5 Biomedical Waste Management — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Cornerstone: BMW Treatment Technologies**
> Each color category has a prescribed treatment train. Examiners love asking: *"Why is autoclaving NOT used for anatomical waste?"* — because anatomical waste is bulky, has high moisture, and its bulk requires incineration. Autoclave cannot handle large tissue masses efficiently.

**Treatment Technology Matrix (KTU High-Yield):**

| Technology | Operating Principle | Application to BMW |
|---|---|---|
| **Incineration** | High-temperature oxidation (800–1200 °C) | Yellow bag waste — anatomical, pathological, pharma |
| **Autoclaving** | Saturated steam at 121 °C, 15 psi, 30–60 min | Red bag (microbiologically contaminated) |
| **Hydroclave** | Steam + agitation + drying | Sharps in white containers |
| **Microwave** | Moist heat + radiofrequency | Cultural/pathological waste |
| **Plasma Pyrolysis** | Ultra-high temperature plasma torch (>3000 °C) | Cytotoxic drugs, chemical waste |
| **Deep Burial** | Pit lined with impervious material, ≥50 m from water source | Rural areas — yellow category |
| **Sharp Pit / Encapsulation** | Sharps embedded in cement/foam blocks | White category |
| **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** | Physico-chemical + biological | Liquid BMW from labs |

**BMW Authorization Hierarchy (KTU Required Knowledge):**

- **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** issues **Authorization** under Section 25 of BMW Rules 2016.
- **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** prescribes guidelines and monitors.
- **Common Biomedical Waste Treatment Facility (CBMWTF)** — shared facility for clusters of HCFs.
- **Healthcare Facility (HCF)** must obtain authorization, maintain records, train staff, and submit **Annual Report** by 31st January every year.

**Hazardous Waste Management — KTU Key Concepts:**

- **Manifest System:** Six-copy document tracking waste from "cradle to grave" (generator → transporter → operator → disposer).
- **Prior Informed Consent (PIC):** Required for transboundary movement under **Basel Convention**.
- **Hazardous Waste Categories:** **Schedule-I (47 categories)** of HOWM Rules 2016 lists process-specific waste streams (e.g., B1 — Waste oil, B12 — Zinc ash).
- **Storage Standards:** HWN (Hazardous Waste Number) labelling, compatibility segregation, secondary containment.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Problem: Energy Recovery from a City Waste Stream

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive KTU Numerical — Worked Example with Every Step**
> A city of population **12 lakh (1,200,000)** generates MSW at **0.50 kg/capita/day**. The waste composition is: biodegradable 55%, recyclable (paper, plastic, metal, glass) 30%, inert 10%, others 5%. The biodegradable fraction undergoes anaerobic digestion yielding **0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS**, with volatile solids being 80% of biodegradable mass. Methane has calorific value of **55.5 MJ/m³**. Calculate:
> (a) Total daily MSW
> (b) Biogas volume produced
> (c) Energy content in MJ
> (d) Equivalent kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)

---

**Step (a): Total Daily MSW Generation**

$$G = P \times W_{pc}$$

$$G = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 0.50 \text{ kg/capita/day}$$

$$G = 600{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 600 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Logic:** Per capita generation multiplied by population yields total daily mass.

---

**Step (b): Biodegradable Fraction Mass**

$$m_{bio} = G \times 0.55 = 600{,}000 \times 0.55$$

$$m_{bio} = 330{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Logic:** Biodegradable share is 55% of total waste.

---

**Step (c): Volatile Solids (VS) Mass — Substrate for Biogas**

$$m_{VS} = m_{bio} \times 0.80 = 330{,}000 \times 0.80$$

$$m_{VS} = 264{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Logic:** 80% of biodegradable solids are volatile (degradable); 20% is inert ash/fixed carbon.

---

**Step (d): Biogas (Methane) Volume Yielded**

$$V_{CH_4} = m_{VS} \times Y_s = 264{,}000 \times 0.30$$

$$V_{CH_4} = 79{,}200 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Logic:** Each kg of volatile solids produces 0.30 m³ of methane.

---

**Step (e): Energy Content in MJ**

$$E_{MJ} = V_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} = 79{,}200 \times 55.5$$

$$E_{MJ} = 4{,}395{,}600 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Logic:** Volume of methane multiplied by its calorific value gives total thermal energy.

---

**Step (f): Conversion to kWh**

$$E_{kWh} = \dfrac{E_{MJ}}{3.6} = \dfrac{4{,}395{,}600}{3.6}$$

$$E_{kWh} = 1{,}221{,}000 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Logic:** Divide MJ by 3.6 to get kWh.

---

**Step (g): Equivalent Household Supply**

$$N_{households} = \dfrac{E_{kWh}}{E_{household}} = \dfrac{1{,}221{,}000}{10 \text{ kWh/day}}$$

$$N_{households} \approx 122{,}100 \text{ households}$$

**Logic:** If a household uses ~10 kWh/day, the biogas energy can power ~1.22 lakh homes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Recognition**
> This **6-step derivation** is a classic KTU question pattern. The examiner tests whether you can chain: **waste generation → composition split → VS mass → biogas yield → energy content → unit conversion**. Master this chain for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem: RDF Calorific Value & Mass Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem — Composition-based CV**
> A Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) is prepared from 100 kg of dry MSW with the following composition:

| Component | Mass (kg) | Calorific Value (kcal/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Paper | 30 | 4,000 |
| Plastics (mixed) | 25 | 9,500 |
| Textiles | 10 | 4,500 |
| Wood/yard waste | 20 | 4,400 |
| Inerts (glass, metal, sand) | 15 | 0 |

Calculate the **weighted average calorific value** of the RDF.

---

**Step 1: Identify the formula**

$$CV_{RDF} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \times CV_i)}{m_{total}}$$

**Logic:** Weighted average, weighted by mass fraction.

---

**Step 2: Compute each numerator term**

$$\text{Paper contribution} = 30 \times 4{,}000 = 120{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\text{Plastic contribution} = 25 \times 9{,}500 = 237{,}500 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\text{Textile contribution} = 10 \times 4{,}500 = 45{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\text{Wood contribution} = 20 \times 4{,}400 = 88{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

$$\text{Inerts contribution} = 15 \times 0 = 0 \text{ kcal}$$

**Logic:** Each component's mass multiplied by its CV gives the energy contributed.

---

**Step 3: Sum the contributions**

$$\sum (m_i \times CV_i) = 120{,}000 + 237{,}500 + 45{,}000 + 88{,}000 + 0$$

$$\sum (m_i \times CV_i) = 490{,}500 \text{ kcal}$$

---

**Step 4: Divide by total mass**

$$CV_{RDF} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{100} = 4{,}905 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Logic:** Total energy distributed over 100 kg gives energy per kg.

---

**Step 5: Interpretation**

$$CV_{RDF} \approx 4{,}905 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 20.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams**
> This CV (~4900 kcal/kg) is comparable to **low-grade coal**. It justifies the use of RDF in cement kilns as a fossil-fuel substitute. Without the high-CV plastic fraction, RDF CV would be much lower.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Energy Recovery Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU 14-Mark Answers**
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Compare incineration, pyrolysis, gasification, and anaerobic digestion as WtE routes."* Use this matrix as your backbone.

| Parameter | Incineration | Pyrolysis | Gasification | Anaerobic Digestion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Required** | Excess (stoichiometric) | None (anaerobic) | Limited (sub-stoichiometric) | None (anaerobic) |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 800–1200 | 300–900 | 700–1500 | 30–55 (mesophilic) or 50–60 (thermophilic) |
| **End Product** | Ash, flue gas, heat | Bio-oil, syngas, char | Syngas (CO + H₂) | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate |
| **Energy Efficiency** | 20–30% (electric) | 50–70% (combined) | 40–60% | 30–40% (CHP) |
| **Air Emissions** | High (NOx, dioxins if uncontrolled) | Low (closed system) | Low | Minimal |
| **Suitability for Wet Waste** | Poor (needs drying) | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| **Residence Time** | Seconds (flame) | Minutes to hours | Seconds | 15–40 days |
| **KTU Mnemonic** | "Burn wet-dry" | "Heat-no-oxygen" | "Heat-less-oxygen" | "Bacteria-no-oxygen" |

---

### 3.4 Recovery Rate Calculation — MRF Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Recovery Rate Numerical (3-Mark Quick-Problem Type)**
> A Material Recovery Facility (MRF) receives **50 tonnes/day** of mixed dry waste. After processing, it recovers:
> - Plastics: 8,500 kg/day
> - Paper/cardboard: 12,000 kg/day
> - Ferrous metals: 3,500 kg/day
> - Non-ferrous metals: 1,000 kg/day
> - Glass: 4,000 kg/day
> - Residue (rejected to landfill): 21,000 kg/day

---

**Step 1: Total Recovered Mass**

$$M_{recovered} = 8{,}500 + 12{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 1{,}000 + 4{,}000$$

$$M_{recovered} = 29{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 29 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

---

**Step 2: Apply Recovery Rate Formula**

$$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{29{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{recovery} = 58\%$$

---

**Step 3: Material-Wise Recovery Breakdown**

$$\eta_{plastic} = \dfrac{8{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 17\%$$

$$\eta_{paper} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 24\%$$

$$\eta_{ferrous} = \dfrac{3{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 7\%$$

$$\eta_{non-ferrous} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 2\%$$

$$\eta_{glass} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 8\%$$

$$\eta_{residue} = \dfrac{21{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 42\%$$

**Interpretation:** 58% material recovery; 42% residue goes to landfill or energy recovery. **World-class MRFs target >70% recovery.**
