# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and expects a **crisp 3-5 sentence answer**. Always include the **scope/context** sentence first.

---

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

Define **E-Waste**. List any **four major sources** of E-waste generation in India.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
E-waste, or **Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE)**, refers to discarded electrical and electronic equipment, including all components, sub-assemblies, and consumables, that have reached the end of their useful life, as defined under the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022**.

**Four Major Sources (½ Mark Each = 2 Marks):**

1. **IT and Telecommunication Equipment** — Computers, laptops, mobile phones, tablets, printers.
2. **Consumer Electrical & Electronics** — Televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners.
3. **Household Appliances** — Mixers, toasters, irons, microwaves.
4. **Medical Equipment** — Diagnostic machines, monitoring devices (with radioactive/lead components).

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

What is the **EPR Collection Target** under the Indian E-Waste Rules 2022? Write any **two advantages** of the **Producer Responsibility Organization (PRO)** model.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**EPR Collection Target (1 Mark):**

The phased EPR targets are **10% (Year 1), 20% (Year 2), 30% (Year 3), 40% (Year 4 & 5), and 70% (Year 6 onwards)**, calculated based on the **sales weight** of products placed on the market in **previous years**.

**Advantages of PRO (1 Mark Each):**

1. **Economies of Scale:** Multiple producers share collection and logistics infrastructure, reducing **per-unit compliance costs**.
2. **Wider Reach:** PROs can establish collection networks in **tier-2 and tier-3 cities** that individual producers cannot reach economically.
3. **Expertise:** PROs bring **technical and regulatory expertise** in channelizing e-waste to authorized recyclers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided as **7 marks + 7 marks** across two sub-parts (typically theory + numerical/analysis). Students must answer **ONE out of TWO** alternatives.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **E-Waste Management Hierarchy** with the **5R Principle**. Discuss **why disposal** is the **least preferred option**, citing **two environmental reasons**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A metropolitan city with **population 5 million** generated **8,500 tonnes** of E-waste in 2023. The formal recycling sector collected **3,825 tonnes**. Calculate the **per capita E-waste generation** and the **collection efficiency**. Comment on whether the collection is **EPR-compliant** for **Year 2** (target 20%). **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. The 5R Hierarchy (4 Marks):**

The E-Waste Management Hierarchy follows the **5R Principle** in descending order of environmental preference:

1. **Refuse / Rethink (1 Mark):** Avoid unnecessary purchases; reject **planned obsolescence**. Example: Decline single-use gadgets and prefer modular, repairable electronics.
2. **Reduce (1 Mark):** Design products with longer life, fewer materials, and lower toxicity. Example: Phones with replaceable batteries instead of sealed units.
3. **Reuse (½ Mark):** Donate, refurbish, or resell functional electronics to extend product life. Example: Refurbished laptops for schools.
4. **Recover / Recycle (½ Mark):** Extract precious and base metals, plastics, and glass from discarded equipment via formal recyclers.
5. **Dispose (1 Mark):** As a **last resort**, secure landfilling or controlled incineration with flue-gas treatment.

**2. Why Disposal is Least Preferred (3 Marks):**

**Reason 1 — Leachate Contamination (1.5 Marks):**
Disposal in **unsecured landfills** releases **heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd)** and **BFRs** into the leachate. This contaminates groundwater and bioaccumulates in the food chain, causing **neurological disorders** and **carcinogenic effects**. Once dispersed, remediation costs can be **10× higher than prevention costs**.

**Reason 2 — Resource Loss (1.5 Marks):**
Landfilling permanently loses **precious metals (Au, Ag, Pt)** and **critical raw materials (Co, In, REEs)**. One metric ton of circuit boards contains **40-800× more gold** than one metric ton of mined ore. Disposal thus represents a **lost economic opportunity** and drives **primary mining**, which is environmentally destructive.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert Total E-Waste to kg**

$$W_{\text{total}} = 8{,}500 \text{ tonnes} \times 1000 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{tonne}} = 8.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Per Capita E-Waste**

$$\text{Per Capita E-Waste} = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{population}}} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg}}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ persons}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Per Capita E-Waste} = 1.7 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{person}\cdot\text{year}}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Collection Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{collection}} = \left( \frac{3{,}825}{8{,}500} \right) \times 100\% = 45\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{collection}} = 45\%}$$

**Step 4 — EPR Compliance Check (Year 2 Target = 20%)**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Year 2 EPR Target | 20% |
| Achieved Efficiency | **45%** |

$$\boxed{\text{The collection is FULLY EPR-compliant (45\% > 20\% target)}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark):**

A per capita E-waste of **1.7 kg/person/year** is below the global average of **7.8 kg/person/year**, indicating either **under-reporting** or dominance of the **informal sector** (~95% of E-waste in India). A collection efficiency of **45%** is impressive but does not capture **informal recycling** which still dominates in India.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** framework under the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** of India. Discuss the **three options** available to producers for compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A recycler processes **8,000 kg** of printed circuit boards. The **theoretical gold content** is **0.025%** by weight. After **hydrometallurgical leaching**, **120 g** of pure gold is recovered. Calculate the **recovery yield (%)** and comment on the **efficiency** against the industrial benchmark of **70-90%**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. EPR Framework Definition (2 Marks):**

**Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** is a policy principle under the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022**, where the **Producer, Manufacturer, Importer, and Brand Owner (PMIBO)** are legally and financially responsible for the **end-of-life management** of their products. It operationalizes the **Polluter Pays Principle** by extending the producer's liability beyond the sale point.

**2. Three EPR Compliance Options (3 Marks):**

**Option 1 — Own Collection Mechanism (1 Mark):**
The producer sets up its **own collection centers, take-back schemes, and reverse logistics**. This is feasible only for **large multinationals** with deep financial and technical capacity (e.g., Apple, Samsung).

**Option 2 — Partner with a Producer Responsibility Organization (PRO) (1 Mark):**
The producer joins a **PRO**, a collective body authorized by CPCB to fulfill EPR obligations on behalf of its members. PROs aggregate volume, share infrastructure, and channelize e-waste to **registered recyclers**.

**Option 3 — Buy-Back Credits and Recycling Credits (1 Mark):**
The producer purchases **buy-back credits** from existing collection systems or **recycling credits** from registered recyclers. This provides flexibility for small producers who lack volume.

**3. Additional Mechanism (2 Marks):**

**EPR Portal (1 Mark):** Producers file **annual returns** with **weight reconciliation** via the CPCB EPR Portal, declaring the **quantity produced, collected, recycled, and disposed**.

**Audit and Penalties (1 Mark):** Non-compliance attracts **environmental compensation** of up to **₹5,000 per metric ton** of un-managed E-waste, plus **registration cancellation** for repeated violations.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate the Theoretical Gold Mass in Feed**

$$M_{\text{feed}} = 8{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.00025 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Grams**

$$M_{\text{feed}} = 2.0 \text{ kg} \times 1000 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{kg}} = 2000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Recovery Yield Formula**

$$Y_{\text{recovery}} = \left( \frac{M_{\text{recovered}}}{M_{\text{feed}}} \right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values**

$$Y_{\text{recovery}} = \left( \frac{120 \text{ g}}{2000 \text{ g}} \right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation**

$$\boxed{Y_{\text{recovery}} = 6.0\%}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency Comment (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Calculated Recovery Yield | 6.0% |
| Industrial Benchmark | 70-90% |

The recovery yield of **6.0%** is **drastically below** the benchmark of **70-90%**. This indicates **major process inefficiencies** such as:

1. **Incomplete leaching** — aqua regia contact time may be insufficient.
2. **Losses in slag and filters** during the precipitation step.
3. **Improper pH and temperature control** during hydrometallurgical extraction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When solving numerical problems on E-waste, students commonly:
> - **Forget to convert tonnes to kg** (1 Mark penalty). Always unify units before substitution.
> - **Misinterpret "weight %" as grams/kg** — verify the unit consistency.
> - **Skip the final interpretation sentence** — KTU rewards analytical commentary beyond raw calculation.
> - **Confuse "generation" with "collection"** — generation = products placed on market; collection = waste channelized to recyclers.
> - **Omit the legal/regulatory citation** (E-Waste Rules 2022, Basel Convention) in theory answers — 1 Mark reserved for "applicable standard" reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for E-Waste (Module 4)**

### Core Definitions
- **E-Waste = WEEE** — discarded electrical and electronic equipment under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022.
- **Urban Mine** — E-waste treated as a secondary source of precious metals.
- **EPR** — Producer's legal liability for end-of-life product management.
- **PRO** — Collective body authorized to fulfill EPR obligations on behalf of producers.
- **TSDF** — Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility for hazardous residues.
- **Basel Convention (1989)** — Controls transboundary movement of hazardous waste including E-waste.
- **EU WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU** — International benchmark for E-waste regulation.

### Numerical Formulas to Master
- **Per Capita E-Waste** = Total E-waste (kg) / Total Population.
- **Collection Efficiency** = (W_collected / W_generated) × 100%.
- **Material Recovery Yield** = (M_recovered / M_feed) × 100%.

### Key Benchmark Values
- Global E-waste (2022): **62 Mt/year**.
- Global recycling rate: **17.4%**.
- Projected (2030): **82 Mt/year**.
- Urban Mine gold factor: **40-800× richer than ore**.
- EPR Year targets: **10% → 20% → 30% → 40% → 70%**.

### Hazardous Component-Hazard Pairings (High-Yield)
- **Lead (Pb)** — Solder, CRT glass → Neurotoxic.
- **Mercury (Hg)** — LCD screens → Brain damage.
- **Cadmium (Cd)** — NiCd batteries → Carcinogen.
- **BFRs** — Plastics → Endocrine disruptor + dioxins on burning.

### Process Hierarchy (5R)
- **Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recover → Dispose.**

### Recovery Technologies Compared
- **Pyrometallurgy:** High T, high yield, high emissions.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Low T, high yield, moderate waste.
- **Bio-hydrometallurgy:** Very low T, low yield, eco-friendly.

### Indian Legal Framework
- **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** — supersedes 2016 rules.
- **CPCB EPR Portal** — online compliance filing.
- **Environmental compensation:** up to **₹5,000/tonne** for non-compliance.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Confusing **gross generation** with **channelized collection**.
- Forgetting **unit conversions** (tonnes ↔ kg).
- Omitting **regulatory citations** (E-Waste Rules 2022, Basel Convention).
- Missing the **interpretation step** after numerical computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spent Oil Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spent Oil Waste** (also termed *used oil*, *waste oil*, or *spent lubricant*) is any petroleum-based or synthetic lubricating oil that has become unsuitable for its originally intended service due to the presence of physical contaminants, chemical degradation products, or depletion of performance additives. As per the **Basel Convention (Annex VIII, A3140)** and the **Indian Hazardous Waste Management Rules (HWM Rules, Schedule I, Category 5.1)**, spent oil is classified as a **hazardous waste** owing to its elevated content of **polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs)**, **polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs)**, **heavy metals** (Pb, Cd, Cr, As, Hg), and **chlorinated solvents**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Spent oil is grouped under **Hazardous Waste Streams of Industrial Origin**. Examiners consistently test: (a) **sources & characteristics**, (b) **management hierarchy (waste minimization → recovery → treatment → disposal)**, and (c) **re-refining vs. energy recovery trade-offs**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine the **engine oil in your motorcycle** as the *blood* of the engine — it lubricates moving parts, carries heat away, and prevents metal-to-metal contact. After 3,000–5,000 km, this "blood" becomes contaminated with **metal shavings, soot, water, acids, and burnt fuel residues**. Just as human blood cannot be transfused back after infection, this oil cannot lubricate again in its current form. However, much like **kidney dialysis + blood filtration** can purify blood, spent oil can be:

- **Dialyzed** (filtered & dewatered) for use as **fuel oil** in furnaces.
- **Re-refined** to yield **base oil** nearly equivalent to virgin lubricant.

The "treatment route" chosen depends on the **degree of contamination** — much like a doctor deciding between an antibiotic (low-cost) and ICU dialysis (high-cost).

## 1.3 Categories of Spent Oil

| Category | Examples | Typical Generation Source |
|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Automotive Lubricating Oils** | Crankcase oils, gear oils, transmission fluids | Service stations, automobile workshops |
| **Industrial Lubricants** | Hydraulic oils, turbine oils, compressor oils | Manufacturing plants, power plants |
| **Process Oils** | Cutting oils, quench oils, mould-release oils | Metal working, forging units |
| **Insulating Oils** | Transformer oil, capacitor oil, switchgear oil | Electrical substations |
| **Marine & Aviation** | Cylinder oils, bunker oils, jet engine oils | Shipping, aviation industry |
| **Sewer/Sump Oils** | Bilge oil, oil-water separator skimmings | Refineries, ports, garages |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Not all spent oils are *equal*. **Spent transformer oil** is the most hazardous because it may contain **PCBs (dielectric fluids)** at concentrations of 50–500 ppm, classifying it as **Hazardous Waste Code A3180** under Basel Convention.

## 1.4 Why Spent Oil is Hazardous

| Contaminant | Source of Entry | Hazard Type |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd)** | Wear metals from engine parts | **Toxic** — carcinogenic, neurotoxic |
| **PCBs** | Old transformer oil, capacitor oil | **Persistent Organic Pollutant (POP)** |
| **PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons)** | Combustion in engine cylinders | **Carcinogen** |
| **Chlorinated solvents** | Cleaning fluids mixed in | **Hepatotoxic, ozone-depleting** |
| **Water (1–10 %)** | Coolant leaks, condensation | **Corrosion, microbial growth** |
| **Soot & Carbon (0.5–3 %)** | Incomplete combustion | **Respiratory irritant** |
| **Sulphated ash (0.5–2 %)** | Additive depletion, metal oxides | **Sludge formation** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Fact for KTU Memory Bank:** Approximately **4–8 litres of spent oil per person per year** is generated globally; **only 35–45 %** is properly collected, and **less than 25 %** is re-refined. The remainder is illegally dumped, contaminating soil and groundwater for decades.

---

## 1.5 Legal Framework in India (Frequently Tested in KTU)

- **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** — Schedule I, Entry **5.1** lists "waste oil unfit for original use."
- **Basel Convention (1989)** — India ratified in 1992; transboundary movement of spent oil requires **prior informed consent (PIC)**.
- **CPCB Guidelines** — Prescribe **storage limits** (≤ 20,000 L per unit) and **manifest system** for tracking.
- **BIS Standards (IS 1060, IS 13656)** — Quality norms for re-refined base oil.

---

## 1.6 Spent Oil Generation vs. Lubricating Oil Consumption (Global Statistics)

```
Global Lubricant Demand ≈ 40–42 million tonnes/year
Spent Oil Generated ≈ 24–28 million tonnes/year (≈ 60 % of fresh consumption)
Properly Collected ≈ 14–16 million tonnes (≈ 50–55 %)
Re-refined to base oil ≈ 6–7 million tonnes (≈ 25 %)
Used as industrial fuel ≈ 8–9 million tonnes (≈ 30 %)
Improperly disposed ≈ 10–14 million tonnes (≈ 40–50 %) — Environmental risk
```
> [!NOTE]
> The **resource recovery potential** is enormous. One litre of used oil can be re-refined to **0.6–0.7 L of base oil** or burnt to release **~38 MJ of energy** (≈ 10 kWh of thermal energy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework: The Spent Oil Management Pyramid

The management strategy follows the **5-tier waste hierarchy** (a KTU-favoured question):

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Tier 1: PREVENTION       │  ← Best (eliminate oil use / long-life lubricants)
                    └──────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Tier 2: MINIMIZATION    │  ← Better (extended drain intervals, leak prevention)
                    └──────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Tier 3: RE-REFINING     │  ← Good (acid-clay, vacuum distillation, hydro-treating)
                    └──────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Tier 4: ENERGY RECOVERY  │  ← Acceptable (burn as fuel in cement kilns, boilers)
                    └──────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │  Tier 5: DISPOSAL         │  ← Last resort (secure landfill, incineration with APCDs)
                    └──────────────────────────┘
```

### Tier 1 — Prevention / Source Reduction
- Use of **synthetic long-life lubricants** (synthetic esters, PAO) with drain intervals of 25,000–50,000 km.
- Adoption of **permanent lubricated bearings** in industrial equipment.
- Implementation of **preventive maintenance** to reduce leak-induced consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why prevention matters:** Producing **1 L of virgin lube base oil** requires **~1.6 L of crude oil** in refining. Hence, *not generating* spent oil saves **~3.5× the embodied energy** compared to recycling.

### Tier 2 — Minimization
- **Segregation at source** — separating transformer oil (PCB risk) from crankcase oil.
- **Drainage & collection efficiency** improvement (target ≥ 80 %).
- **Closed-loop systems** in metal working (centrifuge & reuse cutting oil).

### Tier 3 — Re-Refining (Recovery of Base Oil)

Re-refining converts spent oil to **lube base oil** (Group I/II) for blending new lubricants. Three main industrial routes exist:

#### (A) Acid-Clay Process (Traditional, Low Capital)
1. **Dewatering** — heating to 110 °C under vacuum to strip water & light fuels.
2. **Sulphuric acid treatment (93–98 % H₂SO₄)** — dose: 8–15 % v/v; reacts with asphaltic compounds, metals, and oxidized products to form **acid sludge**.
3. **Sedimentation** — acid sludge settles (sludge yield: 15–30 % v/v of feed).
4. **Clay contact filtration (Fuller's earth, 3–10 % w/w)** — removes residual colour bodies and polar compounds at 150–200 °C.
5. **Filtration** — removes spent clay.
6. **Fractionation** — vacuum distillation yields **base oil cuts** (light, medium, heavy spindle).

**Yield:** 55–70 % base oil, 15–25 % acid sludge, 5–10 % fuel oil, 2–5 % losses.

#### (B) Vacuum Distillation + Hydrotreating (Modern)
1. **Pre-treatment** — demetallization, dewatering.
2. **Vacuum distillation** at 350–400 °C and **5–25 mmHg** pressure.
3. **Hydrotreating** with H₂ over **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo catalysts** at 280–350 °C, 30–80 bar → removes S, N, O, and saturates olefins.
4. **Fractionation** → SN-150, SN-500, Bright Stock base oils.

**Yield:** 75–85 % base oil; **no acid sludge** (environmentally preferred).

#### (C) Solvent Extraction (PROP / Meinken Process)
- **Precipitation by propane** (deasphalting).
- **Solvent extraction with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) or furfural** to remove aromatics.
- Higher base oil yield (80 %), but high operating cost.

### Tier 4 — Energy Recovery (Used as Fuel)

When re-refining is uneconomical, spent oil is burnt as fuel in:
- **Cement kilns** (coprocessing) — provides heat + mineral content becomes clinker (best practice).
- **Industrial boilers** — must have **high-efficiency APCDs** (cyclone, ESP, scrubber) for SO₂, HCl, and heavy metal capture.
- **Asphalt plants** — used as flux for road bitumen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Calorific Value of Spent Oil (CV):** 38–42 MJ/kg (similar to diesel ≈ 43 MJ/kg) — but must be pre-treated (filtered, dewatered) to < 1 % water and < 1000 ppm ash.

### Tier 5 — Disposal (Last Resort)
- **Secure hazardous waste landfill** (engineered facility with HDPE liner, leachate collection).
- **High-temperature incineration** (> 1100 °C, 2 s residence time) with **bag filters, scrubbers, dioxin control**.
- **Stabilization/Solidification** of acid sludge using cement + fly ash before landfilling.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** All absolute values in the table below are rendered using `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60\text{°F}}} - 131.5$ | API gravity from specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho_{oil} = SG_{oil} \times \rho_{water}$ | Oil density from specific gravity | kg/m³ (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ at 4 °C) |
| 3 | $Q_{released} = m_{oil} \times CV_{oil}$ | Energy released on combustion of spent oil | kJ (mass in kg, CV in kJ/kg) |
| 4 | $\eta_{re\text{-}ref} = \dfrac{V_{base\,oil}}{V_{spent\,oil,\,feed}} \times 100$ | Re-refining yield | % |
| 5 | $V_{sludge} = V_{feed} \times \dfrac{D_{acid}}{100} \times k_{sludge}$ | Acid sludge volume (k_sludge ≈ 1.5–2.0) | L |
| 6 | $M_{clay} = M_{feed} \times \dfrac{P_{clay}}{100}$ | Fuller's earth mass required | kg |
| 7 | $C_{PCB,\,blend} = \dfrac{C_1 V_1 + C_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ | PCB concentration on mixing two oil streams | mg/L or ppm |
| 8 | $S_{ash} = \dfrac{m_{ash,\,residue}}{m_{oil,\,sample}} \times 100$ | Sulphated ash content | % |
| 9 | $m_{SO_2} = m_{S} \times \dfrac{64}{32} = 2 \, m_{S}$ | SO₂ mass from sulphur in fuel | kg |
| 10 | $Q_{boiler} = \dot{m}_{oil} \times CV_{oil} \times \eta_{boiler}$ | Boiler heat output | kW (mass flow in kg/s) |
| 11 | $TOC_{leachate} = \dfrac{m_{org.\,C}}{V_{leachate}}$ | Leachate organic load (TOC) | mg/L |
| 12 | $C_{oil,\,soil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{soil,\,dry}}$ | Soil oil contamination concentration | mg/kg |

## 2.3 Key Physical/Chemical Properties of Spent Oil (Numerical Memorization for KTU)

| Property | Virgin Engine Oil | Spent Crankcase Oil | Test Method |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Specific Gravity (15 °C)** | 0.88–0.92 | 0.89–0.95 | ASTM D1298 |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | 200–230 | 100–190 (decreased) | ASTM D93 |
| **Viscosity @ 40 °C (cSt)** | 95–150 | 80–220 (variable) | ASTM D445 |
| **Water Content (% v/v)** | < 0.1 | 0.5–10 | ASTM D95 |
| **Total Acid Number (mg KOH/g)** | 1.0–2.5 | 2.5–8.0 | ASTM D974 |
| **Ash Content (% w/w)** | 0.005–0.02 | 0.5–2.5 | ASTM D482 |
| **Heavy Metals (Pb + Cd + Cr, ppm)** | < 10 | 50–1500 | ICP-OES |
| **PCB content (ppm)** | < 2 | 2–500 (transformer oil) | GC-ECD |

## 2.4 Re-Refining Process Comparison (Likely KTU Comparison Question)

| Parameter | Acid-Clay | Vacuum Dist. + Hydrotreating | Solvent Extraction |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| **Base oil yield (%)** | 55–70 | 75–85 | 78–85 |
| **Acid sludge generated** | 15–25 % | < 1 % | < 2 % |
| **Capital cost (INR crore/1000 L/day)** | 0.5–1.0 | 4–7 | 6–10 |
| **Operating cost** | Low | High | High |
| **Environmental burden** | High (acid sludge) | Low | Medium |
| **Product quality (Group)** | I | II | I/II |
| **Reusability of by-products** | Poor | Good (gases to H₂ plant) | Fair |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Cement Industry Coprocessing:** Spent oil fed to kiln main burner at 1450 °C — complete destruction of organics, PCBs, dioxins; minerals become part of clinker.
2. **Marine Industry:** Bilge oil → centrifuge → water removal → fuel oil bunker.
3. **Automotive Sector:** Re-refined base oil → blending with additives → re-marketed as new lubricant.
4. **Power Generation:** Spent oil → fired boiler → steam → electricity (small-scale, 1–10 MWe).
5. **Railway Workshops:** Indian Railways generate ~5000 kL/year spent oil — sent for re-refining under MoEFCC guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — API Gravity, Density & Mass Calculations

> **A re-refining plant receives a tanker load of 18,000 L of spent crankcase oil at 25 °C. Laboratory analysis reports: API gravity = 22 °API at 60 °F, water content = 4.2 % v/v, ash = 1.6 % w/w. Compute: (a) Specific gravity at 60 °F, (b) Mass of oil (water-free) in kg, (c) Mass of water present, (d) Total ash mass.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert API to Specific Gravity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60\text{°F}}} - 131.5 \\[4pt]
22 &= \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 \\[4pt]
\frac{141.5}{SG} &= 153.5 \\[4pt]
SG &= \frac{141.5}{153.5} = 0.9218
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Rearranging the API equation isolates SG; plugging in °API = 22 gives SG ≈ 0.922, confirming spent oil is denser than water (since SG < 1 ⇒ API > 10; spent oil floats on water).

**Step 2 — Compute Density at 60 °F (≈ 15.6 °C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{oil,\,60\text{°F}} &= SG \times \rho_{water} \\[4pt]
&= 0.9218 \times 1000 \,\text{kg/m}^3 \\[4pt]
&= 921.8 \,\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Volume of Water and Volume of Pure Oil**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{water} &= 18{,}000 \times 0.042 = 756 \,\text{L} \\[4pt]
V_{oil,\,pure} &= 18{,}000 - 756 = 17{,}244 \,\text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Pure Oil Volume to Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oil,\,pure} &= 17{,}244 \,\text{L} = 17.244 \,\text{m}^3 \\[4pt]
m_{oil} &= \rho \times V = 921.8 \times 17.244 \\[4pt]
m_{oil} &= 15{,}894 \,\text{kg} \approx 15.9 \,\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of Water**

$$
m_{water} = 756 \,\text{L} \times 1.0 \,\text{kg/L} = 756 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Ash Mass**

$$
m_{ash} = m_{oil} \times \frac{1.6}{100} = 15{,}894 \times 0.016 = 254.3 \,\text{kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** (a) SG = 0.922, (b) m_oil = 15,894 kg, (c) m_water = 756 kg, (d) m_ash = 254.3 kg.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Acid-Clay Re-Refining Material Balance

> **A small re-refining unit processes 5,000 L/day of spent oil by acid-clay process. Acid dose = 10 % v/v (H₂SO₄, 96 % conc.), clay dose = 6 % w/w, sludge yield factor k_sludge = 1.8. Base oil yield = 65 % v/v of feed. Compute: (a) Acid volume required (L), (b) Clay mass required (kg), (c) Acid sludge volume (L), (d) Base oil output (L/day), (e) Energy equivalent if the base oil is combusted at CV = 40 MJ/kg.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Volume of Sulphuric Acid Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{acid} &= V_{feed} \times \frac{D_{acid}}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times \frac{10}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 500 \,\text{L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Feed Oil (for clay calculation)**

Using SG = 0.92 (assumed spent oil average):

$$
m_{feed} = 5{,}000 \,\text{L} \times 0.92 \,\text{kg/L} = 4{,}600 \,\text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Clay Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{clay} &= m_{feed} \times \frac{P_{clay}}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}600 \times \frac{6}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 276 \,\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Acid Sludge Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sludge} &= V_{feed} \times \frac{D_{acid}}{100} \times k_{sludge} \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 1.8 \\[4pt]
&= 900 \,\text{L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Base Oil Output Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{base\,oil} &= V_{feed} \times \frac{\eta}{100} \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 0.65 \\[4pt]
&= 3{,}250 \,\text{L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Base Oil Mass**

$$
m_{base\,oil} = 3{,}250 \times 0.88 = 2{,}860 \,\text{kg/day}
$$

*(SG of base oil ≈ 0.88)*

**Step 7 — Energy Equivalence on Combustion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m_{base\,oil} \times CV \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}860 \,\text{kg} \times 40{,}000 \,\text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 1.144 \times 10^{8} \,\text{kJ/day} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.144 \times 10^{8}}{86{,}400} \,\text{kW} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1{,}324 \,\text{kW} \,\text{(continuous thermal output)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** (a) V_acid = 500 L/day, (b) m_clay = 276 kg/day, (c) V_sludge = 900 L/day, (d) V_base = 3,250 L/day, (e) Q ≈ 1324 kW thermal.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — PCB Dilution Calculation (Mixing Two Oil Streams)

> **A utility company has 3,000 L of spent transformer oil with PCB = 250 ppm. To dilute below the **regulatory limit of 50 ppm**, they blend it with virgin insulating oil (PCB = 2 ppm). How many litres of virgin oil are required?**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Mass Balance for PCB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 V_1 + C_2 V_2 &= C_{target} (V_1 + V_2) \\[4pt]
250 \times 3{,}000 + 2 \times V_2 &= 50 \times (3{,}000 + V_2) \\[4pt]
750{,}000 + 2 V_2 &= 150{,}000 + 50 V_2 \\[4pt]
600{,}000 &= 48 V_2 \\[4pt]
V_2 &= 12{,}500 \,\text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sanity Check (Final Concentration)**

$$
C_{final} = \frac{250 \times 3{,}000 + 2 \times 12{,}500}{3{,}000 + 12{,}500} = \frac{750{,}000 + 25{,}000}{15{,}500} = \frac{775{,}000}{15{,}500} = 50 \,\text{ppm} \,\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** **12,500 L of virgin oil** is needed. This demonstrates that **dilution is *not* a destruction method** — it merely shifts the disposal problem to a much larger volume of contaminated oil. **Thermal destruction at > 1200 °C** is the only legal destruction route for PCBs.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — Energy Recovery Boiler Sizing

> **A cement plant wants to substitute 30 % of its coal (CV = 24 MJ/kg) with spent oil (CV = 40 MJ/kg) for kiln firing at a thermal input of 50 MW. Calculate: (a) Coal-only consumption (kg/h), (b) Oil-only consumption (kg/h), (c) Blend ratio (mass basis) if both fuels are fired.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert 50 MW to kJ/h**

$$
\dot{Q} = 50 \times 10^{3} \,\text{kW} = 50{,}000 \,\text{kJ/s} = 1.8 \times 10^{8} \,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Coal-Only Consumption**

$$
\dot{m}_{coal} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{8} \,\text{kJ/h}}{24{,}000 \,\text{kJ/kg}} = 7{,}500 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Oil-Only Consumption**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{8} \,\text{kJ/h}}{40{,}000 \,\text{kJ/kg}} = 4{,}500 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Blend with 30 % Thermal Share from Oil**

Energy from oil = 0.30 × 1.8 × 10⁸ = 5.4 × 10⁷ kJ/h

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{oil} &= \frac{5.4 \times 10^{7}}{40{,}000} = 1{,}350 \,\text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{coal} &= \frac{0.70 \times 1.8 \times 10^{8}}{24{,}000} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^{8}}{24{,}000} = 5{,}250 \,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Blend Mass Ratio**

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\dot{m}_{coal}} = \frac{1{,}350}{5{,}250} = 0.257 \;\;(\text{or } \approx 1 : 3.9)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** (a) 7,500 kg/h coal, (b) 4,500 kg/h oil, (c) Mass blend = 1,350 kg/h oil + 5,250 kg/h coal; mass ratio oil:coal ≈ 0.26.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Management Options

| Criterion | Re-refining (Acid-Clay) | Re-refining (Vacuum + Hydro) | Energy Recovery (Cement Kiln) | Direct Disposal (Landfill) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Environmental score** | Poor–Medium | **Excellent** | Excellent (high T destroys organics) | Poor (long-term leach risk) |
| **Economic return** | Low | Medium | High (substitutes fuel) | Negative (cost of disposal) |
| **Resource recovery** | High (60–70 % base oil) | Very high (80 %) | Energy only | None |
| **Capital required** | Low | High | Medium (kiln retrofit) | Low (but HWF required) |
| **KTU examination weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spent Oil Management Hierarchy Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Spent Oil Generated] --> detectA{PCB content gt 50 ppm}
    detectA -- Yes --> routeA[Marked as Hazardous PCB Waste A3180]
    detectA -- No --> routeB[Classify by HWM Rule 5.1]

    routeB --> segA[Segregate at Source]
    segA --> collA[Collection and Storage]

    collA --> tier1A{Management Tier Selection}
    tier1A -- Tier 1 --> t1A[Prevention: Use Long-life Lubricants]
    tier1A -- Tier 2 --> t2A[Minimization: Leak Control, Extended Drains]
    tier1A -- Tier 3 --> t3A[Re-refining: Acid Clay or Vacuum Hydro]
    tier1A -- Tier 4 --> t4A[Energy Recovery: Cement Kiln or Boiler]
    tier1A -- Tier 5 --> t5A[Secure Landfill or Incineration]

    t3A --> subA{Re-refining Route}
    subA -- Traditional --> acA[Acid Treatment H2SO4]
    subA -- Modern --> vacA[Vacuum Distillation plus Hydrotreating]

    acA --> sludgeA[Acid Sludge to Stabilization]
    vacA --> baseA[Base Oil SN150 SN500]

    t4A --> fuelA[Fuel for Furnace Combustion]
    fuelA --> apcdA[APCD: Cyclone plus Scrubber plus ESP]

    t5A --> incA[High Temp Incineration gt 1100 C]
    t5A --> landA[Engineered Landfill with HDPE Liner]

    sludgeA --> landA
    incA --> emissionA[Stack Monitoring for Dioxins SO2 HCl]
    style startA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style t1A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style t5A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style baseA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Re-Refining Plant (Acid-Clay Route)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawOil1[Feed Spent Oil Storage Tank] --> dewater1[Dewatering Unit 110 C Vacuum]
    dewater1 --> preheat1[Preheat 80 C]

    preheat1 --> acidMix1[Acid Reactor 93 percent H2SO4]
    acidMix1 --> settle1[Settler for Acid Sludge]

    settle1 --> clayMix1[Clay Contact Column 5 to 10 percent Fullers Earth]
    clayMix1 --> filt1[Plate Filter for Spent Clay]

    filt1 --> vac1[Vacuum Distillation Column 350 to 400 C 25 mmHg]
    vac1 --> frac1[Fractionator]

    frac1 --> light1[Light Spindle SN 100]
    frac1 --> med1[Medium SN 500]
    frac1 --> heavy1[Heavy Bright Stock]

    light1 --> blend1[Additive Blending Tank]
    med1 --> blend1
    heavy1 --> blend1
    blend1 --> prod1[Finished Lubricant Product]

    settle1 --> waste1[Acid Sludge Tank]
    waste1 --> stab1[Stabilization with Cement and Fly Ash]
    stab1 --> landfill1[Secure Hazardous Waste Landfill]

    style prod1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600

```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Treatment Route (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    intake1[Spent Oil Intake Analysis] --> q1{Water Content}
    q1 -- gt 5 percent --> dw1[Step A: Thermal Dewatering]
    q1 -- lt 5 percent --> q2{PCB Content}

    dw1 --> q2
    q2 -- gt 50 ppm --> tpcb1[Route Z: PCB Thermal Destruction gt 1200 C]
    q2 -- lt 50 ppm --> q3{Market for Re-refined Base Oil}

    tpcb1 --> secure1[Document Destruction Certificate]

    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Plant Capital Available}
    q3 -- No --> routeFuel1[Route Y: Cement Kiln Coprocessing]

    q4 -- Low --> acidC1[Route X1: Acid Clay Process]
    q4 -- High --> vacC1[Route X2: Vacuum plus Hydrotreating]

    acidC1 --> quality1[Output: Group I Base Oil]
    vacC1 --> quality1
    routeFuel1 --> fuelOut1[Output: Thermal Energy 40 MJ per kg]
```

```mermaid
    STAB --> LF[Secure Landfill]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Q1]**

**Q1 (3 marks):** Define *spent oil waste*. List any four physical/chemical characteristics that make it a hazardous waste.

**Model Answer:**

*Spent oil waste* is any petroleum-based or synthetic lubricating oil that has become unsuitable for its original service due to contamination by wear metals, oxidation products, water, and combustion residues, and is classified as hazardous under the Indian Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016 (Schedule I, Entry 5.1).

Four characteristics that make it hazardous:

1. **Elevated heavy-metal content** (Pb, Cd, Cr — 50 to 1500 ppm), causing soil and groundwater toxicity.
2. **Presence of PCBs** (especially in spent transformer oil), classified as persistent organic pollutants.
3. **High Total Acid Number (TAN > 2.5 mg KOH/g)** leading to corrosion and skin irritation.
4. **Low flash point (< 100 °C in some cases)** making it a fire hazard during storage and transport.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Q2]**

**Q2 (3 marks):** State the **Basel Convention** classification code for spent oil and explain its transboundary movement implications.

**Model Answer:**

Under the **Basel Convention (Annex VIII)**, spent oil is listed under **A3140** (waste hydraulic oils, brake fluids, and spent mineral oils) and **A3180** (waste oil containing PCBs). 

**Implication:** Transboundary movement (export/import) of A3140 oil requires **Prior Informed Consent (PIC)** from both the exporting and importing countries' competent authorities. Spent oil containing > 50 mg/kg PCBs (A3180) is **banned from transboundary movement** and must be destroyed domestically. In India, this is administered by the **MoEFCC** and tracked via a manifest system.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> *Common mistakes students make in this topic:*
> 1. *Confusing "re-refining" with "recycling" — re-refining specifically regenerates base oil; recycling is the umbrella term.*
> 2. *Forgetting to state the regulatory code (HWM Rule 5.1, Basel A3140) — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.*
> 3. *Not mentioning **PCB segregation** before re-refining — full marks lost if acid-clay process is recommended for PCB oil.*
> 4. *Mixing up units in yield calculations (vol % vs. wt %). Always state which basis you are using.*
> 5. *Skipping the waste-hierarchy justification — Tier 1 (prevention) is always preferred and examiners want you to *justify* your chosen tier.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, describe the **acid-clay process** of spent oil re-refining. State the function of each unit and the typical yield of base oil.

**(b) [7 marks]** A re-refining unit processes 8,000 L/day of spent oil (SG = 0.93, water = 3 %) using the acid-clay process. Acid dose = 12 % v/v, clay dose = 7 % w/w, sludge yield factor k = 1.6, and base oil yield = 68 % v/v. Calculate: (i) mass of dry feed oil, (ii) acid volume, (iii) clay mass, (iv) acid sludge volume, and (v) mass of base oil produced (SG_base = 0.88).

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Acid-Clay Process Description (7 marks)

**Flowchart (text representation for answer sheet):**

```
Spent Oil → Dewatering → Acid Treatment (H₂SO₄) → Settling (Acid Sludge) → 
Clay Contact → Filtration (Spent Clay) → Vacuum Distillation → Base Oil Cuts
                          ↓                              ↓
                  Acid Sludge → Stabilization      Spent Clay → Landfill
```

**Unit-wise functions (write 1–2 sentences per unit for 7 marks):**

1. **Dewatering (Thermal/Vacuum, 110 °C):** Removes free and emulsified water (3–10 %) and light hydrocarbon fractions; prevents acid foaming and emulsions in subsequent stage. **(1 mark)**

2. **Acid Treatment Reactor (93–98 % H₂SO₄, 8–15 % v/v, 30–60 °C, 30 min contact):** Concentrated sulphuric acid reacts with asphaltic compounds, oxidation products, nitrogenous bases, and metal oxides to form **acid sludge** (a heavy tar-like residue) that settles out. **(1 mark)**

3. **Settler / Decanter:** Allows acid sludge (ρ > 1.0) to settle by gravity for 4–8 hours; sludge yield = 15–25 % v/v of feed. **(1 mark)**

4. **Clay Contact Vessel (Fuller's earth, 3–10 % w/w, 150–200 °C):** Adsorbs residual polar compounds, colour bodies, and remaining acid traces; improves colour and oxidation stability. **(1 mark)**

5. **Filtration (Plate & frame filter or rotary vacuum filter):** Removes spent clay and entrained sludge; produces clear, polished oil. **(1 mark)**

6. **Vacuum Distillation (350–400 °C, 5–25 mmHg):** Fractionates treated oil into light spindle (SN 100), medium (SN 500), and heavy bright stock cuts under vacuum to prevent cracking. **(1 mark)**

7. **Yield Statement:** Typical base oil yield = **60–70 % v/v** of feed, with 15–25 % acid sludge, 5–10 % fuel residue, and 2–5 % losses. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Mass of dry feed oil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{water} &= 8{,}000 \times 0.03 = 240 \,\text{L} \\[4pt]
V_{dry\,oil} &= 8{,}000 - 240 = 7{,}760 \,\text{L} \\[4pt]
m_{dry\,oil} &= 7{,}760 \times 0.93 = 7{,}216.8 \,\text{kg} \approx 7{,}217 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark allocation: 1.5 marks**

**(ii) Acid volume:**

$$
V_{acid} = 8{,}000 \times 0.12 = 960 \,\text{L/day}
$$

**Mark allocation: 1 mark**

**(iii) Clay mass:**

$$
m_{clay} = 7{,}217 \times 0.07 = 505.2 \,\text{kg/day}
$$

**Mark allocation: 1 mark**

**(iv) Acid sludge volume:**

$$
V_{sludge} = 8{,}000 \times 0.12 \times 1.6 = 1{,}536 \,\text{L/day}
$$

**Mark allocation: 1.5 marks**

**(v) Mass of base oil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{base} &= 8{,}000 \times 0.68 = 5{,}440 \,\text{L/day} \\[4pt]
m_{base} &= 5{,}440 \times 0.88 = 4{,}787.2 \,\text{kg/day} \approx 4{,}787 \,\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark allocation: 2 marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Sheet (compact form):** (i) 7,217 kg, (ii) 960 L, (iii) 505.2 kg, (iv) 1,536 L, (v) 4,787 kg.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **management hierarchy** of spent oil waste with examples. Why is **prevention (Tier 1)** preferred over **disposal (Tier 5)** in sustainable waste management?

**(b) [7 marks]** A cement kiln co-processes spent oil at 1,450 °C. The kiln thermal input is 60 MW. Coal has CV = 22 MJ/kg, spent oil has CV = 40 MJ/kg. If spent oil replaces 35 % of the coal (by thermal share), calculate: (i) mass flow rate of oil (kg/h), (ii) mass flow rate of coal (kg/h), (iii) the SO₂ emission from oil (assume S = 0.5 % w/w) and from coal (S = 0.8 % w/w).

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Management Hierarchy (7 marks)

| Tier | Option | Example for Spent Oil | Marks |
|------|--------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Prevention** | Use synthetic long-drain lubricants (50,000 km interval) to reduce oil consumption by 60–70 %. | 1.5 |
| 2 | **Minimization** | Leak detection, drip trays, employee training, segregation at source to prevent mixing with PCB oils. | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Re-refining (Recovery)** | Acid-clay or vacuum-hydrotreating to produce base oil (60–85 % yield) — closes the resource loop. | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Energy Recovery** | Co-processing in cement kilns (1450 °C) or industrial boilers with APCDs. | 1.0 |
| 5 | **Disposal** | High-temperature incineration with dioxin control + secure hazardous waste landfill for final ash. | 1.0 |
| — | **Justification (0.5 mark)** | Prevention saves ~3.5× embodied energy, eliminates contamination risk, and aligns with *Sustainable Development Goal 12* (Responsible Consumption & Production). | 0.5 |

**Why prevention is preferred over disposal (2 marks):**

1. **Polluter-pays principle:** Prevention eliminates waste at source, avoiding the entire cost of end-of-pipe treatment and long-term environmental liability.
2. **Resource conservation:** 1 L of virgin base oil needs 1.6 L of crude oil — prevention saves more energy than recycling.
3. **Avoids secondary pollution:** Landfill leachate, incinerator dioxins, and stack emissions are themselves environmental hazards.
4. **Sustainable Development:** Aligns with circular economy and cradle-to-cradle philosophy.

---

#### Part (b) — Energy & Emission Calculation (7 marks)

**(i) Mass flow rate of spent oil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{oil} &= 0.35 \times 60 \,\text{MW} = 21 \,\text{MW} = 21{,}000 \,\text{kJ/s} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{oil} &= \frac{21{,}000 \,\text{kJ/s}}{40 \,\text{kJ/g}} = 525 \,\text{g/s} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{oil} &= 525 \times 3600 / 1000 = 1{,}890 \,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark allocation: 2 marks**

**(ii) Mass flow rate of coal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{coal} &= 0.65 \times 60 = 39 \,\text{MW} = 39{,}000 \,\text{kJ/s} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{coal} &= \frac{39{,}000}{22} = 1{,}772.7 \,\text{g/s} = 6{,}381.8 \,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark allocation: 2 marks**

**(iii) SO₂ emission calculation:**

**From oil (S = 0.5 % w/w):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{S,\,oil} &= 1{,}890 \times 0.005 = 9.45 \,\text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{SO_2,\,oil} &= 9.45 \times \frac{64}{32} = 9.45 \times 2 = 18.9 \,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**From coal (S = 0.8 % w/w):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{S,\,coal} &= 6{,}381.8 \times 0.008 = 51.05 \,\text{kg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{SO_2,\,coal} &= 51.05 \times 2 = 102.1 \,\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total SO₂:**

$$
\dot{m}_{SO_2,\,total} = 18.9 + 102.1 = 121.0 \,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Mark allocation: 3 marks (1 each for oil, coal, total)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Sheet:** (i) 1,890 kg/h oil, (ii) 6,381.8 kg/h coal, (iii) SO₂_total ≈ 121 kg/h (oil contributes 18.9 kg/h, coal 102.1 kg/h).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD MEMORY POINTS for KTU 2024 Spent Oil Module:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Spent oil = used petroleum/synthetic lubricant unfit for original service; **Hazardous Waste Code 5.1** under HWM Rules, 2016; **Basel Code A3140** (general) and **A3180** (PCB-containing).
- ✅ **Sources (6 main):** Automotive crankcase, industrial lubricants, process oils, transformer/insulating oils, marine oils, sewer/sump skimmings.
- ✅ **Hazardous contaminants:** Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, As), PCBs (transformer oil), PAHs, chlorinated solvents, water, soot, sulphated ash.
- ✅ **5-Tier Management Hierarchy:** Prevention → Minimization → Re-refining → Energy Recovery → Disposal.
- ✅ **Acid-Clay Process:** Dewatering (110 °C) → H₂SO₄ treatment (10–15 % v/v) → Settling → Clay contact (5–10 %) → Filtration → Vacuum distillation. **Yield: 60–70 %** base oil.
- ✅ **Vacuum + Hydrotreating:** 75–85 % base oil yield, no acid sludge, higher capital cost, environmentally preferred.
- ✅ **Re-refining yield formula:** $\eta = (V_{base}/V_{feed}) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Acid sludge volume formula:** $V_{sludge} = V_{feed} \times (D_{acid}/100) \times k_{sludge}$.
- ✅ **API gravity formula:** $\text{°API} = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ (use only at 60 °F reference).
- ✅ **Energy from oil:** $Q = m \times CV$ (CV of spent oil ≈ 38–42 MJ/kg).
- ✅ **SO₂ from fuel sulphur:** $m_{SO_2} = 2 \times m_S$ (molecular weight ratio 64/32 = 2).
- ✅ **PCB destruction:** Only by thermal destruction > 1200 °C with 2 s residence time; dilution is **not destruction** and is illegal beyond 50 ppm.
- ✅ **Cement kiln co-processing:** Best disposal route for high-volume spent oil; 1450 °C ensures complete combustion; minerals become clinker.
- ✅ **Storage limit:** ≤ 20,000 L per unit; manifest system mandatory under CPCB.
- ✅ **Re-refining by-product handling:** Acid sludge → stabilization with cement/fly ash → secure landfill. Spent clay → landfill.
- ✅ **Comparison shortcut for exam:** Acid-clay = cheap, dirty; Hydrotreating = clean, expensive; Cement kiln = best disposal + energy recovery.
- ✅ **Numerical conversions:** 1 L oil × SG = kg oil; 1 m³ = 1000 L; 1 MJ/s = 1000 kW.
- ✅ **One-liner for KTU:** *"Prevention > Re-refining > Energy Recovery > Disposal — always justify your tier choice using the waste hierarchy."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Database Management System (DBMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Database Management System (DBMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Database Management System (DBMS)** is a sophisticated, software-engineered suite of computer programs that enables the **definition, creation, maintenance, manipulation, retrieval, and administration** of a database. A database, in turn, is an organized, persistent, and logically coherent collection of related data items, typically structured to model real-world entities and their interrelationships.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT861 - Solid Waste Management)**, DBMS fundamentals are essential because modern municipal and industrial waste management relies heavily on structured data—ranging from **bin-level fill-status telemetry** to **landfill capacity registers, vehicle GPS logs, hazardous-waste manifests, and recycling facility inventories**. Without a robust DBMS, this data remains fragmented and unexploitable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim, Reformulated):**
> A *Database* is a *self-describing collection of integrated records*; a *DBMS* is the *general-purpose software system that manipulates* that database in a *controlled, secure, concurrent, and recoverable* manner, supporting the **ACID** properties—**Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Municipal Waste Ledger"

Imagine the office of a municipal corporation in Kerala before computerization. Every ward supervisor maintained a **physical ledger** to log:

1. **Date** of waste collection
2. **Vehicle** number deployed
3. **Route** taken
4. **Tonnage** collected
5. **Disposal site** used
6. **Complaints** registered
7. **Penalties** levied

These ledgers are the **Database**—the raw, persistent facts.

Now imagine the **Town Engineer** wanting a quick answer: *"How many tonnes of plastic were diverted to the Material Recovery Facility (MRF) in Thiruvananthapuram in Q3 of 2024?"* The Engineer doesn't rummage through 200 ledgers manually. Instead, a clerk uses a **standardized query form**, hands it to a data officer, and within minutes gets the answer. The clerk, the query form, the rules, and the storage cabinet together form the **DBMS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Tier Reality Check (Indian Solid Waste Context):**
> - **Physical Layer (Storage):** Hard disks in server rooms of Smart City Mission command centers (e.g., Kochi Smart City).
> - **Logical Layer (Schema):** The standardized structure describing "Ward", "Vehicle", "Trip", "Bin".
> - **View Layer (Application):** The Swachh Bharat Mission mobile app dashboards that municipal officers see.

---

### 1.3 Core Constituents of a DBMS

| S.No. | Component | Functional Role | Waste-Management Example |
|:-----:|:----------|:----------------|:-------------------------|
| 1 | **Hardware** | Physical storage & processing units | Server at Corporation HQ, RFID readers in bins |
| 2 | **Software** | The DBMS engine itself | PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, MS SQL Server |
| 3 | **Data** | Operational + historical facts | Tonnage records, route logs, citizen complaints |
| 4 | **Procedures** | Documented rules & SOPs | SOP for entering hazardous-waste manifest |
| 5 | **Users** | Administrators, developers, end-users | Sanitary Inspector, Junior Engineer, Citizen |

---

### 1.4 Why DBMS over Flat Files — A KTU Comparative Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU 2-Mark Questions:** Traditional File System vs. DBMS.

| Parameter | File System | DBMS |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----|
| **Data Redundancy** | High (same data in many files) | Minimized via normalization |
| **Data Sharing** | Difficult, siloed | Concurrent multi-user access |
| **Data Integrity** | Enforced by application code | Enforced by constraints (PK, FK, CHECK) |
| **Security** | OS-level permissions only | Granular user-role privileges |
| **Concurrency Control** | Manual locking, prone to conflicts | Automatic transaction management |
| **Backup / Recovery** | Manual, error-prone | Automated logs, ROLLBACK, REDO |
| **Data Independence** | None — program tied to file format | Logical & Physical data independence |
| **Application in SWM** | Unsuitable for city-scale tracking | Ideal for integrated waste-monitoring systems |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a DBMS — The Three-Schema Architecture

The **Three-Schema (or Three-Level) Architecture**, originally proposed by the **ANSI/SPARC** committee in 1975, is the canonical reference model that all KTU examiners expect students to know. It separates the user view from the physical storage through three distinct abstraction layers.

#### Layer 1: Internal Level (Physical Schema)
- Describes the **physical storage structure** of the database.
- Specifies file organization, indexing (B-Tree, Hash), compression, and partitioning.
- **Solid Waste Example:** How the daily `trip_log` table of a fleet of 50 collection vehicles is physically clustered on disk by `trip_date` for fast range queries on monthly tonnage reports.

#### Layer 2: Conceptual Level (Logical Schema)
- Describes the **logical structure of the entire database** for the whole organization.
- Hides physical details; shows entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints.
- **Solid Waste Example:** The corporate data model listing entities such as `Ward`, `Bin`, `Vehicle`, `Trip`, `DisposalSite`, `CitizenComplaint` and their relationships.

#### Layer 3: External Level (View Schema / Sub-schema)
- Describes the **portion of the database** relevant to a particular user group.
- Provides **customized, restricted views** for security and usability.
- **Solid Waste Example:** A Sanitary Inspector sees only the wards under their zone; a Finance Officer sees only billing and penalty data; the public sees only aggregated, anonymized recycling statistics on a web portal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three schemas? — The 'Why' Question KTU Loves to Ask:**
> - It enables **Data Independence**: changing the storage device (internal) or adding a new entity (conceptual) does not break the applications (external).
> - It supports **customization** without exposing sensitive fields (e.g., the aadhaar-linked citizen ID for complaint tracking).
> - It is the foundation of **Distributed Database Management Systems (DDBMS)**, where different schemas may reside on different nodes of a Smart City network.

---

### 2.2 Data Independence — The Two Canonical Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Independence = The ability of a schema at one level to be modified without affecting the schema at the next higher level.**

| Type | Modified Schema | Unaffected Schema | Waste-Management Example |
|:-----|:----------------|:------------------|:-------------------------|
| **Logical Data Independence** | Conceptual | External | Adding a new entity `E_WasteCollectionCenter` to the city database does not alter the Sanitary Inspector's daily-routes view. |
| **Physical Data Independence** | Internal | Conceptual | Migrating from on-premise SQL Server to cloud-based AWS RDS does not require rewriting the SWM application code. |

---

### 2.3 Database Languages — The Engineer's Working Toolkit

| Language | Stands For | Used By | Typical SWM Operation |
|:---------|:-----------|:--------|:-----------------------|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | DBA / Designer | `CREATE TABLE DisposalSite (...)` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | End-user / App | `INSERT INTO Trip VALUES (...)` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | DBA | `GRANT SELECT ON Tonnage TO Jr_Engineer` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Application | `COMMIT;` after a vehicle trip is logged |
| **DQL** | Data Query Language | Analyst | `SELECT SUM(tonnage) FROM Trip WHERE date = '2024-12-25';` |
| **VCL** | View Control Language | Mixed | `CREATE VIEW Daily_SWM_Summary AS ...` |

---

### 2.4 DBMS Classification Matrix

> [!CAUTION]
> **High-Yield 7-Mark Question Territory:** Compare and contrast the following DBMS models in the context of an integrated waste-management system.

| DBMS Model | Logical Structure | Data Retrieval | Best SWM Use Case | Major Limitation |
|:-----------|:-----------------|:--------------|:------------------|:-----------------|
| **Hierarchical** | Tree (parent-child) | Top-down traversal | Legacy solid-waste billing where one ward owns many bins | Cannot model many-to-many (e.g., a vehicle visits many sites) |
| **Network** | Graph (owner-member, sets) | Path navigation | Mapping complex multi-step recycling streams | Application-coupled navigation; hard to restructure |
| **Relational (RDBMS)** | Tables (relations) with rows/columns | Declarative SQL | Master DB for citizen complaints, vehicle fleet, tonnage | Performance degrades with billions of IoT sensor pings |
| **Object-Oriented (OODBMS)** | Objects with methods & encapsulation | Object identity | Modeling complex waste objects (e.g., radioactive waste containers with attached procedures) | Steep learning curve, niche adoption |
| **NoSQL (Document/Key-Value)** | Schema-less JSON or BSON | API-based reads | Real-time bin-fill sensor streams (semi-structured) | Weaker transactional guarantees; eventual consistency |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** DBMS is fundamentally a *systems* subject, so the formulas are not algebraic. They are formal *invariants* and *normative relationships* that KTU examiners love to test.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbol / Form | Meaning in Solid Waste Context |
|:-:|:-----------------|:--------------|:--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Cardinality of a Relation** | $\vert R \vert$ or $n_R$ | Number of tuples in the `Trip` table on a given day |
| 2 | **Degree of a Relation** | $d(R)$ | Number of attributes/columns in the `Trip` table (e.g., 8) |
| 3 | **Domain of an Attribute** | $dom(A_i)$ | Allowed values of `Tonnage` = `{0, 50}` (in kg) |
| 4 | **Primary Key** | $PK(R) \subseteq \{A_1, \dots, A_n\}$ | `trip_id` uniquely identifies a collection trip |
| 5 | **Foreign Key Reference** | $FK \in R_2 \rightarrow PK(R_1)$ | `vehicle_id` in `Trip` references `vehicle_id` in `Vehicle` |
| 6 | **Closure of Attributes (Armstrong)** | $X^+$ | The set of all attributes functionally determined by $X$ — vital for finding minimal keys |
| 7 | **BCNF Condition** | For every $X \rightarrow Y$, $X$ must be a superkey | Guarantees no redundancy in `HazardousWasteManifest` |
| 8 | **Transaction ACID (qualitative)** | $\{A, C, I, D\}$ | A multi-vehicle tonnage upload must be atomic (all-or-nothing) |
| 9 | **Transaction Schedule Serializability** | Conflict-serializable $\equiv$ Precedence graph acyclic | Ensures concurrent trip uploads don't lose data |
| 10 | **Index Selectivity** | $S = \frac{\text{distinct } PK}{\text{total rows}}$ | Higher selectivity = faster lookups on `bin_id` |

---

### 2.6 The Three-Level Mapping — Real-World Engineering Utility

The mapping between schemas is the **practical bridge** that makes the abstract model useful.

$$\text{External} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Conceptual} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Internal}$$

- **External $\to$ Conceptual:** External view of the *Zonal Engineer* is translated to the city-wide schema by the DBMS's view-resolution engine.
- **Conceptual $\to$ Internal:** The logical schema is translated to physical storage — clustering the `Trip` table by `trip_date` on a 10 TB RAID-6 disk array.
- **External $\to$ Internal (direct, when needed):** Some DBMSs permit a direct external-to-internal mapping for performance-critical, read-only data marts (e.g., the daily SWM dashboard cached in Redis).

---

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Solid Waste Engineering

1. **Smart City Command Centers (e.g., Kochi, Bengaluru):** Use real-time DBMS-backed dashboards integrating IoT bin-fill sensors, GPS vehicle tracking, and citizen-app complaints.
2. **Hazardous Waste Tracking (BMW Rules 2016, India):** A centralized DBMS maintains the manifest lifecycle—from generator to transporter to recycler to disposer.
3. **Landfill Capacity Management:** DBMS stores volumetric surveys, settlement rates, and leachate volumes; query engines predict closure timelines.
4. **E-Waste EPR Compliance:** Producers upload annual collection targets to a CPCB/SPCB DBMS; recyclers update processing data.
5. **Swachh Bharat Mission MIS:** A nation-scale DBMS aggregates ward-level cleanliness scores, app downloads, and budget utilization.
6. **Research Analytics:** Universities (IIT, NIT) use DBMS-backed data lakes to publish peer-reviewed studies on Indian MSW characterization.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Real-World Waste Scenario to Relational Schema

> **Pedagogical Goal:** To demonstrate *how* a Solid Waste Management problem statement is converted into a normalized relational schema — step by step, with no shortcuts.

#### Step 1: Identify the Real-World Entity Set

Suppose the City Engineer of Thrissur Corporation wants to maintain a digital ledger of **waste collection trips**. After interviewing Sanitary Inspectors, the engineer identifies the following real-world entity:

> *"Each Trip is performed by exactly one Vehicle and serves exactly one Disposal Site, and records the Tonnage collected from a particular Ward on a particular Date."*

The candidate entity set is:
$$\text{TRIP} = \{\text{Trip, Vehicle, Ward, DisposalSite, Date, Tonnage}\}$$

#### Step 2: Identify Attributes and Their Domains

Let the attribute set be
$$A = \{\text{trip\_id},\; \text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{ward\_no},\; \text{site\_id},\; \text{trip\_date},\; \text{tonnage\_kg}\}$$

Corresponding domains:
$$
\begin{aligned}
dom(\text{trip\_id}) &= \mathbb{Z}^{+}, \quad \text{auto-incremented} \\
dom(\text{vehicle\_no}) &= \text{String}(\text{KL-08-AA-1234}) \\
dom(\text{ward\_no}) &= \{1, 2, \dots, 100\} \\
dom(\text{site\_id}) &= \{DS001, DS002, DS003, DS004\} \\
dom(\text{trip\_date}) &= \text{DATE (YYYY-MM-DD)} \\
dom(\text{tonnage\_kg}) &= \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why these domains?** They restrict impossible values (e.g., a negative tonnage) at the schema level, eliminating application-code bugs.

#### Step 3: Identify the Primary Key

By definition, a **primary key** $K$ must satisfy two properties:

1. **Uniqueness:** No two distinct tuples agree on $K$.
   $$\forall t_1, t_2 \in R:\; t_1 \neq t_2 \implies t_1[K] \neq t_2[K]$$
2. **Irreducibility (Minimality):** No proper subset of $K$ is a key.
   $$\forall A \subsetneq K:\; A \text{ is NOT a key}$$

In our scenario, the engineer assigns a synthetic `trip_id` as the **surrogate primary key**, which trivially satisfies both.

$$PK(\text{TRIP}) = \{\text{trip\_id}\}$$

#### Step 4: Identify Foreign Keys and Their References

A **foreign key** $FK$ in a relation $R_2$ is a set of attributes that reference the **primary key** of another relation $R_1$:

$$FK \subseteq \text{attrs}(R_2), \quad PK \subseteq \text{attrs}(R_1), \quad \forall t \in R_2: t[FK] \in \pi_{PK}(R_1)$$

The full schema design is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{TRIP}} &= (\text{trip\_id},\; \text{vehicle\_no} \to \text{Vehicle},\; \text{ward\_no} \to \text{Ward}, \\
&\quad \text{site\_id} \to \text{DisposalSite},\; \text{trip\_date},\; \text{tonnage\_kg})
\end{aligned}
$$

This means `vehicle_no` references the primary key of a `Vehicle` table, `ward_no` references `Ward`, and `site_id` references `DisposalSite`. The `→` symbol denotes referential integrity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Functional Dependency Closure (Armstrong's Axioms)

> **Why is this important for a Solid Waste engineer?** To design efficient, anomaly-free databases, the engineer must compute the **closure** of an attribute set so that candidate keys can be identified, justifying normalization.

#### Formal Setup

Given a relation $R$ with attributes $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ and a set of **Functional Dependencies (FDs)** $F$, the closure of an attribute set $X$ is denoted $X^+$, and is computed using **Armstrong's Axioms**:

1. **Reflexivity:** $Y \subseteq X \implies X \rightarrow Y$
2. **Augmentation:** $X \rightarrow Y \implies XZ \rightarrow YZ$
3. **Transitivity:** $X \rightarrow Y \text{ and } Y \rightarrow Z \implies X \rightarrow Z$

Three additional derived rules for practicality:
4. **Union:** $X \rightarrow Y \text{ and } X \rightarrow Z \implies X \rightarrow YZ$
5. **Decomposition:** $X \rightarrow YZ \implies X \rightarrow Y$
6. **Pseudotransitivity:** $X \rightarrow Y \text{ and } YZ \rightarrow W \implies XZ \rightarrow W$

#### Worked Example: SWM Vehicle Schema

Let the `Vehicle` table have the following attributes:
$$V = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{capacity\_kg},\; \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{owner\_dept}\}$$

And the FD set:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \{\, \\
&\text{vehicle\_no} \rightarrow \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{vehicle\_no} \rightarrow \text{capacity\_kg}, \\
&\text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{owner\_dept} \\
&\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Task:** Compute $\{\text{vehicle\_no}\}^+$.

**Step 1 (Initial):** Start with the seed set.
$$X^{(0)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no}\}$$

**Step 2 (Apply `vehicle_no → vehicle_type, capacity_kg`):** Add the RHS to the closure.
$$X^{(1)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{capacity\_kg}\}$$

**Step 3 (Apply `vehicle_type → fuel_type, owner_dept`):** Add two more attributes.
$$X^{(2)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{capacity\_kg},\; \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{owner\_dept}\}$$

**Step 4 (Termination):** $X^{(2)} = V$, so the closure spans the entire relation.
$$\{\text{vehicle\_no}\}^+ = V$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** `vehicle_no` is a **superkey**, and because it is a single attribute, it is also a **candidate key**. Hence it is selected as the primary key — uniquely identifying every vehicle in the SWM fleet.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SWM Data Scenarios and Suitable DBMS Constraints

> **Pedagogical Goal:** Map common real-world waste-management data quality issues to the formal DBMS constraint that solves them.

| # | Real-World SWM Data Issue | Formal DBMS Constraint | SQL-Style Enforcement |
|:-:|:--------------------------|:-----------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Vehicle number cannot be left blank | **NOT NULL** | `vehicle_no VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL` |
| 2 | Tonnage cannot be negative | **CHECK** | `tonnage_kg NUMERIC(7,2) CHECK (tonnage_kg >= 0)` |
| 3 | Each vehicle number must be unique | **PRIMARY KEY / UNIQUE** | `PRIMARY KEY (vehicle_no)` |
| 4 | `site_id` in `TRIP` must exist in `DisposalSite` | **FOREIGN KEY** | `FOREIGN KEY (site_id) REFERENCES DisposalSite(site_id)` |
| 5 | Default trip status = 'Scheduled' | **DEFAULT** | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Scheduled'` |
| 6 | Only Kerala-registered vehicles allowed | **CHECK (Domain constraint)** | `CHECK (vehicle_no LIKE 'KL-%')` |
| 7 | `trip_date` cannot be in the future | **CHECK / Trigger** | `CHECK (trip_date <= CURRENT_DATE)` |
| 8 | Ward numbers in [1, 100] | **CHECK** | `CHECK (ward_no BETWEEN 1 AND 100)` |
| 9 | Complaint status = {Open, Closed, Escalated} | **ENUM / CHECK** | `status ENUM('Open','Closed','Escalated')` |
| 10 | Auto-stamp record creation time | **DEFAULT + Trigger** | `created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU 7-Mark Question Territory:** *"For a Solid Waste Management database, illustrate the role of integrity constraints in ensuring data quality. Provide a minimum of five SWM-specific examples with their formal constraint types."*

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Database Architectures in SWM

| Architecture | Description | SWM Use Case | Pros | Cons |
|:-------------|:------------|:-------------|:-----|:----|
| **Centralized DBMS** | Single DB server, single location | Small municipality with one office | Simple, low cost | Single point of failure |
| **Client-Server DBMS** | DB server, multiple clients | Smart City command center | Concurrent users, scalable | Server overload possible |
| **Distributed DBMS (DDBMS)** | Data spread across geographically separate nodes | Multi-district Kerala SWM data federation | Fault-tolerant, fast local access | Complex consistency management |
| **Federated DBMS** | Loosely coupled independent DBs queried as one | National EPR data from multiple SPCBs | Autonomy preserved | Performance bottleneck at federation layer |
| **Parallel DBMS** | Multiple CPUs/disks working on same DB | Petabyte-scale bin-IoT data | High throughput | Expensive hardware |
| **Cloud-Native DBMS** | DB as a managed service (AWS RDS, Azure SQL) | Swachh Bharat Mission MIS hosted on Meghraj cloud | Auto-scaling, managed backups | Data-sovereignty concerns for government data |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 ACID Transaction Flow for a Solid Waste Trip Upload

<!-- mermaid -->
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver as Vehicle Driver App
    participant App as SWM Server
    participant DB as DBMS Engine
    participant Disk as Persistent Storage

    Driver->>App: Submit Trip Data vehicle_no ward_no tonnage_kg
    App->>DB: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    DB->>DB: Check Constraints tonnage_kg greater than zero
    alt Constraint Passes
        DB->>Disk: WRITE Trip Record with Lock
        DB->>Disk: UPDATE Vehicle Total Tonnage
        DB->>DB: COMMIT Atomicity and Durability
        DB-->>App: ACK Success
        App-->>Driver: Trip Logged Confirmation
    else Constraint Fails
        DB->>DB: ROLLBACK Atomicity
        DB-->>App: NACK Constraint Violation
        App-->>Driver: Re enter Tonnage
    end

    Note over DB,Disk: Isolation: Concurrent trips from other vehicles do not interfere
    Note over DB,Disk: Consistency: Database invariants preserved after every commit
