## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define a Database Management System (DBMS). List any four advantages of DBMS over a traditional file system with specific reference to solid waste management data handling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Database Management System (DBMS)** is a software system that enables the definition, creation, manipulation, retrieval, and administration of a database, ensuring controlled, secure, concurrent, and recoverable data access.

Four advantages over a file system, contextualized to SWM:
1. **Reduced Data Redundancy:** A single `Vehicle` table replaces duplicate vehicle records scattered across ward, finance, and operations files.
2. **Data Integrity:** Tonnage values are non-negative by `CHECK` constraint, eliminating manual entry errors.
3. **Concurrent Access:** Multiple Sanitary Inspectors across zones can update trip records simultaneously without overwriting.
4. **Automated Backup and Recovery:** Daily tonnage data survives hardware failure via Write-Ahead Logging, critical for statutory compliance reports.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the concept of three-schema architecture of DBMS with a neat diagram. Why is data independence important in a waste-management information system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **three-schema (ANSI/SPARC) architecture** separates the database into three levels of abstraction:
- **Internal Schema:** Physical storage (RAID arrays, indexing, partitioning) of trip data.
- **Conceptual Schema:** City-wide logical model with entities `Ward`, `Bin`, `Vehicle`, `Trip`, `DisposalSite`.
- **External Schema:** Customized views for each user (e.g., Zonal Engineer's daily routes view).

**Data Independence Importance in SWM:**
- **Logical Data Independence:** Adding a new entity `E_WasteCollectionCenter` does not affect existing Sanitary Inspector views.
- **Physical Data Independence:** Migrating from on-premise servers to the MeghRaj cloud does not require rewriting the SWM application code, ensuring **uninterrupted waste-monitoring services**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> **Module Choice A (14 Marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

### **Question A**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat Entity-Relationship diagram, design a database for a **Municipal Solid Waste Management System** that tracks wards, bins, collection vehicles, trips, and disposal sites. Clearly identify entities, attributes, primary keys, and relationships with appropriate cardinalities.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ACID properties** of a database transaction. For each property, give a specific solid waste management example illustrating what could go wrong without it.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Entities and Attributes:**

| Entity | Attributes (PK underlined) |
|:-------|:--------------------------|
| **Ward** | <u>ward_no</u>, ward_name, zone, population |
| **Bin** | <u>bin_id</u>, <u>ward_no</u> (FK), bin_type, capacity_litres, fill_status_pct |
| **Vehicle** | <u>vehicle_no</u>, vehicle_type, capacity_kg, fuel_type |
| **Trip** | <u>trip_id</u>, <u>vehicle_no</u> (FK), <u>ward_no</u> (FK), <u>site_id</u> (FK), trip_date, tonnage_kg |
| **DisposalSite** | <u>site_id</u>, site_name, site_type, remaining_capacity_tonnes |

**Relationships and Cardinalities:**

- `Ward` **1 — N** `Bin` (one ward contains many bins).
- `Ward` **1 — N** `Trip` (one ward is served by many collection trips).
- `Vehicle` **1 — N** `Trip` (one vehicle performs many trips).
- `DisposalSite` **1 — N** `Trip` (one site receives many trips).
- `Trip` **M — N** `Bin` (one trip may collect from many bins, and one bin may be collected by many trips over time) — resolved by a bridge table `Trip_Bin(trip_id, bin_id, qty_kg)`.

*(For full marks, a labeled ER diagram must accompany this — refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the complete diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Property | Definition | SWM Example — What Goes Wrong Without It |
|:---------|:-----------|:------------------------------------------|
| **Atomicity** | A transaction is all-or-nothing. | If the system crashes after deducting from `Bin.fill_status` but before logging the trip, partial updates leave the inventory inconsistent. |
| **Consistency** | The database moves from one valid state to another. | Without it, tonnage could be credited to the wrong vehicle, violating the `Trip.tonnage_kg >= 0` invariant. |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions appear to execute serially. | Two vehicles uploading the same ward's data simultaneously could double-count tonnage. |
| **Durability** | Committed data survives crashes. | A power failure after trip entry must not lose the record; the Write-Ahead Log guarantees it. |

---

> **Module Choice B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

### **Question B**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Hierarchical, Network, Relational, and NoSQL** database models. For each model, identify one specific limitation that makes it unsuitable for tracking **India's nation-wide Swachh Bharat Mission MIS data**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the **conceptual schema** of a Solid Waste Management database, derive the **closure of the attribute set** `{vehicle_no}` using Armstrong's axioms, given the following functional dependencies on the `Vehicle` table:

$$F = \{\text{vehicle\_no} \rightarrow \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{capacity\_kg},\; \text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{owner\_dept}\}$$

Show that `vehicle_no` is a **superkey** of the relation.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Model | Strength | Limitation for Swachh Bharat Mission MIS |
|:------|:---------|:----------------------------------------|
| **Hierarchical** | Fast one-to-many traversal | Cannot model many-to-many (one ward ↔ many bins ↔ many trips) — too rigid for nationwide MIS. |
| **Network** | Flexible many-to-many | Schema-coupled navigation makes pan-India restructuring painful as new states onboard. |
| **Relational (RDBMS)** | SQL-based, ACID compliant, mature | Vertical scaling bottleneck when ingesting **billions** of citizen-app feedback events daily. |
| **NoSQL** | Horizontal scaling, schema flexibility | Weak transactional ACID guarantees — risky for **financial penalty collections** in SWM billing. |

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Let the attribute set be:
$$V = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{capacity\_kg},\; \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{owner\_dept}\}$$

**Step 1:** Initialize closure.
$$X^{(0)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no}\}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\text{vehicle\_no} \rightarrow \text{vehicle\_type}$ (Reflexivity/Reflexive rule).
$$X^{(1)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type}\}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{capacity\_kg}$, $\text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{fuel\_type}$, $\text{vehicle\_type} \rightarrow \text{owner\_dept}$.
$$X^{(2)} = \{\text{vehicle\_no},\; \text{vehicle\_type},\; \text{capacity\_kg},\; \text{fuel\_type},\; \text{owner\_dept}\} = V$$

**Step 4:** Since $X^{(2)} = V$, by definition:
$$\{\text{vehicle\_no}\}^+ = V$$

**Conclusion:** `vehicle_no` functionally determines all attributes of the `Vehicle` relation. Therefore, `vehicle_no` is a **superkey** of $R$, and being a single attribute, it is also a **candidate key** suitable to be the primary key.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "cardinality" of a relation with "cardinality" of a relationship.** Cardinality of a relation is the *number of rows*; cardinality of a relationship is the *1:1, 1:N, M:N* mapping between entity sets.
> 2. **Don't claim "DBMS = database".** DBMS is the *software*; database is the *data*. This is a 2-mark difference in KTU valuation.
> 3. **In ACID questions, give a domain-specific (SWM) example for each property.** Generic textbook examples lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **For closure derivations, show the iterative steps explicitly.** Writing only the final answer $X^+ = V$ will fetch zero marks. KTU examiners want the iteration trajectory.
> 5. **Avoid | inside markdown tables.** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to prevent table-rendering breakages.
> 6. **Distinguish "schema" from "instance".** Schema is the *structure*; instance is the *current data* at a point in time. Many students lose marks by conflating the two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4)**

- **DBMS** is a *software* system; **database** is a *collection of related data*.
- The **three-schema architecture** (ANSI/SPARC) has three levels: **Internal (Physical)**, **Conceptual (Logical)**, **External (View)**.
- **Logical Data Independence** = conceptual schema can change without affecting external views; **Physical Data Independence** = internal schema can change without affecting the conceptual schema.
- **Five major components** of a DBMS environment: **Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures, Users**.
- **DBMS Languages:** **DDL** (define schema), **DML** (manipulate data), **DCL** (control access), **TCL** (manage transactions), **DQL** (query).
- **Four classical DBMS models:** **Hierarchical** (tree), **Network** (graph), **Relational** (tables), **Object-Oriented** (objects).
- **NoSQL** models (Document, Key-Value, Column-Family, Graph) address **Big Data and semi-structured IoT** requirements of modern SWM.
- **ACID properties** — **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — are the cornerstone guarantees of reliable transactions in waste-data systems.
- **Primary Key** uniquely identifies rows; **Foreign Key** enforces referential integrity between tables.
- **Armstrong's Axioms** (Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity) are used to compute **attribute closures** and identify candidate keys.
- **BCNF/3NF normalization** eliminates update, insertion, and deletion anomalies in SWM data.
- **Cardinality** of a relation = number of rows; **Degree** of a relation = number of attributes.
- **Domain constraints, Entity integrity, Referential integrity, User-defined integrity** are the four main constraint types in a DBMS.
- **Centralized, Client-Server, Distributed, Federated, Parallel, Cloud-native** are the six architectural variants of DBMS.
- **SWM-specific DBMS applications:** bin-IoT telemetry, fleet management, landfill capacity, BMW manifests, EPR compliance, citizen complaint redressal.
- **Smart City Mission, Swachh Bharat Mission MIS, CPCB/SPCB portals** are real-world Indian deployments of DBMS for waste management.
- **Avoid common errors:** confusing DBMS with database; mixing up logical and physical data independence; omitting iteration steps in closure problems; generic (non-SWM) examples in ACID answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geographic Information System (GIS) and Remote Sensing Data in Planning and Management of MSW; Plastic Waste Management and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR): Plastic Waste Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Geographic Information System (GIS) and Remote Sensing Data in Planning and Management of MSW; Plastic Waste Management and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR): Plastic Waste Definition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Geographic Information System (GIS) — Formal Definition A **Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based integrated toolset designed to capture, store, manipulate, analyze, manage, and spatially visualize geographic (spatial) and non-spatial (attribute) data referenced to positions on the Earth's surface. In the context of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) management, GIS serves as a **decision-support system** that overlays multiple thematic layers — such as waste generation density, road network, landfill sites, demographic zones, water bodies, and slope — to optimize collection routes, site selection, and resource allocation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim):** GIS is "a system of hardware, software, data, procedures and people used for the acquisition, storage, analysis and management of spatial data." — Adapted from KTU PECHT861 Module 4. ### 1.2 Remote Sensing (RS) — Formal Definition **Remote Sensing** is the science and art of obtaining information about objects, areas, or phenomena through analysis of data acquired by sensors without being in direct physical contact. For MSW applications, RS uses satellite imagery (e.g., LANDSAT, SENTINEL-2, CARTOSAT) and aerial photography to monitor open dumps, illegal dumping sites, landfill progression, vegetation degradation, and thermal anomalies from waste fires. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: GIS is like Google Maps on Steroids for Waste Managers.** Imagine you are running a pizza delivery business. Google Maps shows you *where* the customers are and *how* to reach them. Now imagine you also overlay: (a) which customers are vegetarian (waste type), (b) how often they order (generation rate), (c) which roads are congested (logistics), and (d) which customers are too far to deliver economically (transfer station siting). That multi-layered, queryable, color-coded map **is GIS**. > > **Analogy: Remote Sensing is like a Drone with X-Ray Eyes.** You cannot climb every waste hill to check its size, but a satellite can photograph the entire city every 5 days, measure heat (thermal RS), and detect illegal dumping from space. ### 1.4 Plastic Waste — Formal Definition (KTU 2024) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plastic Waste** is defined under the **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2018, 2021, 2022)** as *"any plastic product or article discarded after use, including plastic carry bags, multilayered plastic packaging, plastic sheets, expanded polystyrene (thermocol), and plastic waste generated from industrial, commercial, and household sectors."* Categorically, plastic waste is classified into: - **Thermoplastics** — recyclable (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS) - **Thermosetting plastics** — non-recyclable, energy recoverable - **Bio-plastics / Compostable plastics** — newer category under PWM Rules ### 1.5 Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) — Formal Definition **EPR** is a policy instrument under "Polluter Pays Principle" whereby a **producer (manufacturer/importer/brand owner)** is held financially and/or physically responsible for the end-of-life management of their products, particularly plastics and electronic waste. > [!NOTE] > **EPR Intuition:** A soft-drink company sells you a PET bottle. In the 1990s, once you threw it away, it became the municipality's headache. Under EPR (2016 onwards), the company is now legally obligated to *collect it back, recycle it, or fund its recycling*. They have extended their responsibility to the Product's End-of-Life. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components / Functional Architecture of GIS GIS operates through five interlinked subsystems: 1. **Hardware** — Workstations, GPS receivers, plotters, digitizers, servers. 2. **Software** — ArcGIS, QGIS, MapInfo, GRASS, ERDAS Imagine, Google Earth Engine. 3. **Data** — Spatial (raster + vector) and non-spatial (attribute tables). 4. **People** — GIS analysts, planners, environmental engineers. 5. **Procedures** — Standard operating protocols for data capture, QA/QC, and output. ### 2.2 Data Models in GIS | Data Model | Geometry | Example in MSW Planning | Best Used For | |---|---|---|---| | **Vector — Point** | (x, y) coordinates | Bin locations, transfer stations | Asset inventory | | **Vector — Line (Polyline)** | Series of connected points | Waste collection route, road network | Routing optimization | | **Vector — Polygon** | Closed area | Ward boundary, landfill service area | Zoning, density mapping | | **Raster** | Grid of pixels (cells) | Land-use map, population density surface | Continuous spatial phenomena | ### 2.3 Remote Sensing — Electromagnetic Spectrum Logic Every object on Earth emits or reflects **electromagnetic (EM) radiation**. Sensors on satellites record this radiation in distinct bands: - **Visible (0.4 – 0.7 µm)** — used for land-use classification - **Near Infrared (NIR, 0.7 – 1.3 µm)** — biomass, vegetation vigor - **Shortwave Infrared (SWIR, 1.3 – 3.0 µm)** — moisture content of waste - **Thermal Infrared (TIR, 8 – 14 µm)** — detecting waste fires, methane plumes - **Microwave (Radar, > 1 cm)** — cloud-penetrating, surface deformation via InSAR > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Resolution has four types — **Spatial** (pixel size, e.g., 10 m), **Spectral** (number of bands), **Temporal** (revisit period, e.g., 5 days), and **Radiometric** (bit-depth, 8-bit / 16-bit). ### 2.4 Applications of GIS-RS in MSW Management (Step-by-Step Logic) | Step | Activity | GIS / RS Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Waste Generation Mapping** | Overlay population density (census) with waste generation coefficient to produce kg/day/km² surface | | 2 | **Collection Zone Design** | Network analyst → optimize routes minimizing fuel and time | | 3 | **Landfill Site Suitability Analysis** | Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) using weighted overlay of slope, geology, distance from water bodies, population, road access | | 4 | **Monitoring Existing Landfills** | RS-based change detection to compute volume progression (multi-date DEM differencing) | | 5 | **Illegal Dumping Detection** | Object-based image classification (OBIA) of high-resolution imagery | | 6 | **Environmental Impact Assessment** | Buffer analysis around landfill (e.g., 500 m radius) to identify affected receptors | ### 2.5 Plastic Waste Classification (KTU PWM Rules, 2016/2021) | Category | Examples | Recyclability | EPR Applicable | |---|---|---|---| | **Category I — Rigid Plastics** | PET bottles, HDPE containers | High | Yes | | **Category II — Flexible Plastics** | MLPs, plastic pouches | Low (multi-layer issue) | Yes (mandatory) | | **Category III — EPS / Thermocol** | Packaging, disposable cups | Energy recovery only | Yes | | **Category IV — Compostable Plastics** | PLA, PHA blends | Industrial composting | Conditional | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **board-exam high-yield**. Memorize them with units. | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $A_i = \dfrac{W_i}{P_i \cdot \alpha_i}$ | Per-capita waste from zone *i* | kg/cap/day | | 2 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance between bin *i* and depot *j* | km | | 3 | $S = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot r_{k,ij}$ | Weighted Suitability Score for site *ij* | dimensionless (0–1 normalized) | | 4 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{\Delta h \cdot A}{1 - \text{shrinkage factor}}$ | Landfill volume progression from RS DEM | m³ | | 5 | $\text{EPR Obligation (kg)} = \text{EPR Target (\%)} \times \text{Plastic Sold (kg)}$ | Annual EPR compliance target | kg/year | | 6 | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (RS) | dimensionless (-1 to +1) | | 7 | $L_{eff} = L_o \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order landfill gas (CH₄) generation | m³/year | | 8 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Waste Recycled}}{\text{Waste Generated}} \times 100$ | Recycling rate | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder:** In KTU exams, when computing **landfill volume from RS**, always apply the **shrinkage factor** (typically 0.75–0.85 for MSW) to account for compaction and settlement. Marks are lost when this is omitted. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart City Mission (India):** 100 cities use GIS-based MSW dashboards for real-time bin monitoring. - **ISRO's Bhuvan Platform:** Hosts MSW atlases for Indian ULBs. - **Swachh Bharat Mission:** Mandates GIS-based legacy waste site mapping. - **EPR Portal (CPCB):** Plastic packaging producers register and submit quarterly EPR returns. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Suitability Score for Landfill Site Selection **Problem Setup:** A KTU-style 14-mark question typically asks: *"Using GIS-based Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA), derive the suitability index for selecting a landfill site from 4 candidate zones using 5 criteria with weights."* **Analytical Framework:** $$ S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot N_{ij} $$ Where: - $S_j$ = Suitability score of candidate site $j$ - $w_i$ = Weight assigned to criterion $i$ (such that $\sum w_i = 1$) - $N_{ij}$ = Normalized rating of site $j$ for criterion $i$ (0 to 1 scale) - $n$ = Number of criteria **Step-by-Step Worked Example:** Suppose 3 candidate sites (A, B, C) are evaluated on 3 criteria with weights and ratings as below: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Criterion 1 (Distance from water body, } w_1 = 0.4 \text{)} \\ &\text{Criterion 2 (Slope, } w_2 = 0.35 \text{)} \\ &\text{Criterion 3 (Road access, } w_3 = 0.25 \text{)} \\ &\text{Normalization: } N_{ij} = \dfrac{X_{ij} - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}} \quad \text{(for benefit criteria)} \end{aligned} $$ Let raw data be: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Distance (m): A=300, B=500, C=200} \\ &\text{Slope (\%): A=4, B=2, C=6} \\ &\text{Road (m): A=150, B=400, C=250} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Normalize distance (benefit criterion: more distance = better):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{dist,A} &= \frac{300 - 200}{500 - 200} = \frac{100}{300} = 0.333 \\ N_{dist,B} &= \frac{500 - 200}{300} = \frac{300}{300} = 1.000 \\ N_{dist,C} &= \frac{200 - 200}{300} = 0.000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Normalize slope (cost criterion: less slope = better, hence invert):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{slope,A} &= \frac{6 - 4}{6 - 2} = 0.500 \\ N_{slope,B} &= \frac{6 - 2}{4} = 1.000 \\ N_{slope,C} &= \frac{6 - 6}{4} = 0.000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Normalize road access (cost: closer = better):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{road,A} &= \frac{400 - 150}{250} = 1.000 \\ N_{road,B} &= \frac{400 - 400}{250} = 0.000 \\ N_{road,C} &= \frac{400 - 250}{250} = 0.600 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute weighted suitability score:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_A &= (0.4 \times 0.333) + (0.35 \times 0.500) + (0.25 \times 1.000) \\ S_A &= 0.133 + 0.175 + 0.250 = 0.558 \\ \\ S_B &= (0.4 \times 1.000) + (0.35 \times 1.000) + (0.25 \times 0.000) \\ S_B &= 0.400 + 0.350 + 0.000 = 0.750 \\ \\ S_C &= (0.4 \times 0.000) + (0.35 \times 0.000) + (0.25 \times 0.600) \\ S_C &= 0.000 + 0.000 + 0.150 = 0.150 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Rank the sites:** $$ \boxed{S_B (0.750) > S_A (0.558) > S_C (0.150) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Site B is most suitable}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — NDVI Computation for Landfill Vegetation Health $$ \text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}} $$ **Step-by-Step Numerical:** Given a pixel reflectance: NIR = 0.45, Red = 0.15 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NDVI} &= \frac{0.45 - 0.15}{0.45 + 0.15} \\ &= \frac{0.30}{0.60} \\ &= 0.50 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation Matrix:** | NDVI Range | Surface Type | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | $\leq 0.1$ | Bare soil / waste | Active dumping zone | | $0.1 – 0.3$ | Sparse vegetation | Recovering area, partial closure | | $0.3 – 0.6$ | Moderate vegetation | Capped & vegetated landfill | | $\geq 0.6$ | Dense vegetation | Mature post-closure landfill | --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — EPR Target Compliance Calculation **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** *"A beverage company sells 50,000 tonnes of PET bottles in 2024. As per CPCB EPR Guidelines, the mandatory recycling target is 70%. Compute (a) EPR obligation in kg, (b) if only 30,000 tonnes is recycled, what is the compliance shortfall?"* **Step-by-Step Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) EPR Obligation} &= 70\% \times 50{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \\ &= 0.70 \times 50{,}000 \\ &= 35{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \\ &= 35{,}000 \times 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \\ &= 3.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(b) Compliance Shortfall} &= \text{Obligation} - \text{Recycled} \\ &= 35{,}000 - 30{,}000 \\ &= 5{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \\ &= 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Environmental Fee (under CPCB 2022 Amendment):** $$ \text{Fee (₹)} = \text{Shortfall (tonnes)} \times \text{₹5,000/tonne (approx.)} $$ $$ \text{Fee} = 5{,}000 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹2.5 Crore} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GIS-RS Application Summary | MSW Activity | GIS Component Used | RS Component Used | Output Type | |---|---|---|---| | Waste generation zoning | Spatial interpolation (IDW/Kriging) | Population from census + LU/LC | Thematic map | | Route optimization | Network analyst | Road layer from imagery | Route polyline | | Site selection | MCDA + Buffer analysis | DEM from stereo satellite | Suitability map | | Landfill monitoring | Change detection | Multi-date satellite images | Volume map | | Illegal dumping | Spatial clustering | High-res optical imagery | Hotspot map | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — GIS Functional Architecture for MSW ```mermaid flowchart TD input1[Data Input Layer
Census, GPS, Field Survey, Imagery] input2[Spatial Data
Roads, Wards, Water Bodies, Slope] input3[Non-Spatial Data
Waste Quantity, Type, Population] hw[Hardware Layer
Servers, GPS, Plotters] sw[Software Layer
ArcGIS, QGIS, ERDAS] db[Database Layer
PostGIS, Oracle Spatial] proc[Processing Engine
Spatial Analysis, Overlay, Network] mcd[MCDA Suitability Engine
Weighted Overlay] cls[Image Classification
Supervised Unsupervised] out1[Output Layer 1
Route Maps, Density Maps] out2[Output Layer 2
Landfill Suitability Map] out3[Output Layer 3
Illegal Dumping Hotspots] decision[Decision Support
for ULB Engineers] input1 --> db input2 --> db input3 --> db hw --> proc sw --> proc db --> proc proc --> mcd proc --> cls mcd --> out2 cls --> out3 proc --> out1 out1 --> decision out2 --> decision out3 --> decision ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Remote Sensing Workflow for MSW Monitoring ```mermaid flowchart LR sat[Satellite Sensor
Landsat Sentinel Cartosat] em[EM Radiation
Reflected Emitted] sens[Sensor Capture
Multispectral Panchromatic SAR] corr[Radiometric Geometric Correction] enh[Image Enhancement
Contrast Stretching NDVI] class[Classification
Supervised OBIA] feat[Feature Extraction
Landfill Boundary Illegal Dump] chg[Change Detection
Multi Date Stack] vol[Volume Estimation
DEM Differencing] final[Output Maps and Statistics] sat --> em em --> sens sens --> corr corr --> enh enh --> class class --> feat class --> chg chg --> vol feat --> final vol --> final ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — EPR Operational Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD prod[Producer Brand Owner Manufacturer] sell[Plastic Product Sold in Market] use[Consumer Use Phase] disc[Discarded Plastic Waste] collect[Collection System
Municipality PRO Brand Take Back] sort[Sorting and Segregation] rec[Recycling or Co Processing or Energy Recovery] cert[Certificate Issued to Producer] cpcb[CPCB EPR Portal Upload] next[Next Year Production Plan] prod --> sell --> use --> disc --> collect --> sort --> rec --> cert --> cpcb --> next next --> prod ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Flow — Plastic Waste Hierarchy under EPR ```mermaid flowchart TD top[Reduce at Source
Highest Priority] r1[Reuse] r2[Recycle Mechanical or Chemical] r3[Recovery Energy from Waste or Co Processing] r4[Disposal Engineered Landfill
Last Resort] top --> r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** *Define GIS. List any four components of GIS used in MSW management.* **Model Answer:** A Geographic Information System (GIS) is a computer-based system designed to capture, store, retrieve, analyze, manage, and display spatial data referenced to positions on the Earth's surface. It integrates database operations with location-based visualization. Four components of GIS used in MSW management: 1. **Hardware** — GPS, workstations, servers for processing field-collected waste data. 2. **Software** — ArcGIS / QGIS for spatial analysis of landfill suitability. 3. **Data** — Spatial (ward boundary, road network) and non-spatial (waste quantity, demographic) data. 4. **People/Procedures** — GIS analysts and ULBs who interpret outputs for planning. *(3 marks — definition 1 mark + 4 components 2 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** *Define Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). State its significance in plastic waste management.* **Model Answer:** Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) is a policy tool under the Polluter Pays Principle wherein the **producer (manufacturer, importer, or brand owner)** is held responsible for the end-of-life management of their products, including collection, recycling, and environmentally sound disposal. **Significance in Plastic Waste Management:** 1. Shifts financial burden from municipalities to producers, reducing public expenditure. 2. Drives eco-design — producers are incentivized to use recyclable or minimal plastic. 3. Promotes formal recycling sector growth and traceability via CPCB EPR portal. 4. Ensures compliance with Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022). *(3 marks — definition 1.5 marks + significance 1.5 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. In GIS-based landfill suitability questions, **students frequently forget to NORMALIZE the criteria** before applying weights — this costs 2–3 marks. > 2. In EPR calculation problems, **do not mix units** (kg vs tonnes); always state conversions explicitly. > 3. When explaining Remote Sensing, students often miss stating the **EM band and resolution** — the examiner expects at least one example with band and pixel size. > 4. For 14-mark questions, **labelled diagrams (Mermaid or hand-drawn equivalent) carry 2 marks** — never skip them. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Pattern]` **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the role of **Geographic Information System (GIS)** in the planning and management of Municipal Solid Waste. Discuss with a flowchart how GIS integrates spatial and non-spatial data for landfill site selection. **Model Answer:** GIS plays a pivotal role in MSW management across the entire value chain — from waste generation estimation to final disposal monitoring. **Role of GIS in MSW Planning and Management:** 1. **Waste Generation Mapping** — GIS integrates census population data with per-capita waste coefficients to generate waste density surfaces (kg/ha/day), enabling zonal planning. 2. **Collection Route Optimization** — Using Network Analyst tools, GIS determines shortest-path routes for collection vehicles, minimizing fuel consumption, time, and carbon footprint. The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) algorithms are commonly applied. 3. **Landfill Site Suitability Analysis** — Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) integrates: - Distance from water bodies (> 500 m preferred) - Slope (< 10%) - Geological stability - Distance from habitation (> 1 km) - Road accessibility 4. **Buffer and Overlay Analysis** — Determines impact zones around landfills, identifies affected receptors (schools, hospitals, water sources). 5. **Monitoring and Decision Support** — Periodic update of landfill progression, illegal dumping detection, and citizen grievance geotagging. **Flowchart — GIS Integration for Landfill Site Selection:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Block Diagram of this noteset for the functional architecture) Spatial data (topography, water bodies, road network, geology) is layered with non-spatial data (population, waste quantity) in the database. The processing engine applies MCDA → weighted overlay → ranking of candidate sites → suitability map output. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the application of **Remote Sensing (RS)** in monitoring open dumpsites and detecting illegal dumping. Support your answer with a labelled diagram of the RS workflow. **Model Answer:** Remote Sensing provides **synoptic, repetitive, and cost-effective** monitoring of waste sites — especially open dumpsites — without physical access. **Applications:** 1. **Detection of Open Dumpsites** — High-resolution multispectral imagery (e.g., CARTOSAT-3 with 0.3 m PAN) enables identification of waste mounds by their spectral signature (high reflectance in SWIR due to dry waste, low NDVI). 2. **Volume Estimation** — Multi-date Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) from stereo satellite pairs (e.g., CARTOSAT DEMs) allow DEM differencing to compute fill volumes: $$ \Delta V = (h_2 - h_1) \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 - s}\right) $$ where $s$ is the shrinkage factor. 3. **Illegal Dumping Detection** — Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA) classifies image objects based on shape, texture, and spectral properties to flag unauthorized dumps. 4. **Environmental Monitoring** — Thermal IR detects subsurface fires in landfills; NDVI tracks vegetation recovery in capped/closed sites. 5. **Change Detection** — Multi-temporal stack (e.g., 2015, 2018, 2021) reveals expansion of waste footprints. **Labelled RS Workflow:** (Refer to Section 4.2 Block Diagram for the full workflow — Sensor → Correction → Enhancement → Classification → Feature Extraction → Output) --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Pattern]` **(a)** *(7 marks)* Define **Plastic Waste** as per Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022). Classify plastic waste into categories and explain the challenges in managing **Multi-Layered Plastic (MLP)** waste. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** As per Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022), *plastic waste* means any plastic product or article discarded after use, including carry bags, packaging, sheets, expanded polystyrene (thermocol), and plastic waste from industrial, commercial, and household sectors. The 2022 amendment specifically introduced the **"single-use plastic"** prohibition list. **Classification of Plastic Waste:** | Category | Examples | Management Approach | |---|---|---| | 1. Rigid plastics | PET bottles, HDPE containers | Mechanical recycling | | 2. Flexible plastics | Carry bags, pouches | Limited recycling / energy recovery | | 3. Multi-Layered Plastics (MLP) | Tetra-paks, snack pouches, toothpaste tubes | Co-processing in cement kilns | | 4. EPS / Thermocol | Packaging peanuts, cups | Density reduction + energy recovery | | 5. Compostable plastics | PLA, PHA blends | Industrial composting (CPCB-approved) | **Challenges in MLP Management:** 1. **Multi-material composition** — MLPs combine plastic, aluminum, and paper layers bonded by adhesives, making separation economically non-viable. 2. **Low density and high volume** — Transport is uneconomical; baling density is poor. 3. **Contamination** — Food residues hinder recycling. 4. **Lack of indigenous recycling technology** — India depends on cement kilns for co-processing. 5. **Collection inefficiencies** — MLPs are mostly post-consumer packaging, scattered across urban and rural areas. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** in detail. Discuss the obligations of producers, the role of **PROs (Producer Responsibility Organizations)**, and the CPCB EPR registration process. **Model Answer:** **Concept of EPR:** EPR is an environmental policy approach in which **producers are given responsibility — financial and/or physical — for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products**. It operationalizes the Polluter Pays Principle and incentivizes eco-design. **Obligations of Producers (Brand Owners / Importers / Manufacturers):** 1. Register on the **CPCB EPR Portal** and obtain registration. 2. Meet annual **recycling/collection targets** as a percentage of plastic sold. 3. Submit **quarterly and annual returns** with evidence (recycler certificates). 4. Procure **EPR certificates** from registered recyclers or PROs to fulfil shortfall. 5. Contribute to **End-of-Life Waste Management** including co-processing or waste-to-energy. **Role of Producer Responsibility Organizations (PROs):** - PROs act as **collective compliance agencies** for multiple producers. - They aggregate plastic packaging from individual producers, contract recyclers, and issue EPR certificates. - They reduce transaction costs and administrative burden, especially for small producers. - Examples: **Reliance, Bisleri, and industry-affiliated PROs** registered with CPCB. **CPCB EPR Registration Process (Step-by-Step):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Producer registers on CPCB EPR Portal} \\ &\text{Step 2: Submits details — plastic type, quantity sold, brand owner info} \\ &\text{Step 3: CPCB verifies and issues EPR Registration Certificate} \\ &\text{Step 4: Producer reports quarterly plastic quantity sold} \\ &\text{Step 5: Producer procures EPR certificates from recyclers / PROs} \\ &\text{Step 6: Uploads certificates on portal to demonstrate compliance} \\ &\text{Step 7: CPCB audits; non-compliance attracts Environmental Compensation} \end{aligned} $$ **Recent Amendments (2022):** - Mandatory use of **recycled plastic content** (e.g., 30% for PET bottles). - Prohibition of identified **single-use plastic items**. - Extended Producer Responsibility for **multi-layered plastic packaging** with collection targets. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Must memorize before KTU Exam:** - ✅ **GIS = Hardware + Software + Data + People + Procedures** (5 components, 1 mark each in 5-mark question). - ✅ **GIS Data Models = Vector (point, line, polygon) + Raster (grid of pixels)**. - ✅ **Remote Sensing resolution = Spatial, Spectral, Temporal, Radiometric** (4 R's). - ✅ **EM Bands in MSW context = NIR (vegetation), SWIR (moisture), TIR (fires).** - ✅ **NDVI formula = (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red)** — must memorize. - ✅ **Landfill site selection = MCDA using Weighted Overlay** — normalize each criterion before weighting. - ✅ **Landfill volume from RS = ΔV = Δh × A / (1 − shrinkage factor)**. - ✅ **Plastic Waste Definition** = any discarded plastic product, including carry bags, MLP, EPS, thermocol (PWM Rules 2016/2022). - ✅ **MLP** = multi-layered plastic — recycling is non-viable; **co-processing in cement kilns** is the established Indian solution. - ✅ **EPR = Polluter Pays Principle**; producer is responsible for end-of-life. - ✅ **PROs** = Producer Responsibility Organizations — collective compliance platforms. - ✅ **CPCB EPR Portal** = mandatory for all plastic packaging producers; quarterly returns mandatory. - ✅ **Single-use plastic ban (2022)** = covers identified items like straws, cutlery, earbuds, balloon sticks, etc. - ✅ **Recycled plastic content mandate (2022)** = minimum percentage of recycled content in new plastic products. - ✅ **KTU 14-mark Question Pattern** = Sub-part (a) 7 marks + Sub-part (b) 7 marks; **always include a labelled diagram** (worth 2 marks). - ✅ **Examiner Pet Peeves** = missing normalization, missing EM bands, missing shrinkage factor, mixing kg/tonnes — **avoid these at all costs**. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** GIS and Remote Sensing are **technology-driven tools** but the examiner expects you to link them to **engineering decisions** (site selection, route, volume). Always end a GIS-RS answer with a concluding line on **decision support for sustainable MSW management** to fetch full marks.

topics.title: Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT861 - SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Plastic Waste Management (PWM) Rules, 2022** are the principal statutory framework notified by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), Government of India** on **6th July 2022** under Sections 3, 6, and 25 of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. The rules supersede the earlier PWM Rules, 2016 (and its 2018 & 2021 amendments) and govern the **complete life cycle of plastics** — from manufacture, import, distribution, sale, usage, collection, segregation, processing, and end-of-life disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient Anchor Definitions (KTU Board Frequently Tested):**
> - **Producer:** An entity engaged in manufacturing plastic products, raw materials, or intermediates used in plastic manufacturing.
> - **Brand Owner:** A person who sells any plastic product under a registered brand/trademark.
> - **Importer:** An entity that imports plastic raw materials, intermediates, products, or carry bags.
> - **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):** The responsibility of a producer/brand-owner/importer for the environmentally sound management of plastic products until the end of their life cycle.
> - **Single-Use Plastic (SUP):** Plastic items intended to be used once and discarded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Plastic Passport Analogy"**
> Imagine every plastic carry bag or bottle is like a **traveler** with a **passport**. The 2022 Rules demand that the *maker* of the plastic (the Producer) and the *name on the cover* (the Brand Owner) must track that passport-holder from the moment it enters the country (manufacture/import), through its journey in the city (use), all the way back to its recycling bin (end-of-life). Just like immigration officers, the **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** and **SPCBs/PCCs (State Pollution Control Boards)** verify these passports. If the traveler (plastic) is abandoned, the maker is fined. This concept is called **Polluter Pays + Producer Pays**, known globally as **EPR**.

## 1.3 Key Highlights of PWM Rules 2022

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Phased Ban on Single-Use Plastics** effective from **1st July 2022**.
> - **Minimum thickness** of plastic carry bags increased to **75 microns** (later amended to **120 microns** in 2023).
> - **Recycled content mandate:** 30% of plastic packaging must use recycled content (phased implementation by 2030).
> - **Plastic Credit System** introduced as a market-based mechanism.
> - **Environmental Compensation** to enforce the **Polluter Pays Principle**.
> - **Welfare Fee** for unregistered entities.
> - Strict prohibition on identified **single-use plastic items**.

## 1.4 Scope & Applicability

The rules apply to:
- All **producers**, **importers**, **brand owners**, and **plastic waste processors** operating in India.
- All **plastic packaging** and **plastic products** manufactured, sold, or imported.
- All **single-use plastic items** notified under the Schedule.
- Both **pre-consumer** (industrial scrap) and **post-consumer plastic waste** (after end-use).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Framework of the PWM Rules 2022

The Rules are organized around **four pillars** of plastic governance:

### Pillar 1: Registration of Stakeholders
- **Mandatory online registration** on the **CPCB EPR Portal** for all producers, brand owners, importers, and recyclers.
- Validity: **1 to 3 years**, renewable.
- Key data: plastic quantity (tons), category (rigid/flexible/MLP), recycling target.

### Pillar 2: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Targets
EPR is a **policy principle** under which producers are responsible for the entire life cycle of their products.

$$\text{EPR Target} = \text{Plastic Sold/Used} - \text{Plastic Recovered \& Processed}$$

The obligation is calculated annually:

$$EPR_{obligation} = Q_{sold} \times \left(1 - R_{pre-consumer}\right) \times T_{recovery}$$

Where:
- $EPR_{obligation}$ = Net EPR responsibility (in tons).
- $Q_{sold}$ = Quantity of plastic introduced into the market (in tons).
- $R_{pre-consumer}$ = Fraction available for pre-consumer recycling (e.g., 0.20 for rigid plastic).
- $T_{recovery}$ = Target recycling/recovery percentage (e.g., 0.50, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00 phased by year).

### Pillar 3: Recycling & Recycled Content Mandate

For **brand owners and producers** of plastic packaging, **minimum recycled content** must be incorporated (phased implementation, ultimate target: **30% by 2030** for rigid packaging).

$$\%\text{ Recycled Content} = \frac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total\,plastic}} \times 100$$

### Pillar 4: Single-Use Plastic Ban (Effective 01 July 2022)

Schedule items prohibited from manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use:
- Ear buds with plastic sticks, plastic sticks for balloons, plastic flags.
- Candy sticks, ice-cream sticks, polystyrene (Thermocol) for decoration.
- Plastic plates, cups, glasses, forks, spoons, knives, straws, stirrers.
- Plastic pouches for tobacco products.
- Plastic wrapping around invitation cards/gift packs.
- Plastic or PVC banners **less than 100 microns**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Equation / Statement | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GSM from Thickness** | $\text{GSM} = t \times \rho$ | $t$ in microns, $\rho$ in g/cm³, GSM in g/m² |
| 2 | **Thickness from GSM** | $t = \dfrac{\text{GSM}}{\rho}$ | For LDPE: $\rho \approx 0.92$ g/cm³ |
| 3 | **Recycled Content %** | $\%RC = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | Mandatory 30% target for rigid plastic by 2030 |
| 4 | **EPR Net Obligation** | $EPR_{net} = Q_{sold} - Q_{processed}$ | In tons/year |
| 5 | **Environmental Compensation** | $EC = Q_{default} \times R_{rate} \times k$ | $Q_{default}$ in tons, $R_{rate}$ = ₹/ton (CPCB notified), $k$ = duration factor |
| 6 | **Plastic Waste Generated** | $PW = P \times w_{per\,capita} \times f_{plastic}$ | $P$ = population, $w$ = kg/person/day, $f$ = plastic fraction (~3–8%) |
| 7 | **Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{recover} = \dfrac{Q_{recycled}}{Q_{collected}} \times 100$ | Target ≥ 75% (KTU 2024) |
| 8 | **Mass of Carry Bag** | $M = L \times W \times t \times \rho \times 10^{-2}$ | $L,W$ in cm, $t$ in microns, $M$ in grams |

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Densities for Reference (KTU Exam):**
> - LDPE: **0.91–0.94 g/cm³**
> - HDPE: **0.94–0.97 g/cm³**
> - PET: **1.30–1.40 g/cm³**
> - PP: **0.89–0.92 g/cm³**
> - PVC: **1.32–1.45 g/cm³**

## 2.3 Plastic Credit Mechanism

A **Plastic Credit** is a transferable certificate issued by the **CPCB** to a **Producer/Brand Owner/Importer** for processing plastic waste over and above its EPR obligation. These credits can be **bought or sold** on a centralized exchange, encouraging innovation in plastic recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The PWM Rules 2022 directly drive the **recycling industry**, **bio-plastic R&D**, **multi-layer plastic (MLP) processing** (via pyrolysis), and **circular economy** design in packaging — a high-relevance area for KTU Civil/Chemical Engineering graduates entering waste management consultancies.

## 2.4 Penalty & Enforcement Structure

| Mechanism | Description | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental Compensation (EC)** | Monetary penalty on polluters for non-compliance | CPCB & SPCBs |
| **Cancellation/Suspension** of Registration | For repeated violations | CPCB |
| **Welfare Fee** | Charge for operating without registration | CPCB |
| **Prosecution** under EPA 1986 | Imprisonment up to 5 years + fine | Court of Law |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Equivalent Thickness Calculation (Micron ↔ GSM)

**Problem Statement:** A plastic carry bag is mandated to have a **minimum thickness of 75 microns**. Determine its equivalent weight in **GSM** for **LDPE** material (density $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³).

**Given:**
- Thickness, $t = 75$ microns $= 75 \times 10^{-4}$ cm.
- Density, $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³.

**Step 1: Convert thickness to centimeters.**
$$t = 75 \, \mu m = 75 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 75 \times 10^{-4} \, cm$$

**Step 2: Apply the fundamental relation between mass per unit area, thickness, and density.**
$$\text{GSM} \left(\dfrac{g}{m^2}\right) = t \, (m) \times \rho \, \left(\dfrac{g}{m^3}\right) \times 10^4$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**
$$\text{GSM} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.92 \times 10^6$$
$$\text{GSM} = 75 \times 0.92$$
$$\boxed{\text{GSM} = 69 \, g/m^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The 2022 PWM Rules require bags ≥ 75 micron, which directly translates to ~69 GSM for LDPE. This is the benchmark tested in KTU derivations.

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Mass of a Plastic Carry Bag

**Problem Statement:** A carry bag has dimensions **40 cm × 30 cm** with **thickness 75 microns** (LDPE, $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³). Calculate the **mass of one such bag**.

**Given:**
- Length, $L = 40$ cm; Width, $W = 30$ cm.
- Thickness, $t = 75 \times 10^{-4}$ cm.
- Density, $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³.

**Step 1: Calculate the area of the bag in cm².**
$$A = L \times W = 40 \times 30 = 1200 \, cm^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the volume of plastic in cm³.**
$$V = A \times t = 1200 \times 75 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$V = 1200 \times 0.0075 = 9.0 \, cm^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate the mass of the bag.**
$$M = V \times \rho = 9.0 \times 0.92$$
$$\boxed{M = 8.28 \, g}$$

**Step 4: Mass in kg for 1 million bags (industrial-scale context).**
$$M_{million} = 8.28 \times 10^6 \, g = 8280 \, kg \approx 8.28 \, \text{tons}$$

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Per Capita Plastic Waste Generation

**Problem Statement:** A city has a population of **5 million** with **per capita MSW generation of 0.5 kg/day**. If **8% of the waste is plastic**, calculate the **annual plastic waste generated** and the **EPR obligation** assuming a **70% recovery target**.

**Step 1: Daily plastic waste generation.**
$$PW_{daily} = P \times w_{per\,capita} \times f_{plastic}$$
$$PW_{daily} = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.08$$
$$PW_{daily} = 200{,}000 \, \text{kg/day} = 200 \, \text{tons/day}$$

**Step 2: Annual plastic waste generation.**
$$PW_{annual} = 200 \times 365 = 73{,}000 \, \text{tons/year}$$

**Step 3: Net EPR obligation (30% of plastic must be un-recovered if recovery is 70%).**
$$EPR_{obligation} = 73{,}000 \times 0.30$$
$$\boxed{EPR_{obligation} = 21{,}900 \, \text{tons/year}}$$

## 3.4 Derivative 4: Environmental Compensation (EC) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A brand owner introduced **1000 tons** of plastic packaging in a year and processed only **600 tons** against the **100% target**. Calculate the EC if the CPCB-notified rate is **₹5000 per ton** and the default duration factor $k = 1.2$.

**Given:**
- $Q_{sold} = 1000$ tons.
- $Q_{processed} = 600$ tons.
- $R_{rate} = 5000$ ₹/ton.
- $k = 1.2$.

**Step 1: Calculate default quantity.**
$$Q_{default} = Q_{sold} - Q_{processed} = 1000 - 600 = 400 \, \text{tons}$$

**Step 2: Apply EC formula.**
$$EC = Q_{default} \times R_{rate} \times k$$
$$EC = 400 \times 5000 \times 1.2$$
$$\boxed{EC = 24{,}00{,}000 \, \text{(₹ 24 Lakh)}}$$

## 3.5 Derivative 5: Recycled Content Compliance Check

**Problem Statement:** A producer manufactured **2000 tons** of rigid plastic packaging. As per rules, **20% recycled content is mandatory** in Phase 1. If the producer used **350 tons of recycled plastic**, determine **compliance status**.

**Step 1: Calculate required recycled content.**
$$M_{required} = 2000 \times 0.20 = 400 \, \text{tons}$$

**Step 2: Compare with actual usage.**
$$M_{actual} = 350 \, \text{tons} < M_{required} = 400 \, \text{tons}$$

**Step 3: Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{\text{Producer is non-compliant. Shortfall} = 50 \, \text{tons}}$$

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | Pre-2022 Regime (PWM 2016) | PWM 2022 Regime |
|---|---|---|
| **EPR** | Weak / discretionary | Mandatory, certificate-based |
| **SUP Ban** | Selective, state-led | Uniform national list, since 01 July 2022 |
| **Thickness** | 50 microns (carry bag) | **75 microns** (amended to **120 microns**) |
| **Recycled Content** | Not mandated | **Phased 5–30% by 2030** |
| **Plastic Credit** | Absent | **Introduced (CPCB exchange)** |
| **Penalty** | EPA 1986 prosecution | **EC + Welfare Fee + Cancellation** |
| **Registration** | Limited | **Online EPR Portal, all stakeholders** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) as per Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022. Mention the four categories of obligated entities.**

**Model Answer:**
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) is the **policy principle** under which the **producer, brand owner, and importer** are collectively responsible for the **environmentally sound management** of plastic products throughout their **entire life cycle**, including collection, segregation, recycling, processing, and end-of-life disposal.

The four categories of obligated entities are:
1. **Producer** – manufactures plastic raw materials/intermediates/products.
2. **Brand Owner** – sells plastic products under a registered brand/trademark.
3. **Importer** – imports plastic raw materials or products.
4. **Plastic Waste Processor / Recycler** – processes collected plastic waste.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **salient features** and **applicability** of the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022. Discuss the role of **CPCB** and **SPCBs** in enforcement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plastic carry bag has dimensions **45 cm × 35 cm** with a **thickness of 75 microns** (LDPE, $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³). Calculate:
   (i) The **mass of one bag** in grams.
   (ii) The **GSM** of the bag.
   (iii) The **total mass in tons** for **5 lakh** such bags. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

The PWM Rules, 2022 were notified on **6th July 2022** by the **MoEFCC** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

**Salient Features:**
1. **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):** Mandatory responsibility on producers/brand owners/importers for end-of-life management of plastic products.
2. **Single-Use Plastic Ban:** 19 categories of single-use plastic items banned from **1st July 2022**.
3. **Thickness Criterion:** Carry bags must be **≥ 75 microns** (later increased to 120 microns).
4. **Recycled Content Mandate:** Phased introduction of recycled content in plastic packaging, reaching **30% by 2030**.
5. **Plastic Credit System:** Market-based tradable instrument on CPCB exchange.
6. **Penalty & Environmental Compensation:** Polluter Pays Principle enforced.

**Role of CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board):**
- Implementing agency at the national level.
- Operates the **online EPR Portal** for registration.
- Issues **Plastic Credits** and **EPR Certificates**.
- Computes and levies **Environmental Compensation**.

**Role of SPCBs (State Pollution Control Boards):**
- On-ground enforcement and inspection.
- Verification of **annual returns** filed by industries.
- Coordination with municipal bodies for **waste segregation and collection**.

**Applicability:**
The rules apply to **all producers, brand owners, importers, plastic waste processors, and recyclers** operating in India, covering **pre-consumer and post-consumer plastic waste** of all categories.

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $L = 45$ cm, $W = 35$ cm, $t = 75$ microns = $75 \times 10^{-4}$ cm, $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³.

**(i) Mass of one bag:**
$$A = 45 \times 35 = 1575 \, cm^2$$
$$V = 1575 \times 75 \times 10^{-4} = 11.8125 \, cm^3$$
$$M = 11.8125 \times 0.92 = 10.8675 \, g$$
$$\boxed{M \approx 10.87 \, g}$$

**(ii) GSM of the bag:**
$$\text{GSM} = t \times \rho = 75 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.92 \times 10^6 = 69 \, g/m^2$$
$$\boxed{\text{GSM} = 69 \, g/m^2}$$

**(iii) Total mass for 5 lakh bags:**
$$M_{total} = 10.8675 \times 5 \times 10^5 = 5.43 \times 10^6 \, g$$
$$M_{total} = 5437.5 \, kg = 5.4375 \, \text{tons}$$
$$\boxed{M_{total} \approx 5.44 \, \text{tons}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **EPR mechanism** under PWM Rules 2022. Explain the **Plastic Credit** system and the **Welfare Fee** provisions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brand owner introduced **1500 tons** of rigid plastic packaging in a year. The mandatory **recycled content target is 20%**. If the actual recycled plastic used is **250 tons**, determine:
   (i) The **required** and **actual** recycled content in tons.
   (ii) The **compliance status** and **shortfall**, if any.
   (iii) The **Environmental Compensation** if the CPCB rate is **₹4000/ton** and the duration factor is **1.5**, assuming the un-recycled portion contributes to EC. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):**
Under PWM 2022, EPR is the **statutory obligation** placed on **producers, brand owners, and importers** to ensure the **environmentally sound management of plastic products** up to the **end of their useful life**. The responsibility includes:
- Collection of plastic waste.
- Segregation and sorting.
- Recycling, processing, or environmentally sound disposal.
- Filing of **annual returns** on the CPCB portal.

**Plastic Credit System:**
- A **Plastic Credit** is a transferable certificate issued by the **CPCB** to entities that **process plastic waste in excess** of their EPR obligation.
- Credits can be **traded on a centralized exchange**, allowing other entities to **offset shortfalls** in their compliance.
- Encourages **private sector participation** in plastic waste management.
- Credits are awarded in **tons of plastic waste processed**.

**Welfare Fee:**
- A **Welfare Fee** is levied on entities operating **without valid registration** on the CPCB EPR portal.
- The fee is credited to the **Environment Protection Fund (EPF)** managed by the Central Government.
- The fund supports **plastic waste management infrastructure** in States/UTs.

**Environmental Compensation (EC):**
- EC is levied under the **Polluter Pays Principle** for entities failing to meet EPR targets.
- Computed as: $EC = Q_{default} \times Rate \times Duration\,Factor$.
- Defaulting entities are also liable for **prosecution** under **Section 15** of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $Q_{sold} = 1500$ tons, Target $RC = 20\%$, Actual $RC = 250$ tons, Rate = ₹4000/ton, $k = 1.5$.

**(i) Required and Actual Recycled Content:**
$$M_{required} = 1500 \times 0.20 = 300 \, \text{tons}$$
$$M_{actual} = 250 \, \text{tons}$$

**(ii) Compliance Status:**
$$M_{required} > M_{actual} \Rightarrow 300 > 250$$
$$\boxed{\text{Non-compliant. Shortfall} = 300 - 250 = 50 \, \text{tons}}$$

**(iii) Environmental Compensation:**
$$EC = Q_{default} \times Rate \times k$$
$$EC = 50 \times 4000 \times 1.5$$
$$EC = 3{,}00{,}000$$
$$\boxed{EC = \text{₹ 3 Lakh}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** between microns, cm, m, g, and kg is the #1 error in numerical questions.
> 2. **Mixing up GSM with thickness:** GSM = $\text{thickness} \times \text{density}$; both have different numerical values. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Incorrect categorization** of obligated entities (Producer vs. Brand Owner vs. Importer) — a 1-mark deduction per wrong attribution.
> 4. **Not mentioning dates** ("1st July 2022" for SUP ban, "6th July 2022" for notification) — KTU deducts ½ mark for missing key dates.
> 5. **Confusing** *Plastic Credit* with *EPR Certificate* — they are **different instruments** (credit = tradable, certificate = compliance proof).
> 6. In numerical problems, **omitting duration factor $k$** in EC calculation — students often apply a flat rate and lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Recall Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Notification Date:** 6th July 2022 | **SUP Ban Effective:** 1st July 2022.
- **Parent Act:** Environment (Protection) Act, **1986**.
- **Implementing Agency:** **CPCB** | **Enforcement at State Level:** **SPCBs/PCCs**.
- **EPR:** Producer + Brand Owner + Importer jointly responsible.
- **Thickness Rule:** Carry bags ≥ **75 microns** (LDPE: ~69 GSM, amended to 120 microns in 2023).
- **Recycled Content Target (Phased):** 5% → 10% → 20% → **30% by 2030** (rigid plastic packaging).
- **Banned SUP items (10+):** Ear buds, balloon sticks, plastic flags, candy/ice-cream sticks, thermocol decoration, plastic plates/cups/cutlery/straws/stirrers, plastic tobacco pouches, plastic wrapping on cards, banners < 100 microns.
- **Plastic Credit:** Tradable certificate for **over-compliance**, issued by **CPCB**.
- **Welfare Fee:** On **unregistered** entities; goes to **Environment Protection Fund**.
- **Environmental Compensation:** Based on **Polluter Pays Principle**; $EC = Q \times R \times k$.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\text{GSM} = t \times \rho$
  - $M_{bag} = L \times W \times t \times \rho$ (with unit consistency)
  - $\%RC = (M_{recycled}/M_{total}) \times 100$
  - $EPR_{net} = Q_{sold} - Q_{processed}$
  - $EC = Q_{default} \times R \times k$
- **Penalties:** EC + Registration cancellation + Prosecution under EPA 1986 (5 yrs imprisonment + fine).
- **Pre-consumer vs. Post-consumer:** Industrial scrap vs. consumer-end waste.
- **Densities (must know):** LDPE ~0.92, HDPE ~0.95, PET ~1.38, PP ~0.90, PVC ~1.40 g/cm³.
- **Plastic Waste Fraction in MSW (India):** ~3–8% (urban); ~15% in some metros.
